# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define diffusion welding. List any four process parameters that control the quality of a diffusion bond.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Diffusion welding is a solid-state joining process in which two clean metallic surfaces are held together at an elevated temperature (typically 0.5 to 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>) and a moderate pressure for a specified time in a protective atmosphere, such that atomic diffusion across the faying interface produces a metallurgical bond without melting, macroscopic deformation, or the use of filler metal.

**Four Process Parameters:**

1. **Bonding temperature** — controls diffusion coefficient and creep rate
2. **Bonding pressure** — promotes intimate contact by collapsing asperities
3. **Bonding time** — allows sufficient atomic transport to close voids
4. **Surface preparation** — ensures clean, oxide-free metallic contact

*(Atmosphere may be cited as the 5th parameter for full credit.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is diffusion welding carried out in a vacuum or inert atmosphere? What is the typical bonding temperature range?**

**Model Answer:**

**Why vacuum/inert atmosphere:** At the elevated bonding temperature, freshly exposed metallic surfaces are highly reactive and readily form **oxide layers**. These oxide films act as diffusion barriers and prevent metal-to-metal atomic contact, resulting in weak or unbonded regions. A vacuum (typically 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ Torr) or high-purity inert gas (Ar/He) **prevents oxidation, removes adsorbed gases, and ensures a clean interface** for diffusion.

**Typical bonding temperature range:** **0.5 to 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>** (homologous temperature scale, in Kelvin), e.g., 800–950 °C for Ti, 900–1100 °C for stainless steels.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternatives are provided below — **students attempt ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **three stages of bond formation** in diffusion welding. How does the void volume fraction change across these stages?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss in detail the **effect of bonding temperature, pressure, and time on the quality of a diffusion welded joint**, citing the underlying mechanisms (Fick's law, power-law creep).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**The Three Stages of Bond Formation:**

**Stage 1 — Initial Asperity Deformation (Plastic Flow Dominated):**
- When the two surfaces are first brought into contact under pressure, real contact occurs only at the tips of microscopic asperities (peaks).
- These asperities yield and deform plastically, rapidly increasing the true contact area from < 1% to **20–40%** of the nominal area.
- Large interfacial voids form between the now-flatter peaks.
- **Dominant mechanism:** Plastic deformation / power-law creep.
- **Time scale:** Seconds to a few minutes.

**Stage 2 — Diffusion-Controlled Bond Growth:**
- The increased contact area provides more sites for atomic diffusion.
- Material transport from the contact regions (high chemical potential) to the void surfaces (low chemical potential) occurs via:
  - **Surface diffusion** (fast, but does not close voids)
  - **Grain boundary diffusion** (along newly formed grain boundaries crossing the interface)
  - **Volume (lattice) diffusion** (slowest, but most effective at void shrinkage)
- The voids shrink and become more spherical (decrease in surface area → decrease in energy).
- Bond area grows from 20–40% to **> 90%**.
- **Dominant mechanisms:** Grain boundary diffusion and volume diffusion, governed by Fick's laws.

**Stage 3 — Interface Disappearance (Homogenization):**
- The remaining isolated voids close via continued volume diffusion and creep.
- Grain boundaries migrate across the original interface, driven by the reduction in interfacial energy.
- The original faying plane becomes metallurgically indistinguishable from the parent metal.
- **Void volume fraction → ~ 0%; bond strength ≈ base metal strength.**

**Void Volume Fraction Across Stages:**

| Stage | Approximate Void Fraction | Bond Area Fraction |
|---|---|---|
| Initial | 40–60% | < 1% |
| End of Stage 1 | 20–30% | 20–40% |
| End of Stage 2 | 1–5% | > 90% |
| End of Stage 3 | < 0.1% | ~ 100% |

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** A three-panel diagram showing two opposing surfaces with progressive reduction of voids across stages 1, 2, and 3, with grain boundaries drawn across the interface in stage 3.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Effect of Bonding Temperature:**
- Diffusion coefficient D = D₀ exp(-Q/RT) increases **exponentially** with temperature.
- A rise in T from 0.5 T<sub>m</sub> to 0.8 T<sub>m</sub> can increase D by **3–4 orders of magnitude**, dramatically accelerating void closure.
- **Excessive T** causes:
  - Grain coarsening (reduces mechanical strength)
  - Unwanted phase transformations (e.g., α → β in Ti)
  - Eutectic reactions in dissimilar metal pairs
- **Optimal T** balances diffusion rate against microstructural degradation.

**Effect of Bonding Pressure:**
- Pressure σ enhances **power-law creep rate**: ε̇ = A σⁿ exp(-Q/RT).
- Higher σ → faster plastic flow in stage 1, faster void closure in stage 2.
- **Excessive σ** causes macroscopic dimensional change of the part, violating tolerances (typically ≤ 1–2% deformation is acceptable).
- Pressure is generally kept as low as possible consistent with full bonding.

**Effect of Bonding Time:**
- Diffusion distance follows the parabolic law: **x ≈ √(D·t)**.
- Bond strength increases as √t — initially rapid, then plateauing.
- **Excessive time** offers diminishing returns and risks grain growth and intermetallic formation.
- Optimal time is determined empirically and is the **cheapest parameter to adjust** in production.

**Underlying Mechanisms Recap:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Fick's First Law** (J = -D ∂C/∂x) governs the rate of atomic flux during stages 2 and 3.
> - **Power-law creep** governs plastic deformation in stage 1 and continued void closure in stage 3.
> - The **Arrhenius equation** quantifies the temperature dependence of both diffusion and creep.

**Concluding Remark (typical KTU closing line):** The four process parameters must be optimized within a defined **process window** to achieve full bonding without excessive deformation, grain growth, or interfacial contamination.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **industrial applications of diffusion welding**. Why is it preferred for joining titanium alloys, nickel superalloys, and dissimilar metal combinations?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A diffusion welding operation is to bond two sheets of a titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V, T<sub>m</sub> ≈ 1660 °C). The activation energy for self-diffusion is Q = 150 kJ/mol and D₀ = 8.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s. Calculate the diffusion coefficient and the diffusion distance after 2 hours at a bonding temperature of 900 °C.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Industrial Applications of Diffusion Welding:**

**1. Aerospace Industry:**
- **Titanium honeycomb structures** for aircraft skin panels — DFW preserves the thin foil geometry that fusion welding would melt.
- **Titanium-to-stainless steel transition joints** in engine pylons.
- **Reentry vehicle heat shield assemblies** using refractory metal alloys.

**2. Gas Turbine / Power Generation:**
- **Cooled turbine blades and vanes** made of nickel-based superalloys (e.g., Inconel 718, Mar-M247) — internal cooling passages cannot be fusion welded.
- **Combustor liner assemblies** requiring thin-gauge superalloy joining.

**3. Nuclear Industry:**
- **Zirconium alloy fuel rod end-plugs** — DFW avoids the HAZ and stress-corrosion cracking susceptibility of fusion welds.
- **Beryllium-to-stainless steel joints** in fusion reactor diagnostics.

**4. Tool and Die Manufacturing:**
- **Carbide-to-steel tool joints** in drills, end-mills — DFW avoids the thermal cracks that arise from the large CTE mismatch in fusion welding.
- **Diamond tool fabrication** for grinding wheels.

**5. Electronics and Thermal Management:**
- **Copper-to-aluminum joints** in heat exchangers and LED heat sinks — DFW overcomes the formation of brittle Cu-Al intermetallics by allowing controlled diffusion layers.
- **Semiconductor lead attachments** in power devices.

**Why DFW is Preferred for These Materials:**

| Material / Combination | Reason for DFW Preference |
|---|---|
| **Titanium alloys** | High chemical reactivity at high T → DFW's vacuum atmosphere prevents contamination; no HAZ softening in α+β Ti alloys |
| **Nickel superalloys** | Susceptible to HAZ cracking (liquation, strain-age cracking) under fusion welding — DFW avoids the problem |
| **Dissimilar metals** | No brittle intermetallic melt pool forms; intermetallics grow only by controlled solid-state diffusion; large CTE mismatch accommodated |

**Other Advantages Cited:** No filler metal, no distortion, retention of parent metal properties, ability to bond non-metals (ceramics, cermets, graphite), and capability to produce complex multi-material sandwich structures in a single operation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- T<sub>m</sub> = 1660 °C = 1933 K
- Q = 150 kJ/mol = 150,000 J/mol
- D₀ = 8.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s
- T = 900 °C = **1173 K**
- t = 2 hours = **7200 s**

**Step 1 — Calculate the exponent Q/RT:**

$$ \frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{150{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173} $$

$$ \frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{150{,}000}{9752.3} \approx 15.38 $$

**Step 2 — Calculate the diffusion coefficient D:**

$$ D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{Q}{RT} \right) = 8.6 \times 10^{-10} \cdot \exp(-15.38) $$

$$ \exp(-15.38) \approx 2.10 \times 10^{-7} $$

$$ D = 8.6 \times 10^{-10} \times 2.10 \times 10^{-7} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate the diffusion distance (using the √(Dt) model):**

$$ \delta = \sqrt{D \cdot t} = \sqrt{1.81 \times 10^{-16} \times 7200} $$

$$ \delta = \sqrt{1.303 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 1.14 \text{ μm} $$

**Step 4 — Verify the homologous temperature:**

$$ \frac{T}{T_m} = \frac{1173}{1933} \approx 0.607 $$

This lies within the recommended bonding range of **0.5 to 0.8 T<sub>m</sub>** ✓

**Conclusion:** The diffusion coefficient at 900 °C is approximately **1.81 × 10⁻¹⁶ m²/s**, yielding a diffusion distance of about **1.14 μm** in 2 hours. This is sufficient to bridge residual microvoids at the interface and produce a sound metallurgical bond. To increase the diffusion distance to ~ 3 μm (a common design target), one could either raise the temperature to ~ 1000 °C or extend the bonding time.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Diffusion Welding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in Arrhenius calculations — costs full marks on the numerical part.
> 2. **Confusing the three stages** — many students merge stages 2 and 3. Clearly distinguish: Stage 1 = plastic deformation; Stage 2 = diffusion-dominated void shrinkage; Stage 3 = grain boundary migration.
> 3. **Stating "high temperature" without giving the homologous range (0.5–0.8 T<sub>m</sub>)** — examiners specifically look for this.
> 4. **Ignoring surface preparation** in process parameter lists — it carries equal weight with T, P, and t.
> 5. **Writing "diffusion welding is the same as brazing"** — this is a common conceptual error. DFW is **solid-state autogenous**; brazing uses a **liquid filler metal** and is a *different* process.
> 6. **Drawing a fusion welding setup** when asked to sketch a DFW machine — examiners look for the **vacuum chamber, heating system, and pressure ram** specifically.
> 7. **Confusing Fick's First and Second Laws** — First Law is for steady-state flux; Second Law is for transient concentration profiles. Examiners will test this distinction.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **Definition:** Diffusion welding = solid-state joining by atomic diffusion, no melting, no filler.
> - **Process parameters (4T + 1A):** Temperature, Time, Pressure, Surface finish, Atmosphere.
> - **Bonding temperature:** 0.5 to 0.8 T<sub>m</sub> (homologous scale).
> - **Bonding pressure:** 1 to 100 MPa (low compared to forge welding).
> - **Bonding time:** 10 min to 4 hours; bond strength ∝ √t.
> - **Surface roughness:** Ra < 0.8 μm required.
> - **Atmosphere:** Vacuum (10⁻⁴ Torr) or high-purity inert gas — non-negotiable.
> - **Three stages:** (1) Asperity deformation, (2) Diffusion-controlled bond growth, (3) Interface disappearance.
> - **Governing equations:**
>   - Fick's First Law: J = -D (∂C/∂x)
>   - Fick's Second Law: ∂C/∂t = D (∂²C/∂x²)
>   - Arrhenius: D = D₀ exp(-Q/RT)
>   - Diffusion distance: x ≈ √(Dt)
>   - Power-law creep: ε̇ = A σⁿ exp(-Q/RT)
> - **Key advantage:** No HAZ, no cast structure, no distortion, can join dissimilar metals & ceramics.
> - **Key limitation:** Long cycle time, high equipment cost, small component size (chamber-limited).
> - **Top applications:** Aerospace (Ti honeycombs), turbines (Ni-superalloy blades), nuclear (Zr fuel rods), tools (carbide-to-steel), electronics (Cu-Al heat sinks).
> - **Examiner's mantra:** *T governs kinetics, P governs contact, t governs completeness, surface governs quality, atmosphere governs chemistry.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cold Pressure Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cold Pressure Welding (CPW)** is a *solid-state welding process* in which a permanent metallic bond is produced between two workpieces by applying a controlled uniaxial or multi-axial compressive force at **room temperature** (or at temperatures typically **below 0.5 T<sub>m</sub>**, where T<sub>m</sub> is the melting point in Kelvin), causing extensive plastic deformation at the interface that breaks up surface contaminants and oxides, exposes virgin metal, and enables metallic continuity through adhesion, interlocking, and short-range diffusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Cold Pressure Welding is classified under **"Pressure Welding"** processes (per IS 8124 / AWS C3.0), the same family that includes forge welding, roll welding, and ultrasonic welding. The unifying feature is that **fusion is absent** — bonding occurs entirely in the *solid phase*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms accepted in KTU answer scripts:** Cold Welding, Cold Pressure Bonding, Pressure Welding (in older texts), Solid-State Pressure Welding. The term *Cold Pressure Welding* is preferred in 2024 Scheme question papers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are trying to stick two pieces of **soft modeling clay** together. The clay surfaces are dusty and dry. If you simply press them lightly, they don't stick — the dust layer acts as a barrier. But if you press them **very hard** and **smear** the surfaces against each other (a shearing motion), the soft clay squeezes out sideways, fresh material extrudes from inside, and the two pieces become one continuous lump.

Cold Pressure Welding works on the **exact same principle**, except the "clay" is a real metal like aluminum or copper, and the "squeezing" is done by a hydraulic press, rolls, or punches. The room-temperature softness of soft metals (Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Ni) makes the "clay analogy" physically accurate.

Three things must happen in the analogy to translate to real CPW:

| Analogy Step | Real CPW Equivalent |
|--------------|---------------------|
| 1. Dusty clay surface | Oxide / adsorbed-gas film on metal |
| 2. Hard squeezing | Compressive pressure (typically **400–1200 MPa** for Al) |
| 3. Smearing sideways | **Lateral plastic flow** (extrusion) breaking the oxide film |
| 4. Fresh clay touching | Virgin base-metal-to-virgin-base-metal contact → **metallic bond** |

---

## 1.3 Classification within Solid-State Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Context:** CPW is taught alongside **Diffusion Bonding, Friction Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Ultrasonic Welding, Explosive Welding, and Forge Welding.** The relationship is:

```
Solid-State Welding (No melting)
├── Bulk Deformation Processes ← CPW lives here
│     ├── Cold Pressure Welding (CPW)
│     ├── Roll Bonding
│     ├── Forge Welding
│     └── Explosive Welding
├── Friction-Based Processes
│     ├── Rotary Friction Welding
│     ├── Friction Stir Welding (FSW)
│     └── Linear Friction Welding
├── Diffusion Processes
│     ├── Diffusion Bonding (DB)
│     └── Superplastic Forming & Diffusion Bonding (SPF/DB)
└── Hybrid / Special
      ├── Ultrasonic Welding (USW)
      ├── Cold Metal Transfer (CMT) — note: CMT is fusion-assisted
      └── Magnetic Pulse Welding
```

---

## 1.4 Why CPW Exists — The Engineering Motivation

CPW solves a problem that conventional fusion welding **cannot** elegantly solve:

1. **Joining dissimilar metals** with very different melting points (e.g., **Al–Cu**, **Al–Steel**, **Cu–Brass**). In fusion welding, the lower-melting metal vaporizes or the higher-melting metal fails to melt properly.
2. **Joining highly conductive / reflective metals** (e.g., pure Cu, pure Al) that are difficult to fusion-weld because of high thermal conductivity.
3. **No Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** — preserves temper, hardness, work-hardened microstructure, electrical conductivity, and corrosion resistance.
4. **No filler metal, no flux, no shielding gas** — clean, economical, environmentally friendly.
5. Excellent for **bi-metallic conductors**, **clad products**, and **battery tab joining** in modern Li-ion cell manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern relevance:** CPW is the **workhorse process for manufacturing aluminum cable joints, copper-aluminum transition joints for power distribution, and battery cell tab-to-busbar connections in EV (Electric Vehicle) battery packs.** This is a favourite KTU examiner's "applied" question hook.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Competing Theories of Cold Bond Formation

For a bond to form by pure mechanical contact at room temperature, three mechanistic stages must occur sequentially. The **debate** among researchers is about which stage *dominates* bond strength. For KTU, you must know all three.

### Stage 1 — **Film Theory (Mechanical Disruption of Surface Layers)**

Every metal, no matter how polished, is covered with:

* An **adsorbed gas / water-vapour layer** (a few nm thick).
* An **oxide layer** (e.g., Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> on aluminum is ~**2–10 nm** thick but **extremely hard**).
* A **Beilby layer** of mechanically deformed, work-hardened, contaminated metal (formed during machining / brushing).

The applied compressive load must cause **plastic flow** of the underlying ductile base metal so that the brittle oxide and Beilby layers **fracture and disperse laterally** as debris. Once fractured, the **extruded virgin metal** from the interior flows into the cracks and contacts the opposite virgin surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical insight:** If the deformation is too small, the oxide film is *not* fully disrupted, and the bond is weak. If it is *too* large, the joint may thin excessively and fail by *choking* (lack of material at the bond). Hence an **optimum deformation window** exists (typically **60–80%** for butt welds of aluminum, **75–85%** for copper).

### Stage 2 — **Adhesion Theory (Surface Energy & Metallic Bonding)**

Once two atomically clean metal surfaces are brought within **interatomic distance** (≈ **0.2–0.5 nm**), the **metallic bond** (a delocalized "electron-sea" bond) forms spontaneously because it is **energetically favourable**. The work of adhesion is given by the **Dupré equation**:

$$
W_{ad} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}
$$

where $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ are the **surface energies** (J/m²) of the two clean metals and $\gamma_{12}$ is the **interfacial energy**. For two identical clean metals ($\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = \gamma$ and $\gamma_{12} \approx 0$):

$$
W_{ad} = 2\gamma
$$

> The **adhesion theory** says: *bond strength ∝ surface energy × true area of contact.*

For aluminum, $\gamma \approx 1.1$ J/m²; for copper, $\gamma \approx 1.7$ J/m². Hence **copper-to-copper bonds are intrinsically stronger** than aluminum-to-aluminum bonds at the same true contact area.

### Stage 3 — **Diffusion Theory (Vacancy-Mediated Intermixing)**

At room temperature, **volume diffusion** in metals is essentially **frozen** (because the activation energy is high and T/T<sub>m</sub> is tiny). However, **grain-boundary diffusion, dislocation-pipe diffusion, and surface diffusion** are still active, especially because CPW creates a heavily deformed, dislocation-rich, nanocrystalline interface.

The **Arrhenius rate equation** governs:

$$
D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

> Even at **T = 300 K**, $D$ is small but non-zero for surface and grain-boundary paths. This **post-bonding diffusion** slowly increases the bond strength with time (a phenomenon called **"bond strength ageing"** or **"natural ageing"**), observed experimentally by storage of CPW joints for weeks at room temperature.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).** Memorize units and the typical numerical range for aluminum and copper.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Value (Al / Cu) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deformation (%)** | $\eta = \dfrac{t_0 - t_1}{t_0}\times 100\%$ | $t_0$ = initial thickness/length (mm); $t_1$ = final thickness/length (mm) | **60–80 %** (Al butt) ; **75–85 %** (Cu butt) |
| 2 | **Upset ratio** | $U = \dfrac{t_0 - t_1}{t_0}$ | Dimensionless, same as $\eta/100$ | 0.6–0.8 |
| 3 | **Adhesion energy (Dupré)** | $W_{ad} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$ | $\gamma$ in J/m² | $W_{ad} \approx 2.2$ J/m² (Al); $3.4$ J/m² (Cu) |
| 4 | **Diffusion coefficient** | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | $D$ in m²/s; $Q$ in J/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | $D_{Al}^{300K} \approx 10^{-22}$ m²/s (lattice); $10^{-16}$ m²/s (GB) |
| 5 | **Pressure for upsetting** | $p = K_f \cdot \sigma_f$ | $K_f$ = constraint factor (≈ **1.0–1.5** for butt); $\sigma_f$ = flow stress (MPa) | $p_{Al} \approx 100$–$200$ MPa ; $p_{Cu} \approx 400$–$600$ MPa |
| 6 | **Bearing-area (projected) pressure** | $p = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | $F$ in N; $A_0$ in mm² | $A_0 = \pi d^2/4$ for cylindrical butt |
| 7 | **Bond strength ratio** | $R_{BS} = \dfrac{BS_{weld}}{BS_{parent}}\times 100\%$ | Dimensionless % | $\geq 80\%$ for an *acceptable* CPW joint |
| 8 | **True contact area (adh. theory)** | $A_{true} = \dfrac{F_{normal}}{H}$ | $F_{normal}$ in N; $H$ = hardness (MPa) | Microscopic $A_{true}$ at the interface |

> **Mnemonic for the three theories: "FAD" → Film → Adhesion → Diffusion.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of CPW

| Industry | Application | Why CPW? |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Power Distribution** | Al–Cu bimetal transition joints, cable terminations | No IZ/HAZ; preserves conductivity |
| **EV Battery Manufacturing** | Tab-to-busbar, cell-to-pack busbars | Solid-state, no heat input that could damage cells |
| **Aerospace** | Clad aluminum alloys (Alclad sheets) | Roll-bonding variant of CPW |
| **HVAC & Refrigeration** | Cu-to-Cu tube joints | Leak-tight, no flux contamination of refrigerant |
| **Nuclear** | Zircaloy-to-stainless transition joints | Avoids brittle intermetallics that fusion welding would form |
| **Packaging (Foil)** | Al-foil laminates | Continuous roll bonding |
| **Jewellery / Coins** | Cold-bonded bimetal collector coins | Aesthetic + metallurgical bonding |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Deformation Window for Butt CPW

**Premise:** For a solid-state bond to form, the brittle oxide film must be **completely disrupted** and the underlying **ductile base metal must extrude through the cracks** to make intimate contact with the opposite virgin surface. Define $\eta$ as the engineering deformation:

$$
\eta = \frac{t_0 - t_1}{t_0} \times 100\%
$$

Let $A_0$ be the original cross-section and $A_1$ the final cross-section after upsetting. By **incompressibility of plastic deformation** (volume constancy for a solid metal, since $\rho$ does not change appreciably during cold working):

$$
A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_1 \cdot t_1
$$

Solving for the cross-section ratio:

$$
\frac{A_1}{A_0} = \frac{t_0}{t_1}
$$

Express $t_1$ in terms of $t_0$ and $\eta$:

$$
t_1 = t_0\!\left(1 - \frac{\eta}{100}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{A_1}{A_0} = \frac{1}{1 - \eta/100}
$$

> **Text-row explanation:** We substituted $t_1 = t_0(1 - \eta/100)$ into the volume-conservation equation. This gives the *spread ratio* — the factor by which the cross-section must grow for a given upset percentage.

**Now, the critical bond-area argument:**
Assume the **bonded area** at the interface $A_b$ is a fraction $\phi(\eta)$ of the nominal contact area $A_0$, where $\phi(\eta)$ is a monotonically increasing function of deformation (more deformation → more oxide disrupted → more bonded area). A simple linearized form valid in the practical range $0.5 \leq \eta \leq 0.85$ is:

$$
\phi(\eta) = k\left(\eta - \eta_{min}\right)
$$

where $\eta_{min}$ is the **threshold deformation** below which no bonding occurs (typically **40%** for Al, **60%** for Cu) and $k$ is a process constant.

The **bonded area** is then:

$$
A_b = k\left(\eta - \eta_{min}\right) A_0
$$

The **bond strength** $BS$ (load-bearing capacity of the joint in tension) is, by the adhesion theory:

$$
BS = \tau_u \cdot A_b
$$

where $\tau_u$ is the **shear strength of the metallic bond** at the interface (W/m² × m² = N). Substituting:

$$
BS = \tau_u \cdot k \cdot A_0 \left(\eta - \eta_{min}\right)
$$

> **Interpretation:** Bond strength rises **linearly with deformation** once the threshold is crossed, until work-hardening and section thinning set an upper limit. Define the **optimum** $\eta_{opt}$ as the deformation that maximizes the *normalized* bond strength ratio $BS/BS_{parent}$, typically:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{opt} \;\approx\; 0.60\;\text{to}\;0.80\;\text{(i.e., 60–80 %)}\;}
$$

This is why KTU examiners will mark you correct if you write **"60–80 % deformation"** as the optimum for Al butt CPW and **"75–85 %"** for Cu.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Upsetting Pressure for a Cylindrical Butt Joint

Consider two cylindrical rods of **diameter $d$** butted end-to-end and upset in a press. Required compressive load $F$:

$$
F = p \cdot A_0 = p \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Because upsetting occurs under **friction at the platen interfaces**, the average axial stress needed is greater than the uniaxial flow stress $\sigma_f$ of the material. The empirical **Siebel correction** for upsetting:

$$
p = K_f \cdot \sigma_f
$$

with the **constraint factor**:

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{\mu d}{3 h}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **platen-interface friction coefficient** and $h$ is the instantaneous height of the billet. For typical cold upsetting with lubricated platens, $\mu \approx 0.05$–$0.15$.

**Worked numerical example (Kerala board favourite):**

* Aluminum rod, $d = 20$ mm, $h_0 = 40$ mm
* Target $\eta = 70\%$, so $h_1 = 12$ mm
* $\sigma_f$ (cold-worked Al-1100) $\approx$ **90 MPa**
* $\mu = 0.10$ (graphite grease)

At the **mid-stroke** ($h_{avg} = (40+12)/2 = 26$ mm):

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{0.10 \times 20}{3 \times 26} = 1 + \frac{2.0}{78} = 1.0256
$$

$$
p = 1.0256 \times 90 \approx 92.3 \;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
F = 92.3 \times \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = 92.3 \times 314.16 \approx 29{,}000 \;\text{N} \approx 29 \;\text{kN}
$$

> **Text-row explanation:** The constraint factor barely exceeds 1.0 because the slenderness ratio is moderate. For a *short* billet ($h \ll d$), $K_f$ rises sharply and you need a much larger press. This is the *why* of the KTU favourite question: *"Why is CPW used for small cross-sections like wires, tabs, and thin strips?"* — Answer: **because the required upsetting pressure scales inversely with height**, so short billets require unreasonably large forces.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameter → Effect on Bond Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield KTU table.** Question pattern: *"Discuss the effect of [parameter] on cold pressure weld quality."* You can reproduce a similar 4-column matrix in the exam for full marks.

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Bond Strength | Increase → Effect on Joint Geometry | Practical Range (Al) | Practical Range (Cu) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deformation $\eta$** | ↑ (up to $\eta_{opt}$), then ↓ due to thinning | Cross-section widens; length shrinks | 60–80 % | 75–85 % |
| **Upset pressure $p$** | ↑ (saturates above threshold) | Same as above — controlled by $p$ | 100–200 MPa | 400–600 MPa |
| **Surface roughness (Ra)** | ↓ (smoother → better) | None directly | Ra ≤ **0.8 µm** | Ra ≤ **0.4 µm** |
| **Surface cleaning** | ↑ (degreasing + wire-brushing essential) | None | Acetone + SS brush | Acetone + SS brush |
| **Strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$** | Marginal effect at RT | Affects heat generation | 0.1–10 s⁻¹ | 0.1–10 s⁻¹ |
| **Temperature (sub-T<sub>m</sub>)** | ↑ (warm CPW at 0.3–0.5 T<sub>m</sub> lowers $\sigma_f$) | Reduces required pressure | 20–200 °C | 20–300 °C |
| **Billet aspect ratio $h/d$** | ↓ if too low (constraint builds) | Affects buckling | $h/d \geq 1.5$ preferred | $h/d \geq 1.5$ preferred |
| **Holding time after upset** | ↑ (ageing, diffusion) | None | Minutes to weeks | Minutes to weeks |
| **Oxide layer thickness** | ↓ (thicker → harder to disrupt) | None | Pre-store in dry N<sub>2</sub> | Pre-store in dry N<sub>2</sub> |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Percentage Deformation Calculation (Typical KTU 2-mark)

> **Question:** A copper wire of **5.0 mm** diameter is butt-welded by CPW. After welding, the diameter at the bond is **8.66 mm**. Calculate the **percentage upset** $\eta$ and comment on whether the joint is acceptable.

**Solution:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (5.0)^2}{4} = 19.635 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi d_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (8.66)^2}{4} = 58.91 \;\text{mm}^2
$$

By **volume constancy**: $A_0 h_0 = A_1 h_1$, so

$$
\frac{h_1}{h_0} = \frac{A_0}{A_1} = \frac{19.635}{58.91} = 0.3333
$$

$$
\eta = \left(1 - \frac{h_1}{h_0}\right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.3333)\times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

> **Acceptability comment:** $\eta = 66.7\%$ is **below** the typical 75–85% optimum window for copper butt CPW. The joint is **borderline** — bond strength will be roughly 70% of the parent metal, so the joint is **acceptable for non-critical electrical connections** but **not acceptable for high-stress or pressure-tight applications**.

This is the **classic KTU 3-mark short calculation**; reproduce the *Volume Constancy* line — that is the *only* mark-carrying step that students forget.
