# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question pattern compliance:** Part A = 3-mark short answers. Part B = 14-mark with **internal choice** (KTU ESE regulation 2024 — Module-level choice). Each Part-B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solutions are written in **valuation-key style** with explicit "Step 1 / Step 2" markers.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. Define Cold Pressure Welding. List any **four** engineering materials that can be joined by this process.  *(Model: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1a]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Definition = 2 marks ; List = 1 mark.

**Model Answer:**

**Cold Pressure Welding (CPW)** is a solid-state welding process in which two clean metal surfaces are bonded at room temperature (or slightly elevated, but **below 0.5 T<sub>m</sub>**) by applying a **compressive force** sufficient to cause **extensive plastic deformation** at the interface. The deformation breaks up the **surface oxide film**, extrudes virgin metal through the cracks, and establishes **metallic continuity** without any melting.

**Four materials that can be joined by CPW:**

1. **Aluminum and its alloys** (Al-1100, Al-2024, Al-6061).
2. **Copper and its alloys** (C110, C260 brass, C360 free-machining brass).
3. **Lead and lead alloys** (Pb-Sb battery grids).
4. **Zinc and zinc alloys** (galvanizing-line overlays).

(Other acceptable: **Nickel**, **low-carbon steel**, **Au, Ag, Pt** in jewellery/electronics.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write *"welding at 0 °C"* or *"using ice cooling."* This is **wrong**. *Cold* in CPW means **no externally applied fusion heat**, not sub-zero. Also, do not list **cast iron** or **high-carbon steel** — they are not ductile enough at RT for CPW.

---

### Q2. What is the role of **surface preparation** in cold pressure welding? Name **two** common methods. *(Model: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1c]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Role = 2 marks ; Methods = 1 mark.

**Model Answer:**

The oxide film and adsorbed contaminants on the metal surface act as a **barrier** to metallic bonding. Without surface preparation, the applied pressure deforms the **oxide** (which is hard and brittle for Al, Cu) rather than the underlying base metal, and no virgin-to-virgin contact occurs. Hence **surface preparation is critical** to:

1. Remove the **oxide layer** (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> on aluminum is ~**2–10 nm** thick but extremely hard, HV ≈ 1800–2000).
2. Remove the **adsorbed gas / water-vapour layer** and organic contaminants (oils, greases).
3. Provide a **chemically active** surface that can form metallic bonds upon contact.
4. Increase the **real area of contact** by smoothening asperities.

**Two common surface-preparation methods:**

* **Degreasing** with acetone, isopropyl alcohol, or alkaline cleaners, followed by **stainless-steel wire brushing** (rotary brush at 1500–3000 rpm) to mechanically break up the oxide just before butting.
* **Lathe turning / face milling** to expose a fresh, oxide-free surface in-situ, immediately followed by butting (typical for wire and rod production).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not write *"use sandpaper"* as the primary method. Sandpaper embeds abrasive particles (SiC, Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) which become contaminants. Also, students forget to mention that the **prepared surface must be butted within minutes**; otherwise, a new oxide layer forms in air and the prep is wasted.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two full-length 14-mark questions are given per module. The student attempts **one**. We provide BOTH alternatives below for revision, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Cold Pressure Welding: Theory and Process

#### Q.A(a) [7 marks] — Explain the **mechanism of bond formation** in cold pressure welding. Discuss the **three theories** with neat sketches.
*(Model: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, Q2a]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Mechanism overview = 2 ; Film theory = 2 ; Adhesion theory = 1.5 ; Diffusion theory = 1.5.

**Model Solution:**

Bond formation in CPW occurs by **three sequential, overlapping stages** described by the **Film–Adhesion–Diffusion (FAD) framework**:

**1. Film Theory — Mechanical Disruption (2 marks):**

Every metal surface is covered with a **native oxide film**, an **adsorbed gas/water layer**, and a **Beilby layer** (work-hardened, contaminated metal from prior machining). For Al, the Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> layer is **2–10 nm** thick with hardness **~1800 HV**, far harder than the base metal (~30 HV). When compressive load is applied, the **underlying ductile base metal** yields and flows **laterally**, while the brittle oxide film **fractures and is squeezed out** sideways as debris. The **deformation $\eta$** must exceed a **threshold** (≈ 40% for Al) to ensure complete disruption. Without sufficient deformation, oxide islands remain and act as **stress concentrators**, giving a weak, "peanut-brittle" bond.

**2. Adhesion Theory — Metallic Bonding (1.5 marks):**

Once the oxide is disrupted, **fresh base-metal extrusions** from the two halves meet at the interface. When the gap between them reduces to the **interatomic spacing** (≈ **0.2–0.5 nm**), the **metallic bond** (delocalized electron cloud) forms spontaneously. The work of adhesion per unit area is given by the **Dupré equation**:

$$
W_{ad} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}
$$

For two identical clean metals ($\gamma_{12} \approx 0$): $W_{ad} = 2\gamma$. For **Cu–Cu**, $W_{ad} \approx 3.4$ J/m²; for **Al–Al**, $\approx 2.2$ J/m². The bond strength is therefore directly proportional to the **true area of clean metallic contact**, $A_{true}$, which in turn depends on the **upset ratio** $U = (h_0 - h_1)/h_0$.

**3. Diffusion Theory — Short-Range Atom Intermixing (1.5 marks):**

At room temperature, **volume self-diffusion** in Al and Cu is negligible ($D \approx 10^{-22}$ m²/s for lattice diffusion). However, the **severely plastically deformed interface** has a high density of **dislocations, vacancies, and freshly created sub-grain boundaries**, which provide **fast diffusion paths** (grain-boundary and dislocation-pipe diffusion). The governing Arrhenius equation is:

$$
D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

Grain-boundary $D$ in aluminum at 300 K is of the order **$10^{-16}$ m²/s**, which over weeks of natural ageing allows **nanometre-scale intermixing**, gradually raising the bond strength. This explains the **"ageing effect"** of CPW joints, where the strength rises with storage time.

**Closing remark (for full marks):** The three theories are **complementary, not competing**. The Film theory is the *enabler*, the Adhesion theory provides the *instantaneous bond*, and the Diffusion theory explains the *time-dependent strengthening*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes in this 7-mark answer:** (1) Writing only one theory in detail — examiner wants **all three** in correct sequence. (2) Confusing **volume diffusion** with **grain-boundary diffusion** — you must state that volume diffusion is frozen at RT but GB/pipe diffusion is active. (3) Omitting the **threshold deformation** concept — without it, the Film theory is incomplete.

#### Q.A(b) [7 marks] — With the aid of a diagram, describe the **butt cold pressure welding** process. Discuss the effect of **deformation percentage** on bond strength and state the **optimum range** for aluminum.
*(Model: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1, Q2b]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Process description + diagram = 3 ; Effect of deformation = 3 ; Optimum range for Al = 1.

**Model Solution:**

**Butt CPW Process (3 marks):**

In butt CPW, two prepared rods, wires, or bars are **butted end-to-end** in a holding die, and a **uniaxial compressive force** is applied by a **hydraulic or mechanical press** along the common axis. The sequence is:

1. **Cut** the two members to length and **face** the ends square (lathe or shear).
2. **Degrease** with acetone and **wire-brush** the faces with a stainless-steel rotary brush.
3. **Insert** the parts into the **butt-welding die / gripping jaws** with a small axial pre-load to ensure alignment.
4. **Apply** a controlled upsetting force $F$ corresponding to a bearing stress $p = 4F/(\pi d^2)$ of order **100–200 MPa** for Al and **400–600 MPa** for Cu.
5. **Hold** the upset load for a few seconds to allow for diffusion.
6. **Unload and eject** the welded part.

The deformation is characterized by:

$$
\eta = \frac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0} \times 100\%
$$

where $h_0$ and $h_1$ are the initial and final lengths of the upset region.

**Effect of Deformation on Bond Strength (3 marks):**

The bond strength $BS$ varies with $\eta$ in a **three-region curve**:

* **Region I — Sub-threshold (0 ≤ $\eta$ < $\eta_{min}$):** Bond strength is essentially **zero** (or < 10% of parent). The deformation is insufficient to disrupt the oxide film, and the joint separates at the original interface.
* **Region II — Bond-strength growth ($\eta_{min}$ < $\eta$ < $\eta_{opt}$):** Bond strength **rises approximately linearly** with $\eta$, because the area of clean metallic contact $A_{true}$ grows as more oxide is disrupted and more virgin metal extrudes.
* **Region III — Over-deformation ($\eta$ > $\eta_{opt}$):** Bond strength **plateaus or declines** because the cross-section is severely thinned at the bond, and the joint fails by **choking (necking)** at the parent metal adjacent to the bond rather than at the interface. Geometrically, $A_0$ grows, but the **net load-bearing cross-section** at the bond also spreads sideways, so the *normalized* BS/BS<sub>parent</sub> ratio drops.

**Optimum Range for Aluminum (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{opt}(\text{Al butt CPW}) \;=\; 60\text{ – }80\%\;}
$$

For copper: **75–85%**. Below 60% (Al), the joint is under-deformed; above 80% (Al), the joint is over-deformed and chokes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students draw a *linear* BS-vs-$\eta$ graph from origin. The **correct shape is a "lazy-S" or "saturating" curve** with a flat plateau. Examiner deducts 1 mark if you draw a straight line through the origin.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Cold Pressure Welding of Dissimilar Metals & Applications

#### Q.B(a) [7 marks] — Explain **dissimilar-metal cold pressure welding** with suitable examples. Why is CPW preferred over fusion welding for **Al–Cu joints**?
*(Model: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2021, Module 1, Q3a]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Concept of dissimilar CPW = 2 ; Examples = 2 ; Al–Cu comparison with fusion welding = 3.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Dissimilar-Metal CPW (2 marks):**

CPW can join **two different metals** provided both are sufficiently ductile at room temperature and their flow stresses are within a factor of ~2–3 of each other. The mechanism is identical to that of similar-metal CPW: surface prep → upsetting → oxide disruption → atomic-scale contact → metallic bond. The bond at the dissimilar interface is between **two different atomic species**, and the bond strength is governed by the **Dupré equation**:

$$
W_{ad} = \gamma_{Al} + \gamma_{Cu} - \gamma_{Al/Cu}
$$

A small $\gamma_{Al/Cu}$ (good chemical affinity) yields a strong bond; a large $\gamma_{Al/Cu}$ (immiscible pair) yields a weak bond. Al and Cu are partially miscible in the solid state and form a few **intermetallics** (Al<sub>2</sub>Cu, AlCu, Al<sub>4</sub>Cu<sub>9</sub>) — but in CPW the brief contact time and low temperature **prevent** extensive intermetallic growth, which is the *exact opposite* of what happens in fusion welding.

**Examples (2 marks):**

1. **Al–Cu bimetal cable joints** for power distribution (overhead-line connectors).
2. **Cu–Brass** tube joints in refrigeration and HVAC (brazing alternative).
3. **Al–Steel** transition joints in automotive bodies (resistance spot welding alternative).
4. **Ni–Cu** joints in coin blanks and battery tabs.
5. **Pb–Sn** (solder-alloy) cladding on copper substrates for corrosion protection.

**Why CPW is preferred over fusion welding for Al–Cu joints (3 marks):**

| Issue in Fusion Welding Al–Cu | How CPW Solves It |
|---|---|
| Melting points differ: Al = 660 °C, Cu = 1085 °C. In fusion welding, **Al vaporizes** before Cu melts fully. | No melting — both remain solid. |
| **Brittle Al–Cu intermetallics** (Al<sub>2</sub>Cu, AlCu<sub>3</sub>) form a thick, brittle layer in the fusion zone → joint cracks under vibration. | Brief RT contact → **negligible intermetallic growth**. |
| Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> tenaciously floats in the weld pool → **porosity, slag inclusions**. | Oxide is squeezed out laterally as solid debris. |
| High thermal conductivity of both Cu and Al → **large HAZ, distortion**. | No HAZ — joint retains parent-metal properties. |
| Dissimilar **thermal expansion** → residual stress in the HAZ. | No thermal cycle → **no residual stress**. |
| Requires flux / shielding gas / filler. | None required. |

Hence CPW is the **industrial standard** for Al–Cu power connectors (e.g., the **Bimetallic Lug** market in Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) applications).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students write *"intermetallics form"* in CPW too — but in CPW the time at elevated temperature is **seconds at room temperature**, so intermetallic thickness is in **nanometres**, not microns as in fusion welds. Quantify this in the exam.

#### Q.B(b) [7 marks] — List the **advantages and limitations** of cold pressure welding. Describe with a sketch the **lap CPW** process used in **bimetallic strip** manufacture.
*(Model: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q3b]`)*
**Marks distribution:** Advantages = 2 ; Limitations = 2 ; Lap CPW description + sketch = 3.

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of CPW (2 marks):**

1. **No heat input** → no HAZ, no fusion zone, no microstructural change, no distortion.
2. **Joins dissimilar metals** that are difficult or impossible to fusion-weld (e.g., Al–Cu, Al–steel).
3. **Preserves parent-metal properties** (conductivity, corrosion resistance, temper).
4. **Economical** — no filler, no flux, no shielding gas, low energy (only the mechanical press consumes power).
5. **Environmentally clean** — no fumes, no UV, no spatter.
6. **Easily automated** — high production rates in roll-bonding lines.
7. **Excellent electrical and thermal conductivity** of the joint (important for busbars, cables).
8. **Can be combined with subsequent heat treatment** for further strengthening.

**Limitations of CPW (2 marks):**

1. **Restricted to ductile metals** — cannot join **cast iron, high-carbon steel, ceramics, hard alloys**.
2. **High deformation required** (60–85%) → the joint geometry must be designed to accommodate the upset.
3. **Heavy presses / rolling mills** needed → high capital cost for large cross-sections.
4. **Critical surface preparation** → oxide/contamination → weak joint; meticulous pre-cleaning is mandatory.
5. **Not suitable for very thick sections** because required force scales with area.
6. **Joint configuration limited** — primarily butt and lap; complex 3-D joints are difficult.
7. **Bond strength rarely matches** the parent metal fully (typical 70–90% of parent).
8. **Sensitive to operator skill** for manual surface prep on small-batch production.

**Lap CPW Process for Bimetallic Strip (3 marks):**

In **lap CPW** (also called *co-extrusion bonding* or *die-bonding*), two strips of dissimilar metals are **overlapped** and forced through a **tapered die** under a punch pressure. The lap region undergoes **compressive + shear deformation**, and the bond forms along the overlap length. For continuous production, a **roll-bonding mill** is used: the cleaned strips are fed together between two counter-rotating rolls under high roll pressure, plastically deforming the lap region and producing a continuous **clad strip** (e.g., **Alclad** aluminum sheets used in aerospace skin panels, where a thin layer of pure Al is clad over a stronger Al-alloy core for corrosion resistance).

The schematic of the lap CPW process (block-form, for the KTU answer script):

```
   ┌─────────┐  Punch
   │  PUNCH  │  ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓
   └────┬────┘
   ┌────┴────────────────┐
   │ Strip A (e.g. Cu)   │  ← overlap
   │                    ─┼───→ Strip B (e.g. Al)
   │                     │
   └─────────┬───────────┘
             ▼
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │  Tapered Die     │  ← outlet
   └────────┬─────────┘
            ▼
        Bonded Strip
```

The deformation is localized in the **die-throat** and the **overlap length** controls the bond area. A **reduction of 50–70%** in the lap region is typical for Cu–Al strip.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students confuse *lap CPW* with *resistance spot welding (RSW)*. The two are entirely different: in **lap CPW**, deformation is **macroscopic and global** (the whole lap region deforms); in **RSW**, deformation is absent — bonding is by **localized fusion nugget**. Do not mix them up.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section on a single index card and revise the night before the exam.**

* ✅ **Definition:** CPW = solid-state welding, room temperature, plastic-deformation-driven bond.
* ✅ **Three theories, in order:** Film → Adhesion → Diffusion (**FAD** mnemonic).
* ✅ **Threshold deformation:** Al ≈ 40%, Cu ≈ 60%. **Optimum:** Al 60–80%, Cu 75–85%.
* ✅ **Dupré equation:** $W_{ad} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$; for identical metals $W_{ad} = 2\gamma$.
* ✅ **Siebel constraint factor:** $K_f = 1 + \mu d / (3h)$.
* ✅ **Volume constancy:** $A_0 h_0 = A_1 h_1$ — *the* equation for deformation calculation.
* ✅ **Surface prep:** Degrease + wire-brush; re-oxidizes in minutes, so butting must be **immediate**.
* ✅ **Materials that work:** Al, Cu, Pb, Zn, Ni, low-carbon steel, Au, Ag.
* ✅ **Materials that don't work:** Cast iron, high-carbon steel, ceramics, refractory metals (W, Mo at RT).
* ✅ **Joint configurations:** Butt, lap, spot, roll-bonding (continuous).
* ✅ **Compared to fusion welding:** No HAZ, no intermetallic growth, dissimilar-metal friendly.
* ✅ **Modern applications:** EV battery tab-to-busbar (CMT/CPW hybrid), KSEB Al-Cu lugs, Alclad aerospace sheets, refrigeration Cu-to-Cu tube joints, nuclear Zircaloy-to-SS transitions.
* ✅ **Examiner pet questions:** (i) Bonding mechanism with three theories. (ii) Why CPW for Al–Cu? (iii) Why can't CPW join cast iron? (iv) Sketch and explain lap CPW. (v) Calculate % upset from given dimensions.
* ✅ **One-line rule:** *If the metal is ductile at RT and can flow > 60% without cracking, it can be CPW-bonded; otherwise not.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesive Bonding: Principle, Types of Adhesives, Bonding Methods, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adhesive Bonding: Principle, Types of Adhesives, Bonding Methods, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Adhesive Bonding** is a material joining process in which two adherends (substrates) are held together by a non-metallic substance called an **adhesive**, which develops internal strength (cohesion) and surface attachment (adhesion) to produce a load-bearing joint. The bond is formed by the solidification of the adhesive through a physical change (solvent loss/cooling) or a chemical reaction (polymerization/cross-linking), without melting the parent materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *Adhesive bonding is the joining of similar or dissimilar materials by means of a substance (adhesive) that adheres to the surfaces of the parts, is capable of transmitting load from one part to the other, and possesses adequate strength to hold the parts together under service conditions.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Gecko & The Tape"

Imagine a strip of **transparent cellophane tape** sticking two sheets of paper together. The tape is soft and sticky (like an adhesive), it flows into the microscopic valleys of the paper, and when you press it, it forms millions of tiny bonds. Now scale this up to a fighter-jet wing — engineers use aerospace-grade epoxy films to bond aluminium honeycomb to titanium skin. The principle is the same: a soft material flows, wets the surface, then hardens to lock everything together.

In short — **the adhesive does NOT melt the parts; it spreads, wets, and hardens.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three keywords to remember for the KTU exam:**
> 1. **Wetting** — adhesive must spread over the substrate.
> 2. **Adhesion** — molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate.
> 3. **Cohesion** — internal strength of the cured adhesive itself.

### 1.3 Why This Process Matters in KTU Scope

| Joining Method | Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) | Dissimilar Metals | Stress Distribution | Weight Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Welding | High | Difficult | Concentrated at weld | Moderate |
| Riveting/Bolting | None | Easy | Stress concentration at holes | High |
| **Adhesive Bonding** | **None** | **Very Easy** | **Uniform over area** | **Lowest** |

**Key metric to memorize:** Surface energy of clean metal ≈ **500–3000 mJ/m²**, while surface tension of typical epoxy ≈ **30–50 mJ/m²**, which is what enables spontaneous wetting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Theories of Adhesion (KTU High-Yield)

For KTU Module-1 derivation and short-answer questions, the bonding mechanism is explained by **five overlapping theories**:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking Theory**
   - Adhesive flows into surface pores, micro-cracks, and roughness.
   - Upon hardening, mechanical keying provides anchorage.
   - Dominant on porous substrates (wood, etched metals, anodized aluminium).

2. **Adsorption (Wetting) Theory**
   - Adhesive molecules make intimate molecular contact (van der Waals forces) with substrate.
   - Requires the adhesive to **wet** the surface — quantified by **Young's equation**.

3. **Chemical Bonding Theory**
   - Primary valence bonds (covalent/ionic) form between adhesive functional groups and surface.
   - Example: Epoxy –OH reacting with –OH on etched aluminium → Al–O–C bond.

4. **Diffusion Theory**
   - Adhesive polymer chains inter-diffuse with substrate polymer chains.
   - Mostly applicable to bonding of polymers and elastomers.

5. **Electrostatic Theory**
   - Electron transfer creates a double-layer at the interface, producing an electrostatic attraction.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the principle of adhesive bonding", structure your answer as: *Surface preparation → Wetting → Adhesion (one of the 5 theories) → Cohesion build-up → Load transfer*. This matches the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

### 2.2 Joint Design Types (Essential for KTU Drawing Questions)

| Joint Type | Loading Type | Relative Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Lap joint (single/double) | Shear | High |
| Butt joint | Tension/Compression | Low |
| Scarf joint | Shear + Tension | High (long taper) |
| Strap joint (double lap) | Shear | Very High |
| Tapered joint | Mixed | High |
| Cylindrical sleeve joint | Shear/torsion | Moderate |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-essential** for KTU 2024 Part-B numericals.

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Equation | $\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Contact angle $\theta$ determines wettability |
| Work of Adhesion | $W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl} \;=\; \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Energy released per unit area on bonding |
| Critical Surface Tension | $\gamma_c \;\ge\; \gamma_{lv}$ (spreading condition) | Wetting happens only when this holds |
| Lap Shear Stress | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{P}{w \cdot l}$ | Average shear stress in single lap |
| Shear Strength | $S_{shear} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{bond}}$ | MPa or psi |
| Peel Force (per width) | $P \;=\; k \cdot w$ | $k$ = peel strength (N/mm) |
| Young's Modulus ratio | $\dfrac{E_{adhesive}}{E_{substrate}}$ | $\le 0.1$ ideal to minimize peel stresses |
| Cure Shrinkage Stress | $\sigma_s \;=\; E_a \cdot \Delta\varepsilon_{shrink}$ | Internal stress on cooling |
| Stress in cylindrical bond | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | For tubular/shaft bonded sleeves |
| Thermal mismatch stress | $\sigma_T \;=\; E_a \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | CTE mismatch $\Delta\alpha$ problem |

> [!NOTE]
> In every formula above, $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ represent **solid–vapour**, **solid–liquid**, and **liquid–vapour** interfacial energies, respectively.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing-skin to stringer bonding in Airbus A350 — eliminates **~50% of rivets**, reducing weight by ~1.5 tonnes per aircraft.
- **Automotive:** Brake-pad bonding, composite body panels in EVs (Tesla Model Y, BMW i3).
- **Electronics:** Die-attach in semiconductor packaging (silver-filled epoxy).
- **Biomedical:** Bone cements (PMMA), transdermal patches.
- **Civil:** Carbon-fibre wrap bonding for retrofitting concrete columns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young–Dupré Work of Adhesion

**Starting premise:** A sessile drop of adhesive sits on a rigid substrate. The drop makes a contact angle $\theta$ with the solid surface. At the three-phase contact line, thermodynamic equilibrium demands the vector sum of interfacial tensions to be zero.

**Step 1 — Force balance along the solid surface (horizontal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pulling the drop toward vapour:} \quad & \gamma_{sv} \\
\text{Pulling the drop along solid-liquid interface:} \quad & -\gamma_{sl} \\
\text{Component of liquid–vapour tension pulling inward:} \quad & -\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Setting the sum to zero at equilibrium:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging to the standard form of Young's Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Deriving the thermodynamic work of adhesion $W_a$ (Dupré, 1869):**

By definition, the work of adhesion is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of the liquid–solid interface into separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a \;&\equiv\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Young's expression for $\gamma_{sl}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a \;&=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \left(\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\right) \\
\;&=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \\
\;&=\; \gamma_{lv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Dupré–Young Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\left(1 + \cos\theta\right)\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** If $\theta \to 0$ (perfect wetting, $\cos\theta \to 1$), then $W_a \to 2\gamma_{lv}$, the **maximum** possible work of adhesion — this is the ideal case for high-strength joints.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Shear Stress in a Single-Lap Adhesive Joint

**Premise:** A single-lap joint of bond length $l$ and width $w$ is loaded axially by a force $P$.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear plane area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{bond} \;=\; w \cdot l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium — the load must be fully transferred across the adhesive layer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \;=\; \tau_{avg} \cdot A_{bond} \;=\; \tau_{avg} \cdot w \cdot l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the average shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{w \cdot l}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Shear strength of the joint (maximum load divided by bond area):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;S \;=\; \dfrac{P_{max}}{w \cdot l} \quad \text{[MPa]}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bond length optimization (Volkersen's shear-lag insight):**

Because the substrate is elastic, shear stress is **NOT uniform** — it is highest at the ends. Volkersen showed the peak stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{peak} \;\approx\; \tau_{avg} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_a \, l^2}{E \, t \, h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $G_a$ = adhesive shear modulus, $E$ = substrate Young's modulus, $t$ = substrate thickness, $h$ = adhesive thickness. This is why **increasing bond length beyond a critical point gives no additional strength** — a classic KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spreading Coefficient $S_c$

**Step 1 — Define the condition for spontaneous spreading (wetting):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;-\; \left(\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\right) \;<\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the spreading coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;S_c \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wetting condition using Young's equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_c \;&=\; \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv} \\
\;&=\; \left(\gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta\right) - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv} \\
\;&=\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta - \gamma_{lv} \\
\;&=\; \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Spontaneous spreading ($S_c > 0$) requires $\cos\theta > 1$, which is **physically impossible**. Therefore, complete spreading ($\theta = 0$) is the practical maximum — a result examiners love to test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of derived equations (board-ready):**
> 1. $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ — **Young (1805)**
> 2. $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ — **Dupré–Young**
> 3. $\tau_{avg} = P/(wl)$ — **Lap joint**
> 4. $S_c = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$ — **Spreading**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process-Architecture Flow (Adhesive Bonding Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate A + Substrate B] --> B[Step1 Surface Preparation]
    B --> B1[Degrease with solvent]
    B --> B2[Mechanical Abrasion - Grit Blasting]
    B --> B3[Chemical Etching - Chromic Acid / Anodizing]
    B1 --> C[Step2 Surface Activation]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Step3 Primer or Coupling Agent Application]
    D --> E[Step4 Adhesive Application Method]
    E --> E1[Brush / Roller]
    E --> E2[Spray / Dip]
    E --> E3[Film / Tape Layup]
    E --> E4[Hot Melt Extrusion]
    E1 --> F[Step5 Joint Assembly and Clamping]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Step6 Curing Process]
    G --> G1[Room Temp Cure - 24h]
    G --> G2[Heat Cure - Oven 80 to 180 C]
    G --> G3[Radiation Cure - UV / Electron Beam]
    G --> G4[Pressure + Heat - Autoclave]
    G1 --> H[Step7 Inspection and Testing]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Final Bonded Component]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Adhesives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Adhesives] --> T1[By Chemical Nature]
    T --> T2[By Physical Form]
    T --> T3[By Load Capability]

    T1 --> T1A[Thermoplastic]
    T1 --> T1B[Thermosetting]
    T1 --> T1C[Elastomeric]
    T1A --> T1A1[Polyvinyl Acetate - PVA]
    T1A --> T1A2[Acrylic Resins]
    T1A --> T1A3[Polyamides - Nylon Hot Melt]
    T1B --> T1B1[Epoxy]
    T1B --> T1B2[Phenolic]
    T1B --> T1B3[Polyurethane]
    T1B --> T1B4[Silicone Resin]
    T1C --> T1C1[Natural Rubber]
    T1C --> T1C2[Neoprene]
    T1C --> T1C3[Silicone Rubber]

    T2 --> T2A[Liquid]
    T2 --> T2B[Paste]
    T2 --> T2C[Film]
    T2 --> T2D[Tape]
    T2 --> T2E[Powder]

    T3 --> T3A[Structural - Load Bearing 7 to 35 MPa]
    T3 --> T3B[Non-Structural - Less than 7 MPa]
    T3 --> T3C[Pressure Sensitive - Tape Type]
```

### 4.3 Joint Design Block Diagram — Load Transfer Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Applied Tensile Load P] --> LP1[Substrate A - High E]
    LP1 --> LA1[Adhesive Layer - Shear Mode]
    LA1 --> LP2[Substrate B - High E]
    LP2 --> L2[Reaction P]
    LA1 -. Peel Stress at ends .-> LS1[Peak at Overlap Ends]
    LA1 -. Shear Stress Distribution .-> LS2[Parabolic Volkersen]
    LS1 --> NA[Failure Mode: Cohesive vs Adhesive]
    LS2 --> NA
```

### 4.4 Bonding Method Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Bonding Methods] --> M1[Cold Setting]
    M --> M2[Hot Setting]
    M --> M3[Activation Curing]
    M --> M4[Pressure Sensitive]

    M1 --> M1A[Epoxy at RT]
    M1 --> M1B[Cyanocrylate - Super Glue]
    M1 --> M1C[Silicone RTV]

    M2 --> M2A[Phenolic Resin 150 C]
    M2 --> M2B[Epoxy with Hardener 120 C]
    M2 --> M2C[Hot Melt EVA]

    M3 --> M3A[UV Curable Acrylic]
    M3 --> M3B[Anaerobic Adhesive]
    M3 --> M3C[Moisture Cure Polyurethane]

    M4 --> M4A[Acrylic Tape VHB]
    M4 --> M4B[Rubber Based Tape]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Set B]**
*Define adhesive bonding. Differentiate between adhesion and cohesion with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Adhesive bonding is a joining process in which a non-metallic substance (the adhesive) is used to hold two adherends together by surface attachment and internal strength, without melting the parent materials. *(1 mark)*

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Adhesion** | Molecular/chemical forces acting **at the interface** between adhesive and adherend. | Epoxy sticks to aluminium — interfacial force. |
| **Cohesion** | Internal molecular forces acting **within the bulk** of the adhesive itself. | Epoxy does not split in the middle of the bond line. |

*(1 mark for difference + 1 mark for examples = full 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Explain briefly the "wetting" criterion for adhesive bonding and its dependence on contact angle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wetting is the ability of a liquid adhesive to spread and make intimate molecular contact with a solid surface. *(1 mark)*

It is quantified by the **contact angle $\theta$**:
- $\theta \to 0°$ → **complete wetting** (ideal).
- $\theta < 90°$ → **good wetting** (joint strength possible).
- $\theta > 90°$ → **poor wetting** (weak joint). *(1 mark)*

Young's equation $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ governs this. For a strong bond, the **surface energy of the substrate** must exceed the **surface tension of the adhesive**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a) + Q1(b)]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle of adhesive bonding** with the help of the five theories of adhesion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Classify the various **types of adhesives** based on chemical composition, physical form, and load-bearing capability. Give two engineering applications of each major class. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Principle of Adhesion (7 marks):**

The principle of adhesive bonding rests on two simultaneous phenomena: **adhesion** (forces at the interface) and **cohesion** (forces within the adhesive). For a joint to transmit load, the adhesive must first **wet** the surface, then **solidify** to build cohesive strength. *(1 mark)*

The five theories explaining the adhesion mechanism are:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking (1 mark):** Adhesive penetrates micro-pores, scratches, and cavities on the substrate. After hardening, mechanical keying resists separation. Most effective on roughened or anodized metals.

2. **Adsorption / Wetting Theory (1 mark):** When the adhesive wets the surface ($\theta \to 0$), van der Waals forces establish across the interface. Quantified by Young–Dupré: $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$.

3. **Chemical Bonding (1 mark):** Primary covalent or ionic bonds form between reactive groups (e.g., –OH of epoxy and –OH on etched aluminium). These provide the highest strength but require specific surface chemistry.

4. **Diffusion Theory (1 mark):** Polymer chains of adhesive interdiffuse with chains of the substrate, forming an entangled interphase. Predominant in polymer-to-polymer bonding.

5. **Electrostatic Theory (1 mark):** Electron transfer across the interface creates a double electric layer, producing Coulombic attraction.

The dominant theory depends on the substrate and the adhesive chemistry. In practice, **all five act simultaneously**, but mechanical interlocking and adsorption dominate for metals. *(Summary 1 mark)*

---

**(b) Model Solution — Classification of Adhesives (7 marks):**

**Based on chemical composition:** *(3 marks — ½ mark each + 1 mark for examples)*

| Class | Examples | Key Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastic** | PVA, Acrylic, Nylon (hot-melt) | Re-soften on heating, reversible | Book-binding, packaging |
| **Thermosetting** | Epoxy, Phenolic, Polyurethane | Irreversible cross-linking | Aircraft structures, brake linings |
| **Elastomeric** | Natural rubber, Neoprene, Silicone | Flexible, high peel strength | Shoe soles, gaskets, splicing tapes |

**Based on physical form:** *(2 marks)*
- **Liquid / Paste** — applied by brush, spray, or trowel.
- **Film** — pre-formed dry film, placed between adherends.
- **Tape** — pressure-sensitive, no curing needed.
- **Powder / Granule** — sprinkled, then melted to bond.

**Based on load-bearing capability:** *(2 marks)*
- **Structural adhesives:** shear strength > 7 MPa, used in load-bearing applications (e.g., epoxy in aerospace).
- **Non-structural adhesives:** < 7 MPa, used in light-duty applications (e.g., PVA in wood).
- **Pressure-sensitive adhesives:** removable, no chemical curing (e.g., cellophane tape, VHB acrylic foam tape).

**Engineering applications summary (1 mark):**
- Epoxy: Aircraft wing-skin to honeycomb, composite auto body panels.
- Phenolic: Brake-pad bonding, foundry sand binders.
- Polyurethane: Shoe-sole bonding, automotive windshield sealing.
- Silicone: High-temperature gaskets, medical implants.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2(a) + Q2(b)]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **bonding methods / process steps** for adhesive joining, including surface preparation, adhesive application, and curing techniques. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **load transfer mechanism in a single-lap adhesive joint**. Derive the expression for average shear stress. List four engineering applications of adhesive bonding. *(7 marks)*

---

**(a) Model Solution — Bonding Process Steps (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Surface Preparation (2 marks):** Most critical step. Determines 70–80% of joint strength.
- *Degreasing:* Solvent cleaning (acetone, MEK) to remove oils.
- *Mechanical abrasion:* Grit blasting, sanding (Sa 2.5 finish) to increase surface area.
- *Chemical treatment:* Chromic acid anodizing (for Al alloys), alkaline cleaning, plasma treatment.
- *Priming:* Application of coupling agents (silanes) to enhance chemical bonding.

**Step 2 — Adhesive Application (1 mark):** Method depends on viscosity and part geometry — brush, roller, spray gun, trowel, syringe, hot-melt extrusion, or pre-placed film.

**Step 3 — Joint Assembly (1 mark):** Adherends are brought together within the *open time* of the adhesive (working life before gel). Clamping fixtures ensure uniform pressure and bond-line thickness (typically 0.1–0.2 mm).

**Step 4 — Curing (2 marks):**
- *Room-temperature cure:* Epoxy + polyamine hardener, 24 h.
- *Heat cure:* Phenolic at 150°C, epoxy at 80–180°C, in oven or autoclave.
- *Radiation cure:* UV or electron-beam for acrylics.
- *Pressure + heat:* Autoclave curing for aerospace prepreg bonds (0.3 MPa at 175°C).

**Step 5 — Inspection (1 mark):** Non-destructive tests — ultrasonic C-scan, thermography, tap-test.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Load Transfer & Applications (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```
   ┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐
   │  Substrate A│─Adhesive│ Substrate B │
   │  (E high)   │  Layer  │  (E high)   │
   └──────┬──────┘         └──────┬──────┘
          │                       │
        Load P                 Reaction P
          │                       │
          ▼                       ▼
       ──►────►────►────►────►── (shear flow)
```

**Derivation of average shear stress (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{avg} \;&=\; \dfrac{P}{w \cdot l} \quad \text{[MPa]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $P$ = applied load (N), $w$ = bond width (mm), $l$ = bond length (mm). The stress is **not uniform** — it peaks at the overlap ends (Volkersen's shear-lag effect).

**Four engineering applications (2 marks — ½ each):**
1. **Aerospace:** Bonding of aluminium honeycomb core to face sheets in aircraft wings.
2. **Automotive:** Composite body-panel bonding in electric vehicles (e.g., BMW i3 carbon-fibre passenger cell).
3. **Electronics:** Die-attach adhesive (silver-filled epoxy) in semiconductor chip packaging.
4. **Civil engineering:** Carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP) wrap bonding for seismic retrofitting of concrete columns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. ❌ **Conflating "adhesion" with "cohesion"** — keep them strictly separate. Adhesion is at the interface, cohesion is within the bulk.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting to mention "wetting" before "curing"** — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the wetting/contact-angle step.
> 3. ❌ **Writing "epoxy" as a generic answer** — KTU expects the *class* (thermosetting) and *type* (e.g., polyamine-cured structural epoxy).
> 4. ❌ **Skipping Volkersen's insight** — for a 14-mark question, saying "stress is uniform" without mentioning **peak stress at the overlap ends** costs a full mark.
> 5. ❌ **Units missing or wrong** — always write MPa for stress, N/mm for peel force.
> 6. ❌ **No surface-preparation detail** — "the surface is cleaned" is incomplete. Mention *degreasing + abrasion + chemical treatment + primer*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision summary (memorize before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Adhesive bonding = joining via non-metallic substance; uses **adhesion** + **cohesion**, no parent-metal melting.
- **Five theories:** Mechanical Interlocking, Adsorption (Wetting), Chemical Bonding, Diffusion, Electrostatic.
- **Wetting condition:** $\theta \to 0$ is ideal; surface energy of substrate > surface tension of adhesive.
- **Key equations:**
  - Young: $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$
  - Dupré: $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$
  - Spreading: $S_c = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$
  - Lap shear: $\tau = P/(wl)$
  - Peak stress: governed by Volkersen's shear-lag.
- **Adhesive types:** Thermoplastic (reversible), Thermosetting (irreversible, structural), Elastomeric (flexible).
- **Process steps:** Surface prep → Application → Assembly → Curing → Inspection.
- **Cure types:** Room-temp, Heat, UV, Anaerobic, Moisture-cure, Autoclave.
- **Joint types:** Lap (shear), Butt (tension), Scarf, Strap, Tapered, Sleeve.
- **Surface prep is everything:** 70–80% of joint strength depends on it.
- **Advantages:** No HAZ, joins dissimilar metals, uniform stress, low weight, no fasteners.
- **Limitations:** Heat resistance < 250°C for most organics, surface sensitivity, curing time, peel weakness.
- **Applications (memorize 4):** Aerospace honeycomb, automotive composites, semiconductor die-attach, biomedical bone cement.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEMET416 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Explosive Welding: Principle and Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosive Welding: Principle and Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Explosive Welding (EXW)** is a solid-state, cold-pressure welding process in which the controlled detonation of an explosive charge accelerates one metallic component (the *flyer plate*) against a stationary base component (the *parent plate*) at extremely high velocities, producing an oblique collision. The collision generates a self-cleaning metallic *jet* that strips surface oxides and contaminants, enabling metallurgical bonding between the two surfaces without the base metals reaching their melting point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Explosive welding is a **solid-state joining process**. There is **no melting of the parent metals** in the bond zone. The joint is formed by a combination of plastic deformation, jetting, and atomic-level metallic bonding under extreme but brief impact pressure (typically **1–25 GPa** lasting only a few microseconds).

**Standard Industrial Terms (must memorize for KTU exam):**

- **Flyer Plate (Cladding Plate):** The upper, mobile plate accelerated by detonation.
- **Parent Plate (Base Plate):** The stationary lower plate.
- **Stand-off Distance ($s$):** The initial vertical gap between the two plates before detonation.
- **Collision Angle ($\beta$):** The angle between the flyer plate and the parent plate at the instant of impact.
- **Collision Point (Weld Front):** The localized region where the two surfaces meet.
- **Jet:** A thin stream of metal ejected ahead of the collision point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on a tall building and throwing a flat metal sheet downward at a slight angle onto a stationary steel plate on the ground.

1. If you drop it **flat** (parallel collision) — the sheet just slaps down flat. No jet. Weak or no bond.
2. If you **tilt it** and throw it with **tremendous speed** so one edge strikes first — a thin stream of metal (a *jet*) sprays out from the impact point, like water spraying off a skipping stone, and the two plates **weld together** along the collision line.

That is essentially explosive welding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the jet matters:** Atmospheric oxygen, oil, paint, and oxide layers coat every metal surface. In conventional welding, we burn them away with heat. In explosive welding, the **jet acts as a built-in cleaning agent** — it physically blasts the contaminants aside just before the two virgin metal surfaces meet. The cleaner the surfaces at the atomic level, the stronger the bond.

The **jet** is the secret sauce of EXW. It is what allows dissimilar metals (e.g., titanium to steel, aluminum to copper) that are metallurgically incompatible in fusion welding to be joined reliably.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Detonation velocity, $V_d$ | **1500 – 8000** | m/s |
| Flyer plate velocity, $V_p$ | **200 – 1000** | m/s |
| Collision angle, $\beta$ | **5° – 25°** | degrees |
| Stand-off distance, $s$ | **0.5 $t$ – 2 $t$** | mm |
| Impact pressure at weld point | **1 – 25** | GPa |
| Weld zone dwell time | **1 – 10** | $\mu$s |
| Bond zone width | **5 – 50** | mm |

Where $t$ is the flyer plate thickness in mm.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking the *weldability window* — a 2D plot of $V_p$ vs. $\beta$ with bounded regions for "no bond," "good weld," and "over-melting." Memorize the parameter ranges above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The explosive welding process unfolds in **four sequential stages**. Understanding each stage is essential for both theory and numerical problems.

### Stage 1 — Explosive Charge Arrangement
An explosive charge (typically **ammonia-based slurry**, ANFO, or powder explosives like PETN/RDX) is uniformly distributed over the **flyer plate**. The flyer plate is positioned parallel to the parent plate at a precisely controlled **stand-off distance** $s$.

- A **detonator** is placed at one edge to initiate a **planar, parallel detonation wave** that sweeps across the flyer plate at velocity $V_d$.

### Stage 2 — Flyer Plate Acceleration
As the detonation front passes beneath the explosive layer, the gases generated by the chemical reaction expand at supersonic speeds and **bend the flyer plate into an arc** (a dynamic bend angle, $\beta$).

The flyer plate accelerates toward the parent plate. The local velocity of the flyer plate just before impact is called the **plate (or terminal) velocity**, denoted $V_p$.

### Stage 3 — Oblique Collision and Jet Formation
When the curved flyer plate meets the flat parent plate, the collision is **oblique** (not perpendicular) because of the geometry. The two surfaces meet at the **collision point** at angle $\beta$.

At the collision point, the combined kinetic energy of the flyer plate and the geometry of impact cause a thin layer of metal from **both plates** to be ejected as a **jet**. This jet:

- Removes surface oxides, adsorbed gases, and contaminants.
- Travels at **2–3 times** the flyer plate velocity.
- Ejects **forward and upward**, away from the bond zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oblique collision is mandatory:** A purely perpendicular (flat) collision produces **no jet** and **no bonding**. The jet forms because the asymmetrical impact creates a pressure gradient that ejects metal ahead of the collision point. The minimum collision angle required is approximately **2° – 5°** depending on the materials.

### Stage 4 — Bond Formation
Immediately behind the collision point, where the jet has cleaned the surfaces, the flyer plate is pressed against the parent plate under **extreme impact pressure**. At this moment:

- Atomic-level metallic bonds form (similar to cold welding).
- A characteristic **wavy interface** (periodic wave pattern) develops, whose wavelength $\lambda$ depends on $V_p$ and $\beta$.
- The bond is created in **microseconds** with negligible heat-affected zone (HAZ).

## 2.2 The Three Bond Interface Morphologies

KTU examiners frequently ask students to **identify and describe the three interface types**. Memorize this:

1. **Straight Bond (No Wave):** Forms at low $V_p$ and low $\beta$. Weak bond, often rejected.
2. **Wavy Bond:** The **ideal, strongest** bond. Forms within the optimum weldability window. The wave amplitude grows with increasing $V_p$ and $\beta$.
3. **Melted (Continuous Melt Layer) Bond:** Forms at excessive $V_p$ or $\beta$. A thick interfacial melt layer forms, which is **brittle** and unacceptable. Indicates the **upper bound** of the weldability window.

The wavelength of the wavy interface is empirically given by:

$$
\lambda = K \cdot V_p^{\,2} \cdot \beta
$$

where $K$ is a material-dependent constant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the formulae below. These are the most tested expressions in Module 2 questions.**

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_p = 2 \, V_d \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)$ | **Plate velocity** from detonation velocity and dynamic bend angle. For small $\beta$, $V_p \approx V_d \, \beta$. | m/s |
| 2 | $\beta = 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_p}{2 V_d}\right)$ | Collision angle solved in terms of $V_p$ and $V_d$. | radians |
| 3 | $V_c = \dfrac{V_p}{\sin \beta}$ | **Collision-point velocity** (velocity of the weld front propagating along the interface). | m/s |
| 4 | $s = k \cdot t$ | Stand-off distance as a multiple of flyer plate thickness; $k \approx 0.5$ to $2.0$. | mm |
| 5 | $W_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_m \, t \, V_p^{\,2}$ | **Kinetic energy per unit area** of the flyer plate (drives plastic deformation). | J/m² |
| 6 | $P_{\text{impact}} = \rho_m \, V_p \, V_c$ | **Dynamic pressure** at the collision point (Hugoniot-type relation). | Pa |
| 7 | $\lambda = K \, V_p^{\,2} \, \beta$ | **Wavelength of the wavy interface**. | m |
| 8 | $\beta_{\min} \approx 2^\circ \text{ to } 5^\circ$ | Minimum collision angle for jet formation. | degrees |
| 9 | $\beta_{\max} \approx 25^\circ$ | Maximum collision angle before the sound barrier in the parent plate is violated. | degrees |
| 10 | $V_{p,\min} \approx 0.3 \, V_s$ | Minimum plate velocity (linked to bulk sound velocity of the softer material). | m/s |

Where:
- $V_d$ = detonation velocity
- $V_p$ = flyer plate velocity
- $V_c$ = collision point velocity
- $V_s$ = bulk sound velocity in the softer metal (typically **5000–6000 m/s** for steels)
- $\rho_m$ = density of the flyer plate material
- $t$ = flyer plate thickness
- $\beta$ = dynamic collision angle (bend angle)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Explosive welding is the only **commercially reliable** method to clad large surface areas of dissimilar metals that cannot be welded by fusion processes. Critical applications include:

- **Bimetallic transition joints** in shipbuilding (aluminum superstructure to steel hull) — saves weight, prevents galvanic corrosion.
- **Tube-to-tubesheet joints** in heat exchangers and nuclear steam generators.
- **Clad plates for pressure vessels, refinery columns, and chemical reactors** (corrosion-resistant layer over structural steel).
- **Armor plates** for defense (titanium / aluminum clads on rolled homogeneous armor).
- **Electrical transition joints** (aluminum-to-copper bus bars in substations).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters industrially:** A 3 mm titanium layer explosively clad on a 30 mm steel base plate gives **85% cost savings** over a solid titanium plate, with identical corrosion performance. This is the principal economic driver behind the technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Plate Velocity from Detonation Velocity and Bend Angle

This is the **single most important derivation in Module 2**. Almost every KTU question on explosive welding expects you to derive or apply this relation.

### Statement
Show that the velocity of the flyer plate just before impact is given by:

$$
V_p = 2 \, V_d \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)
$$

### Derivation (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

Consider the flyer plate as it is bent by the detonation wave. At the instant just before impact, the flyer plate has a dynamic bend angle $\beta$, and the detonation front moves along the surface at velocity $V_d$.

**Step 1 — Vector Setup.**
The detonation front sweeps along the flyer plate with velocity $V_d$. A small element of the flyer plate at the bend is being pushed **inward (downward)** and **forward (in the direction of detonation)**.

**Step 2 — Decompose the Detonation Velocity.**
The component of $V_d$ acting along the **tangent to the bent flyer plate** (perpendicular to the bend) drives the plate forward. Using the geometry of a wedge with half-angle $\beta/2$:

$$
V_p = 2 \, V_d \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)
$$

The factor of 2 arises because the detonation wave imparts momentum from **both the top and bottom surfaces** of the bent region effectively; geometrically, the flyer plate is bent through a total angle $\beta$, and the closing speed of the two arms of the bend is the sum of the two tangential projections.

**Step 3 — Verify Limiting Behavior.**

- As $\beta \to 0$ (no bend, parallel collision): $V_p \to 0$. ✓ (No jet, no useful welding.)
- As $\beta \to 90^\circ$ (extreme bend, vertical impact): $V_p \to 2 V_d \sin(45^\circ) = \sqrt{2} V_d$. ✓ (Maximum closing speed for this geometry.)

For the small-angle regime typical of EXW ($\beta < 25^\circ$), the approximation holds:

$$
\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right) \approx \dfrac{\beta}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_p \approx V_d \, \beta \quad (\beta \text{ in radians})
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A flyer plate is explosively welded with detonation velocity $V_d = 3500$ m/s and a measured dynamic bend angle $\beta = 12^\circ$. Calculate the flyer plate velocity.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $V_d = 3500$ m/s
- $\beta = 12^\circ = \dfrac{12 \pi}{180} = 0.2094$ rad

Applying the plate velocity equation:

$$
V_p = 2 \, V_d \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)
$$

Substitute:

$$
V_p = 2 \times 3500 \times \sin\!\left(\dfrac{12^\circ}{2}\right)
$$

$$
V_p = 7000 \times \sin(6^\circ)
$$

$$
V_p = 7000 \times 0.10453
$$

$$
V_p \approx 731.7 \; \text{m/s}
$$

The flyer plate is travelling at approximately **732 m/s** at impact — roughly **Mach 2.1**, fully consistent with explosive welding practice. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Collision Point Velocity

The collision point (weld front) propagates along the parent plate at velocity $V_c$. Geometrically, in the time the flyer plate closes the gap, the detonation moves a horizontal distance, and the collision point moves with it.

From the velocity triangle of the flyer plate impact:

$$
\sin \beta = \dfrac{V_p}{V_c}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{V_p}{\sin \beta}
$$

Substituting $V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)}{\sin \beta}
$$

Using the identity $\sin \beta = 2 \sin(\beta/2) \cos(\beta/2)$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)}{2 \sin(\beta/2) \cos(\beta/2)}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{V_d}{\cos(\beta/2)}
$$

This elegant result shows that the **collision point travels slightly faster than the detonation wave itself**, which is critical for sustaining the jetting action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students confuse $V_p$, $V_d$, and $V_c$. Remember: $V_d < V_c$ always, and $V_p \ll V_d$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kinetic Energy per Unit Area of the Flyer Plate

The driving energy for plastic deformation at the bond zone is the kinetic energy of the flyer plate per unit contact area. For a plate of thickness $t$, density $\rho_m$, and impact velocity $V_p$, the mass per unit area is $\rho_m t$. The kinetic energy per unit area is therefore:

$$
W_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho_m t) \cdot V_p^{\,2}
$$

$$
W_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_m \, t \, V_p^{\,2} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^2}\right]
$$

**Numerical Example:**

- $\rho_m$ (steel) = **7850 kg/m³**
- $t$ = 5 mm = 0.005 m
- $V_p$ = 700 m/s

$$
W_k = 0.5 \times 7850 \times 0.005 \times (700)^2
$$

$$
W_k = 0.5 \times 7850 \times 0.005 \times 490000
$$

$$
W_k \approx 9.62 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J/m}^2 = 9.62 \; \text{MJ/m}^2
$$

This enormous energy density is what enables the violent plastic flow and jetting, yet the process remains solid-state because the dwell time is in microseconds.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Effects on Weld Quality

> [!NOTE]
> **The matrix below is a KTU high-yield revision table. Print and memorize.**

| Parameter | Increased → Effect on $V_p$ | Increased → Effect on $\beta$ | Increased → Effect on Bond Quality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Explosive charge mass, $m_e$ | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | Improves up to optimum, then melts |
| Stand-off distance, $s$ | ↑ Increases (more acceleration space) | ↑ Increases | Improves up to a maximum, then over-accelerates |
| Flyer plate thickness, $t$ | ↓ Decreases (more inertia) | ↑ Increases | Reduces $V_p$; need to compensate with explosive mass |
| Flyer plate density, $\rho_m$ | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Heavier plates are harder to accelerate |
| Detonation velocity, $V_d$ | ↑ Increases (linear) | ↑ Increases | Higher-quality welds within the weldability window |
| Initial collision angle (geometry) | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | Critical for jet formation |

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Weldability Window — Conceptual Limits

The **weldability window** is the bounded region in the $(V_p, \beta)$ parameter space where a high-quality wavy bond is obtained. It is bounded by:

- **Lower bound on $V_p$:** Below this, the kinetic energy is insufficient to generate the jet, and bonding does not occur. Empirically:

$$
V_{p,\min} \approx 0.3 \, V_s
$$

where $V_s$ is the bulk sound velocity of the softer material.

- **Lower bound on $\beta$:** Below $\beta_{\min} \approx 2^\circ$ to $5^\circ$, the geometry does not produce a jet, and the bond is straight or absent.

- **Upper bound on $V_p$ and $\beta$:** If exceeded, a continuous **interfacial melt layer** forms at the bond zone. This occurs when the impact generates heat faster than it can be conducted away, in the timescale:

$$
\tau_{\text{dwell}} = \dfrac{\text{bond zone width}}{V_c}
$$

A useful relation linking heat generation to local strain rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{V_p \cdot \sin \beta}{t}
$$

When $\dot{\gamma}$ exceeds a critical value (typically $10^5$ /s for steels), localized melting occurs.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU problems:** Always quote both the lower bound and upper bound. Students who state only "optimum range" without numerical limits lose 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Explosive Welding Process

The diagram below maps the sequential functional blocks, parameter inputs, and quality outputs of the explosive welding process. Each block represents a stage or a governing physical phenomenon.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Explosive Charge Layer] --> B[Detonator Initiation]
    B --> C[Detonation Wave Travels at Vd]
    C --> D[Flyer Plate Bends Through Angle beta]
    D --> E[Plate Acceleration to Terminal Velocity Vp]
    E --> F[Oblique Collision at Weld Front]
    F --> G[Jet Formation Ahead of Collision Point]
    G --> H[Surface Oxide and Contaminant Stripping]
    H --> I[Atomic Level Metallurgical Bond]
    I --> J[Characteristic Wavy Interface Forms]
    J --> K{Quality Check}
    K -- Straight Bond --> L[Reject Low Energy Weld]
    K -- Wavy Bond --> M[Acceptable Good Weld]
    K -- Melt Layer --> N[Reject Over Melting]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Geometry of Oblique Collision

The following diagram represents the geometric configuration at the moment of impact. The flyer plate is bent through angle $\beta$, the detonation wave moves horizontally at $V_d$, and the flyer plate closes downward at $V_p$ along the tangent to the bend.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Detonation_Region
        D1[Detonation Wave Vd] -->|horizontal sweep| D2[Flyer Plate Top Surface]
    end
    subgraph Bend_Region
        D2 -->|bends downward| D3[Bent Flyer Plate at Angle beta]
        D3 -->|terminal velocity Vp| D4[Collision Point]
    end
    subgraph Bond_Region
        D4 -->|jet ejected| D5[Jet and Oxide Removal]
        D4 -->|metallic bond| D6[Wavy Interface]
        D6 --> D7[Parent Plate]
    end
    style D1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D3 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D5 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D6 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Weldability Window Map (Block Representation)

The figure below is a **block representation of the weldability window** as a function of collision angle $\beta$ and plate velocity $V_p$. It is the single most important conceptual diagram in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Region1[Region 1: NO BOND]
        R1A[beta less than 2 degrees OR Vp less than 0.3 Vs]
    end
    subgraph Region2[Region 2: GOOD WELDABLE ZONE]
        R2A[beta between 5 and 25 degrees]
        R2B[Vp between 0.3 Vs and 0.7 Vs]
        R2C[Wavy Interface Forms]
    end
    subgraph Region3[Region 3: OVER MELTING]
        R3A[beta greater than 25 degrees OR Vp greater than 0.7 Vs]
        R3B[Continuous Interfacial Melt Layer]
    end
    Region1 --> Region2
    Region2 --> Region3
    style Region1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Region2 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Region3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "explain the weldability window," always include: (1) the two axes ($V_p$ and $\beta$), (2) the three bounded regions, and (3) at least one **physical reason** for each boundary (e.g., lower bound — insufficient jet; upper bound — interfacial melting). A 14-mark answer without this triad will not score full marks.

## 4.4 Process Parameter Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Explosive Mass per Unit Area] --> B[Detonation Velocity Vd]
    C[Fuel Oxygen Ratio] --> B
    B --> D[Plate Velocity Vp]
    E[Stand-off Distance s] --> D
    F[Flyer Plate Thickness t] --> D
    D --> G[Collision Angle beta]
    G --> H[Jet Formation]
    H --> I[Interface Morphology]
    I --> I1[Straight]
    I --> I2[Wavy]
    I --> I3[Melted]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I2 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define explosive welding. List any four process parameters that govern the quality of an explosive weld.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Explosive welding is a solid-state joining process in which the controlled detonation of an explosive charge is used to accelerate a *flyer plate* against a *parent plate* at high velocity, producing an oblique collision that forms a metallurgical bond without melting the base metals.

**Process Parameters (1.5 marks — any four):**

1. **Detonation velocity** ($V_d$) of the explosive.
2. **Stand-off distance** ($s$) between the two plates.
3. **Collision angle** ($\beta$) at the weld front.
4. **Flyer plate thickness and density** ($\rho_m$, $t$).
5. Explosive charge mass per unit area.
6. Initial geometry (parallel vs. angular plate arrangement).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "explosive and plate" as parameters. Examiners expect **four distinct, named parameters with units or symbols**. Missing units cost 0.5 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]
**What is a weldability window in explosive welding? State the typical range of collision angle and plate velocity for a successful weld.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **weldability window** is the bounded region in the parameter space (collision angle $\beta$ vs. plate velocity $V_p$) within which a strong, wavy metallurgical bond forms during explosive welding. Outside this region, either no bonding occurs (lower bound) or a melted, brittle interface forms (upper bound).

**Typical Range (2 marks):**
- Collision angle: $\beta = 5^\circ$ to $25^\circ$ (minimum $\approx 2^\circ$ for jet initiation)
- Plate velocity: $V_p \approx 0.3 \, V_s$ to $0.7 \, V_s$ (where $V_s$ is the bulk sound velocity of the softer material, e.g., $\approx 1500$–$3500$ m/s for common metals).

A **wavy interface** indicates a sound weld; a **melted interfacial layer** indicates the upper bound has been exceeded.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the relationship between the flyer plate velocity, detonation velocity, and dynamic bend angle in explosive welding. State clearly all assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**(b) A 4 mm thick steel flyer plate ($\rho = 7850$ kg/m³) is explosively welded to a steel parent plate using an explosive of detonation velocity 3200 m/s. The measured dynamic bend angle is $10^\circ$. Calculate: (i) the flyer plate velocity, (ii) the collision point velocity, and (iii) the kinetic energy per unit area delivered to the bond zone. (7 marks)**

#### Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. The expected model answer should include:

- A clear **free-body / vector diagram** showing the detonation wave moving horizontally and the flyer plate bending through angle $\beta$.
- The geometric argument that the **closing speed of the two arms of the bent flyer plate** equals $2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$.
- The **assumptions** (1 mark): small-angle geometry, planar detonation wave, flyer plate treated as rigid during bend, no plastic dissipation in the bulk of the flyer plate.
- Final result: $V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $t = 4$ mm = $0.004$ m
- $\rho_m = 7850$ kg/m³
- $V_d = 3200$ m/s
- $\beta = 10^\circ = \dfrac{10 \pi}{180} = 0.1745$ rad

**(i) Flyer plate velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$
V_p = 2 \, V_d \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
V_p = 2 \times 3200 \times \sin(5^\circ)
$$

$$
V_p = 6400 \times 0.08716
$$

$$
\boxed{V_p \approx 557.8 \; \text{m/s}}
$$

**(ii) Collision point velocity (2.5 marks):**

$$
V_c = \dfrac{V_p}{\sin \beta} = \dfrac{557.8}{\sin(10^\circ)} = \dfrac{557.8}{0.1736}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 3213 \; \text{m/s}}
$$

(Note: $V_c \approx V_d / \cos(\beta/2) = 3200 / \cos(5^\circ) = 3200 / 0.9962 \approx 3213$ m/s. ✓)

**(iii) Kinetic energy per unit area (2 marks):**

$$
W_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_m \, t \, V_p^{\,2}
$$

$$
W_k = 0.5 \times 7850 \times 0.004 \times (557.8)^2
$$

$$
W_k = 0.5 \times 7850 \times 0.004 \times 311{,}141
$$

$$
\boxed{W_k \approx 4.88 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J/m}^2 \approx 4.88 \; \text{MJ/m}^2}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted Model Question]**

**(a) Explain the mechanism of bond formation in explosive welding with neat sketches of the three possible interface morphologies. Discuss the role of the metallic jet. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the weldability window of explosive welding. What are the consequences of operating outside its lower and upper limits? (7 marks)**

#### Solution

**(a) Mechanism of Bond Formation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Oblique collision (1.5 marks):**
When the flyer plate strikes the parent plate at the collision angle $\beta$, two surfaces meet at the **collision point** (weld front). The geometry is critical: a parallel collision produces no bonding.

**Step 2 — Jet formation (2 marks):**
At the collision point, the combined kinetic energy and the asymmetry of impact eject a thin **metallic jet** ahead of the weld front. The jet:

- Travels at **2–3 times** the flyer plate velocity.
- Strips **oxides, adsorbed gases, paint, oil**, and other surface contaminants.
- Is formed from material of **both plates** (not just the surface layer).

**Step 3 — Clean metallic contact and bonding (1.5 marks):**
Immediately behind the jet, two atomically clean metal surfaces are pressed together under **1–25 GPa pressure** for **microseconds**. At this intimacy, **metallic bonds** (electron-sharing) form across the interface, creating a true metallurgical joint in the solid state.

**Step 4 — Interface morphologies (2 marks):**
- **Straight (flat) interface:** low $V_p$, low $\beta$ — insufficient jet, weak bond.
- **Wavy interface:** optimum $V_p$ and $\beta$ — strongest bond, characteristic morphology.
- **Continuous melt layer:** excessive $V_p$ or $\beta$ — localized melting, brittle, unacceptable.

**Role of the jet:** The jet is the **self-cleaning agent** that replaces the flux and shielding gas of fusion welding. Without the jet, the bond would be weak due to interfacial contamination.

**(b) Weldability Window (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The weldability window is the bounded region in the $(V_p, \beta)$ parameter space inside which a strong, wavy, metallurgical bond forms.

**Construction (2 marks):** Two axes — $V_p$ (vertical) and $\beta$ (horizontal). Three regions are demarcated: *no bond*, *weldable zone*, *over-melting zone*.

**Lower bound (1.5 marks):**
- If $\beta < \beta_{\min} \approx 2^\circ$ to $5^\circ$, the geometry does not produce a jet → **no metallic bond**.
- If $V_p < 0.3 V_s$, kinetic energy is too low to overcome the material's yield strength and produce plastic flow → **no bond or weak bond**.

**Upper bound (1.5 marks):**
- If $\beta > 25^\circ$ or $V_p > 0.7 V_s$, the local strain rate at the bond zone is so high that frictional heat cannot dissipate → **interfacial melting** occurs.
- A continuous, brittle melt layer forms → **rejected weld**.

**Practical example (1 mark):** For a steel–steel weld, $V_s \approx 5900$ m/s, so the practical $V_p$ window is approximately **1770 m/s to 4130 m/s**, with $\beta$ between $5^\circ$ and $25^\circ$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_p$ with $V_d$ and $V_c$:** $V_d$ is the chemical detonation speed; $V_p$ is the mechanical velocity of the flyer plate just before impact (much smaller); $V_c$ is the speed of the weld front (slightly larger than $V_d$). Examiners *specifically* test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting assumptions in the $V_p$ derivation:** A full-mark derivation must state the assumption of a **rigid flyer plate** and **planar detonation wave**. Skipping this costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Not mentioning the jet:** Any answer about explosive welding bond formation that omits the metallic jet is considered incomplete. Always mention the jet in the first paragraph.
>
> 4. **Units:** Plate velocity must be in m/s, collision angle in degrees (or radians with explicit conversion), energy in J/m². Missing units in the final boxed answer → lose 0.5 mark.
>
> 5. **Weldability window without numerical limits:** Stating "an optimum range exists" without quoting $\beta$ and $V_p$ numerical bounds → lose 1 mark per missing bound.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Explosive Welding**

- [ ] **Explosive welding is a *solid-state* process** — no melting of parent metals.
- [ ] **Three components:** explosive, flyer plate (mobile), parent plate (stationary).
- [ ] **Four stages:** charge layout → flyer acceleration → oblique collision + jetting → atomic bonding.
- [ ] **The metallic jet is the cleaning agent** — removes oxides and contaminants.
- [ ] **Oblique collision is mandatory** — $\beta_{\min} \approx 2^\circ$–$5^\circ$.
- [ ] **Three interface morphologies:** straight, wavy (best), melted (worst).
- [ ] **Key formula 1:** $V_p = 2 V_d \sin(\beta/2)$.
- [ ] **Key formula 2:** $V_c = V_d / \cos(\beta/2) > V_d$.
- [ ] **Key formula 3:** $W_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_m t V_p^{\,2}$.
- [ ] **Key formula 4:** $P_{\text{impact}} = \rho_m V_p V_c$.
- [ ] **Weldability window bounds:** $V_p \in [0.3 V_s,\; 0.7 V_s]$ and $\beta \in [5^\circ,\; 25^\circ]$.
- [ ] **Wavy interface wavelength:** $\lambda = K V_p^{\,2} \beta$.
- [ ] **Stand-off distance:** $s = (0.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot t$.
- [ ] **Typical industrial use:** bimetallic transition joints (Al–steel, Ti–steel), clad plates, armor.
- [ ] **KTU rule:** Always state $V_p$ in m/s, $\beta$ in degrees (convert if used in radians), and $W_k$ in J/m² or MJ/m².
- [ ] **Common examiner trap:** Don't confuse detonation velocity $V_d$ with plate velocity $V_p$ or collision point velocity $V_c$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
