topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipment Used

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — EQUIPMENT USED IN ADVANCED METAL JOINING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET416)**, **"Equipment Used"** refers to the complete set of mechanical, electrical, electronic, pneumatic, and hydraulic hardware assemblies that are required to deliver the necessary energy (thermal, mechanical, electrical, or kinetic) and consumables (filler metal, shielding gas, flux, electrode, beam medium) to the joint interface in order to produce a metallurgically sound weld in advanced metal joining processes.

Advanced metal joining equipment is broadly classified into:

1. **Power Source Unit (PSU)** — Provides regulated electrical energy of the required waveform.
2. **Wire Feed System (WFS)** — Delivers filler wire/electrode at a controlled rate.
3. **Torch / Gun Assembly** — Directs the arc, beam, or energy to the workpiece.
4. **Shielding Gas Delivery System (SGDS)** — Supplies inert/reactive gas to protect the weld pool.
5. **Cooling System** — Removes heat from the torch, electrode, and cables.
6. **Control & Automation System** — Sequences current, voltage, travel, gas pre-flow/post-flow, and arc initiation.
7. **Work Handling & Positioning System** — Includes jigs, fixtures, manipulators, and turning rolls.
8. **Auxiliary Equipment** — Traction units, seam tracking, arc sensors, and feedback systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO Alignment):**
> - **CO2**: *Understand the equipment, working principles, and process parameters of advanced metal joining techniques.*
> This module directly addresses **CO2** at cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** under **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a dish on a gas stove:
- The **gas cylinder + regulator** is like the **shielding gas cylinder + flowmeter** of a welding machine.
- The **gas stove burner** is the **welding torch** — both deliver controlled energy to a focused spot.
- The **gas lighter** is the **HF arc starter** in TIG welding — both initiate the flame/arc.
- The **pressure regulator on the cylinder** is the **voltage/current regulator** on the welding power source.
- The **gas pipeline** is the **cable conduit** carrying current and shielding gas to the torch.
- The **cook's hands moving the pan** is the **mechanized travel carriage / robot** of an automated welding system.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a chef cannot cook without the right tools, an advanced welding process cannot produce a quality weld without precisely engineered equipment. Every knob, sensor, and feedback loop in welding equipment exists to control **ONE** of the four welding variables: **Current, Voltage, Travel Speed, or Heat Input**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Duty Cycle:** Typically **60% at rated current** as per **IS 8130 / IEC 60974-1**.
- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Limited to a maximum of **80 V (DC)** and **48 V (AC)** for operator safety per **IEC 60974-1**.
- **Shielding Gas Purity:** **99.99% pure Argon** minimum for TIG/GTAW; **99.998% (5N)** for critical aerospace applications.
- **Standard Power Source Output:** Constant Current (CC), Constant Voltage (CV), or Constant Power (CP) — measured in **Amperes, Volts, and kVA**.
- **Wire Feed Speed (WFS):** Expressed in **m/min** or **in/min**.
- **Cooling Water Conductivity:** Must be **< 5 μS/cm** for water-cooled TIG torches to prevent electrical leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 80 V OCV limit?** Beyond this, the human body's skin resistance breaks down in humid/wet conditions, posing a lethal shock hazard. The IEC standard mandates this cap for operator protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Welding Equipment

Advanced metal joining equipment is classified on multiple axes:

### A. Based on Energy Source

| Category | Examples | Energy Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Electric Arc** | GTAW, GMAW, PAW, SMAW | Electrical discharge |
| **High Energy Beam** | LBW, EBW | Photonic / Electron kinetic |
| **Solid-State** | FSW, USW, Diffusion Bonding | Mechanical / Thermal |
| **Hybrid** | Laser-Arc Hybrid, Plasma-MIG | Combined energy |
| **Resistance** | Spot, Projection, Seam, Flash | Joule heating ($I^2R$) |
| **Thermochemical** | Oxy-fuel, Thermite | Combustion |

### B. Based on Automation Level

| Level | Description | Equipment Class |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual** | Operator-held torch | Hand-held GTAW/GMAW |
| **Semi-automatic** | Mechanized wire feed, manual travel | Mig with hand travel |
| **Mechanized** | Automated travel, manual parameter setting | Carriage-mounted GMAW |
| **Automatic** | Sensors, seam tracking, parameter feedback | Robotic GMAW/PAW |
| **Adaptive/Intelligent** | Closed-loop control with AI/ML | Smart welding cells |

### C. Based on Portability

| Type | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Static / Stationary** | Workshop-based, high-duty production |
| **Portable Inverter** | Field welding, construction sites |
| **Engine-Driven** | Remote sites without grid power |

---

## 2.2 Equipment Details for Each Advanced Process

### 2.2.1 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG/MAG) Equipment

The GMAW system consists of:

1. **Power Source** — Constant Voltage (CV) type, drooping characteristic < 5 V per 100 A. Inverter-based IGBT/MOSFET power sources dominate modern industry.
2. **Wire Feeder** — Two main types:
   - **Push type** (feeder ahead of gun) — used for soft wires up to **0.8–1.2 mm**, distances up to **3–4 m**.
   - **Push-Pull type** (feeder at source + secondary at gun) — for longer distances up to **8–10 m**, aluminum/stainless.
3. **Welding Gun** — Rated for current (e.g., **200 A, 300 A, 400 A, 500 A CO₂-cooled**; up to **600 A water-cooled**).
4. **Shielding Gas System** — Regulator, flowmeter (typically **10–25 L/min**), gas hose, and gas nozzle.
5. **Cooling System** — Water recirculator for high-current water-cooled guns (flow rate **1–1.5 L/min**, pressure **2–3 bar**).
6. **Earth Clamp & Return Cable** — Completes the electrical circuit.

### 2.2.2 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) Equipment

1. **Power Source** — Constant Current (CC) type with high OCV (typically **60–80 V**). Supports **DCEN, DCEP, AC/DC** modes. Inverter-based units offer **square wave AC** for aluminum.
2. **TIG Torch** — Rated current **125 A (air-cooled)**, **200–400 A (water-cooled)**. Duty cycle 60%.
3. **High-Frequency (HF) Arc Starter** — Produces **1–2 MHz, 2500–3000 V** spark to ionize the gap without contact.
4. **Gas Lens / Collet Body** — Provides laminar shielding gas flow for better coverage.
5. **Filler Rod Feed** — Manual dabbing or mechanized cold/hot wire feed.
6. **Foot Pedal / Amptrol** — Variable current control via potentiometer.

### 2.2.3 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) Equipment

PAW equipment includes everything in GTAW **PLUS**:
1. **Plasma Torch with Constricting Nozzle** — Orifice diameter typically **0.8–3.2 mm**.
2. **Secondary Shielding Gas** — Distinct from primary plasma gas.
3. **Plasma Gas Control Unit** — Two flowmeters (primary orifice gas, secondary shielding gas).
4. **Water Cooling Jacket** — Around the tungsten electrode for high-current operation.
5. **Keyhole PAW** requires **pilot arc** to initiate transferred arc.

> [!NOTE]
> The constricting nozzle in PAW is what differentiates it from GTAW. It physically squeezes the arc, increasing energy density from **~10⁴ W/cm² (GTAW)** to **~10⁵–10⁶ W/cm² (PAW)**.

### 2.2.4 Laser Beam Welding (LBW) Equipment

1. **Laser Source** — CO₂ (10.6 μm) or Nd:YAG (1.06 μm) or Fiber/Disc laser.
2. **Beam Delivery System** — Mirrors (CO₂) or fiber optic cable (YAG/Fiber).
3. **Focusing Head** — ZnSe lens (CO₂) or fused silica lens (YAG/Fiber); focal length **50–200 mm**.
4. **Nozzle Assembly** — Coaxial or side-jet shielding gas.
5. **Motion System** — High-precision XY or gantry stages with **±0.05 mm** repeatability.
6. **Power Monitor & Beam Profiler** — Real-time quality control.

### 2.2.5 Electron Beam Welding (EBW) Equipment

1. **Electron Gun** — Tungsten filament cathode, anode, grid cup (triode configuration).
2. **High-Voltage Power Supply** — Accelerating voltage **30–200 kV DC**.
3. **Magnetic Focusing & Deflection Coils** — Shape and steer the beam.
4. **Vacuum Chamber** — Pressure **10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ mbar** (or 10⁻⁶ mbar for high-vacuum EBW).
5. **Work Manipulation System** — CNC-controlled inside vacuum.
6. **Viewing System** — Optical telescope or CCTV through lead-glass window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X-ray hazard in EBW:** When accelerating voltage exceeds **30 kV**, the electron beam striking the workpiece generates **bremsstrahlung X-rays**. The chamber must be lead-shielded, and operators must wear **TLD badges** for dosimetry per **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India)** guidelines.

### 2.2.6 Friction Stir Welding (FSW) Equipment

1. **Machine Frame** — Heavy-duty gantry or C-frame to resist forces up to **50–100 kN**.
2. **Spindle & Tool Holder** — Rotates tool at **200–2000 RPM** (typical) with tilt angle **0–3°**.
3. **FSW Tool** — Consists of **shoulder** (diameter 1.5–3× pin diameter) and **pin** (threaded, fluted, or feature-rich).
4. **Clamping System** — Hydraulic/anvil fixture to prevent workpiece "flash" formation.
5. **Force/Position/Torque Control System** — Modern machines use **load cells** and **LVDTs** for closed-loop control.
6. **Cooling System** — Optional chilled backing plate for high-melting-point alloys.

### 2.2.7 Resistance Welding Equipment

1. **Welding Transformer** — Step-down to **2–20 V** with very high current (**5000–30,000 A**).
2. **Electrodes** — Water-cooled Cu-Cr-Zr or Cu-Be alloy; tip diameter **4–8 mm** (spot).
3. **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Actuator** — Electrode force **1.5–10 kN**.
4. **Weld Timer / Synchronous SCR Control** — Precisely times weld current (number of cycles at 50 Hz).
5. **Sequence Controller** — Squeeze → Weld → Hold → Off timing.

### 2.2.8 Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) Equipment

1. **Power Source** — Either CC or CV (typically **600–1500 A** rating).
2. **Wire Feed System** — Constant-speed or voltage-sensing.
3. **Flux Hopper & Delivery Tube** — Granular flux fed ahead of the arc.
4. **Flux Recovery System** — Suction-based recycling of unfused flux.
5. **Contact Nozzle / Conduit** — Current transfer to wire.
6. **Travel Carriage** — Tractor or bogie.

---

## 2.3 Power Source Technologies — Deep Dive

| Generation | Technology | Weight (10 kVA unit) | Efficiency | Switching Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Transformer (50 Hz) | ~150 kg | 50–60% | 50 Hz |
| **2nd Gen** | Thyristor-Phase Control | ~80 kg | 65–75% | 300 Hz |
| **3rd Gen** | Transistor (BJT) Inverter | ~40 kg | 80–85% | 20 kHz |
| **4th Gen** | MOSFET/IGBT Inverter | ~25 kg | 85–92% | 20–100 kHz |
| **5th Gen** | Digital/Smart Inverter | ~20 kg | >92% | 100 kHz+ |

> [!NOTE]
> Higher switching frequency → smaller transformer → lighter, more portable, and more responsive power source with better arc dynamics.

---

## 2.4 The 'Why' & 'How' Behind Each Step

1. **Why Constant Voltage (CV) for GMAW?**  
   CV sources self-regulate current via arc length feedback. If the torch-to-work distance fluctuates, voltage changes, current self-adjusts, and **wire burn-off rate remains constant** — ideal for self-shielding wire-feed processes.

2. **Why Constant Current (CC) for GTAW?**  
   In TIG, arc length is not maintained by wire feed (electrode is non-consumable). CC ensures the current remains stable even as the operator varies torch height, preventing burn-through on thin sheets.

3. **Why HF Starter in TIG instead of scratch start?**  
   Scratch start contaminates the tungsten electrode with workpiece material, degrading arc stability and weld pool cleanliness. HF start is **non-contact** and preserves electrode integrity.

4. **Why Vacuum in EBW?**  
   Electrons are scattered by air molecules over short distances. Vacuum allows the beam to travel ballistically and prevents plasma formation that would otherwise defocus the beam.

5. **Why High-Pressure Water in FSW cooling?**  
   The tool and workpiece interface can reach **0.8–0.9 × T_melting**. Chilled backing plates maintain dimensional accuracy and prevent dissolution of strengthening precipitates in age-hardenable alloys like **AA2024, AA7075**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input (arc processes) | J/mm | All arc processes |
| $H = \dfrac{I^2 R t}{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Heat input (resistance) | J | Resistance welding |
| $E_{beam} = e \cdot V_{acc}$ | Electron beam energy | eV (J) | EBW |
| $Power_{laser} = E_{pulse} \cdot f$ | Average laser power (pulsed) | W | Pulsed LBW |
| $I = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{total}}$ | Welding current (initial) | A | Arc circuits |
| $F_{el} = k \cdot p \cdot A_{el}$ | Electrode force | N | Resistance welding |
| $Q_{nugget} = I^2 R t \cdot \eta_{th}$ | Nugget heat | J | Spot welding |
| $Nugget_{d} = 4\sqrt{t} \, \text{(steel)}$ | Nugget diameter empirical | mm | Spot welding (steel) |
| $v_{pin} = 2 \pi r \cdot RPM$ | Tool tangential velocity | mm/s | FSW |
| $F_{axial} \approx 0.6 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot A_{pin}$ | Approx. FSW axial force | N | FSW mechanics |
| $Duty \, Cycle = \dfrac{t_{on}}{t_{on}+t_{off}} \times 100$ | Equipment duty cycle | % | All equipment |
| $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Coolant heat removal | W | Water-cooled torches |
| $E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Welding energy | J | All processes |
| $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \cdot H_f}{V \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Thermal efficiency | dimensionless | All processes |
| $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Arc power | W | All arc processes |
| $Current \, Density \, J = \dfrac{I}{A_{elec}}$ | Current density at electrode | A/mm² | Arc physics |
| $Beam \, Intensity = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Beam power density | W/mm² | LBW, EBW |
| $Vacuum \, Mean \, Free \, Path \propto \dfrac{T}{P}$ | Vacuum quality metric | m | EBW chamber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Heat input (H)** controls weld metallurgy, HAZ width, and distortion.
> - **Beam intensity** determines the welding mode (conduction vs keyhole) in LBW/EBW.
> - **Nugget diameter formula** is used as a quality check in resistance spot welding.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: FSW and EBW are used for wing skin panels and engine casings — equipment must achieve **±0.1 mm** positional accuracy and **< 1%** porosity.
- **Automotive Industry**: Robotic GMAW cells perform **60–80 welds per minute** on car bodies; synchronized control systems reduce cycle time.
- **Shipbuilding**: SAW with **1500 A** power sources lay down **10 kg/hr** of weld metal for thick plate butt joints.
- **Nuclear Industry**: EBW in vacuum chambers is used for fuel cladding, where contamination is unacceptable.
- **Pipeline Construction**: Portable inverter-based SMAW/GMAW machines with **in-built hot-start, arc-force, and anti-stick** features allow field welds in extreme conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input in Arc Welding

The **heat input (H)** is the total energy per unit length delivered to the workpiece. It is the single most important process control parameter in arc welding.

### Step 1: Define Electrical Energy

The electrical energy delivered to the arc is the product of voltage, current, and time:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

where:  
- $E$ = energy in Joules  
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts  
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes  
- $t$ = time of welding in seconds

### Step 2: Account for Process Efficiency

Not all electrical energy reaches the workpiece — some is lost in radiation, spatter, and the electrode. The **heat transfer efficiency (η)** accounts for this:

$$H_{delivered} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

Common efficiency values (MSSP — measured standard values):  
- **GMAW (Steel)**: $\eta = 0.80$  
- **GTAW (Steel)**: $\eta = 0.60$  
- **SAW**: $\eta = 0.95$  
- **SMAW**: $\eta = 0.75$  
- **PAW (keyhole)**: $\eta = 0.55$  
- **LBW**: $\eta = 0.40$  
- **EBW**: $\eta = 0.80$

### Step 3: Convert to Energy Per Unit Length

To express energy per unit length, divide by the travel distance $L = v \cdot t$:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{v \cdot t} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

Resulting in the **standard arc heat input formula**:

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}}$$

with units of **J/mm** (or kJ/mm in practice).

### Step 4: Numerical Example

A GMAW weld uses 28 V, 250 A, at 6 mm/s travel speed.  
Given: $\eta_{GMAW} = 0.80$

$$H = \dfrac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250}{6} = \dfrac{5600}{6} = 933.33 \, J/mm$$

$$H = 0.933 \, kJ/mm$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Limit:** For most low-carbon steels, H should be kept between **0.5–2.5 kJ/mm** to balance penetration, HAZ size, and distortion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density and Welding Mode in LBW/EBW

### Step 1: Define Beam Power Density

Power density (intensity) at the workpiece:

$$I_{beam} = \dfrac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \dfrac{P_{beam}}{\pi \, r^2}$$

where:  
- $P_{beam}$ = beam power in W  
- $r$ = focal spot radius in mm  
- $I_{beam}$ = power density in W/mm²

### Step 2: Threshold for Keyhole Formation

When $I_{beam} \geq 10^6$ W/mm², the metal vaporizes violently, forming a **vapor capillary (keyhole)** that allows deep penetration welding.

### Step 3: Numerical Example

A fiber laser delivers 4 kW through a 200 μm focal spot.

$$A_{spot} = \pi \times (0.1)^2 = 0.0314 \, mm^2$$

$$I_{beam} = \dfrac{4000}{0.0314} = 1.27 \times 10^5 \, W/mm^2$$

This is **conduction-mode** LBW (no keyhole). To get keyhole, spot size must be reduced or power increased.

> [!NOTE]
> **CO₂ laser at 10.6 μm** is more efficiently absorbed by metals when focused. **Fiber laser at 1.06 μm** has 5–10× better absorptivity on aluminum — making fiber lasers dominant in EV battery welding.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resistance Spot Welding Heat Generation

### Step 1: Joule Heating in Stack

The heat generated in the workpiece stack is:

$$Q = I^2 R t \cdot \eta_{th}$$

where:  
- $I$ = welding current in A  
- $R$ = total stack resistance in ohms  
- $t$ = weld time in seconds  
- $\eta_{th}$ = thermal efficiency ≈ 0.5–0.8 for spot welding

### Step 2: Nugget Formation Criterion

For a quality nugget, the heat must be sufficient to melt a volume:

$$Q = m \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + H_f)$$

where:  
- $m$ = mass of metal melted  
- $H_f$ = latent heat of fusion  
- $c_p$ = specific heat  
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise

### Step 3: Empirical Nugget Diameter (Steel)

For low-carbon steel sheets, the empirical rule:

$$d_{nugget} = 5 \, \sqrt{t} \quad (t \text{ in seconds, } d \text{ in mm})$$

For a weld time of $t = 0.4$ s:

$$d_{nugget} = 5 \sqrt{0.4} = 5 \times 0.632 = 3.16 \, mm$$

Acceptable nugget for **1 mm + 1 mm** sheet combination.

### Step 4: Electrode Force Calculation

Electrode force is empirically:

$$F = 4 \times d_{nugget} \, \text{(in daN)}$$

$$F = 4 \times 3.16 = 12.64 \, daN = 1264 \, N$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — FSW Tool Velocity & Heat Generation

### Step 1: Tangential Velocity of Pin Surface

$$v_{tan} = 2 \pi r_{pin} \cdot \dfrac{RPM}{60}$$

For pin radius $r = 4 \, mm$, $RPM = 800$:

$$v_{tan} = 2 \pi \times 4 \times \dfrac{800}{60} = 335.1 \, mm/s$$

### Step 2: Heat Generation from Friction

$$Q_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot v_{tan}$$

where:  
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (0.3–0.5 at working temperature)  
- $F_N$ = axial force (N)

### Step 3: Heat Generation from Plastic Deformation

$$Q_{plastic} = \tau_{flow} \cdot A_{pin} \cdot v_{tan}$$

### Step 4: Total Heat Input in FSW

$$Q_{total} = Q_{friction} + Q_{plastic}$$

$$\boxed{H_{FSW} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot RPM}{v_{travel}}}$$

where: $M$ is the torque, and $\eta \approx 0.85$ for aluminum.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Duty Cycle & Equipment Sizing

### Step 1: Definition

$$DC = \dfrac{t_{weld}}{t_{weld} + t_{cool}} \times 100\%$$

### Step 2: Effective Current Rating

A machine rated at 300 A at 60% duty cycle means it can deliver 300 A for 6 minutes out of every 10 minutes.

### Step 3: Derating for Different Duty Cycles

Using the constant load convention:

$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{DC_1}{DC_2}}$$

If a machine is rated 300 A @ 60%, its current at 100% DC is:

$$I_{100} = 300 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{60}{100}} = 300 \times 0.7746 = 232.4 \, A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exceeding the duty cycle** causes insulation failure, transformer overheating, and ultimately equipment burnout. Welding machines in production must be **sized** correctly for the expected current and duty cycle.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Process vs Equipment

| **Process** | **Power Source** | **Type** | **OCV (V)** | **Special Equipment** | **Automation Level** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | CV Inverter | DC | 18–30 | Wire feeder, gas regulator | Semi-Auto to Robot |
| **GTAW (TIG)** | CC Inverter | DC/AC | 60–80 | HF starter, gas lens, foot pedal | Manual to CNC |
| **PAW** | CC Inverter + Plasma Control | DC | 60–80 | Constricting nozzle, dual gas | Manual to CNC |
| **LBW** | Solid-state/YAG/CO₂ laser | CW/Pulsed | N/A | Beam delivery, focus head, scanner | CNC / Robotic |
| **EBW** | High-voltage DC | DC | 30–200 kV | Vacuum chamber, magnetic lens | CNC (in vacuum) |
| **FSW** | Servo-hydraulic/Mech. | N/A | N/A | Spindle, tool, clamping, load cell | CNC |
| **Spot Welding** | Single-phase AC transformer | AC | <20 | Pneumatic actuator, timer, electrodes | Manual to Robotic |
| **SAW** | CC or CV Transformer/Inverter | DC | 30–40 | Flux hopper, recovery, tractor | Mechanized |
| **USW** | High-frequency piezo | 20–40 kHz | N/A | Transducer, horn, anvil | Manual to Auto |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | Vacuum furnace + Hydraulic press | N/A | N/A | Vacuum chamber, heater, load frame | Automatic |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the major components of a Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW) equipment setup."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major components of GMAW equipment are:
1. **Power Source** — Constant Voltage (CV) type inverter, 200–500 A rating.
2. **Wire Feeder** — Push-type or push-pull type with speed control in m/min.
3. **Welding Gun** — With contact tip, gas nozzle, and trigger; air or water-cooled.
4. **Shielding Gas System** — Cylinder, regulator, flowmeter, gas hose, and nozzle.
5. **Earth Clamp & Return Cable** — Completes the electrical circuit to the workpiece.
6. **Cooling System** — For water-cooled high-current guns.

*(1 mark for power source + wire feeder, 1 mark for gun & gas system, 1 mark for cooling & return path.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Constant Current (CC) and Constant Voltage (CV) welding power sources with one example process each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **CC (Constant Current)** | **CV (Constant Voltage)** |
|---|---|---|
| **V-I Characteristic** | Steep droop | Flat |
| **Arc Length Effect** | Small change in current | Large self-correction |
| **Application** | GTAW, PAW, SMAW | GMAW, FCAW, SAW |
| **Self-Regulation** | Limited | Excellent via wire burn-off |
| **Arc Stability with distance change** | Manual control needed | Automatic |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for application example, 1 mark for characteristic difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 | Module 2 Choice]**

**Q. (a)** Describe in detail the equipment used in **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** with a neat block diagram. List the functions of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Inverter-based welding power source** with its block diagram. Compare it with conventional transformer-based power source. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**GTAW Equipment — Components and Functions (7 marks):**

1. **Power Source (CC type)** — Provides constant current with high OCV (60–80 V); supports DCEN for steel/stainless, AC for aluminum/magnesium. *(1 mark)*

2. **TIG Torch** — Holds the tungsten electrode, channels shielding gas, and conducts current. Air-cooled (up to 200 A) or water-cooled (up to 400 A). *(1 mark)*

3. **High-Frequency (HF) Arc Starter** — Generates 1–2 MHz, 2500–3000 V high-frequency voltage to ionize the electrode-to-workpiece gap for **non-contact arc initiation**, preventing tungsten contamination. *(1 mark)*

4. **Shielding Gas System** — Argon cylinder (99.99% pure), two-stage regulator, flowmeter (5–15 L/min), gas hose, gas lens in torch for laminar flow. *(1 mark)*

5. **Foot Pedal (Amptrol) / Remote Current Control** — Potentiometer-based device allowing the welder to vary current in real-time during welding. *(1 mark)*

6. **Cooling System** — For water-cooled torches: pump, tank, radiator, flow switch, and thermostat. *(1 mark)*

7. **Filler Rod** — Manually or mechanically fed (cold wire or hot wire) into the leading edge of the weld pool. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> The HF starter unit is housed inside the power source cabinet. The argon line enters through the rear of the torch handle, the current cable is screwed to the back of the torch, and the water hoses (in/out) connect to a chiller.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Inverter-Based Welding Power Source (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Grid 415V AC → EMI Filter → 3-Phase Rectifier → DC Link Capacitor 
→ Inverter (IGBT @ 20–100 kHz) → HF Transformer (Step-down) 
→ Output Rectifier → DC Output → Welding Arc
```

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The 50 Hz mains supply is first rectified to DC using a 3-phase diode bridge.
2. The DC is filtered by a large DC-link capacitor.
3. An IGBT-based chopper/inverter switches this DC at high frequency (20–100 kHz).
4. The high-frequency AC feeds a small ferrite-core step-down transformer.
5. The secondary is rectified to provide low-voltage, high-current DC for welding.
6. Closed-loop feedback controls output current/voltage via PWM.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Transformer** | **Inverter-Based** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Frequency** | 50 Hz | 20–100 kHz |
| **Transformer Size** | Large iron core | Small ferrite core |
| **Weight (10 kVA)** | ~150 kg | ~20–25 kg |
| **Efficiency** | 50–60% | 85–92% |
| **Power Factor** | 0.6–0.7 | 0.95 |
| **Dynamic Response** | Slow (50 Hz) | Fast (PWM control) |
| **Cost (Initial)** | Low | High |
| **Weld Quality** | Basic | Excellent, precise control |
| **Energy Saving** | No | Yes (up to 30%) |
| **Cooling** | Big fans | Small fans or natural |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 | Module 2 Alternative]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the equipment and working of **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**. Compare it with GTAW equipment. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the equipment used in **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)**. Why is a vacuum chamber mandatory? What safety precautions are needed? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**PAW Equipment (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Power Source (CC) → Pilot Arc Circuit → Tungsten Electrode
Shielding Gas (Secondary) → Nozzle Annular → Workpiece
Plasma Gas (Primary) → Through Orifice → Constricting Nozzle
Cooling Water → Torch Body → Drain
```

**Major Components:**

1. **Power Source** — CC type, similar to GTAW but with additional pilot arc unit. *(1 mark)*
2. **Plasma Torch** — Tungsten electrode recessed inside a copper constricting nozzle with orifice (0.8–3.2 mm). *(1 mark)*
3. **Plasma Gas** — Argon (primary) flows through the orifice, constricting the arc to high energy density. *(1 mark)*
4. **Shielding Gas** — Secondary gas (Ar/He mixture) protects the weld pool externally. *(1 mark)*
5. **Pilot Arc** — Non-transferred arc initiated between tungsten and nozzle to ionize the gas; then transferred arc strikes between tungsten and workpiece. *(1 mark)*
6. **Cooling System** — Water jacket around the nozzle carries away intense heat. *(1 mark)*
7. **Gas Control Unit** — Two flowmeters and solenoid valves to sequence primary and secondary gas flows. *(1 mark)*

**Comparison with GTAW:**

| **Aspect** | **GTAW** | **PAW** |
|---|---|---|
| Arc constriction | None (free arc) | Mechanical (nozzle orifice) |
| Energy density | ~10⁴ W/cm² | ~10⁵–10⁶ W/cm² |
| Orifice | Absent | Present |
| Process modes | One | Non-transferred / Transferred / Keyhole / Microplasma |
| Cooling | Air/water | Always water |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Pilot arc | No (only HF) | Yes |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**EBW Equipment (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Filament Heater Supply (6–10 V AC) → Tungsten Cathode
High-Voltage Supply (30–200 kV DC) → Anode (grounded)
Grid Bias Supply → Beam Current Control
Magnetic Lens Coil → Focus Beam
Magnetic Deflection Coil → Steer Beam
Vacuum Chamber (10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ mbar) → Workpiece
CNC Worktable → X-Y-Z Motion
Optical Telescope + CCTV → Observation
```

**Components:**

1. **Electron Gun** — Tungsten filament cathode, anode, and grid cup (triode gun). *(1 mark)*
2. **High-Voltage Power Supply** — 30–200 kV accelerating voltage; ripple < 1%. *(1 mark)*
3. **Magnetic Focusing & Deflection Coils** — Shape the electron beam and steer it. *(1 mark)*
4. **Vacuum Chamber** — Required to prevent electron scattering and beam defocusing. Pressure ranges from 10⁻² mbar (soft vacuum) to 10⁻⁴ mbar (hard vacuum). *(1 mark)*
5. **Workpiece Manipulation System** — CNC-controlled X-Y-Z table inside the chamber. *(1 mark)*
6. **Viewing System** — Optical telescope or CCTV with lead-glass window for observation. *(1 mark)*
7. **Cooling System & Safety Interlocks** — Water cooling, vacuum interlocks, X-ray shielding. *(1 mark)*

**Why is vacuum mandatory? (3 marks covered above)**
- Electrons are scattered by gas molecules; vacuum provides a collision-free path.
- Beam energy would otherwise be absorbed in air.
- Plasma formation (in air) would defocus the beam.
- Prevents oxidation of molten weld pool.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Lead shielding around chamber (X-rays form above 30 kV).
- TLD badges for operators.
- Interlocks to prevent chamber opening under vacuum or high voltage.
- AERB licensing required.
- Emergency stop and grounding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often confuse the **grid cup** function — it controls beam current (like a triode valve), not focusing.
> - Many students wrongly state that "vacuum is to prevent oxidation only" — the *primary* reason is to prevent **electron scattering** and maintain beam coherence. Oxidation is a secondary benefit.
> - A common error is stating accelerating voltage in "volts" without units; the correct answer is **kV** (e.g., 60 kV, 150 kV).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Equipment Used**

- ✅ **Power Source Types:** CC (for GTAW, SMAW, PAW) vs CV (for GMAW, FCAW, SAW) — know one example of each.
- ✅ **Inverter Power Source:** Operates at 20–100 kHz; 5–10× lighter than conventional transformer; 85–92% efficient.
- ✅ **GMAW Wire Feeders:** Push type (≤ 4 m, soft wire); Push-Pull type (≤ 10 m, Al/Stainless).
- ✅ **TIG Torch:** Air-cooled up to 200 A; Water-cooled up to 400+ A. Argon purity ≥ 99.99%.
- ✅ **HF Starter:** 1–2 MHz, 2500–3000 V — used for **non-contact** arc initiation in TIG/PAW.
- ✅ **PAW Orifice Diameter:** 0.8–3.2 mm. Energy density ~ 10× higher than GTAW.
- ✅ **Laser Wavelengths:** CO₂ = 10.6 μm, Nd:YAG = 1.06 μm, Fiber = 1.07 μm.
- ✅ **Keyhole Mode Threshold:** Beam intensity ≥ 10⁶ W/mm².
- ✅ **EBW Voltage:** 30–200 kV DC; Vacuum 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ mbar; X-ray hazard above 30 kV.
- ✅ **FSW Tool:** Shoulder Ø 12–25 mm; Pin length = joint depth; Tilt angle 0–3°.
- ✅ **Spot Welding Current:** 5,000–30,000 A; Time 200–600 ms; Electrode force 1.5–10 kN.
- ✅ **Heat Input Formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ (J/mm) — **MUST MEMORIZE**.
- ✅ **Duty Cycle:** 60% @ rated current is industry standard. Exceeding = insulation failure.
- ✅ **OCV Limit:** Max 80 V DC / 48 V AC per IEC 60974-1 for operator safety.
- ✅ **Cooling Water Conductivity:** < 5 μS/cm for water-cooled TIG torches.
- ✅ **FSW Tangential Velocity:** $v_{tan} = 2\pi r \cdot RPM / 60$ — controls heat generation.
- ✅ **Resistance Spot Welding — Steel Nugget Rule:** $d_{nugget} = 5\sqrt{t}$ mm (t in seconds).
- ✅ **Equipment Sizing Formula:** $I_2 = I_1 \sqrt{DC_1 / DC_2}$ — for derating at different duty cycles.
- ✅ **Vacuum in EBW:** Primary purpose = prevent electron scattering; Secondary = prevent oxidation.
- ✅ **AERB Licensing:** Mandatory for EBW equipment in India above 30 kV.
- ✅ **Modern Power Sources:** Digital inverter, cloud-connected, with adaptive AI control — industry trend (Industry 4.0).

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Exam Tip:**  
> Most KTU questions on Module 2 test your ability to **draw block diagrams with labeled components** and **justify component selection** based on process requirements. Always link each equipment component to the **welding variable it controls** (current, voltage, travel, gas flow, heat input).

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Parameters in Advanced Metal Joining** are the controllable input variables that govern the energy transfer, material flow, thermal cycle, and metallurgical response of a joining process. They directly influence weld bead geometry, fusion zone microstructure, heat-affected zone (HAZ) properties, residual stress distribution, and ultimately the **mechanical integrity of the joint**.

In the context of the KTU PEMET416 syllabus (Module 2), the term *"Process Parameters and Characteristics"* refers to the quantitative descriptors of arc-based, resistance-based, high-energy-beam, and solid-state joining processes. These parameters are grouped into four fundamental classes:

| Parameter Class | Definition | Example Variables |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Parameters** | Variables governing energy input | Arc current ($I$), arc voltage ($V$), power ($P$) |
| **Mechanical Parameters** | Variables governing motion and force | Travel speed ($v$), electrode force ($F$) |
| **Thermal Parameters** | Variables governing heat distribution | Heat input ($H$), preheat temperature ($T_p$) |
| **Geometric Parameters** | Variables governing joint design | Groove angle ($\theta$), root gap ($g$), electrode diameter ($d_e$) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Cooking a Dosa on a Tawa:**
> Think of a welding arc as a chef cooking a dosa. The **heat input (H)** is like the stove's flame setting. The **travel speed (v)** is how fast the chef moves the ladle. The **arc current (I)** is the size of the ladle (more material deposited per pass). The **arc voltage (V)** is the distance between the ladle and the tawa (too close burns, too far leaves it raw). Just as the chef must balance these to make a perfect crispy dosa, a welding engineer must balance these parameters to produce a sound weld — too much heat causes burn-through, too little causes lack of fusion, and uneven travel speed creates defects.

## 1.3 Why Process Parameters Matter in KTU Examinations

The KTU board examiner typically tests:
- The **relationship** between parameters (e.g., how heat input varies with travel speed).
- The **effect** of changing a parameter on weld characteristics (penetration, width, HAZ size).
- The **numerical calculation** of heat input, power density, and deposition rate.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 explicitly demands an understanding of how process parameters influence weld pool dynamics, bead profile, and metallurgical transformations. Questions are commonly asked on the formulation and application of the heat input equation in arc welding processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Process Parameters in Metal Joining

### 2.1.1 Primary (Independent) Parameters
These are inputs set directly by the operator or machine controller:
- **Arc Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Arc Voltage (V)** — measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Travel Speed (v)** — measured in **mm/min** or **m/min**
- **Wire Feed Rate (WFR)** — measured in **m/min**
- **Electrode Force (F)** — measured in **kN** or **N**
- **Shielding Gas Flow Rate (Q_g)** — measured in **L/min**

### 2.1.2 Secondary (Dependent) Parameters
These are outputs determined by the primary parameters:
- **Heat Input (H)** — energy per unit length of weld
- **Power Density (P/A)** — concentration of energy at the workpiece
- **Deposition Rate (DR)** — mass of filler metal deposited per unit time
- **Melting Efficiency (η)** — fraction of arc heat actually used to melt the base metal
- **Penetration Depth (d_p)** — depth of fusion into the base material
- **Bead Width (W_b)** — width of the visible weld bead on the surface
- **Reinforcement Height (h_r)** — height of bead above the base metal surface

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of Heat Input

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input (H)** is the single most important process parameter in arc welding. It quantifies the amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of the weld and directly controls the HAZ size, cooling rate, and residual stresses.

### Logical Deduction Chain:

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Generation:**
The welding power source delivers electrical power equal to the product of arc voltage and arc current.

$$P = V \times I \quad [\text{Watts, W}]$$

**Step 2 — Accounting for Process Efficiency:**
Not all the electrical power is transferred to the workpiece as useful heat. A fraction defined by the **process thermal efficiency (η)** is actually absorbed by the joint.

$$P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Energy per Unit Length:**
If the torch travels a distance $L$ in time $t$, then the travel speed is $v = L/t$. The total useful energy supplied during this traversal is $P_{useful} \times t$. The energy per unit length (heat input) is therefore:

$$H = \frac{P_{useful} \times t}{L} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Unit Normalization for Engineering Use:**
To express $H$ in the conventional engineering unit of **kJ/mm**, the numerical constant **60/1000** is introduced (since 1 W·s = 1 J, 1 min = 60 s, 1 kJ = 1000 J):

$$H = \frac{60 \times \eta \times V \times I}{1000 \times v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

where:
- $V$ is in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ is in **Amperes (A)**
- $v$ is in **mm/min**

## 2.3 Power Density and Its Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Density** is the arc power concentrated over the area of the arc spot. It governs the **mode of metal transfer** and the **depth-to-width ratio** of the weld pool.

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{arc}} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{\pi r_{arc}^2} \quad \left[\frac{\text{W}}{\text{mm}^2}\right]$$

where $r_{arc}$ is the effective radius of the arc at the workpiece surface.

| Process | Typical Power Density | Penetration Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** | Low (~10² W/mm²) | Conduction-limited |
| **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)** | Medium (~10³ W/mm²) | Transition mode |
| **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** | High (~10³ – 10⁴ W/mm²) | Keyhole possible |
| **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** | Very High (~10⁴ – 10⁵ W/mm²) | Keyhole mode |
| **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** | Extremely High (~10⁶ W/mm²) | Deep keyhole |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Memorize the heat input formula and the units carefully. Most marks are lost in unit conversion errors. Always state whether your answer is in J/mm or kJ/mm.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | Heat input (energy per unit length) | **J/mm** |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{60 \eta V I}{1000 \cdot v}$ | Heat input (engineering form) | **kJ/mm** |
| 3 | $P = V \times I$ | Electrical arc power | **W (J/s)** |
| 4 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{\pi r_{arc}^2}$ | Power density at workpiece | **W/mm²** |
| 5 | $Q = I^2 R t$ | Heat generated in resistance welding (Joule heating) | **J (Joules)** |
| 6 | $DR = \dfrac{WFR \times A_w \times \rho}{10^6}$ | Deposition rate (mass per time) | **kg/h** |
| 7 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Mass melted}}{\text{Theoretical mass}}$ | Melting efficiency (fraction) | **dimensionless** |
| 8 | $T_{cool} \propto \dfrac{1}{(T_{800-500})}$ | Cooling time parameter (Rykalin 3D) | **seconds** |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Electrical resistance of work | **Ohms (Ω)** |
| 10 | $F = \dfrac{\eta \cdot I^2 \cdot R}{d}$ | Equivalent forge force concept | **N** |

> **Note on Symbols:** $A_w$ = cross-section of wire (mm²), $\rho$ = density of filler (kg/m³), $WFR$ = wire feed rate (m/min), $L$ = length of current path, $A$ = cross-sectional area of current path.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):** All the parameters discussed above are explicitly recorded in a WPS document as required by **AWS D1.1** and **ISO 15614**. Understanding them is mandatory for any certified welding engineer.
- **Heat Input Control in Critical Applications:** In pressure vessel fabrication (ASME Section IX), the maximum allowable heat input is restricted to prevent **lamellar tearing** and excessive **HAZ softening** in low-alloy steels.
- **Process Selection:** The required **power density** dictates whether a process can perform **deep-penetration single-pass welding** (e.g., EBW, LBW) or requires **multi-pass groove welding** (e.g., SMAW, GMAW).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Heat Input from First Principles

We begin with a generic arc welding scenario. The welding power source is energized, an arc is established between the electrode and the workpiece, and the heat source traverses along the joint line at a steady travel speed.

**Given Information:**
- Arc voltage: $V$ (volts)
- Arc current: $I$ (amperes)
- Travel speed: $v$ (mm/min)
- Process thermal efficiency: $\eta$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$)

**Step 1 — Define electrical power delivered to the arc**

The instantaneous electrical power consumed by the arc (which is essentially a plasma column acting as a non-linear resistor) is given by the product of voltage and current:

$$P_{arc} = V \times I$$

This represents the total rate of energy dissipation in the arc. The unit is the watt, equivalent to **1 Joule per second**.

**Step 2 — Apply the process thermal efficiency factor**

In real welding, not all of $P_{arc}$ is absorbed by the workpiece. Some energy is lost through:
- Radiation from the arc (~5–10%)
- Convection in the shielding gas (~10–20%)
- Fumes and spatter (~5%)
- Conduction into the electrode (for non-consumable electrodes in GTAW/PAW, this can be 30–70%)

The fraction actually delivered to the workpiece is captured by $\eta$:

$$P_{workpiece} = \eta \times V \times I$$

**Step 3 — Express the energy delivered over a finite travel distance**

If the heat source moves at a constant travel speed $v$ (in mm/min), then in a time interval $\Delta t$ (in minutes), the source covers a distance:

$$\Delta L = v \times \Delta t$$

The total useful energy delivered to the workpiece in this time interval is:

$$\Delta Q = P_{workpiece} \times \Delta t = \eta \times V \times I \times \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by the travel distance to obtain heat input**

By definition, the **heat input H** is the useful thermal energy per unit length of the weld:

$$H = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta L} = \frac{\eta \times V \times I \times \Delta t}{v \times \Delta t}$$

The $\Delta t$ terms cancel out, yielding the fundamental heat input equation:

$$H = \frac{\eta \times V \times I}{v}$$

**Step 5 — Unit conversion to kJ/mm**

Since $V$ is in volts and $I$ is in amperes, $V \times I$ is in watts (J/s). The travel speed $v$ is typically given in mm/min. To convert W·s/mm to kJ/mm, we multiply by 60/1000:

$$H = \frac{60 \times \eta \times V \times I}{1000 \times v} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

**Final Compact Form (KTU Board Convention):**

$$\boxed{H = \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v} \quad \text{kJ/mm, when } v \text{ is in mm/min}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern)

**Problem:**
A GMAW operation is performed with the following parameters:
- Arc voltage: $V = 28$ V
- Arc current: $I = 250$ A
- Travel speed: $v = 350$ mm/min
- Process thermal efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**Required:** Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Substitute into the heat input equation:

$$H = \frac{0.06 \times \eta \times V \times I}{v}$$

Step 2 — Plug in numerical values:

$$H = \frac{0.06 \times 0.85 \times 28 \times 250}{350}$$

Step 3 — Compute the numerator:

$$0.06 \times 0.85 = 0.051$$
$$0.051 \times 28 = 1.428$$
$$1.428 \times 250 = 357$$

Step 4 — Divide by denominator:

$$H = \frac{357}{350} = 1.02 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $H = 1.02$ kJ/mm

**Verification by Dimensional Analysis:**
Numerator units: (kJ/s·min·V·A) → (kJ/min) when 60 cancels correctly. Dividing by v (mm/min) gives kJ/mm. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Variation on Weld Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** A "↑" means the parameter increases. Each cell shows the directional effect on the listed weld output. This matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 exam topic.

| Parameter ↑ | Penetration | Bead Width | HAZ Size | Distortion | Spatter | Cooling Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Current (I)** | ↑ Increases | ↑ Slightly | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ Decreases |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | ↓ Decreases (arc spreads) | ↑ Increases | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Travel Speed (v)** | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ Increases |
| **Heat Input (H)** | Mixed | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ | ↓ Decreases |
| **Electrode Stickout** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Shielding Gas Flow** | — | — | — | — | ↓ (less turbulence) | — |
| **Joint Gap** | ↑ (root penetration) | — | — | ↑ | ↑ | — |

## 3.4 Derivation of Cooling Time (Rykalin's 3D Thermal Model)

For thick plates (3D heat flow), Rykalin's equation gives the cooling time from 800°C to 500°C (critical for HAZ transformation in steels):

$$t_{8/5} = \frac{E}{2\pi \lambda \left[ \frac{1}{(T_1 - T_0)^2} - \frac{1}{(T_2 - T_0)^2} \right]}$$

where:
- $E$ = heat input (J/mm)
- $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity (W/mm·K)
- $T_1 = 800°C$, $T_2 = 500°C$, $T_0$ = preheat temperature (°C)

**Step-by-step substitution for $T_0 = 20°C$:**

$$t_{8/5} = \frac{E}{2\pi \lambda} \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{780^2} - \frac{1}{480^2}\right)}$$

This parameter is **critical for KTU questions** on **HAZ hardness control** in low-carbon and low-alloy steels.
