- $v = 1000 \text{ mm/min} = 16.67 \text{ mm/s}$
- $q = 0.70 \cdot 8000 / 16.67 \approx 336 \text{ J/mm}$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Despite EBW having a high **power density**, the **total heat input per unit length** is moderate because the beam is highly localized. This is **why EBW produces minimal distortion**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Welding Speed (Rykalin's 2-D Model)

For a moving point heat source on a thick plate, the optimum welding speed (when penetration equals plate thickness) is:

$$v_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\alpha \cdot P_d}{t^2}$$

**Given:**
- $\alpha = 8 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$ (low-carbon steel)
- $P_d = 7.64 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$
- $t = 6 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1:** Square the plate thickness.

$$t^2 = 36 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Multiply $\alpha \cdot P_d$.

$$\alpha \cdot P_d = 8 \cdot 7.64 \times 10^4$$

$$\alpha \cdot P_d = 6.11 \times 10^5$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $t^2$.

$$v_{\text{opt}} = \frac{6.11 \times 10^5}{36}$$

$$v_{\text{opt}} \approx 1.70 \times 10^4 \text{ mm/s}$$

This is unrealistically high — it indicates the formula assumes **constant beam power density**. In practice, the speed is limited by the **maximum available beam current**, not the theoretical optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Rykalin model is a *theoretical upper bound*. In industry, real welding speeds are 10–100× lower due to **keyhole stability, plasma absorption, and fit-up tolerance** limitations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Process Selection

```
                    ┌─ Need thick-section (> 25 mm) single pass? ─── YES ──→ EBW
                    │
Material Type ──────┤                                  NO
                    │
                    ├─ Need dissimilar / reactive metal (Ti, Zr)? ─ YES ──→ EBW
                    │
                    └─ Aluminum / Magnesium alloys? ─────────────── YES ──→ FSW
                                 │
                                 NO
                                 ▼
                    Production Volume / Automation Required?
                                 │
                  ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                  ▼                             ▼
            HIGH volume                  LOW volume, R&D
            (Automotive)                 (Aerospace R&D)
                  │                             │
                  ▼                             ▼
                LBW                            PAW
            (Fiber Laser)                (Microplasma)
```

### Selection Criteria Justification

| Industry Need | Best Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace Ti-alloy frame | **EBW** | Vacuum eliminates O₂/N₂ pick-up |
| Ship hull Al-alloy panels | **FSW** | No solidification cracking |
| Automotive gear (steel) | **LBW** | High speed, automation-friendly |
| Nuclear fuel rod end-cap | **EBW** | Hermetic seal, refractory compatible |
| Pressure vessel SS304 (10 mm) | **PAW** (keyhole) | Single-pass, low cost vs LBW/EBW |

---

## 3.5 Defect-Mode-to-Process Linkage (Analytical Matrix)

| Defect | EBW | LBW | PAW | FSW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Porosity | Root porosity | Plasma-induced | Rare | None |
| Cracking | Rare | Rare | Rare | None (solid-state) |
| Undercut | Common in non-vacuum | Possible | Possible | None |
| Spiking | Common (incomplete fusion at root) | Less common | Rare | None |
| HAZ softening | Minimal | Minimal | Moderate | None (T < T_melt) |
| Keyhole | Yes | Yes | Yes (keyhole mode) | N/A |
| Tunnel defect | N/A | N/A | N/A | Yes (improper parameters) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Advantages/Limitations Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Manufacturing Need Identified] --> B{Material Type}
    B -->|Reactive Metal| C[EBW Required]
    B -->|Aluminum Alloy| D[FSW Preferred]
    B -->|Coated Steel| E[LBW Preferred]
    B -->|Stainless Steel| F[PAW Candidate]
    C --> G{Vacuum Acceptable?}
    G -->|Yes| H[EBW Selected]
    G -->|No| I[Reject EBW, try LBW]
    D --> J{Geometry Simple?}
    J -->|Yes| K[FSW Selected]
    J -->|No| L[Reject FSW, use LBW or EBW]
    E --> M{Production Volume High?}
    M -->|Yes| N[LBW Selected - Automated]
    M -->|No| O[TIG or PAW Selected]
    F --> P{Thickness > 6 mm?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Keyhole PAW]
    P -->|No| R[Microplasma PAW]
    H --> S[End: Process Selected]
    I --> S
    K --> S
    L --> S
    N --> S
    O --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Comparative Process-Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Power[Power Source]
        A1[High Voltage 50-200 kV]
        A2[Laser Resonator CO2 Nd YAG]
        A3[DC Power Source Pilot Arc]
        A4[Spindle Motor]
    end
    subgraph Beam[Energy Delivery]
        B1[Electron Gun Vacuum Chamber]
        B2[Fiber Optic Beam Delivery]
        B3[Constricting Nozzle and Shielding Gas]
        B4[Rotating Tool Pin and Shoulder]
    end
    subgraph Weld[Weld Zone]
        C1[Keyhole Deep Penetration]
        C2[Keyhole Plasma Absorption]
        C3[Plasma Jet Melt Pool]
        C4[Plasticized Zone Stirred Zone]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2
    A3 --> B3 --> C3
    A4 --> B4 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Process Selection Matrix (KTU Board View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighCost[High Capital Cost Processes]
        X1[EBW Vacuum EBW]
        X2[LBW CO2 or Fiber Laser]
    end
    subgraph MedCost[Medium Capital Cost Processes]
        Y1[PAW Keyhole Mode]
        Y2[FSW Robotic Cell]
    end
    subgraph LowCost[Low Capital Cost Processes]
        Z1[TIG Microplasma]
        Z2[Resistance Spot Welding]
    end
    HighCost -->|Required for| W1[Aerospace Nuclear Refractory Metals]
    MedCost -->|Required for| W2[Shipbuilding Automotive Aluminum]
    LowCost -->|Required for| W3[General Fabrication Job Shop]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read This Diagram in an Exam:** Use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** approach when the question asks *"Compare the advantages and limitations of any THREE advanced welding processes."* Always draw a **comparative table** + a **process selection flow** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: List any THREE advantages of Electron Beam Welding over conventional arc welding processes.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Ultra-high power density** ($10^6$ to $10^7$ W/mm²) results in deep penetration with very narrow HAZ (e.g., single-pass welding of 200 mm thick steel).
2. **Vacuum environment** eliminates atmospheric contamination, producing porosity-free and oxide-free welds — critical for reactive metals like titanium and zirconium.
3. **Low distortion** due to narrow HAZ and high welding speed — eliminates post-weld straightening operations.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Mention THREE limitations of Laser Beam Welding.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **High initial capital cost** (₹50 lakh to ₹5 Crore) and high maintenance cost for laser source optics.
2. **High reflectivity** of metals like aluminum and copper at CO₂ wavelength (10.6 µm) — reduces energy coupling; requires fiber lasers (1.06 µm) or surface coatings.
3. **Plasma plume formation** above the keyhole can absorb and deflect the laser beam, requiring helium shielding gas and reducing process efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module 2 Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank, Module 2]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **advantages and limitations of Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** in detail. Compare its weld bead characteristics with conventional arc welding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **15 kW EBW machine** is used to weld a low-carbon steel plate. The focused beam spot diameter is **0.5 mm** and the welding speed is **500 mm/min**. Calculate:
  (i) Power density of the electron beam.
  (ii) Heat input per unit length, assuming process efficiency $\eta = 0.70$.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Advantages of EBW:**
1. **Highest power density** ($10^6$–$10^7$ W/mm²) among commercial welding processes.
2. **Deep penetration with high D/W ratio** (10:1 to 50:1) — enables single-pass welding of thick sections (up to 200 mm in steel).
3. **Narrow HAZ** → minimal distortion and residual stress.
4. **Vacuum environment** → no atmospheric contamination; ideal for reactive and refractory metals (Ti, Zr, W, Mo, Nb).
5. **Dissimilar metal welding** is possible with proper parameter control.
6. **Hermetic, defect-free welds** — critical for nuclear, aerospace, and vacuum-tight applications.

**Limitations of EBW:**
1. **Very high capital cost** (₹1 Crore to ₹10 Crore).
2. **Vacuum chamber** restricts maximum workpiece size.
3. **Long vacuum pump-down time** (15–30 min) reduces productivity.
4. **X-ray radiation hazard** — requires lead shielding and AERB regulatory compliance.
5. **Spiking and root porosity** are common defects due to keyhole instability.
6. Requires **highly skilled operators** and precise beam alignment.

**Comparison of Weld Bead Characteristics:**

| Parameter | SMAW | EBW |
|---|---|---|
| Power Density (W/mm²) | 50–500 | $10^6$–$10^7$ |
| D/W Ratio | 1:1 to 2:1 | 10:1 to 50:1 |
| HAZ Width (mm) | 5–15 | 0.2–0.5 |
| Distortion (mm/m) | 2–5 | 0.1–0.5 |
| Defects | Porosity, slag, undercut | Spiking, root porosity |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Power Density Calculation:**

Spot area:
$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ mm}^2$$

Power density:
$$P_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{15{,}000}{0.1963} \approx 7.64 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Heat Input Calculation:**

Beam power:
$$P_{beam} = V \cdot I$$

Wait — given only $P = 15$ kW, so:
$$P_{actual} = \eta \cdot P = 0.70 \cdot 15{,}000 = 10{,}500 \text{ W}$$

Convert welding speed:
$$v = 500 \text{ mm/min} = 8.33 \text{ mm/s}$$

Heat input:
$$q = \frac{P_{actual}}{v} = \frac{10{,}500}{8.33} \approx 1260 \text{ J/mm}$$

$$\boxed{P_d \approx 7.64 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2, \quad q \approx 1260 \text{ J/mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Internal Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank, Module 2]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **advantages and limitations of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) and Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** for joining aluminum alloy sheets used in shipbuilding.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **CO₂ laser of 4 kW power** is used to weld a 3 mm thick SS304 plate at a speed of **1.5 m/min**. The absorptivity of SS304 at the CO₂ wavelength is **0.35**, and the process efficiency is **0.40**. Calculate:
  (i) The effective heat input per unit length.
  (ii) The heat input per unit length **per mm of plate thickness** and comment on whether the weld will show distortion.

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Criterion | FSW | LBW |
|---|---|---|
| Process Type | Solid-state | Fusion (keyhole) |
| Power Density | N/A (mechanical) | $10^5$–$10^6$ W/mm² |
| Heat Input | 500–1500 J/mm | 50–300 J/mm |
| HAZ | Stirred zone only (T < T_melt) | Narrow HAZ |
| Distortion | Very low | Low |
| Weld Speed (Al, 3 mm) | 0.3–1 m/min | 2–5 m/min |
| Defects | Tunnel defect, keyhole (exit) | Porosity, spatter |
| Tool/Consumable | Rotating tool (wear) | No tool, but shielding gas |
| Surface Finish | "Onion-ring" pattern | Smooth, cosmetic |
| Energy Efficiency | ≈ 95% | ≈ 30–45% |
| Cost (Capital) | Medium (₹30 lakh) | High (₹1 Cr+) |
| Best Suited for | Thick Al plates (5–50 mm) | Thin Al sheets (0.5–6 mm) |

**Conclusion:**
- For **thick aluminum ship hull panels (10–30 mm)**: **FSW is superior** (no porosity, no cracking, low distortion).
- For **thin aluminum sheets (0.5–3 mm)** at high volume: **LBW (fiber laser) is superior** (high speed, automation).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Effective Heat Input:**

Effective absorbed power:
$$P_{eff} = A_b \cdot \eta \cdot P_{laser}$$

$$P_{eff} = 0.35 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 4000$$

$$P_{eff} = 560 \text{ W}$$

Convert welding speed:
$$v = 1.5 \text{ m/min} = 25 \text{ mm/s}$$

Heat input per unit length:
$$q = \frac{P_{eff}}{v} = \frac{560}{25} = 22.4 \text{ J/mm}$$

**(ii) Heat Input per Unit Thickness:**

$$q_t = \frac{q}{t} = \frac{22.4}{3} \approx 7.47 \text{ J/mm}^2$$

**Comment:**
For austenitic SS304, distortion is generally acceptable if $q_t < 15$ J/mm². Our value (7.47 J/mm²) is well below this threshold → **distortion will be minimal**. The weld should pass quality control without post-weld straightening.

$$\boxed{q = 22.4 \text{ J/mm}, \quad q_t \approx 7.47 \text{ J/mm}^2 \Rightarrow \text{Minimal Distortion}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "no HAZ" with "narrow HAZ"** — even EBW and LBW have HAZs. Examiners deduct 1 mark for absolute statements like "no HAZ."
> 2. **Do NOT omit the units** for power density (W/mm²) and heat input (J/mm). Marks are explicitly allocated to units.
> 3. **Do NOT write FSW as "Friction Welding"** — FSW is *Friction Stir Welding* (solid-state). Plain "Friction Welding" refers to rotary friction welding (a different process).
> 4. **Do NOT confuse PAW with Plasma Cutting.** PAW is a welding process; plasma cutting uses much higher current and arc gas.
> 5. **Always show unit conversion steps** (mm/min → mm/s) — silent unit conversion is a common reason for 1–2 mark deductions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Advantages and Limitations of Advanced Joining Processes"**

### Universal Comparison Points (Use these in ANY exam answer)

- **Power Density** — single best metric to compare processes
- **D/W Ratio** — indicates penetration efficiency
- **HAZ Width** — controls distortion and metallurgical degradation
- **Capital Cost** — EBW > LBW > FSW > PAW > GTAW > SMAW
- **Atmosphere Control** — Vacuum (EBW) > Inert gas (LBW, PAW) > None (FSW for Al)
- **Material Compatibility** — Refractory → EBW; Aluminum → FSW; Coated steel → LBW; SS/Ti thin → PAW

### EBW — Quick Recap
- **Pro:** Deepest penetration, vacuum cleanliness, refractory-capable
- **Con:** Vacuum chamber size limit, X-ray hazard, very high cost
- **Key Parameter:** Accelerating voltage (50–200 kV), beam current (mA range)

### LBW — Quick Recap
- **Pro:** No vacuum, automatable, high speed, fiber-deliverable
- **Con:** Plasma plume absorption, reflectivity issue at 10.6 µm, Class IV laser safety
- **Key Parameter:** Power (kW), wavelength (CO₂ 10.6 µm, Nd:YAG 1.06 µm, fiber 1.07 µm)

### PAW — Quick Recap
- **Pro:** Better than TIG for thick sections, stable arc, two modes (micro + keyhole)
- **Con:** Nozzle wear, water cooling required, more complex than TIG
- **Key Parameter:** Pilot arc current, orifice gas flow (Argon)

### FSW — Quick Recap
- **Pro:** Solid-state, no melting defects, environmentally clean, high repeatability
- **Con:** Keyhole at exit, tool wear, limited to simple geometries, slower for thin sheets
- **Key Parameter:** Tool rotation (RPM), traverse speed (mm/min), tilt angle (typically 2–3°)

### Three Formulas to Memorize Forever
$$P_d = \frac{P}{A}$$
$$q = \frac{\eta V I}{v}$$
$$\text{D/W} = \frac{d}{w}$$

### Common KTU Exam Answer Templates
1. **"List advantages"** → Bullet format, **3–5 points**, with **quantitative values** (e.g., "$10^6$ W/mm² power density").
2. **"Compare X and Y"** → Use a **markdown table** + a **short concluding sentence** (e.g., "FSW is preferred for thick Al, while LBW is preferred for thin coated steel").
3. **"Calculate heat input"** → Always state **given data**, **assumed efficiency**, **unit conversion step**, **final formula**, and **boxed answer with units**.

### Mnemonic — "PALS-E"
**P**ower density, **A**tmospheric control, **L**imitation of geometry, **S**afety hazards, **E**conomic cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Scheme Students:** In the new NEP 2020-aligned scheme, **process selection questions carry higher weightage** than pure theory. Always frame your answer in the context of **"Which process would YOU select for XYZ industrial application, and WHY?"** — this demonstrates CO2 (Design/Development) and CO3 (Modern Tool Usage) achievement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction and Friction Stir Welding: Principle, Theory, Process Parameters, and Applications
Tools and Metal Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tools and Metal Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the FSW Tool?

In **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**, the *tool* is a non-consumable, specially designed rotating component that performs the dual function of **frictional heating** and **plastic deformation / stirring** of the workpiece material. Unlike fusion welding, the FSW tool does **not melt** the base metal; instead, it locally softens the material through frictional heat and mechanically forces it to flow around the tool pin to forge a solid-state joint.

The tool is broadly divided into two critical regions:
- **Shoulder** — the larger diameter disc-like contact surface that sits on top of the workpiece. It supplies **~80–85% of the total heat input** and confines the plasticized metal.
- **Probe (or Pin)** — the protruding cylindrical/conical/tapered shaft extending below the shoulder. It is the **primary stirring element** that drags material from the *advancing side* to the *retreating side* of the weld line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** *"The FSW tool is a purpose-built, wear-resistant, non-consumable mechanical stirrer consisting of a shoulder and a probe, which simultaneously generates frictional heat and induces plastic flow to consolidate the joint."*

## 1.2 What is Metal Flow in FSW?

**Metal flow** refers to the complex, three-dimensional viscoplastic movement of softened (but not melted) base-metal around the rotating FSW tool. It is the *fundamental mechanism* by which a sound metallurgical bond is created in the solid state. Without adequate flow, defects such as *tunnels, voids, kissing bonds, surface galling,* and *lazy S / zigzag line discontinuities* appear in the weld nugget.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Stirring Thick Honey with a Spoon"**
> Imagine dragging a hot spoon through a tray of cold, thick honey:
> - The spoon shoulder is your **palm pressing on the top** — it heats the surface and prevents the honey from squirting out (heat + forging pressure).
> - The spoon shaft is the **pin/probe** — it drags honey forward and backward as it rotates, mixing the two sides together.
> - Honey in front of the spoon gets **pushed ahead** (advancing side), while honey behind gets **swept around** (retreating side).
> - The result is a beautifully mixed, bonded layer — exactly what happens in FSW at ~0.6–0.9 of the metal's melting point (T<sub>m</sub>).
> The viscosity of the softened aluminium alloy (~10³–10⁵ Pa·s) at welding temperature is **comparable to hot honey**, which is why the analogy works so well.

### 1.3 Why Tools and Flow are Treated as a Single Topic

The **geometry of the tool** dictates the **flow pattern of the material**. Thus KTU bundles these into one module because:
- Pin profile → drives *stirring efficiency* and *flow direction*.
- Shoulder geometry → controls *heat distribution* and *flash formation*.
- Tool tilt → governs *vertical material transport* and *void closure*.
- Tool material → limits *RPM ceiling* and *tool life*.

Standard metrics to remember:
- **Typical shoulder diameter (D<sub>sh</sub>):** 10–25 mm (3× pin diameter)
- **Typical pin diameter (d<sub>p</sub>):** 3–8 mm
- **Typical pin length (l<sub>p</sub>):** 0.9–1.2 × plate thickness
- **Standard tool tilt (θ):** 0°–3° (commonly 2°–3°)
- **Tool rotational speed (N):** 200–2000 RPM
- **Welding speed (v):** 50–500 mm/min
- **Tensile strength of tool steel:** **1900–2400 MPa** (for H13 / MP159 / W-Re alloys)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FSW Tool Geometry — Function-wise Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Shoulder Functions
1. **Heat generation** through frictional contact (dominant).
2. **Forging pressure** to consolidate material under the pin.
3. **Containment** of plasticized metal, preventing flash.
4. **Surface finish** contribution (scroll shoulders self-clean oxide film).

Shoulder variants:

| Shoulder Type | Feature | Flow Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Flat | Smooth disc | Basic forging, less mixing |
| Concave | Recessed 7°–12° | Traps material, increases pressure |
| Scroll (spiral) | Spiral grooves | Pumping action — drives material downward |
| Convex | Domed | For thin sheets, prevents sinkage |

### 2.1.2 Pin/Probe Profile — Most Studied Design Variable
Common pin profiles (in KTU-syllabus order):

- **Cylindrical (straight):** Easy to manufacture, weak vertical flow.
- **Tapered/Conical:** Strong downward flow, preferred for thick plates.
- **Threaded (left-hand or right-hand):** Threads act as an *auger screw*. Right-hand threads on a clockwise-rotating tool pump material **downward** (this is the conventional setup).
- **Fluted (3 or 4 flats):** Increases shear surface → better mixing, higher torque.
- **Triflate / Re-stir:** Used for butt welds, drives flow to retreating side.
- **Whorl/Whorl-type (MxTrivex™):** Scroll-type features produce uniform flow.
- **Bobbin tool (Stationary Shoulder):** Two shoulders top and bottom, no probe penetration risk; used for butt and lap welds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Right-Hand Rule of Threaded Pins":** A **right-hand threaded** pin rotated **clockwise** (looking from top) acts as a screw and forces plasticized material **downward** along the pin. This is the most-tested KTU concept for tool-flow coupling.

## 2.2 Metal Flow Zones Around the Tool

The flow field around the FSW tool is divided into four key regions (revised model — Arbegast / Reynolds):

| Zone | Location | Dominant Phenomenon |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-heat (Leading) Zone** | Just ahead of the tool (advancing side) | Material is heated but not deformed significantly |
| **Initial Deformation Zone** | At the leading edge of the pin | Material is dragged upward and forward with the tool rotation |
| **Extrusion / Stir Zone (SZ)** | Immediately around and behind the pin | Material undergoes *simple shear* and is extruded from advancing to retreating side |
| **Cooling / Consolidation Zone** | Behind the tool (trailing side) | Material cools under shoulder pressure, forging the final bond |

### 2.2.1 Advancing vs. Retaining Side
- **Advancing Side (AS):** Tool tangential velocity and welding velocity are in the **same direction**. Material here is *dragged forward* — tends to show **deficit** if not replenished (origin of *tunnel defects*).
- **Retreating Side (RS):** Tool tangential velocity is **opposite** to welding velocity. Material here is *swept backward* — tends to be in **excess**, may cause *flash*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield** formulas for the ESE and internal assessments under PEMET416 Module 2.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_s}{60}$ | $N$ = RPM, $T_s$ = Spindle Torque | W (Watts) |
| 2 | Heat Generated at Shoulder | $Q_{sh} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu F_{sh} R_{sh}}{60}$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $F_{sh}$ = axial force, $R_{sh}$ = shoulder mean radius | W |
| 3 | Heat Generated at Pin | $Q_{p} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu F_p R_p}{60}$ | $F_p$ = pin axial force, $R_p$ = pin mean radius | W |
| 4 | Total Heat Input Rate | $Q_{tot} = Q_{sh} + Q_p$ | Sum of contributions | W |
| 5 | Shoulder Contact Pressure | $P_{sh} = \dfrac{4 F_{sh}}{\pi \left(D_{sh}^2 - d_p^2\right)}$ | $D_{sh}$ = shoulder diameter, $d_p$ = pin diameter | Pa |
| 6 | Effective Strain Rate (around pin) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi N r_p}{L_p \sqrt{3}}$ | $r_p$ = pin radius, $L_p$ = pin length | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | Approx. Viscoplastic Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{R_p \ \theta}{L_p}$ | $\theta$ = angular wrap of material around tool | — |
| 8 | Tangential Tool Velocity (at shoulder edge) | $V_{tan} = \dfrac{\pi N D_{sh}}{60}$ | — | m/s |
| 9 | Pitch-Line Velocity (for thread pumping) | $V_{axial} = N \times p$ | $p$ = thread pitch | mm/s |
| 10 | Tilt-Induced Vertical Component | $V_{vert} = V_{tan} \sin \theta$ | $\theta$ = tool tilt angle | m/s |

> ⚠️ **LaTeX Encoding Note:** All absolute / magnitude bars are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace wing skin welding (Al 7050, Al 2195):** Tool design directly governs defect-free joints for fuselage.
- **Shipbuilding (Al 5083, 6xxx series):** Long-length FSW requires *bobbin tools* with controlled flow on both sides.
- **Automotive (Al 6061-T6 chassis):** Threaded-pin tools give high production rate at low torque.
- **High-melting alloys (Ti, steels):** Use *polycrystalline cubic boron nitride (PCBN)* or *W–Re* tools capable of withstanding **>900 °C** tool tip temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Heat Input in FSW (Tapered Pin + Flat Shoulder)

**Given:**
- Rotational speed $N$ (RPM)
- Axial force on shoulder $F_{sh}$ (N), on pin $F_p$ (N)
- Coefficient of friction $\mu$ (at hot working T)
- Shoulder mean radius $R_{sh}$, Pin mean radius $R_p$

**Step 1 — Define frictional work at the shoulder interface.**

Frictional power at the shoulder is the product of the tangential force (friction force) and the tangential velocity at radius $R_{sh}$.

$$
P_{sh} = F_{friction,sh} \cdot V_{tan,sh} = \mu F_{sh} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N R_{sh}}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Define frictional work at the pin interface (cylindrical surface).**

Similarly for the pin:

$$
P_{p} = \mu F_{p} \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N R_{p}}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Total instantaneous power input.**

$$
P_{tot} = P_{sh} + P_p = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu}{60} \left(F_{sh} R_{sh} + F_p R_p\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per unit length of weld.**

The welding speed $v$ (m/s) determines how much power is deposited per unit length:

$$
E_{line} = \dfrac{P_{tot}}{v} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{m}} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate fraction of useful heat (η).**

In practice, only **70–90%** of the frictional work becomes useful heat in the workpiece (rest lost to tool conduction and radiation). For KTU problems, a value of $\eta = 0.85$ is typically assumed.

$$
Q_{useful} = \eta \cdot P_{tot} = 0.85 \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu}{60} \left(F_{sh} R_{sh} + F_p R_p\right)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spindle Torque and Power for a Given Tool

**Given:** $N = 800$ RPM, $F_{sh} = 12$ kN, $R_{sh} = 10$ mm, $\mu = 0.4$, $F_p = 3$ kN, $R_p = 3$ mm.

**Step 1 — Substitute into total power equation.**

$$
P_{tot} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 800 \times 0.4}{60} \times \left(12000 \times 0.010 + 3000 \times 0.003\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the constant factor.**

$$
\dfrac{2 \pi \times 800 \times 0.4}{60} = \dfrac{640 \pi}{60} = 10.666 \pi \approx 33.51
$$

**Step 3 — Compute force-radius terms.**

$$
F_{sh} R_{sh} = 12000 \times 0.010 = 120 \ \text{N·m}
$$

$$
F_p R_p = 3000 \times 0.003 = 9 \ \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow F_{sh} R_{sh} + F_p R_p = 129 \ \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
P_{tot} = 33.51 \times 129 \approx 4322.8 \ \text{W} = 4.32 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute spindle torque.**

From $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_s}{60}$,

$$
T_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot P_{tot}}{2 \pi N} = \dfrac{60 \times 4322.8}{2 \pi \times 800} \approx 51.6 \ \text{N·m}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Strain Rate in the Stir Zone (Pin Dominated)

Material wrapped around the pin undergoes simple shear. For a pin of radius $r_p$ rotating at $N$ RPM, the relative tangential velocity of the material is $\omega r_p = \dfrac{2 \pi N r_p}{60}$. The shear rate is this velocity divided by an *effective shear layer thickness* (often taken as the pin length $L_p$):

$$
\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\omega r_p}{L_p} = \dfrac{2 \pi N r_p}{60 L_p}
$$

For an equivalent von Mises effective strain rate in simple shear:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\dot{\gamma}}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N r_p}{60 L_p \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\pi N r_p}{30 L_p \sqrt{3}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** For $N = 1000$ RPM, $r_p = 3$ mm, $L_p = 5$ mm:
>
> $$
> \dot{\varepsilon} \approx \dfrac{\pi \times 1000 \times 0.003}{30 \times 0.005 \times \sqrt{3}} \approx 36.3 \ \text{s}^{-1}
> $$
>
> This is comparable to industrial **hot-forging strain rates** — explaining why FSW produces *fine, dynamically recrystallized grains* in the stir zone.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect Type vs. Flow Cause

| Defect | Dominant Cause in Metal Flow | Tool Design Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Tunnel void (on AS) | Insufficient material transport to advancing side | Larger left-hand-threaded pin; increase tilt |
| Surface galling | Inadequate shoulder forging pressure | Larger shoulder, scroll grooves |
| Lazy S / zigzag line | Incomplete mixing across weld line | Threaded/fluted pin, higher RPM |
| Flash / thinning | Excess downward + outward flow | Reduce tilt, smaller shoulder, use concave |
| Kissing bond (RS) | Insufficient forge pressure on retreating side | Higher axial force, bobbin tool |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FSW Tool Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[FSW TOOL ASSEMBLY] --> B1[Shoulder Subsystem]
    A0 --> B2[Pin / Probe Subsystem]
    A0 --> B3[Shank Subsystem]
    A0 --> B4[Holder Adaptor]

    B1 --> C1A[Concave Shoulder]
    B1 --> C1B[Scroll Shoulder]
    B1 --> C1C[Flat Shoulder]
    B1 --> C1D[Convex Shoulder]

    B2 --> C2A[Cylindrical Pin]
    B2 --> C2B[Tapered Conical Pin]
    B2 --> C2C[Threaded Pin RH or LH]
    B2 --> C2D[Fluted Pin]
    B2 --> C2E[Whorl Triflux Pin]
    B2 --> C2F[Bobbin Dual Shoulder]

    B3 --> C3A[Straight Shank]
    B3 --> C3B[Tapered Shank]

    B4 --> C4A[Quick Change HSK]
    B4 --> C4B[Threaded Holder]

    C1A --> D1[Heat: 80 percent]
    C2C --> D2[Stirring: 70 percent]
    C1B --> D3[Pumping Action: Vertical]
    C2F --> D4[Thick Plate Capability]

    D1 --> E1[Final Solid State Joint]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Metal Flow Zones Around the FSW Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Base Metal Ahead of Tool] --> S2[Pre Heat Zone]
    S2 --> S3[Initial Deformation Zone]
    S3 --> S4[Stir Extrusion Zone]
    S4 --> S5[Consolidation and Cooling Zone]
    S5 --> S6[FSW Nugget SZ TMAZ HAZ]

    P1[Advancing Side\nVelocity aligned] -.->|Drag forward| S3
    P2[Retreating Side\nVelocity opposite] -.->|Sweep back| S4
    P3[Pin Rotation ω] -.->|Simple shear| S4
    P4[Shoulder Forging] -.->|Pressure| S5
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Diagram — Effect of Tool Geometry on Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TG[Tool Geometry Input] --> T1[Pin Profile]
    TG --> T2[Shoulder Type]
    TG --> T3[Tilt Angle]
    TG --> T4[Thread Pitch Direction]

    T1 --> O1[Stirring Efficiency]
    T2 --> O2[Heat Distribution]
    T3 --> O3[Vertical Forging Component]
    T4 --> O4[Material Pumping Direction Down or Up]

    O1 --> OUT[Resulting Metal Flow Pattern]
    O2 --> OUT
    O3 --> OUT
    O4 --> OUT

    OUT --> R1[Defect Free Weld]
    OUT --> R2[Defective Weld: Tunnel Flash Kiss Bond]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`
**Explain the function of the shoulder in an FSW tool. Why is the shoulder diameter typically chosen as 3× the pin diameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The shoulder in an FSW tool serves three primary functions:
1. **Heat generation** — through frictional contact with the workpiece top surface. It contributes approximately **80–85%** of the total heat input, with the remaining 15–20% coming from the pin.
2. **Forging pressure** — applies the downward axial force that consolidates the material behind the pin and prevents tunnel defects.
3. **Containment** — confines the plasticized metal beneath it, preventing flash and supporting the formation of a sound stir zone.

**Why 3× ratio:**
- Ensures a *heat-affected zone* wide enough to soften the surrounding material uniformly.
- Provides sufficient *plasticized material reservoir* to replenish the trailing side.
- Prevents *excess flash* (which would occur if shoulder were >4×) and *insufficient forging* (which would occur if <2×).
- Recommended by **Mishra & Ma (2005)** as the empirical optimum for aluminium alloys. **(1 mark for ratio justification)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`
**Differentiate between the advancing side and the retreating side in FSW. What defect is most likely on the advancing side and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Advancing Side (AS) | Retreating Side (RS) |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity relation | Tool rotation tangent velocity **aligned** with welding direction | Tool rotation tangent velocity **opposite** to welding direction |
| Material behavior | Material is **dragged forward** with the tool | Material is **swept backward** |
| Material supply | Often **deficient** | Often **excess** |
| Defect type | **Tunnel voids** (longitudinal cavities) | **Flash** (excess material) and surface galling |

**Defect on AS — Tunnel void (1 mark for explanation):**
On the AS, the tool removes material as it advances, but insufficient plasticized metal flows in to refill the void. This creates a continuous tunnel-like cavity along the weld line, particularly when the tool is too cold, the pin is too short, or the welding speed is too high for the chosen rotational speed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU ESE - Module 2 Choice Option A]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the various pin profiles used in FSW tools. Explain the effect of **threaded pin profile** on the direction of material flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the total heat input rate during FSW, including both shoulder and pin contributions. A 6 mm thick Al 6082 plate is welded at $N = 1000$ RPM, $v = 80$ mm/min, with shoulder force $F_{sh} = 10$ kN, shoulder radius $R_{sh} = 9$ mm, pin radius $R_p = 3$ mm, pin force $F_p = 2$ kN, and $\mu = 0.45$. Calculate the **spindle power** and the **heat input per unit length** of weld, assuming heat efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pin Profiles and Threaded Pin Effect (7 marks):**

Pin profiles (1 mark for sketch, 1 mark per profile × 4 = 4 marks, 2 marks for threaded-pin flow effect):

1. **Cylindrical / Straight pin** — constant cross-section, simplest design. Good for thin sheets; limited vertical flow.
2. **Tapered / Conical pin** — diameter reduces from top to bottom; provides strong downward and lateral flow; ideal for thick plates.
3. **Threaded pin** — helical ridges act as an *auger*. **Right-hand threads with clockwise rotation** drive material *downward* (most common). **Left-hand threads with clockwise rotation** drive material *upward* (used to avoid flash on exit side).
4. **Fluted / Triflate pin** — three or four flat surfaces act as additional shear planes; increases surface area, torque, and material mixing.

**Sketch (descriptive):** A vertical cross-section of a tool showing shoulder (D_sh) at top and pin (d_p) below with various profiles annotated. *(1 mark)*

**Threaded Pin and Flow (2 marks):** A right-hand threaded pin when rotated clockwise acts as a screw pump, generating an *axial downward material flow* along with the rotational tangential flow. This downward component is critical for:
- Refilling the advancing side to prevent tunnel defects.
- Forging the retreating side material into the weld line.
- Improving the consolidation of onion-ring features.

---

**(b) Heat Input Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Same as in **Section 3.1** above. Key results:
- $P_{sh} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu F_{sh} R_{sh}}{60}$
- $P_p = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu F_p R_p}{60}$
- $P_{tot} = P_{sh} + P_p$
- $E_{line} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot P_{tot}}{v}$

**Numerical (3 marks):**
Given: $N = 1000$ RPM, $v = 80$ mm/min $= 80/60$ m/s $= 1.333$ m/s, $F_{sh} = 10\,000$ N, $R_{sh} = 0.009$ m, $F_p = 2000$ N, $R_p = 0.003$ m, $\mu = 0.45$, $\eta = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Power at shoulder.**

$$
P_{sh} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.45 \times 10000 \times 0.009}{60}
$$

$$
P_{sh} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 40.5}{60} = \dfrac{254.47}{60} \approx 4.241 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Power at pin.**

$$
P_p = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 0.45 \times 2000 \times 0.003}{60}
$$

$$
P_p = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 2.7}{60} = \dfrac{16.96}{60} \approx 0.283 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Total spindle power.**

$$
P_{tot} = 4.241 + 0.283 = 4.524 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Useful heat per unit length.**

$$
Q_{useful} = 0.85 \times 4524 = 3845.4 \ \text{W}
$$

$$
E_{line} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{v} = \dfrac{3845.4}{1.333} \approx 2885 \ \text{J/m} = 2.885 \ \text{kJ/m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{tot} \approx 4.52$ kW; $E_{line} \approx 2.89$ kJ/m

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU ESE - Module 2 Choice Option B]`

**(a)** Discuss the **four metal flow zones** around an FSW tool. What are the major flow defects, and how are they related to inadequate flow? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-threaded cylindrical pin FSW tool is used to weld 4 mm AA 6061 plates. The tool has $D_{sh} = 15$ mm, $d_p = 5$ mm, $N = 1200$ RPM, $v = 100$ mm/min, $F_{sh} = 8$ kN, $F_p = 1.5$ kN, $\mu = 0.4$, $\eta = 0.85$. Determine the **total spindle power**, **spindle torque**, and the **effective strain rate** in the stir zone, assuming $L_p = 3.9$ mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four Metal Flow Zones and Flow Defects (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-heat (Leading) Zone** — material ahead of the tool, *not yet deformed*. Heated by conduction from shoulder. (1 mark)
2. **Initial Deformation Zone** — material first contacts the leading edge of the pin. *Heated + plastically deformed*, dragged upward with the tool rotation. (1 mark)
3. **Stir / Extrusion Zone (SZ)** — material undergoes **simple shear** around the pin; forced from AS to RS. The **onion-ring pattern** forms here due to alternating layers of material. (2 marks)
4. **Consolidation / Cooling Zone** — material behind the tool cools under shoulder pressure, *forging the joint*. Coarse recrystallized grains form in this zone. (1 mark)

**Flow Defects (2 marks):**
- **Tunnel void** on AS — insufficient downward/refilling flow.
- **Lazy S / Zigzag line** — incomplete intermixing across weld line.
- **Kissing bond on RS** — inadequate forging pressure on retreating side.
- **Flash on top surface** — over-flow due to excessive tilt or low viscosity.
- **Surface galling** — inadequate shoulder contact.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Spindle power (3 marks).**

$P_{tot} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu}{60}(F_{sh} R_{sh} + F_p R_p)$

$R_{sh} = D_{sh}/2 = 7.5$ mm = 0.0075 m; $R_p = 2.5$ mm = 0.0025 m

$P_{tot} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1200 \times 0.4}{60} \times (8000 \times 0.0075 + 1500 \times 0.0025)$

$P_{tot} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 480}{60} \times (60 + 3.75) = 16 \pi \times 63.75$

$P_{tot} = 50.265 \times 63.75 \approx 3204$ W = 3.20 kW

**Step 2 — Spindle torque (2 marks).**

$T_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot P_{tot}}{2 \pi N} = \dfrac{60 \times 3204}{2 \pi \times 1200}$

$T_s = \dfrac{192240}{7539.8} \approx 25.5$ N·m

**Step 3 — Effective strain rate (2 marks).**

$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi N r_p}{30 L_p \sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1200 \times 0.0025}{30 \times 0.0039 \times 1.732}$

$\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{9.425}{0.2026} \approx 46.5$ s⁻¹

**Final Answer:** $P_{tot} \approx 3.20$ kW; $T_s \approx 25.5$ N·m; $\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 46.5$ s⁻¹

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PEMET416 Module 2 — Tools and Metal Flow:**
> 1. **Mixing up AS/RS:** Students often say "defects form on the retreating side" — WRONG for tunnel voids. The tunnel is on the **advancing side**. The retreating side shows *flash* and *lazy S*. *(–1 mark)*
> 2. **Forgetting units in $E_{line}$:** Heat input per unit length must be in **J/m or kJ/m**. If you write watts (W), you have *forgotten to divide by v*. *(–1 mark)*
> 3. **Wrong pin-thread rule:** "Right-hand thread always pumps material down" is correct ONLY if the tool rotates **clockwise (looking from above)**. State both conditions. *(–1 mark)*
> 4. **Conflating shoulder and pin heat contributions:** Examiner expects you to show $Q_{sh} \approx 80\%$ and $Q_p \approx 20\%$. A single lumped formula without this ratio breakdown loses **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting efficiency factor $\eta$:** Always state $\eta$ explicitly; otherwise the answer is "idealized".
> 6. **Mermaid diagrams used as physical sketches:** Mermaid is for *flow charts*, not engineering drawings. Draw the pin profile with ASCII art or describe it clearly. *(No negative mark, but 0 for the sketch sub-part.)*
> 7. **Ignoring tool tilt in flow direction:** At a $2°$–$3°$ tilt, the shoulder has a *vertical forging component* $V_{vert} = V_{tan} \sin \theta$ that helps close the AS tunnel. Students often forget this and lose the conceptual mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Tools and Metal Flow**

- ✅ FSW tool = **Shoulder (heat + forge)** + **Pin (stir + flow)** + **Shank**.
- ✅ Tool is **non-consumable** and works in the **solid state** (no melting).
- ✅ Shoulder generates **~80%** of heat; pin generates **~20%**.
- ✅ Standard shoulder-to-pin diameter ratio = **3 : 1**.
- ✅ Tool tilt = **2°–3°** (typical) to ensure vertical forging and AS refill.
- ✅ Common pin materials: **H13 tool steel, MP159, W–Re, PCBN** (for steel/Ti).
- ✅ **Right-hand threaded pin + clockwise rotation = downward material pumping** (key KTU fact).
- ✅ Flow zones: Pre-heat → Initial Deformation → Stir/Extrusion → Consolidation.
- ✅ **AS (advancing side) → tunnel void**; **RS (retreating side) → flash & kissing bond**.
- ✅ **Onion ring** formation is the *visual signature* of adequate flow.
- ✅ Spindle power formula: $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_s}{60}$ — must be **memorized**.
- ✅ Heat input rate: $Q_{tot} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \mu}{60}(F_{sh}R_{sh} + F_p R_p)$; useful = $\eta Q_{tot}$ with $\eta \approx 0.85$.
- ✅ Heat input per unit length: $E_{line} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{v}$ (J/m) — **always divide by v**.
- ✅ Shoulder pressure: $P_{sh} = \dfrac{4 F_{sh}}{\pi (D_{sh}^2 - d_p^2)}$.
- ✅ Effective strain rate in SZ: $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\pi N r_p}{30 L_p \sqrt{3}}$ — order of **10¹–10² s⁻¹** (hot-forging regime).
- ✅ Tangential tool velocity at shoulder edge: $V_{tan} = \pi N D_{sh}/60$.
- ✅ **Bobbin / stationary-shoulder tools** are used for thick-plate butt welds and lap joints.
- ✅ **PCBN tools** are mandatory for welding **steels and titanium** (above 900 °C tool tip).
- ✅ Scroll and fluted shoulders/pins improve **material pumping** and **defect-free flow**.
- ✅ Inadequate flow → tunnel, lazy S, kissing bond, flash, surface galling.
- ✅ Examiner's hot words to use: *"viscoplastic flow", "simple shear", "stir zone", "onion ring", "forging consolidation", "advancing vs. retreating side"*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Welding: Principle and Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Welding: Principle and Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** is a solid-state welding process in which coalescence of materials is produced by the localized application of high-frequency vibratory energy (typically **20 kHz to 40 kHz** for metals and up to **70 kHz** for plastics) while the workpieces are held together under a static clamping force (clamp pressure). Because the base metal never reaches its melting point, ultrasonic welding is classified as a **solid-state joining process** under the AWS (American Welding Society) classification system, alongside diffusion bonding, friction welding, and forge welding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The principle, theory, and process parameters governing ultrasonic metal welding (USMW) are core examinable content. Students must clearly distinguish USMW from ultrasonic plastic welding (USPW), as the underlying metallurgical mechanisms differ significantly.

The welding action is generated by converting high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibrations through a **piezoelectric transducer** (usually Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT), amplifying these vibrations through a **mechanical/acoustic horn (sonotrode)**, and transmitting them perpendicular to the joint interface of the clamped workpieces.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a tailor using a sewing machine to stitch two pieces of cloth together. The needle moves up and down extremely fast (**vibration**), while the presser foot pushes the fabric down (**static clamping force**). Friction between the rapidly oscillating needle and the fabric creates localized heat that binds the threads. Similarly:

- The **sonotrode (horn)** is the "sewing needle" — it vibrates at ultrasonic frequency.
- The **anvil (base)** is the "presser foot" — it applies the static clamping pressure.
- The **workpieces** are the "fabric" — the high-frequency shear oscillations create frictional heat at the microscopic asperity level, causing plastic deformation and atomic-level bonding without melting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it does NOT melt the metal:** Ultrasonic welding is "cold" in the sense that the bulk material temperature remains well below the melting point (typically <50% of the absolute melting temperature for most metals). Bonding occurs via plastic deformation, diffusion across micro-asperities, and mechanical interlocking — all driven by shear stresses at the interface.

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (Metals) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | **20 – 40 kHz** (commonly 20 kHz or 35 kHz) | Above human hearing threshold (~20 kHz) |
| **Vibration Amplitude** | **10 – 50 µm** (peak-to-peak) | Microscopic surface displacement |
| **Clamp Pressure (Weld Force)** | **0.2 – 0.6 MPa** (low for Al, higher for steel) | Static axial force |
| **Weld Time** | **0.5 – 2.0 seconds** | Short cycle time |
| **Power Input** | **500 W – 3000 W** | Function of material and thickness |
| **Joint Temperature** | Typically **< 0.5 × T_melting** | Solid-state regime |

## 1.4 Classification of Ultrasonic Welding

1. **Ultrasonic Metal Welding (USMW):** Used for joining similar/dissimilar metals, foils, and wires. Frequency: 20–40 kHz.
2. **Ultrasonic Plastic Welding (USPW):** Used for thermoplastics. Frequency: 20–70 kHz. Bonding here involves localized melting.
3. **Ultrasonic Wire Bonding (USWB):** Specialized form used in microelectronics (aluminum/copper wires to IC pads).

For the KTU PEMET416 course, **USMW (Ultrasonic Spot Welding of Metals)** is the primary focus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "How" Behind USMW

The ultrasonic welding process can be decomposed into six sequential, overlapping stages:

### Stage 1 — Application of Static Clamp Force
- The top sonotrode and bottom anvil clamp the workpieces together with a static axial force $F_c$ (clamp pressure).
- This force establishes intimate mechanical contact between the two mating surfaces, breaking down macro-scale surface waviness and aligning the microscopic asperities.

### Stage 2 — Initiation of High-Frequency Shear Oscillations
- The piezoelectric transducer (PZT crystals) receives high-frequency electrical energy (~20 kHz sinusoidal AC).
- Through the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, electrical energy → mechanical strain → longitudinal vibration.
- The booster and horn (sonotrode) **amplify** this amplitude by a factor of 1.5× to 4× (gain ratio).

### Stage 3 — Surface Asperity Deformation and Slip
- The shear oscillations cause microscopic asperities on the interface to deform plastically.
- A thin surface oxide layer (e.g., $Al_2O_3$ on aluminum) is mechanically disrupted and dispersed.
- This is the **"breaking of the oxide barrier"** — a critical step for clean metallic bonding.

### Stage 4 — Frictional Heat Generation at Interface
- The oscillating interface generates **interfacial frictional heat**. The power dissipated per unit area is:
- This heat softens the micro-asperity tips without raising the bulk temperature to the melting point.

### Stage 5 — Plastic Deformation and Material Flow
- The softened micro-asperities undergo **plastic flow** (extrusion), spreading out and exposing fresh, oxide-free base metal.
- True metallic contact is established across an increasingly large fraction of the nominal joint area.

### Stage 6 — Bond Formation (Solid-State Metallurgical Bonding)
- Under the combined action of pressure, shear, and localized heat, atomic diffusion across the interface occurs.
- **Micro-welds** form at the contact points, which grow and coalesce, eventually forming a continuous joint.
- When the vibration stops, the joint cools rapidly under pressure, "freezing" the bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Solid-State"?** No bulk melting occurs. The bond is formed by plastic deformation, mechanical interlocking, and diffusion at sub-melting temperatures. The microstructure of the parent metal is largely preserved.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas for the KTU PEMET416 Module 2 examination.** Memorize SI units and physical significance.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F_c \cdot v_{tip}$ | Power dissipated at interface (product of static clamping force and tip velocity) | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $v_{tip} = 2 \pi f A$ | Peak tip velocity of sonotrode (where $A$ is peak amplitude and $f$ is frequency) | m/s |
| 3 | $P = F_c \cdot 2 \pi f A$ | Substituting #1 and #2 — **core power equation** | W |
| 4 | $E = P \cdot t_w$ | Total energy input (weld time $t_w$) | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $\tau = \frac{F_c}{A_{contact}} = F_c / A_c$ | Static contact pressure / clamp pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | $A_{horn} = G \cdot A_{transducer}$ | Horn gain equation — amplitude amplification | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma_y(T) = \sigma_0 \left(1 - \frac{T - T_{room}}{T_m - T_{room}}\right)$ | Yield stress reduction with interfacial heating (linear approximation) | Pa |
| 8 | $f_{resonance} = \frac{c}{2 L}$ | Longitudinal resonant frequency of horn (length $L$, speed of sound in tool steel $c \approx 5{,}050$ m/s) | Hz |
| 9 | $A_{contact}(t) = A_{nominal}\left[1 - e^{-k t}\right]$ | Time-evolution of true metallic contact area (exponential approach to nominal) | m² |
| 10 | $Q = \frac{f_{res}}{\Delta f}$ | Acoustic Q-factor of the resonant system (typical: 500–3000) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Warning:** In the power equation $P = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A$, the amplitude $A$ is the **peak (single-sided) amplitude**, NOT peak-to-peak. Some textbooks use peak-to-peak; check the convention. $v_{tip}$ is the **peak velocity** during oscillation, not the RMS value.

## 2.3 Physical Interpretation of Variables

- **$F_c$ (Clamp Force):** Too low → insufficient contact, poor bonding. Too high → excessive plastic deformation, thinning of sheets, tool wear.
- **$f$ (Frequency):** Higher frequency = finer vibrations, better for thin foils and micro-welding. Lower frequency = deeper penetration of vibration, suited for thicker sheets.
- **$A$ (Amplitude):** The displacement of the sonotrode tip. Higher amplitude increases frictional power, but excessive amplitude causes slip-stick instabilities and tool fatigue.
- **$t_w$ (Weld Time):** Energy-accumulating parameter. Determines total heat input and bond maturity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Battery tab welding (EV battery packs), aluminum sheet joining for body panels |
| **Aerospace** | Honeycomb core splicing, thermocouple wire joining, foil sealing |
| **Electronics** | Wire bonding in IC packaging, flex-circuit interconnections |
| **Solar/PV** | Copper ribbon to silicon cell tabbing in photovoltaic modules |
| **Medical Devices** | Hermetic sealing of pacemaker components, joining dissimilar metal foils |
| **Packaging** | Hermetic seals on food, pharmaceutical blister packs (USPW) |
| **Nuclear** | Joining zirconium alloy fuel rod end-plugs (Zircaloy-4) |

## 2.5 Advantages and Limitations

### Advantages
- **Solid-state** → no HAZ (Heat Affected Zone), no grain coarsening, no solidification defects.
- Joins **dissimilar metals** (e.g., Al-Cu, Al-Steel) that are difficult to fusion weld.
- **Low thermal input** → minimal distortion, no warping.
- **Fast cycle time** (sub-2 seconds) → ideal for high-volume production.
- No filler metal, no flux, no shielding gas required.
- **Energy efficient** compared to resistance or arc welding.

### Limitations
- Limited to **thin sections** (typically < 2 mm for each sheet; some special USMW systems up to ~3 mm).
- Joint geometry is primarily **lap or spot** configurations.
- High initial capital cost for the equipment.
- Tooling (sonotrode/anvil) wears and requires periodic replacement.
- Sensitive to surface contamination (oils, heavy oxides).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Pattern:** Numerical problems on ultrasonic welding typically test the power equation, energy equation, weld time calculation, and horn resonance frequency. Practice each derivation below with full algebraic transparency.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Dissipated at the Weld Interface

### Given
- Static clamp force: $F_c$ (N)
- Frequency of oscillation: $f$ (Hz)
- Peak amplitude of sonotrode tip: $A$ (m)

### Step 1 — Express Tip Velocity from Simple Harmonic Motion
The sonotrode tip undergoes simple harmonic motion. The instantaneous displacement is:
$$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The instantaneous velocity is the time derivative:
$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = 2 \pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Peak Velocity
The peak (maximum) velocity occurs when $\cos(2\pi f t) = 1$:
$$v_{tip} = 2 \pi f A$$

This is the magnitude of the maximum tangential velocity at the weld interface.

### Step 3 — Calculate the Frictional Power Dissipated
The frictional force resisting the relative motion of the interface is equal to the static clamp force $F_c$ (Coulomb friction model in the limit of small relative velocity). The instantaneous power dissipated is:
$$P(t) = F_c \cdot v(t) = F_c \cdot 2 \pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)$$

### Step 4 — Average the Power Over One Cycle
The time-averaged (mean) power dissipation over one complete cycle $T = 1/f$ is:
$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T F_c \cdot 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t) \, dt$$

Carrying out the integration:
$$\bar{P} = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \cos(2\pi f t) \, dt$$

Since $\int_0^T \cos(2\pi f t) \, dt = \left[\frac{\sin(2\pi f t)}{2\pi f}\right]_0^T = \frac{\sin(2\pi f) - \sin(0)}{2\pi f} = 0$

This gives the **average** value, but for energy input calculations, the standard textbook approach for USMW uses the **peak power** formulation (since work is done on the interface during both half-cycles via frictional/plastic deformation):

$$P = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The clamp force $F_c$ multiplied by the peak interfacial velocity gives the rate of frictional/plastic work. This is the **primary energy source** for ultrasonic welding.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Energy Input for the Weld

### Given
- Power dissipation: $P = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A$
- Weld time: $t_w$ (seconds)

### Step 1 — Apply the Definition of Energy
Energy is the time-integral of power. Assuming $P$ is approximately constant over the short weld duration:
$$E = \int_0^{t_w} P \, dt = P \cdot t_w$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Power Expression
$$E = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A \cdot t_w$$

### Step 3 — Express in Convenient Form
$$\boxed{E = 2\pi f A F_c t_w}$$

This is the **total frictional energy delivered to the weld interface** during the welding operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency of a Solid Horn (Half-Wave Resonator)

### Given
- Material of horn: tool steel, with longitudinal speed of sound $c = 5{,}050$ m/s
- Length of horn: $L$ (m)

### Step 1 — Wave Equation for Longitudinal Vibration
A solid cylindrical bar with one free end (at the tip) and one node (where it is clamped to the booster) forms a **half-wave resonator**. The boundary conditions are:
- Displacement node at $x = 0$ (clamped end)
- Displacement antinode at $x = L$ (free tip)

### Step 2 — Apply Standing Wave Condition
The standing wave displacement is $u(x,t) = U_0 \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$, where $k = \omega / c$.

The boundary conditions require:
- $u(0,t) = 0$ → satisfied by the $\sin(kx)$ form.
- Maximum displacement at $x = L$ → requires $kL = \pi/2$ for a quarter-wave, but for a horn with displacement node at the booster interface (which is at a standing wave node), the horn is **half a wavelength** long:
$$L = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{c}{2f_{res}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Resonant Frequency
$$\boxed{f_{res} = \frac{c}{2L}}$$

> **Practical Example:** For a tool-steel horn with $c = 5{,}050$ m/s and $L = 126.25$ mm:
> $$f_{res} = \frac{5050}{2 \times 0.12625} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$
> This is exactly the standard USMW operating frequency used in industrial systems (e.g., Branson, Telsonic, Schunk).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time-Evolution of True Metallic Contact Area

The growth of true metallic contact area $A_c(t)$ follows an exponential approach to the nominal contact area $A_{nom}$:

$$A_c(t) = A_{nom}\left[1 - e^{-k t}\right]$$

### Step 1 — Initial Condition
At $t = 0$, the surfaces are just in nominal contact, with true metallic contact area $A_c(0) = 0$:
$$A_c(0) = A_{nom}\left[1 - e^{0}\right] = A_{nom}[1 - 1] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Steady-State Limit
As $t \to \infty$:
$$A_c(\infty) = A_{nom}\left[1 - e^{-\infty}\right] = A_{nom} \cdot 1 = A_{nom} \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 3 — Rate Constant
The constant $k$ (units: s⁻¹) is material-dependent and increases with vibration amplitude and interface pressure. For aluminum-aluminum welds, typical values of $k$ range from **2 to 8 s⁻¹** depending on the surface preparation.

### Step 4 — Physical Meaning
The bond strength $S(t)$ of the ultrasonic weld is approximately proportional to the true contact area:
$$S(t) \propto A_c(t) = A_{nom}\left[1 - e^{-k t}\right]$$

This explains the experimentally observed **S-shaped weld strength vs. weld time curve** — initial slow rise (incubation period for asperity heating), rapid rise (active bonding), and saturation (no further improvement even with longer welding).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Question Type:** "Explain with a diagram/neat sketch how weld strength varies with (a) weld time, (b) weld pressure, (c) vibration amplitude, and (d) surface roughness."

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Bond Strength | Mechanism | Risk if Excessive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weld Time $t_w$** | Increases (up to a plateau) | More energy delivered, more diffusion | Tool erosion, sheet thinning, no further strength gain |
| **Clamp Force $F_c$** | Increases (up to a peak, then decreases) | More asperity deformation, larger contact | Sheet thinning, indentation, expulsion of material |
| **Amplitude $A$** | Increases (up to a peak, then decreases) | Higher frictional power, faster plastic flow | Slip-stick instability, tool fatigue, surface damage |
| **Frequency $f$** | Marginal effect in 20–40 kHz range | Finer vibration pattern, more cycles per second | Limited improvement; tool design constraints |
| **Surface Roughness** | Optimal at moderate roughness | Moderate roughness increases local stress and heating | Too rough → poor initial contact, voids |
| **Sheet Thickness** | Decreases (for fixed parameters) | Energy density drops as volume increases | Unbonded joints, cold welds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Ultrasonic Welding System

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram:** Since actual physical drawings of a USW machine are difficult in Mermaid, this block-level functional architecture maps all sub-system interactions and signal/power/force flow paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Power Supply 50 Hz] --> B[High Frequency Generator 20 kHz]
    B -->|Electrical 20 kHz AC| C[Piezoelectric Transducer PZT]
    C -->|Mechanical Vibration Low Amplitude| D[Booster Amplitude Modulator]
    D -->|Amplified Vibration| E[Sonotrode Horn Welding Tool]
    F[Anvil Base Stationary] --> G[Workpiece Clamping Assembly]
    G -->|Clamp Force Fc| H[Upper Sheet Metal Foil]
    H <-->|Vibration Interface| I[Lower Sheet Metal Foil]
    I --> F
    E -->|Coupled Vibration| H
    J[Pneumatic Hydraulic Pressure System] --> G
    K[PLC Control Unit] --> B
    K --> J
    K --> L[Weld Time Display and Recorder]
    L --> M[Quality Output Joint Strength Data]

    stepA[Step 1 Electrical Energy Input] --> stepB[Step 2 Piezoelectric Conversion]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Amplitude Boosting]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Mechanical Vibration Transmission]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Interfacial Friction and Plastic Deformation]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 Solid State Bond Formation]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Mechanism of Bond Formation at the Interface (Cross-Sectional View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** This schematic-style flowchart represents the cross-sectional progression of the metal-metal interface during ultrasonic welding, from initial contact (left) to a fully formed weld (right).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Initial Contact] -->|Clamp Force Applied| P2[Stage 2 Asperity Engagement]
    P2 -->|Vibration Starts| P3[Stage 3 Oxide Break-up and Asperity Deformation]
    P3 -->|Frictional Heat Softening| P4[Stage 4 Plastic Flow and Material Extrusion]
    P4 -->|True Metallic Contact| P5[Stage 5 Micro Weld Nucleation]
    P5 -->|Continued Vibration| P6[Stage 6 Weld Growth and Coalescence]
    P6 -->|Cooling Under Pressure| P7[Stage 7 Solid State Bond Complete]

    Q1[Upper Sheet Sonotrode Side] --- P1
    Q2[Lower Sheet Anvil Side] --- P1
    R1[Asperities Microscopic Peaks] --- P2
    R2[Oxide Layer Al2O3 / Cu2O / etc] --- P3
    R3[Fresh Oxide Free Base Metal] --- P5
    R4[Micro Welds] --- P6
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Energy Flow Path in USMW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Electrical Energy W in] --> S2[Transducer Piezoelectric Conversion]
    S2 -->|85 to 90 percent efficiency| S3[Mechanical Vibrational Energy]
    S3 -->|Horn Coupling Loss less than 5 percent| S4[Sonotrode Tip Kinetic Energy]
    S4 -->|Interfacial Friction and Plastic Deformation| S5[Heat at Interface Q in]
    S5 -->|Diffusion Across Asperities| S6[Metallurgical Bonding Energy]
    S6 --> S7[Weld Joint Useful Output]
    S5 -->|Conduction to Bulk| S8[Heat Loss to Surrounding Metal Q out]
    S1 -->|10 to 15 percent| S9[Electrical Losses in Generator and Transducer]
```

```mermaid

    Z2 -->|Same| Z3[Choose Standard USMW Parameters]
    Z2 -->|Dissimilar| Z4[Refer Compatibility Chart Al Cu Steel]
    Z3 --> Z5[Select Frequency 20 kHz for Thick to 35 kHz for Thin]
    Z4 --> Z5
    Z5 --> Z6[Set Amplitude 20 to 40 microns peak]
    Z6 --> Z7[Apply Clamp Force 0.2 to 0.6 MPa]
    Z7 --> Z8[Run Trial Weld 0.5 to 2.0 seconds]

    Z9 -->|Yes| Z10[Production Ramp Up]
```
