# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each module typically carries two Part A questions (3 marks each) and one Part B question (14 marks with internal choice). Part B usually has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Total for the module: 20 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define ultrasonic welding. State any three advantages of ultrasonic welding over conventional resistance spot welding.**

**Model Answer:**

Ultrasonic welding is a solid-state welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by the application of high-frequency vibratory energy (typically 20–40 kHz) under a static clamping force, without reaching the melting point of the base metals.

Three advantages over resistance spot welding:
1. **No fusion / no HAZ** — preserves the original microstructure and mechanical properties of the base metal; no cast dendritic structure is formed.
2. **Joins dissimilar metals** with very different melting points (e.g., aluminum to copper, aluminum to stainless steel), which is extremely difficult in resistance spot welding.
3. **Lower heat input** — minimal thermal distortion, negligible warping, and no burn-through of thin sheets, making it ideal for foils and battery tabs.
4. *(Bonus)* No sparks, fumes, or shielding gas required, providing a cleaner working environment.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
**List the four main components of an ultrasonic welding system. State the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. **High-Frequency Generator (Power Supply)** | Converts 50 Hz mains AC into high-frequency (20–40 kHz) electrical AC signal. |
| 2. **Piezoelectric Transducer (PZT)** | Converts the high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibrations of the same frequency via the inverse piezoelectric effect. |
| 3. **Booster (Amplitude Modulator)** | Mechanically amplifies (or sometimes reduces) the vibration amplitude from the transducer to the desired level before transmission to the horn. |
| 4. **Sonotrode (Horn / Welding Tool)** | The final mechanical element that transmits and further amplifies the vibration to the workpiece; also delivers the static clamping force. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Expected Pattern]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic diagram, explain the principle of operation of ultrasonic metal welding. Label all major components. (7 marks)
**(b)** Derive the expression for the power dissipated at the weld interface in ultrasonic welding. State the typical range of values for each variable. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle of Ultrasonic Metal Welding:**

The principle of USMW is based on the conversion of high-frequency electrical energy into mechanical vibrations, which are transmitted across the joint interface of two clamped workpieces under static pressure. The high-frequency shear oscillations cause localized plastic deformation, surface asperity interlocking, and atomic-level diffusion, producing a solid-state metallurgical bond without melting the parent metal.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. **Power Conversion:** The high-frequency generator converts 50 Hz AC mains into a 20 kHz (or 35 kHz) AC signal.
2. **Piezoelectric Conversion:** The PZT transducer converts the electrical signal into mechanical vibration of the same frequency (inverse piezoelectric effect).
3. **Amplitude Boosting:** The booster and horn progressively amplify the vibration amplitude (typically from ~10 µm at the transducer to 30–50 µm at the horn tip).
4. **Clamping:** The workpieces are clamped between the sonotrode (top) and anvil (bottom) with a static force $F_c$.
5. **Vibration Transmission:** The sonotrode tip oscillates tangentially relative to the lower workpiece, generating high shear stresses at the interface.
6. **Bond Formation:** Microscopic asperities deform, surface oxides break up, plastic flow occurs, and micro-welds form and coalesce.

**Key Components of the System:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **High-frequency generator** | Produces 20 kHz AC |
| **Piezoelectric transducer (PZT)** | Converts electrical to mechanical energy |
| **Booster** | Amplifies vibration amplitude |
| **Sonotrode/Horn** | Final amplification and transmission to workpiece |
| **Anvil** | Stationary support; provides reaction force |
| **Pneumatic/Hydraulic actuator** | Provides static clamp force $F_c$ |
| **PLC/Control unit** | Controls weld time, force, and amplitude |

*Schematic diagram:* Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of the Power Dissipated at the Weld Interface:**

**Given:** Clamp force $F_c$ (N), frequency $f$ (Hz), peak amplitude $A$ (m).

**Step 1 — Equation of SHM:** The sonotrode tip undergoes simple harmonic motion:
$$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Velocity:** Differentiating with respect to time:
$$v(t) = 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 — Peak velocity:** The peak (maximum) tangential velocity is:
$$v_{tip} = 2\pi f A$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipated:** The frictional force at the interface equals the clamp force $F_c$ (Coulomb friction). The power dissipated is the product of the frictional force and the relative velocity:
$$P = F_c \cdot v_{tip}$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression:**
$$\boxed{P = 2\pi f A F_c}$$

**Typical Range of Variables:**

| Variable | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ | 20,000 – 40,000 Hz |
| Peak amplitude $A$ | 10 – 50 µm (i.e., $10 \times 10^{-6}$ to $50 \times 10^{-6}$ m) |
| Clamp force $F_c$ | 200 – 2000 N (depending on joint area) |
| Resulting Power $P$ | 500 – 3000 W |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in amplitude:** If $A$ is given in micrometers, you **must** convert to meters (× 10⁻⁶) before substituting in the formula, otherwise the answer will be off by a factor of 10⁶. Common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Distinguish peak vs. peak-to-peak amplitude:** If the question states "peak-to-peak amplitude = 30 µm," the peak amplitude is $A = 15$ µm = $15 \times 10^{-6}$ m. Another frequent 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the Coulomb friction assumption** (i.e., that frictional force = $F_c$) costs 1 mark. State it explicitly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the mechanism of bond formation in ultrasonic metal welding with suitable stages. (7 marks)
**(b)** An ultrasonic metal welding machine operates at 20 kHz with a peak amplitude of 30 µm. The static clamping force applied is 500 N. Calculate (i) the peak tip velocity, (ii) the power dissipated at the weld interface, and (iii) the total energy input if the weld time is 1.5 seconds. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mechanism of Bond Formation in USMW:**

The bond formation in ultrasonic metal welding occurs through a series of overlapping stages, all taking place in the **solid state** (no melting):

**Stage 1 — Static Clamping:**
The two workpieces are brought together under a static clamping force $F_c$ (~0.2–0.6 MPa). This establishes intimate contact and aligns the microscopic asperities of the mating surfaces.

**Stage 2 — Initiation of Vibration:**
High-frequency (20 kHz) shear oscillations are transmitted through the sonotrode to the top workpiece. The relative tangential displacement across the interface is in the order of 10–50 µm.

**Stage 3 — Surface Asperity Deformation and Oxide Break-Up:**
The microscopic asperities undergo plastic deformation under the combined action of static pressure and dynamic shear. The brittle surface oxide layers (e.g., $Al_2O_3$, $Cu_2O$) fracture and disperse, exposing fresh, oxide-free base metal.

**Stage 4 — Frictional Heat Generation:**
Interfacial friction and plastic deformation generate localized heat. The interface temperature can rise to 50–80% of the absolute melting point, softening the micro-asperities.

**Stage 5 — Plastic Flow and Material Extrusion:**
The softened micro-asperities undergo plastic flow, extruding sideways and increasing the **true metallic contact area** at the interface.

**Stage 6 — Micro-Weld Nucleation and Growth:**
Atomic diffusion across the clean metallic contacts forms **micro-welds**. These micro-welds grow and coalesce, eventually forming a continuous metallurgical bond.

**Stage 7 — Cooling and Solidification of the Bond:**
When the vibration is stopped, the joint cools rapidly under static pressure, "freezing" the bond in its final configuration.

**Key Microstructural Feature:** No fusion zone, no HAZ. The bond line may show slight grain refinement due to dynamic recrystallization, but the parent metal microstructure is largely retained.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Frequency: $f = 20 \text{ kHz} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz}$
- Peak amplitude: $A = 30 \text{ µm} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Clamping force: $F_c = 500 \text{ N}$
- Weld time: $t_w = 1.5 \text{ s}$

---

**(i) Peak Tip Velocity:**

Using the formula:
$$v_{tip} = 2\pi f A$$

Substituting the values:
$$v_{tip} = 2 \times \pi \times 20{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$v_{tip} = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 20{,}000 \times 0.00003$$

$$v_{tip} = 6.2832 \times 0.6$$

$$\boxed{v_{tip} = 3.77 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

**(ii) Power Dissipated at the Weld Interface:**

Using the formula:
$$P = F_c \cdot v_{tip} = F_c \cdot 2\pi f A$$

Substituting:
$$P = 500 \times 3.77$$

$$\boxed{P = 1885 \text{ W} \approx 1.89 \text{ kW}}$$

(Alternatively, direct substitution: $P = 500 \times 2 \times \pi \times 20{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 1884.96$ W)

---

**(iii) Total Energy Input:**

Using the formula:
$$E = P \cdot t_w$$

Substituting:
$$E = 1885 \times 1.5$$

$$\boxed{E = 2827.5 \text{ J} \approx 2.83 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Loss Alert for Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Unit conversion of amplitude** is the most common mistake. The amplitude must be in **meters** in SI, not micrometers. Convert 30 µm to $30 \times 10^{-6}$ m before substitution. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
> 2. **No final unit declaration** in the answer costs 0.5 mark. Always write the unit explicitly (m/s, W, J).
> 3. **Rounding errors:** If you use $\pi = 3.14$, the answer is $P \approx 1884$ W. If you use $\pi = 3.1416$, $P \approx 1885$ W. Both are accepted, but show your work.
> 4. **Energy = Power × Time** is sometimes confused with Power ÷ Time. State the formula before substitution.
> 5. **Significant figures:** Maintain 3–4 significant figures in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as your one-page quick revision guide the night before the exam.**

### ⚡ Core Concepts
- [ ] USMW is a **solid-state welding process** — no melting, no HAZ.
- [ ] Operating frequency is **20 kHz – 40 kHz** (above human hearing).
- [ ] Vibration amplitude is **10 – 50 µm** (microscopic).
- [ ] Clamp pressure: **0.2 – 0.6 MPa**; weld time: **0.5 – 2.0 s**.
- [ ] Bonding mechanism: plastic deformation + asperity interlocking + atomic diffusion.

### ⚡ Essential Formulae (Memorize These!)
- [ ] $v_{tip} = 2\pi f A$
- [ ] $P = 2\pi f A F_c$ ← **THE power equation**
- [ ] $E = P \cdot t_w = 2\pi f A F_c t_w$
- [ ] $f_{res} = c / (2L)$ for half-wave horn
- [ ] $A_c(t) = A_{nom}[1 - e^{-kt}]$ (true contact area growth)

### ⚡ Key Components
- [ ] **Generator** → **Transducer (PZT)** → **Booster** → **Sonotrode (Horn)** → **Anvil**

### ⚡ Must-Know Applications
- [ ] EV battery tab welding (Al-Cu, Al-Al)
- [ ] Aerospace honeycomb splicing
- [ ] Photovoltaic cell tabbing
- [ ] Microelectronic wire bonding
- [ ] Nuclear fuel rod end-plug sealing (Zircaloy)

### ⚡ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Amplitude in µm vs. m — always convert!
- [ ] Peak vs. peak-to-peak amplitude — confirm the convention.
- [ ] Don't confuse ultrasonic metal welding (USMW) with ultrasonic plastic welding (USPW) — the bonding mechanism is different.
- [ ] State SI units explicitly in numerical answers.
- [ ] Remember: bond formation in USMW is a **solid-state** process, NOT fusion.

### ⚡ Comparison Anchors (for comparative questions)
- [ ] USMW vs. Resistance Spot Welding: USMW is solid-state, no HAZ, can join dissimilar metals.
- [ ] USMW vs. Friction Stir Welding: USMW is for thin sheets; FSW is for thick plates.
- [ ] USMW vs. Diffusion Bonding: USMW is faster (seconds vs. hours); DB requires higher temperatures and vacuum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Welding Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types and Welding Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of KTU PEMET416, **welding is broadly classified into three primary families** based on the state of the base metal during joining: (1) **Fusion Welding** (where the base metal melts), (2) **Solid-State Welding** (joining occurs below the melting point through atomic diffusion or plastic deformation), and (3) **Brazing/Soldering** (where a filler metal melts but the base metal does not). Each family is further sub-classified based on the **energy source** used to accomplish the joining, such as electrical arc, resistance heating, chemical combustion, high-energy beam, or mechanical friction.

The **welding environment** refers to the **ambient physical, chemical, and atmospheric conditions** in which the welding operation takes place — including medium (air, water, vacuum, inert gas), temperature, pressure, gravity, and presence of contaminants — which significantly influence process selection, joint integrity, and operator safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly differentiate between **fusion welding processes** (arc, gas, high-energy beam) and **solid-state welding processes** (friction, ultrasonic, diffusion). Examiner questions are frequently framed around this distinction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two pieces of clay that you wish to join:

- **Fusion welding analogy:** You heat both clay pieces until they melt into a soft paste, press them together, and let them solidify into one block. This is exactly what happens in arc welding — the **electric arc melts the edges** of the metal plates.
- **Solid-state welding analogy:** Instead of melting, you vigorously rub the two clay pieces against each other until they become hot, soft, and sticky due to friction, and then press them together. No melting occurred — only plastic deformation. This is **friction welding**.
- **Brazing analogy:** You take a third material (say, glue that melts at a low temperature) and apply it between the two clay surfaces. The glue melts and bonds them, but the clay itself never melts. This mirrors **brazing**, where a filler metal with a melting point **below 450 °C** (soldering) or above it (brazing) flows into the joint by capillary action.

For the **welding environment**, think of it like cooking in different kitchens:
- Cooking in an **open-air kitchen** (normal welding in atmosphere) is easy.
- Cooking **underwater** requires special equipment and waterproof ingredients — analogous to **underwater wet welding** for offshore pipelines.
- Cooking in **outer space** (vacuum environment) needs sealed containers — analogous to **space welding** where gravity-driven filler flow fails and special techniques are needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The Arc Temperature in a typical **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process reaches approximately **6000 °C to 7000 °C**, which is roughly **twice the surface temperature of the Sun's photosphere** (~5500 °C). This illustrates why welding is considered one of the most concentrated industrial heat sources.

---

### 1.3 Master Classification Table — Welding Process Families

| Welding Family | Energy Source | Base Metal State | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding** | Electrical arc discharge | Molten (fusion) | SMAW, GTAW (TIG), GMAW (MIG), FCAW, SAW, PAW |
| **Resistance Welding** | Electrical resistance + pressure | Plastic state / partial melt | Spot, Seam, Projection, Butt, Flash |
| **Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding** | Combustion of fuel gas + O₂ | Molten (fusion) | Oxy-acetylene, Oxy-hydrogen |
| **High-Energy Beam Welding** | Focused electron/laser/photon beam | Molten (fusion) / keyhole | LBW, EBW, Plasma |
| **Solid-State Welding** | Mechanical / thermal-mechanical | Solid (no melting) | Friction (FW, FSW), Ultrasonic, Diffusion, Forge, Explosion |
| **Brazing** | Indirect heating | Solid base + molten filler | Torch, Furnace, Induction, Dip |
| **Soldering** | Low-temp indirect heating | Solid base + molten filler (< 450 °C) | Iron, Wave, Reflow |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Sub-Classification of Welding Processes

#### A. Arc Welding Processes (Fusion Family)

**Definition:** Arc welding uses an **electric arc** struck between an electrode and the workpiece (or between two electrodes) to generate the heat required for fusion. The arc temperature typically ranges between **6000 °C and 20,000 °C** depending on the process.

**Sub-classification by Electrode Type:**

1. **Consumable Electrode Arc Welding:**
   - **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding):** Manual, uses a flux-coated consumable electrode. Slag protects the weld pool.
   - **GMAW (Gas Metal Arc Welding) / MIG:** Continuous solid wire electrode fed automatically; external shielding gas (Ar, CO₂, or Ar/CO₂ mix).
   - **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding):** Continuous tubular electrode filled with flux; may or may not require external gas.
   - **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding):** Wire electrode submerged under a granular flux bed; arc is invisible.

2. **Non-Consumable Electrode Arc Welding:**
   - **GTAW (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) / TIG:** Tungsten electrode in an inert gas shield (Argon/Helium). Can use filler or be autogenous.
   - **PAW (Plasma Arc Welding):** Constricted arc through a copper nozzle producing high-velocity plasma jet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test the distinction between **GMAW and FCAW** — both use continuous wire feed, but GMAW uses a **solid wire** and **external gas**, while FCAW uses a **flux-cored tubular wire** which may be self-shielded.

#### B. Resistance Welding Processes

**Definition:** Heat for joining is generated by **resistance to the flow of electric current (I²R heating)** through the parts being joined, simultaneously applying forge pressure.

**Heat Generated (H) Formula:**

$$H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

where:
- $H$ = Heat generated in **Joules (J)**
- $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes (A)**
- $R$ = Electrical resistance of the joint interface in **Ohms (Ω)**
- $t$ = Time of current flow in **seconds (s)**

**Sub-types:**
- **Spot Welding:** Resistance spot welding produces individual Nuggets at each electrode contact point. Used extensively in automotive sheet metal assembly.
- **Seam Welding:** Similar to spot, but uses roller electrodes to produce a continuous leak-tight weld (e.g., fuel tanks).
- **Projection Welding:** Uses embossed projections to localize current and pressure.
- **Flash Welding & Upset Butt Welding:** For joining rods, tubes, and rails end-to-end.

#### C. Solid-State Welding Processes

**Definition:** Joining occurs at temperatures **below the melting point of the base metal**, primarily through **plastic deformation, diffusion, or a combination of both**. No fusion zone, no cast structure, and minimal HAZ.

**Major Sub-types:**

1. **Friction Welding (FW):** One component is rotated against a stationary component under axial pressure; frictional heat softens the interface and forging pressure completes the bond.
2. **Friction Stir Welding (FSW):** A non-consumable rotating tool with a pin and shoulder traverses the joint line, locally plasticizing the material without melting. Widely used for **Al, Mg, Cu alloys**.
3. **Ultrasonic Welding (USW):** High-frequency (typically **20–40 kHz**) mechanical vibrations under pressure produce solid-state bonds. Used for thin foils and electrical connections.
4. **Diffusion Bonding:** Parts are held under pressure at elevated temperature (typically 0.5–0.8 × T_m) for extended periods; bonding occurs through atomic diffusion.
5. **Explosion Welding (EXW):** Controlled detonation drives one plate into another at high velocity, creating a metallurgical bond with a characteristic wavy interface.
6. **Forge Welding:** Ancient process; parts heated to forging temperature and hammered together.

#### D. High-Energy Beam Welding

- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** A focused **CO₂ or Nd:YAG fiber laser** produces a deep keyhole weld with a very narrow Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ). Typical power densities exceed **10⁶ W/mm²**.
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** A focused beam of **accelerated electrons** in a vacuum chamber. Power densities up to **10⁹ W/mm²** — produces deep, narrow welds with very high depth-to-width ratios (e.g., 25:1).

#### E. Brazing and Soldering

- **Brazing:** Filler metal melts **above 450 °C** but below the base metal's melting point. Capillary action draws the filler into the joint gap.
- **Soldering:** Filler metal melts **below 450 °C**. Common in electronics and plumbing.

---

### 2.2 Welding Environments — Detailed Analysis

The environment in which welding is performed has a **profound effect on arc stability, weld pool dynamics, shielding effectiveness, and operator safety**.

#### 1. **Open Air / Atmospheric Welding**
The most common industrial environment. Requires **shielding gas** (in GMAW/GTAW) or **flux** (in SMAW/SAW) to protect the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂O vapor).

#### 2. **Underwater Welding**
Used for repair of offshore platforms, ships, and pipelines.
- **Wet Welding:** Done directly in water using waterproof electrodes. Severe quenching → brittle martensite in the HAZ.
- **Dry Welding (Hyperbaric):** Performed in a sealed chamber at ambient pressure; uses conventional GTAW/GMAW equipment.

#### 3. **Space Welding**
Microgravity and vacuum environment challenges:
- **No natural buoyancy** → molten filler does not flow downward.
- **No convection** → heat dissipation is purely by radiation.
- Solutions: Use of electron beam or laser welding in vacuum; containment of filler metal in a foil envelope.

#### 4. **Nuclear Environment Welding**
Requires low-cobalt, low-boron consumables to minimize activation products. Performed in **hot cells** with **remote manipulators** (telech welding).

#### 5. **Vacuum Chamber Welding**
Used for reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb) and EBW. Eliminates atmospheric contamination completely.

#### 6. **Inert Atmosphere Chamber (Glove Box) Welding**
For titanium, zirconium, and refractory metals that absorb O₂ and N₂ above ~400 °C. The entire component is enclosed in an argon-purged chamber; operator manipulates tools through sealed gloves.

#### 7. **High-Altitude / Low-Pressure Welding**
At high altitudes, reduced atmospheric pressure affects arc stability and shielding gas coverage.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|
| $H = I^{2} R t$ | Heat generated in resistance welding | $H$ in J, $I$ in A, $R$ in Ω, $t$ in s |
| $P = V \cdot I$ | Arc power input | $P$ in W (or J/s), $V$ in V, $I$ in A |
| $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length (most commonly tested KTU formula) | $E$ in J/mm, $v$ in mm/s |
| $\eta \cdot E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Effective heat input considering process efficiency | $\eta$ is process transfer efficiency |
| $T_{arc} \approx 6000 - 20000 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | Typical arc welding temperature range | Varies by process |
| $T_{melt,Filler,Solder} < 450 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | Soldering upper temperature limit | Definition boundary |
| $T_{melt,Filler,Braze} \geq 450 \,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ | Brazing lower temperature limit | Definition boundary |
| $f_{USW} = 20 - 40 \, \mathrm{kHz}$ | Ultrasonic welding frequency range | Standard industrial range |
| Power density (EBW) $\approx 10^{9} \, \mathrm{W/mm^{2}}$ | Electron beam power density | Highest among industrial processes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The formula $E = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ (Heat Input) appears in **almost every KTU welding module paper**. Always include the units (J/mm) in your final answer for full marks.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Resistance spot welding assembles **over 4000 spot welds** in a single car body (e.g., Tesla Model Y uses ~17,000 aluminum FSW joints for battery enclosures).
- **Aerospace:** EBW and LBW are used for turbine blade joining and fuel tank fabrication due to minimal distortion.
- **Shipbuilding:** SAW is preferred for thick plate butt joints (efficiency up to **4–5 kg/h** deposition rate).
- **Pipeline Construction:** Mechanized GMAW and FCAW dominate the cross-country pipeline laying industry.
- **Defense:** Friction stir welding is used for joining **aluminum armor plates** on military vehicles.
- **Electronics:** Ultrasonic wire bonding and reflow soldering are the backbone of PCB assembly.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Equation in Arc Welding

This is the **most important formula** in Module 2 because it links electrical process parameters to metallurgical outcomes (grain size, HAZ width, distortion).

**Given:**
- Arc voltage: $V$ (Volts)
- Welding current: $I$ (Amperes)
- Travel speed of the torch: $v$ (mm/s)

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of electrical power delivered to the arc.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

where $P$ is the arc power in **Watts (W)** or equivalently **Joules per second (J/s)**.

**Step 2:** Note that arc power represents the **rate** at which thermal energy is supplied to the workpiece.

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = V \cdot I$$

**Step 3:** To find the energy supplied **per unit length of weld**, divide by the travel speed $v$ (length traversed per unit time).

$$\frac{dQ}{dx} = \frac{dQ / dt}{dx / dt} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the **process transfer efficiency** $\eta$ to account for losses to the surrounding atmosphere, spatter, and radiation. The effective heat that actually reaches the workpiece is:

$$E = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Step 5:** Standard numerical values for $\eta$ used in KTU board exam problems:

| Process | Typical Efficiency ($\eta$) |
|---|---|
| SMAW | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| GMAW (spray transfer) | 0.70 – 0.85 |
| GMAW (short-circuit) | 0.75 – 0.85 |
| GTAW | 0.30 – 0.60 |
| SAW | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| PAW | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| LBW | 0.40 – 0.70 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GTAW has the lowest efficiency:** The majority of heat in TIG welding goes into heating the non-consumable tungsten electrode and the surrounding shielding gas, leaving a smaller fraction for the workpiece.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A fabricator is using **SAW (Submerged Arc Welding)** on a **12 mm thick low-carbon steel plate**. The arc voltage is **32 V**, the welding current is **600 A**, and the travel speed is **6 mm/s**. The process efficiency for SAW is **0.90**. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** of the weld in **kJ/mm**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.

$$V = 32 \, \mathrm{V}, \quad I = 600 \, \mathrm{A}, \quad v = 6 \, \mathrm{mm/s}, \quad \eta = 0.90$$

**Step 2:** Apply the heat input formula.

$$E = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$E = \frac{0.90 \times 32 \times 600}{6}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the numerator.

$$0.90 \times 32 \times 600 = 0.90 \times 19200 = 17280 \, \mathrm{J/mm}$$

**Step 5:** Divide by the travel speed.

$$E = \frac{17280}{6} = 2880 \, \mathrm{J/mm}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kJ/mm as requested.

$$E = 2.88 \, \mathrm{kJ/mm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E = 2.88 \, \mathrm{kJ/mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** Always state the formula before plugging in numbers. Marks are awarded for the **formula statement (½ mark)**, the **substitution (½ mark)**, and the **final numerical answer with correct units (1 mark)** in a typical 3-mark short problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Heat Generated in Resistance Welding

**Starting Equation:**

$$H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

**Step 1:** This is a direct application of **Joule's First Law of Heating**, which states that the heat produced by an electric current flowing through a conductor is proportional to the square of the current, the resistance, and the time.

**Step 2:** For spot welding, $R$ is dominated by the **contact resistance** between the two sheets, not the bulk metal resistance. Contact resistance depends on:
- Surface roughness (rougher → higher R initially → more localized heating)
- Material resistivity (higher resistivity → more heat)
- Oxide layers (act as resistors initially, then break down)

**Step 3:** Practical range of values:
- Spot welding current: $I \approx 5000 - 12000 \, \mathrm{A}$
- Time: $t \approx 0.1 - 1.0 \, \mathrm{s}$ (typically **cycles** of 60 Hz AC)
- Total heat per spot: typically **50–500 J**

**Step 4:** Heat balance for spot welding:

$$H_{generated} = H_{required\,to\,melt\,nugget} + H_{losses\,to\,electrodes} + H_{losses\,to\,surroundings}$$

For efficient welding, the electrode losses must be minimized — this is why electrodes are made of **water-cooled Class 2 chromium-copper (CuCr1Zr)** alloys with high thermal conductivity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Fusion vs. Solid-State Welding

| Parameter | Fusion Welding | Solid-State Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Base metal state | Molten | Solid (plastic state) |
| HAZ | Present | Minimal or absent |
| Cast structure in weld | Yes (columnar grains) | No (recrystallized/refined structure) |
| Liquation cracking risk | Possible | Negligible |
| Filler metal required | Usually (except autogenous GTAW, LBW) | Usually not required |
| Dissimilar metal joining | Difficult | Excellent (FW, EXW) |
| Typical processes | SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, SAW, LBW, EBW | FSW, FW, USW, Diffusion, EXW |
| Energy efficiency | 30–95% (varies) | Often 100% effective at interface |
| Equipment cost | Lower to moderate | Moderate to high (FSW, EBW) |
| Vacuum / shielding required | Sometimes (GTAW, LBW) | Often (Diffusion, EXW) |

---

### 3.5 Welding Environment Influence on Process Selection

| Environment | Key Challenge | Recommended Process / Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Open air (workshop)** | Atmospheric contamination | SMAW (flux), GMAW (shielding gas) |
| **Open field (site)** | Wind disrupts shielding gas | FCAW-SS (self-shielded), SAW |
| **Underwater** | Quenching → brittle HAZ | Wet SMAW (with special electrodes), Hyperbaric GMAW |
| **Vacuum chamber** | Arc stability (GTAW difficult) | EBW, PAW |
| **Inert glove box** | Operator access | Manual GTAW with remote manipulation |
| **Space / microgravity** | No buoyancy for filler | EBW with filler pre-placed |
| **Nuclear / hot cell** | Radiation dose to operator | Remote mechanized GTAW (TIG) |
| **High altitude** | Lower breakdown voltage | Adjust arc length and gas flow |
