## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between fusion welding and solid-state welding processes with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Fusion Welding:**
- The base metals are heated until they reach their **melting point** and fuse together.
- A **cast structure** with columnar grains forms in the weld zone.
- A **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)** with distinct microstructural changes is present.
- Examples: **(i) Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)**, **(ii) Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)**.

**Solid-State Welding:**
- Joining occurs **without melting** the base metals, typically below their melting points.
- Bonding occurs through **plastic deformation, diffusion, or friction**.
- No fusion zone, minimal HAZ, refined recrystallized microstructure.
- Examples: **(i) Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**, **(ii) Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner awards full 3 marks only if the student clearly mentions **melting vs. no melting** and provides **two correct examples** in each category.

---

#### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is meant by the "welding environment"? List any three special welding environments.**

**Model Answer:**

The **welding environment** refers to the **ambient physical and atmospheric conditions** in which the welding process is performed, including the surrounding medium (air, water, vacuum, inert gas), temperature, pressure, and the presence of contaminants or radiation.

**Three special welding environments:**

1. **Underwater welding** — performed in marine environments for repair of offshore structures and ship hulls (e.g., wet SMAW or hyperbaric GMAW).

2. **Space (microgravity) welding** — performed in vacuum with no gravitational pull; requires special techniques such as electron beam welding with pre-placed filler.

3. **Inert atmosphere / glove box welding** — used for reactive metals such as titanium and zirconium which readily absorb oxygen and nitrogen at elevated temperatures; the entire workpiece is enclosed in an argon-filled chamber.

*(Other valid environments: nuclear hot cell welding, high-altitude welding, vacuum chamber welding.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q: (a)** Classify the various **arc welding processes** with a neat sketch-based explanation. Compare **SMAW and GMAW** on the basis of electrode type, shielding mechanism, deposition rate, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the concept of **heat input in arc welding**. Derive the heat input equation. A GMAW operation is performed with **V = 28 V**, **I = 250 A**, travel speed **v = 5 mm/s**, and process efficiency **η = 0.80**. Calculate the heat input per unit length in **kJ/mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Classification of Arc Welding Processes:**

Arc welding processes are classified into **two main categories** based on the electrode type:

**1. Consumable Electrode Processes:**
The electrode is consumed during welding and becomes part of the weld deposit.

| Process | Electrode | Shielding |
|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** | Flux-coated stick electrode | Flux decomposition produces shielding gas + slag |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | Continuous solid wire | External inert/active gas (Ar, CO₂, mix) |
| **FCAW** | Continuous flux-cored tubular wire | Self-shielded (no gas) or gas-shielded |
| **SAW** | Continuous solid wire | Granular flux bed submerged around arc |

**2. Non-Consumable Electrode Processes:**
The electrode remains intact during welding; filler may be added separately.

| Process | Electrode | Shielding |
|---|---|---|
| **GTAW (TIG)** | Tungsten | Inert gas (Ar or He) |
| **PAW** | Tungsten (constricted arc) | Inert gas + plasma jet |

**Comparison: SMAW vs. GMAW**

| Parameter | SMAW | GMAW |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode type | Flux-coated stick (consumable) | Continuous solid wire (consumable) |
| Shielding mechanism | Flux decomposition + slag cover | External shielding gas (Ar/CO₂) |
| Deposition rate | Low (~1–3 kg/h) | High (~3–8 kg/h) |
| Skill requirement | Moderate (manual) | Lower (semi-automatic) |
| Outdoor usability | Good (flux shields against wind) | Poor (wind disrupts gas shield) |
| Typical applications | Construction, repair, field work | Automotive, fabrication, production |
| Slag removal | Required after each pass | Not required (gas shield) |

**Sketch (describe in words):**

```
   ┌──────────────────┐
   │   Flux Coating   │
   │ ┌──────────────┐ │
   │ │  Core Wire   │ │  ← Stick electrode
   │ └──────────────┘ │
   └──────║───────────┘
          ║ (electrode held in holder)
   ══════╬═════════════════ Arc
          ▽
   ═════════════════════════
        Base Metal Plate
   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ (Weld bead with slag on top)
```

```
   Wire Spool → ═══════╗
                        ║
   Shielding Gas (Ar) ─→ ╫───── Arc ──→ Workpiece
                        ║
   Contact Tip ─────────╝
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Concept of Heat Input:**

Heat input is the **amount of thermal energy supplied per unit length of the weld joint** during arc welding. It directly controls the **weld bead geometry, HAZ width, grain size, residual stresses, and distortion**. Higher heat input → coarser grains, wider HAZ, more distortion.

**Derivation:**

Given:
- Voltage across the arc: $V$ (Volts)
- Welding current: $I$ (Amperes)
- Torch travel speed: $v$ (mm/s)

The electrical power delivered to the arc is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

Since power is the rate of energy delivery ($P = dQ/dt$), the energy supplied per unit time is:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = V \cdot I$$

To find the energy supplied per unit length of weld (heat input $E$), we divide by the travel speed $v = dx/dt$:

$$\frac{dQ}{dx} = \frac{dQ/dt}{dx/dt} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v}$$

Including process efficiency $\eta$ (fraction of arc energy that actually reaches the workpiece):

$$E = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given values:**

$$V = 28 \, \mathrm{V}, \quad I = 250 \, \mathrm{A}, \quad v = 5 \, \mathrm{mm/s}, \quad \eta = 0.80$$

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$E = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$E = \frac{0.80 \times 28 \times 250}{5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$0.80 \times 28 \times 250 = 0.80 \times 7000 = 5600 \, \mathrm{J/mm}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by travel speed.

$$E = \frac{5600}{5} = 1120 \, \mathrm{J/mm}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kJ/mm.

$$E = 1.12 \, \mathrm{kJ/mm}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E = 1.12 \, \mathrm{kJ/mm}}$$

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **various welding environments** encountered in modern industry. Discuss the specific challenges and recommended processes for **underwater welding** and **space welding**. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Describe the principle of **resistance spot welding** with a neat schematic. State the formula for heat generated and explain the role of **electrode force, current, and time** on weld nugget formation. A spot welding operation uses **I = 8000 A**, **R = 200 μΩ**, and **t = 0.25 s**. Calculate the heat generated per spot in **kJ**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Welding Environments in Modern Industry:**

A welding environment is the **medium and ambient conditions** in which the joining process is executed. Modern industry demands welding in increasingly diverse and challenging environments.

**1. Open-Air / Workshop Environment:**
The most common environment. Challenges include atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂O vapor) of the molten weld pool. Solution: Use of **flux (SMAW, SAW)** or **shielding gas (GTAW, GMAW)**.

**2. Field / Outdoor Site Environment:**
Wind disturbs shielding gas coverage. Solution: **Self-shielded FCAW** or **SAW** where the flux bed physically shields the arc.

**3. Underwater Welding Environment:**
Used for offshore platforms, ship hulls, and pipeline repairs.

**Challenges:**
- Rapid quenching of the weld → formation of **hard, brittle martensite** in the HAZ.
- Hydrostatic pressure complicates arc stability.
- Hydrogen embrittlement from dissociation of water.
- Operator safety (electrical hazards in water).

**Recommended Processes:**
- **Wet SMAW:** Uses specially formulated **rutile or basic-coated waterproof electrodes**. Deposition rate is lower than dry welding; HAZ is hard and may require **post-weld heat treatment**.
- **Dry (Hyperbaric) Welding:** Performed inside a sealed chamber (habitat) at ambient pressure. Uses conventional **GTAW** or **GMAW**. Quality is comparable to surface welding. Used for critical repairs.

**4. Space / Microgravity Welding Environment:**
Used for assembling structures in orbit (ISS, future space stations).

**Challenges:**
- **No gravitational force** → molten filler does not flow downward into the joint; surface tension dominates.
- **Vacuum** → no convective cooling; heat dissipation is by radiation only.
- Arc stability is poor in vacuum.

**Recommended Processes:**
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** Operates naturally in vacuum; high power density allows deep penetration welds.
- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** Possible with beam delivered through optical fiber.
- Filler metal is **pre-placed as a foil or wire** before welding.

**5. Inert Atmosphere / Glove Box Environment:**
Used for reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb). Oxygen absorption above ~400 °C severely embrittles the weld. The entire assembly is enclosed in an **argon-purged chamber**; the operator works through sealed gloves.

**6. Nuclear / Hot Cell Environment:**
Welding of radioactive components requires **remote manipulation**. Cobalt and boron content in consumables is strictly controlled to prevent activation products.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Principle of Resistance Spot Welding:**

Resistance spot welding (RSW) is a **fusion welding process** in which two or more sheets of metal are joined by the **heat generated from resistance to the flow of electric current** through the workpieces, combined with the application of **forge pressure** from copper electrodes.

**Schematic:**

```
    ┌────────────────────────┐
    │   Upper Electrode      │  (Water-cooled Cu alloy)
    │   ┌──────────┐         │
    │   │  Force ↓ │         │
    │   └──────────┘         │
    │  ═══════════════════   │  ← Top sheet
    │  ░░░░░NUGGET░░░░░░░   │  ← Fused zone
    │  ═══════════════════   │  ← Bottom sheet
    │   ┌──────────┐         │
    │   │  Force ↑ │         │
    │   └──────────┘         │
    │   Lower Electrode      │
    └────────────────────────┘
        AC Current Source (Thyristor-controlled)
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The sheets are clamped between two **water-cooled copper electrodes**.
2. A large **low-voltage, high-amperage alternating current** (typically 5000–12000 A) is passed through the stack.
3. The highest electrical resistance occurs at the **faying surfaces** (sheet-to-sheet contact).
4. According to **Joule's heating law**, this localized resistance generates intense heat.
5. The metal at the interface melts and forms a **fused nugget (cast structure)**.
6. The electrodes apply **continuous forge pressure**, which contains the molten metal and consolidates the nugget.
7. Current is switched off; the nugget solidifies under pressure to form the spot weld.

**Formula for Heat Generated:**

$$H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

where $H$ = heat generated (J), $I$ = current (A), $R$ = resistance at interface (Ω), $t$ = time (s).

**Role of Process Parameters on Nugget Formation:**

| Parameter | Effect on Nugget |
|---|---|
| **Welding Current (I)** ↑ | Larger nugget (heat ∝ I²); too high → expulsion of metal, porosity, electrode sticking |
| **Weld Time (t)** ↑ | Larger, deeper nugget; too long → overheating, indentation |
| **Electrode Force (F)** ↑ | Lowers contact resistance → smaller, but denser nugget; reduces expulsion |
| **Hold Time (after current off)** | Allows nugget to solidify under pressure; prevents cracking |

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given values:**

$$I = 8000 \, \mathrm{A}, \quad R = 200 \, \mu\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega, \quad t = 0.25 \, \mathrm{s}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$H = (8000)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.25$$

**Step 3:** Compute $I^{2}$.

$$I^{2} = (8000)^{2} = 64 \times 10^{6} \, \mathrm{A^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by R.

$$64 \times 10^{6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 64 \times 200 = 12800$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by t.

$$H = 12800 \times 0.25 = 3200 \, \mathrm{J}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kJ.

$$H = 3.2 \, \mathrm{kJ}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{H = 3.2 \, \mathrm{kJ \, per \, spot}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit conversion error:** Students frequently forget to convert **μΩ to Ω** in resistance welding calculations. Always write the conversion explicitly: $R = 200 \, \mu\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6} \, \Omega$.
> - **Missing efficiency:** In arc welding heat input, students often forget to multiply by $\eta$. Always check whether the question specifies "raw" or "effective" heat input.
> - **Confusing brazing vs. soldering boundary:** The 450 °C boundary is critical. Examiners frequently test this.
> - **Incorrect process names:** Writing "TIG welding" instead of "GTAW" (Gas Tungsten Arc Welding) loses a mark in formal KTU answer scripts. Use the full technical name.
> - **No diagram in long answers:** Even a hand-drawn rough sketch earns 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Types and Welding Environment**

- [ ] Welding is broadly classified into **Fusion**, **Solid-State**, and **Brazing/Soldering** families based on the state of the base metal.
- [ ] **Arc welding** uses an electric arc as the heat source; arc temperatures reach **6000–20000 °C**.
- [ ] **SMAW** uses a flux-coated consumable electrode; **GTAW (TIG)** uses a non-consumable tungsten electrode.
- [ ] **GMAW (MIG)** uses continuous solid wire + external shielding gas; **FCAW** uses continuous tubular flux-cored wire (may be self-shielded).
- [ ] **SAW** is submerged under a granular flux bed — highest deposition rate and best suited for thick plates.
- [ ] **Resistance welding** heat follows $H = I^{2}Rt$; uses **5000–12000 A** low-voltage current.
- [ ] **Solid-state welding** (FSW, FW, USW, Diffusion, EXW) joins without melting the base metal.
- [ ] **Brazing** uses filler metal melting **above 450 °C**; **Soldering** uses filler metal melting **below 450 °C**.
- [ ] **EBW** achieves power densities up to **10⁹ W/mm²** — highest in industrial welding.
- [ ] **Heat input** $E = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ is the **single most important formula** for KTU Module 2.
- [ ] **Welding environments** include atmospheric, underwater, vacuum, space, inert glove box, and nuclear hot cell.
- [ ] **Underwater welding** challenges: quenching → brittle HAZ, hydrogen cracking; uses wet SMAW or hyperbaric GMAW.
- [ ] **Space welding** challenges: microgravity, no buoyancy; uses EBW or LBW with pre-placed filler.
- [ ] **Reactive metals** (Ti, Zr, Nb) require **inert atmosphere** welding above 400 °C.
- [ ] Process transfer efficiency $\eta$: SAW (0.85–0.95) > SMAW ≈ GMAW (0.70–0.85) > GTAW (0.30–0.60).
- [ ] **Ultrasonic welding** frequency range: **20–40 kHz**.
- [ ] Always specify **units** in numerical answers (J/mm or kJ/mm for heat input).
- [ ] Provide **neat sketches** in long answer questions for 1–2 additional marks.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Module 2 Takeaway:** Master the **heat input equation** and the **classification of welding processes** — these two topics alone account for over **60% of the marks** typically asked from this module in KTU board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Used, Process Parameters, and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipment Used, Process Parameters, and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** is a solid-state joining process in which a non-consumable rotating tool, consisting of a shoulder and a profiled pin, is plunged into the joint line between two abutting workpieces. The frictional heat generated at the tool–workpiece interface plasticizes the material without reaching the melting point, and the plasticized material is transported from the leading edge to the trailing edge of the tool, creating a continuous solid-phase bond as the tool traverses along the joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** FSW is the **only commercially significant solid-state welding process** that has achieved industrial-scale production in aerospace, marine (aluminium ship decks), railway, and automotive sectors. It was invented and patented by **The Welding Institute (TWI), UK, in 1991**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine stirring a very thick, cold honey with a heated spoon. The spoon (tool) does not melt the honey; it softens it by friction. As you move the spoon along a line, the honey behind it solidifies again into one continuous mass. FSW does exactly this — but with metal. The **spoon is the rotating FSW tool**, the **honey is the plasticized metal**, and the **stirring motion is the rotation + traverse** of the tool. Because no melting occurs, the joint retains most of the original metal's strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology in FSW:**
> - **Advancing Side (AS):** Side where the tool's tangential velocity adds to the welding velocity (higher heat input).
> - **Retreating Side (RS):** Side where the tool's tangential velocity opposes the welding velocity (forms the characteristic "wormhole" or kissing-bond defect if parameters are wrong).
> - **Onion Ring:** A characteristic banded microstructure in the nugget indicating good material flow.

---

## 1.2 Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

### Formal Definition
**Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** is a fusion welding process that uses a highly coherent, monochromatic, and focused beam of photons (typically from a **CO₂, Nd:YAG, fiber, or disc laser**) to melt and fuse the joint edges. The concentrated energy density (**10⁶ – 10⁷ W/mm²**) produces a deep, narrow keyhole through vaporization of metal, enabling deep penetration welds with minimal Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two principal industrial laser types are:
> 1. **CO₂ Laser** — wavelength 10.6 μm (cannot be transmitted via fibre, requires mirrors).
> 2. **Nd:YAG / Fibre Laser** — wavelength 1.06 μm (can be transmitted via optical fibre, hence the modern dominance of fibre lasers in robotic welding cells).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine focusing sunlight with a magnifying glass onto a sheet of paper. The paper burns at a tiny spot because the light energy is concentrated. A laser does the same thing, but at **10⁷ times the intensity**, and it can pierce through several millimetres of steel. Because the energy arrives at one point, the surrounding metal stays relatively cool — like using a needle to cut butter rather than a hot knife that melts the surroundings.

---

## 1.3 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

### Formal Definition
**Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** is a fusion welding process in which a high-velocity, focused beam of electrons, accelerated through a voltage of **30–200 kV** in a **vacuum of 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa**, is directed at the joint. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into heat upon impact, vaporizing the metal and forming a deep keyhole. The high energy density (**up to 10⁸ W/mm²**) and absence of atmospheric contamination produce extremely narrow, deep welds (depth-to-width ratios > 25:1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** EBW **must be performed in a vacuum** to prevent:
> - Electron scattering by air molecules (which would defocus the beam).
> - Oxidation/nitriding of the molten metal at very high temperatures.
> - X-ray generation hazards (mitigated by chamber shielding).

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a tiny, ultra-precise cannon firing invisible "bullets" (electrons) at a metal target inside an airless chamber. When the bullets hit, their motion energy turns into intense heat at a pinpoint — so intense that the metal instantly boils away, creating a deep hole that the metal flows around to form a weld. The vacuum acts like a transparent window that allows the bullets to fly straight without being knocked off course.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Process | Energy Density (W/mm²) | State of Base Metal | Atmosphere | Typical Joint Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FSW | 10³ – 10⁴ | Solid-state | Air / Inert | Butt, Lap |
| LBW | 10⁶ – 10⁷ | Fusion | Air / Inert shield | Butt, Lap, T-fillet |
| EBW | 10⁷ – 10⁸ | Fusion | Vacuum (mandatory) | Butt, T-fillet, lap |
| Conventional Arc | 10³ – 10⁴ | Fusion | Air / Shield gas | All |

> [!NOTE]
> All energy density values are **order-of-magnitude ranges** used in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Memorize the powers of 10 — they directly drive the penetration depth, HAZ width, and distortion.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** (electron energy in EBW)
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴** (used in heat-input calculations)
- **Typical FSW tool rotation: 300 – 3000 rpm**
- **Typical FSW traverse speed: 30 – 500 mm/min**
- **Typical LBW power: 0.5 – 25 kW**
- **Typical EBW accelerating voltage: 30 – 200 kV**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Friction Stir Welding — Operational Concept Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Clamping:** The two plates are rigidly clamped onto a **backing plate (anvil)** to prevent upward displacement (flash formation) and lateral movement.
2. **Plunge Stage:** The rotating tool is forced into the joint line. Frictional heat from the **shoulder–workpiece contact** dominates (≈ 80% of total heat).
3. **Dwell Stage:** The tool is held stationary for a few seconds to reach a thermal steady state and to pre-heat the surrounding material.
4. **Traverse Stage:** The tool moves along the joint at a constant **welding speed (v)**. The pin stirs the plasticized material; the shoulder contains it and provides forging pressure.
5. **Retract Stage:** The tool is withdrawn, leaving the characteristic **exit-hole** at the end of the weld.

### The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why a shoulder?** It generates the bulk of the frictional heat (because contact area is large) AND applies downward forging pressure, preventing void formation. **Heat = μ × N × ω × r** for annular shoulder contact.
- **Why a pin with threads or flutes?** Threads/flutes promote downward material flow (called **"stirring"**), reduce the load on the machine, and break up the oxide layer on the original faying surfaces.
- **Why tilt the tool by 2–5°?** A slight forward tilt increases the forging pressure at the trailing edge and ensures the shoulder consolidates the material behind the pin. A tilt angle of **0°** produces a weak weld with surface galling; **> 5°** causes excessive flash and tool failure.

### Heat Generation in FSW

Heat is generated by two primary mechanisms:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{shoulder} + Q_{pin} + Q_{deformation}$$

The dominant shoulder contribution (for a solid shoulder of radius $R_{sh}$) is approximated as:

$$Q_{shoulder} = \int_{0}^{R_{sh}} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \mu \, \omega \, r \, \sigma_{y}(T) \, r \, d\theta \, dr$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (≈ 0.3–0.5 in FSW)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in **rad/s**
- $r$ = radial position on shoulder (m)
- $\sigma_{y}(T)$ = temperature-dependent yield stress (Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> For exam purposes, the simplified KTU-accepted heat-input formula is:
> $$\boxed{\text{HI} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot \omega \cdot R_{sh} \cdot \frac{1}{v}}$$
> where F = axial force (N) and v = traverse speed (m/s). The factor 2/3 accounts for the linear velocity distribution across the shoulder (zero at centre, max at edge).

---

## 2.2 Laser Beam Welding — Theory & Key Parameters

### Beam-Material Interaction Modes

There are two distinct welding modes in LBW:

1. **Conduction Mode:** Power density < 10⁶ W/mm². Heat diffuses into the metal. Produces a **wide, shallow, hemispherical weld pool**. Used for thin sheets, cosmetic welds, and where deep penetration is not required.
2. **Keyhole Mode:** Power density > 10⁶ W/mm². The metal vaporizes, creating a cavity (keyhole) that traps the laser beam by multiple reflections, enabling **deep penetration** (depth/width > 5:1).

### Threshold Power for Keyhole Formation

For a given material, keyhole formation begins when the absorbed intensity $I$ exceeds a critical value $I_{th}$:

$$I_{th} = \frac{\rho \, L_v \, v_d}{A_{abs}}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $v_d$ = drilling/ablation velocity (m/s)
- $A_{abs}$ = absorptivity of the metal surface (typically **0.3–0.5 for CO₂ on polished steel**, **0.6–0.8 for fibre laser on steel**)

### Heat Input in LBW

$$\boxed{\text{HI}_{LBW} = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{v}}$$

Where:
- $\eta$ = coupling efficiency (0.3 for CO₂, 0.7–0.9 for fibre)
- $P$ = laser power (W)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always multiply by the coupling efficiency. A 4 kW fibre laser at 50 mm/s does NOT deliver 80 J/mm — it delivers $0.8 \times 4000/50 = 64$ J/mm. Students who forget $\eta$ lose 1–2 marks consistently.

---

## 2.3 Electron Beam Welding — Theory & Key Parameters

### Electron Energy

The energy of an accelerated electron is given by:

$$E = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

For a typical **accelerating voltage V = 60 kV**:

$$E = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 60 \times 10^3 = 9.612 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

Converting to electron-volts gives **60,000 eV (60 keV)**. The velocity of the electron at 60 keV is approximately **0.45 c (45% the speed of light)** — relativistic corrections are small at < 100 keV.

### Penetration Depth (Schwartzold–Kindl Formula)

For depth of penetration in EBW:

$$d = k \cdot \sqrt{I \cdot V} \cdot \frac{1}{v}$$

Where:
- $I$ = beam current (mA)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (kV)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)
- $k$ = process constant (depends on material; ≈ 0.5 for steels, ≈ 0.7 for aluminium)

### Heat Input in EBW

$$\boxed{\text{HI}_{EBW} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{v}}$$

Where $\eta$ ≈ 0.7–0.85 (energy transfer efficiency). For V in volts, I in amperes, and v in mm/s, HI is in **J/mm**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Process | Heat Input Formula | Key Output Parameter | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| FSW (simplified) | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu F \omega R_{sh}}{v}$ | Peak Temperature $T_p \approx 0.8 T_m$ | J/mm |
| LBW | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta P}{v}$ | Penetration depth $d = k \sqrt{P/v}$ | J/mm |
| EBW | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | Penetration $d = k \sqrt{I \cdot V}/v$ | J/mm |
| Power Density | $I_d = P / A_{spot}$ | Must exceed $10^6$ for keyhole | W/mm² |
| FSW Pitch | $\text{Pitch} = v / \omega$ | Defines heat per revolution | mm/rev |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heat input directly controls:
> - **HAZ width** ↑ with HI
> - **Residual stress & distortion** ↑ with HI
> - **Grain size in HAZ** ↑ with HI (lower toughness)
> - **Weld bead width** ↑ with HI
>
> The KTU board examiner's standard yardstick: an "ideal" weld has **lowest possible HI that still gives full penetration and no lack-of-fusion defects**.

---

## 2.5 The Process Parameter Interaction Triangle

For FSW, three parameters dominate the response surface:

```
         ROTATIONAL SPEED (ω)
                 ▲
                 │
                 │
   TILT ANGLE ──►●◄── TRAVERSE SPEED (v)
                 │
                 │
                 ▼
         AXIAL FORCE (F)
```

- **Increase ω** → more heat, larger nugget, coarser grains, possible surface galling.
- **Increase v** → less heat per unit length, narrower nugget, possible tunnel defect.
- **Increase F** → more forging, consolidates nugget, but excessive F causes flash.
- **The dimensionless Pitch = v/ω** is the single most important ratio in FSW optimization.
  - Low pitch (≤ 0.05 mm/rev) → high heat, suitable for high-melting materials.
  - High pitch (≥ 0.2 mm/rev) → low heat, suitable for low-melting / soft alloys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in Friction Stir Welding

### Starting from First Principles

Consider a shoulder of radius $R_{sh}$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ against a workpiece with applied axial force $F$ and coefficient of friction $\mu$. The pressure distribution under the shoulder is:

$$p(r) = \frac{F}{\pi R_{sh}^2} \quad \text{(assumed uniform)}$$

### Step 1: Differential Shear Force on Annular Ring

Consider a thin annular ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ on the shoulder. The contact area is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

The normal force on this ring is:

$$dN = p(r) \cdot dA = \frac{F}{\pi R_{sh}^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{2 F r \, dr}{R_{sh}^2}$$

### Step 2: Differential Frictional Force

The friction force on this ring is:

$$dF_{fric} = \mu \cdot dN = \frac{2 \mu F r \, dr}{R_{sh}^2}$$

### Step 3: Linear Velocity at Radius r

The linear velocity at radius $r$ is:

$$v_{lin} = \omega \cdot r$$

### Step 4: Differential Power Dissipated

Power dissipated as heat on the ring is:

$$dP = dF_{fric} \cdot v_{lin} = \frac{2 \mu F r \, dr}{R_{sh}^2} \cdot \omega r = \frac{2 \mu F \omega r^2 \, dr}{R_{sh}^2}$$

### Step 5: Total Power from Shoulder

Integrate from $0$ to $R_{sh}$:

$$P_{shoulder} = \int_{0}^{R_{sh}} \frac{2 \mu F \omega r^2}{R_{sh}^2} dr = \frac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_{sh}^2} \cdot \left[\frac{r^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{R_{sh}}$$

$$P_{shoulder} = \frac{2 \mu F \omega}{R_{sh}^2} \cdot \frac{R_{sh}^3}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \mu F \omega R_{sh}$$

### Step 6: Convert Power to Heat Input per Unit Length

Since the tool traverses at welding speed $v$, the heat input per unit weld length is:

$$\text{HI}_{FSW} = \frac{P_{shoulder}}{v} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu F \omega R_{sh}}{v}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HI}_{FSW} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu F \omega R_{sh}}{v}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** This formula is the basis of every FSW process map used in industry. The Welding Institute (TWI) Process Map plots **rotation speed vs. traverse speed** and superimposes iso-HI contours derived from this expression. Aluminium alloys are welded with HI ≈ 200–600 J/mm; steels require 800–1500 J/mm.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Threshold Keyhole Power in LBW

### Step 1: Energy Required to Vaporize a Unit Mass of Metal

The total energy per unit mass to raise metal from ambient to vaporization temperature and vaporize it is:

$$E_v = c_p (T_v - T_0) + L_v$$

Where:
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $T_v$ = vaporization temperature (K)
- $T_0$ = ambient temperature (K)
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)

For **mild steel**: $c_p \approx 660$ J/kg·K, $T_v \approx 3100$ K, $T_0 \approx 300$ K, $L_v \approx 6.1 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

### Step 2: Calculate Evaporation Flux

The mass evaporation rate per unit area is:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P \cdot A_{abs}}{A_{spot} \cdot E_v} = \frac{I \cdot A_{abs}}{E_v}$$

### Step 3: Threshold Intensity

For the keyhole to be sustained, the energy delivered per unit area must exceed $E_v$ times the evaporation flux. Setting $\dot{m} = \rho v_d$:

$$I_{th} = \frac{\rho v_d E_v}{A_{abs}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **mild steel with fibre laser ($A_{abs} = 0.7$)**:
> $$I_{th} \approx \frac{7860 \times 0.05 \times 7.1 \times 10^6}{0.7} \approx 4 \times 10^{9} \text{ W/m}^2 = 4 \times 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2$$
> Conduction-mode limit is at ~$10^3$ W/mm²; keyhole requires > $10^4$ W/mm² for steel under fibre laser. This is why a 4 kW fibre laser with a 0.4 mm spot easily achieves keyhole.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effect on Weld Quality

| Process | Parameter ↑ | Effect on Penetration | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Distortion | Effect on Defects |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FSW | Rotational speed ω | ↑ (more heat) | ↑ | ↑ | Onion rings improve; flash at very high ω |
| FSW | Traverse speed v | ↓ (less heat) | ↓ | ↓ | Tunnel voids at high v |
| FSW | Axial force F | Indirect ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Flash at high F; voids at low F |
| LBW | Power P | ↑↑ (keyhole deepens) | Slight ↑ | ↑ | Undercut at high P |
| LBW | Travel speed v | ↓ (keyhole collapses) | ↓ | ↓ | Porosity at high v (keyhole instabilities) |
| LBW | Defocus distance | ↓ (penetration drops off rapidly) | ↓ | ↓ | Loss of fusion at high defocus |
| EBW | Beam current I | ↑ (penetration ↑) | Slight ↑ | ↑ | Root porosity at high I |
| EBW | Accelerating V | ↑ (penetration ↑) | Slight ↑ | ↑ | Spiking defects at high V |
| EBW | Travel speed v | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | Lack of fusion at high v |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The "↑↓" arrows are directional; the NUMBER of arrows indicates the *strength* of effect. EBW is most sensitive to beam current, FSW is most sensitive to traverse speed, and LBW is most sensitive to power. Memorize this for Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FSW Equipment Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PowerPack[AC Servo Motor Power Pack]
    Spindle[High-Speed Spindle 300-3000 rpm]
    Tool[FSW Tool Assembly]
    Shoulder[Rotating Shoulder Heat Source 80%]
    Pin[Profiled Pin Material Stirrer]
    Clamp[Rigidity Clamping Fixture]
    Anvil[Backing Plate / Anvil]
    WorkpieceA[Workpiece Plate A]
    WorkpieceB[Workpiece Plate B]
    CNC[CNC Motion Controller X-Y-Z]
    DAQ[Data Acquisition System Force Torque Temp]
    Argon[Shielding Gas Optional Argon]

    PowerPack --> Spindle
    Spindle --> Tool
    Tool --> Shoulder
    Tool --> Pin
    Pin --> WorkpieceA
    Pin --> WorkpieceB
    Shoulder --> WorkpieceA
    Shoulder --> WorkpieceB
    WorkpieceA --> Anvil
    WorkpieceB --> Anvil
    Anvil --> Clamp
    CNC --> Spindle
    CNC --> DAQ
    Argon --> WorkpieceA
    Argon --> WorkpieceB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The FSW machine has THREE main functional subsystems:
> 1. **Rotation Generation** (Motor → Spindle → Tool)
> 2. **Forcing & Containment** (Clamp + Anvil + Axial Force)
> 3. **Traverse Control** (CNC drives spindle along joint)
>
> Modern FSW machines also have **force/torque/temperature data acquisition** to enable closed-loop control.

---

## 4.2 FSW Tool Geometry (Functional Anatomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ToolBody[Tool Body Cylindrical Shank H13 Tool Steel]
    Shoulder[Shoulder Concave or Scroll Dia 12-40 mm]
    Pin[Pin Protrudes Below Shoulder 2.5-7 mm Length]
    Thread[Threaded or Fluted Pin Surface for Material Flow]
    Probe[Probe Tip Slight Conical]

    ToolBody --> Shoulder
    Shoulder --> Pin
    Pin --> Thread
    Pin --> Probe
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool Material Selection Hierarchy** (KTU 2024): For aluminium → **H13 tool steel** (sufficient); for steels → **PCBN (Polycrystalline Cubic Boron Nitride)** or **W-Re alloy**; for titanium → **W-Re or Mo alloys**. PCBN tools cost 10× more but last 100× longer in steel FSW.

---

## 4.3 Laser Beam Welding System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pump[Pumping Source Electrical or Optical]
    Resonator[Optical Resonator Cavity with Mirrors]
    BeamOut[Coherent Laser Beam Wavelength 1.06 um or 10.6 um]
    Fiber[Beam Delivery Fibre Optic or Mirror Array]
    FocusingHead[Focusing Head with Collimating F-Theta Lens]
    Spot[Focused Spot Diameter 0.1-0.6 mm on Workpiece]
    Keyhole[Keyhole Cavity Vaporized Metal]
    WP[Workpiece]
    Shield[Shielding Gas Nozzle He or Ar]
    CNC2[5/6-Axis Robot or CNC Gantry]

    Pump --> Resonator
    Resonator --> BeamOut
    BeamOut --> Fiber
    Fiber --> FocusingHead
    FocusingHead --> Spot
    Spot --> Keyhole
    Keyhole --> WP
    Shield --> WP
    CNC2 --> FocusingHead
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Block Distinction:** The **fibre laser** uses a **fibre-optic cable** between the source and the focusing head, while the **CO₂ laser** requires a **mirror-based articulated arm** because the 10.6 μm wavelength is absorbed by glass fibres. This is why fibre lasers dominate robotic welding cells — they are flexible and immune to misalignment.

---

## 4.4 Electron Beam Welding System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Cathode[Tungsten Cathode Heated Filament 2500 K]
    BiasGrid[Bias Grid for Beam Current Control]
    Anode[Anode Plate at -ve Potential 30-200 kV]
    Accel[Accelerated Electron Beam keV Energy]
    MagLens[Magnetic Focusing Lens Electromagnetic Coil]
    Deflect[Electromagnetic Deflection Coils XY Scan]
    Chamber[Vacuum Chamber 10 to Power 10 to minus 4 Pa]
    WP2[Workpiece on XY Manipulator]
    Pump2[Vacuum Pump System Diffusion + Rotary]
    View[Optical Viewing System Telescope]
    Xray[X-ray Shielding Chamber Wall]

    Cathode --> BiasGrid
    BiasGrid --> Anode
    Anode --> Accel
    Accel --> MagLens
    MagLens --> Deflect
    Deflect --> Chamber
    Chamber --> WP2
    Chamber --> Pump2
    Chamber --> View
    Chamber --> Xray
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sub-Systems for EBW:**
> 1. **Electron Gun** (Cathode + Grid + Anode) — generates and accelerates the beam.
> 2. **Magnetic Lens Column** — focuses the beam to a 0.2–0.8 mm spot.
> 3. **Vacuum System** — must reach < 10⁻² Pa for aluminium; < 10⁻¹ Pa acceptable for steel.
> 4. **Workpiece Manipulator** — typically CNC XY stage inside the chamber.
>
> Deflection coils allow the beam to be **raster-scanned** (beam oscillation) for improved keyhole stability and porosity reduction — a key research topic in KTU M.Tech projects.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Equipment Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FSW[FSW Equipment\nRotating Tool\n+ CNC Traverse]
    LBW[LBW Equipment\nLaser Source\n+ Fibre Optic\n+ Focusing Head]
    EBW[EBW Equipment\nElectron Gun\n+ Vacuum\n+ Magnetic Lens]

    FSW --- F1[Atmospheric OK]
    LBW --- L1[Atmospheric or Shield Gas]
    EBW --- E1[Vacuum Mandatory]

    FSW --- F2[Solid-State Process]
    LBW --- L2[Fusion Process]
    EBW --- E2[Fusion Process]

    FSW --- F3[Forced Material Flow]
    LBW --- L3[No Forced Flow]
    EBW --- E3[No Forced Flow]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the main components of an FSW tool. Explain the function of the shoulder and pin briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per logical point):**

1. The FSW tool consists of a **shoulder**, a **profiled pin**, and a **shank (body)** [1 mark].
2. The **shoulder** is the larger-diameter flat (or slightly concave/scroll) surface that contacts the top of the workpiece. It generates the **majority (~80%) of the frictional heat** and also provides **downward forging pressure** that consolidates the material behind the pin. It prevents the plasticized metal from being expelled upwards [1 mark].
3. The **pin** is the smaller-diameter protrusion that extends below the shoulder into the joint. It **stirs and mixes** the plasticized material, breaks up the oxide layer, and transports material from the advancing side to the retreating side. It is profiled (threaded, fluted, or with re-entrant features) to enhance material flow [1 mark].

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is EBW performed in a vacuum? State any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per reason):**

1. **Preventing electron scattering:** The accelerated electrons in EBW travel at ~0.4c and can be deflected by collision with air molecules. A vacuum (10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa) ensures the beam reaches the workpiece in a tight, well-focused spot.
2. **Avoiding contamination:** At the extreme temperatures in EBW (10,000 K in the keyhole), atmospheric O₂, N₂, and H₂ would readily dissolve into the molten metal, causing porosity, embrittlement, and oxide inclusions.
3. **Safety from X-ray generation:** The high-energy electron beam striking the metal produces soft X-rays (Bremsstrahlung). The vacuum chamber walls are lined with lead, and operation must be inside the chamber to protect the operator from radiation.
4. **Preventing plasma formation:** At atmospheric pressure, the high-density electron beam ionizes air and creates a plasma cloud above the workpiece that scatters the beam ("plasma blow-out" effect), reducing penetration.

*(Any 3 of these 4 reasons = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the FSW process. Discuss the role of tool rotation speed and traverse speed on weld quality. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 6 mm thick AA6061-T6 aluminium plate is being FSW-welded. The tool shoulder diameter is 18 mm, coefficient of friction is 0.4, axial force is 8 kN, and the tool rotates at 800 rpm while traversing at 100 mm/min. Calculate the heat input per unit length of weld. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Schematic (3 marks):**
- Draw the two workpieces abutting on a backing plate [1 mark].
- Show the FSW tool with shoulder and pin penetrating the joint [1 mark].
- Indicate rotation arrow and traverse arrow, and label Advancing Side (AS) and Retreating Side (RS) [1 mark].

**Role of Rotation Speed ω (2 marks):**
- Higher ω increases frictional heat generation $Q \propto \omega$, which lowers the flow stress and improves material mixing.
- However, excessive ω causes surface galling, flash formation, and grain coarsening in the nugget.
- Optimal ω for AA6061 is typically 600–1200 rpm.

**Role of Traverse Speed v (2 marks):**
- Higher v means less time per unit length → lower heat input per mm → narrower nugget and HAZ.
- Too high v → "tunnel defect" (wormhole) on the retreating side due to insufficient material flow.
- Optimal v for 6 mm AA6061 is typically 50–150 mm/min.
- The **pitch ratio v/ω** must be in the range 0.05–0.2 mm/rev for defect-free welds.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $R_{sh} = 9$ mm = $9 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\mu = 0.4$
- $F = 8000$ N
- $\omega = 800 \text{ rpm} = 800 \times 2\pi / 60 = 83.78 \text{ rad/s}$
- $v = 100$ mm/min = $100/60 = 1.667 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

**Step 1:** Apply the FSW heat input formula:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu F \omega R_{sh}}{v}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{0.4 \times 8000 \times 83.78 \times 0.009}{1.667 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$0.4 \times 8000 = 3200$$
$$3200 \times 83.78 = 268096$$
$$268096 \times 0.009 = 2412.86$$
$$2\pi/3 \times 2412.86 = 5052.0$$

**Step 4:** Divide by velocity:

$$\text{HI} = \frac{5052.0}{1.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.03 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to J/mm:

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = 3.03 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/mm} = 3030 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $\omega = 800$ (treating rpm as rad/s) and get an answer 1/80 of the correct value. Always convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. This is the #1 reason for lost marks in FSW calculations.
>
> **Second Common Mistake:** Forgetting to include the $2\pi/3$ factor, which accounts for the linear velocity profile across the shoulder. If omitted, answer is 33% too low.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a block diagram, describe the equipment used in Laser Beam Welding. Compare the fibre laser and CO₂ laser based on wavelength, beam delivery, and coupling efficiency with steel. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 4 kW fibre laser ($\eta = 0.85$) is used to weld 3 mm thick stainless steel (AISI 304) at a travel speed of 30 mm/s. Calculate the heat input per unit length and the absorbed power intensity if the spot diameter is 0.4 mm. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Draw and label: laser source → beam delivery → focusing head → shielding gas nozzle → workpiece on CNC/robot. Briefly explain each block.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | CO₂ Laser | Fibre Laser |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | **10.6 μm** (far IR) | **1.06 μm** (near IR) |
| Beam Delivery | Mirror-based articulated arm (cannot use fibre) | **Optical fibre** (flexible, up to 100 m) |
| Coupling with steel | Low (0.3–0.4) due to high reflectivity at 10.6 μm | **High (0.7–0.8)** at 1.06 μm |
| Beam Quality (BPP) | 5–10 mm·mrad | **0.5–2 mm·mrad** (better focus) |
| Electrical Efficiency | 10–15% | **35–45%** |
| Maintenance | Mirror alignment critical | Maintenance-free fibre |
| Cost per kW | Lower initial | Higher initial, lower lifecycle |

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:**
- $P = 4000$ W
- $\eta = 0.85$
- $v = 30$ mm/s
- Spot diameter $d = 0.4$ mm

**Step 1: Heat Input per unit length**

$$\text{HI} = \frac{\eta P}{v} = \frac{0.85 \times 4000}{30} = \frac{3400}{30} = 113.33 \text{ J/mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HI} = 113.33 \text{ J/mm}}$$

**Step 2: Spot area**

$$A_{spot} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.4)^2}{4} = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Power Density (Intensity)**

$$I_d = \frac{P}{A_{spot}} = \frac{4000}{0.1257} = 31831 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_d \approx 3.18 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2}$$

**Step 4: Verify Keyhole Mode**
Since $I_d = 3.18 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 > 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$ (the threshold for keyhole in steel with fibre laser), the process is in **keyhole mode**, ensuring deep penetration. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion:** $A_{spot}$ in mm² and P in W gives intensity in W/mm² directly. Do NOT convert to m² unless you also convert W. (Many students do: P/A in W/m² and then forget to convert — answer becomes $10^6$ times wrong.)
> 2. **Coupling Efficiency:** Some students multiply HI by η and intensity by η, others vice versa. The standard KTU convention is: **HI = η·P/v (coupling is "absorbed" energy), but intensity = P/A (incident power at the surface).** Coupling efficiency is applied to the *energy absorbed* for HI calculations, NOT to the geometric intensity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2**
>
> **FSW:**
> - Solid-state process; invented by TWI (1991).
> - Tool = Shoulder (80% heat) + Pin (stirring) + Shank.
> - Tool material: H13 (Al), PCBN (Steel), W-Re (Ti).
> - Key parameters: ω, v, F, tilt angle (2–5°).
> - Pitch = v/ω in mm/rev (0.05–0.2 typical).
> - AS = advancing side, RS = retreating side (defect-prone).
> - HI formula: $HI = \frac{2\pi}{3} \cdot \frac{\mu F \omega R_{sh}}{v}$.
> - Always convert ω from rpm to rad/s: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
>
> **LBW:**
> - Two modes: Conduction (wide, shallow) vs Keyhole (deep, narrow).
> - Threshold for keyhole in steel: ~$10^4$ W/mm² with fibre laser.
> - CO₂ (10.6 μm) requires mirrors; Fibre (1.06 μm) uses optical fibre.
> - HI = ηP/v; coupling η for fibre on steel ≈ 0.7–0.85.
> - Beam quality: BPP (mm·mrad) — lower is better.
> - Defects: porosity, undercut, spiking, lack of fusion.
>
> **EBW:**
> - Mandatory vacuum (10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ Pa).
> - Energy density up to $10^8$ W/mm².
> - Accelerating voltage 30–200 kV; beam current 10–500 mA.
> - HI = ηVI/v; Penetration $d = k\sqrt{IV}/v$.
> - Defects: root porosity, spiking, cracking (in carbon steels).
> - No shielding gas needed; chamber itself is the protective environment.
>
> **Comparative Hierarchy (memorize):**
> - **Power density:** EBW > LBW > FSW.
> - **Penetration:** EBW > LBW > FSW.
> - **HAZ width:** FSW (smallest, no melting) < LBW < EBW.
> - **Distortion:** FSW (lowest) < EBW < LBW.
> - **Equipment cost:** EBW (highest) > LBW > FSW.
> - **Atmosphere requirement:** EBW (vacuum) > FSW/LBW (air or shield).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld Joint Design and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weld Joint Design and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Weld Joint Design and Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Weld Joint Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the type, size, geometry, and location of welds in a fabricated structure to safely transmit applied loads while satisfying strength, economy, and serviceability requirements under static, dynamic, and environmental conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, weld joint design is treated as an **applied mechanics problem** where the welded joint becomes a critical load-transferring element, and the design must be validated against **allowable stresses** prescribed by codes such as **IS 800:2007**, **AWS D1.1**, and **IS 816:1969**.

### Fundamental Weld Joint Types (AWS / IS Classification)

| Joint Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Butt Joint** | Two plates placed edge-to-edge in the same plane | Pressure vessels, pipelines |
| **Lap Joint** | Two plates overlapping each other | Sheet metal structures |
| **Tee Joint** | Two plates meeting at 90° forming a T | Beam-to-column connections |
| **Corner Joint** | Two plates meeting at right angle at edge | Box sections, frames |
| **Edge Joint** | Two plates placed edge-to-edge, parallel | Sheet metal flooring |

### Fundamental Weld Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fillet Welds** and **Groove Welds** are the two principal weld types used in structural design. Approximately **70% of all structural welds** are fillet welds, making them the most economically significant category.

| Weld Type | Geometry | Primary Loading |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Fillet Weld** | Triangular cross-section (typically 45°) | Shear |
| **Square Groove Butt** | Plates abutted with no edge preparation | Axial tension/compression |
| **Single-V Groove Butt** | One side beveled (typically 30°) | Heavy plates (>6 mm) |
| **Double-V Groove Butt** | Both sides beveled | Very thick plates (>20 mm) |
| **Plug/Slot Weld** | Filled hole or slot | Stiffener attachment |
| **Spot/Projection Weld** | Resistance welding nugget | Sheet metal assemblies |

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy – The Handshake:** Imagine a bolted connection as a "handshake" — discrete contact points transmit load. Now imagine a **welded joint** as a "sealed embrace" — the load is distributed continuously along the entire joint line, just like two hands gripping firmly along their full length. **Weld joint design** is the engineering art of ensuring this "embrace" is strong enough, in the right direction, and at the right place to handle the forces applied. A poorly designed weld is like a weak handshake — it fails at the weakest point under load.

### Key Geometric Parameters

- **Leg Size (s):** The shortest distance from the weld root to the weld face (the two catheti of the triangular cross-section).
- **Throat Thickness (t):** The perpendicular distance from the weld root to the hypotenuse. For an equal-leg 45° fillet: **t = 0.707 × s**.
- **Weld Length (L):** The effective length over which the weld transmits load.
- **Effective Throat Area (A):** A = t × L = 0.707 × s × L.
- **Convexity / Concavity:** Deviation of the weld face from the ideal triangular profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Weld Sizes (IS 800:2007):** Minimum leg size depends on the thickness of the thicker plate. For plates up to 10 mm thick, minimum fillet leg size = **3 mm**; for 20–32 mm, minimum = **6 mm**; for thicker plates, minimum = **8 mm**. The maximum leg size is limited to **1.5 mm less than the edge thickness** for plates along the edge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stresses in Welded Joints

A fillet weld is treated as a line of **uniform strength**, and the load is assumed to be resisted by **shear on the throat area**. This is a simplification that aligns with experimental evidence and is universally adopted by **IS 800:2007** and **AWS D1.1**.

### Failure Modes in Fillet Welds

> [!WARNING]
> **The Throat is the Critical Plane:** A fillet weld ALWAYS fails through the **throat**, not the leg. The throat is the smallest cross-section and lies on the plane of maximum shear stress (45° to both the leg and the weld axis). This is the foundation of all fillet weld design equations.

1. **Throat Shear Failure** — most common, the design basis.
2. **Fusion Zone Failure** — at the weld-parent metal interface (controlled by weld procedure).
3. **Parent Metal Shear / Tear-out** — when weld is too near the edge (controlled by edge distance).
4. **Base Metal Failure** — in the parent material (controlled by plate strength).

### Types of Loading on Fillet Welds

| Loading Type | Description | Critical Stress |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Parallel / Longitudinal** | Load parallel to weld axis | Pure shear (τ ∥) |
| **Transverse** | Load perpendicular to weld axis | Combined (higher strength) |
| **Bending** | Moment about weld group centroid | Linear shear distribution |
| **Torsional** | Moment about weld group centroid (in-plane) | Shear + torsion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Fact:** A transverse fillet weld is approximately **30% stronger** than a parallel fillet weld of the same size, because the transverse weld develops a component of normal stress on the throat that contributes to the load capacity. The simplified IS 800:2007 design treats both as throat shear, but applies a **higher allowable stress for shop welds** (typically **150 N/mm²** for E41 electrodes, **165 N/mm²** for E43 electrodes at shop) and a lower value for field welds.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $t = 0.707 \cdot s$ | Throat thickness from leg size | mm |
| 2 | $A_{throat} = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L$ | Effective throat area | mm² |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}$ | Average throat shear stress (parallel loading) | N/mm² |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot \tau_{allow}}$ | Required weld length for given load | mm |
| 5 | $s = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}}$ | Required leg size for given load | mm |
| 6 | $\tau_{\perp} = \dfrac{P_{\perp}}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}$ | Stress due to perpendicular force | N/mm² |
| 7 | $\tau_{\parallel} = \dfrac{P_{\parallel}}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}$ | Stress due to parallel force | N/mm² |
| 8 | $\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{\tau_{\perp}^{2} + \tau_{\parallel}^{2}}$ | Combined stress (vector sum) | N/mm² |
| 9 | $I_{x} = \sum_{i} (I_{xi} + A_i \cdot y_i^2)$ | Moment of inertia of weld group (parallel axis theorem) | mm⁴ |
| 10 | $I_{p} = I_{x} + I_{y}$ | Polar moment of inertia (for torsion) | mm⁴ |
| 11 | $\tau_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending shear stress in weld | N/mm² |
| 12 | $\tau_{T} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{I_{p}}$ | Torsional shear stress in weld | N/mm² |
| 13 | $q = \dfrac{P}{L} \pm \dfrac{M \cdot \bar{y}}{I}$ | Linear shear stress (direct + bending) per unit length | N/mm |
| 14 | $f_{wd} = f_{y} / (\gamma_{mw} \cdot \sqrt{3})$ | Design strength of fillet weld (LRFD) | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Minimum effective weld length ≥ **4 × leg size** (else size governs)
> - Effective length = **Actual length − 2 × leg size** (for end returns/undercuts)
> - Maximum effective length in lap joints ≤ **40 × throat thickness** (to avoid uneven stress distribution)
> - For end-loaded fillet welds (transverse), **end returns** of length ≥ **2s** must be provided to prevent cracking.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Weld joint design is foundational to:
- **Structural steel buildings** (moment connections, beam-column joints)
- **Pressure vessel and boiler fabrication** (ASME Sec. VIII)
- **Bridges and offshore platforms** (cyclic and dynamic loading)
- **Automotive chassis** (resistance spot welds, MIG/MAG robotic welds)
- **Aerospace structures** (FSW of aluminum alloys, laser welding of titanium)
- **Shipbuilding** (submerged arc welding of thick plates)
- **Pipeline construction** (API 1104, circumferential girth welds)

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Examinations:** Always state the code reference (IS 800 / IS 816) when quoting allowable stresses. Examiners award **2 marks** just for the correct clause and stress value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throat Thickness of a 45° Fillet Weld

The fillet weld cross-section is an **isosceles right triangle** with both legs equal to the leg size **s**, and the hypotenuse inclined at 45° to each leg.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

Consider the right triangle with legs along the vertical and horizontal faces of the two joined plates, and the hypotenuse forming the weld face.

$$
\text{Legs} = s, \quad \text{Hypotenuse} = s\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Throat**

The throat is the perpendicular from the root (the right-angle vertex) to the hypotenuse.

$$
\text{For an isosceles right triangle: } t = \frac{s \cdot s}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the Denominator**

$$
t = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707 \cdot s
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t = 0.707 \cdot s\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion factor 0.707 = sin(45°) = cos(45°) = 1/√2.** This is the most-tested number in weld design problems. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shear Stress in a Fillet Weld Carrying a Direct Load

**Step 1 — Consider a Fillet Weld of Leg Size s and Length L**

The throat area resisting the applied shear load P is:

$$
A_{throat} = t \cdot L = 0.707 \cdot s \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Basic Stress Equation**

Average shear stress is force divided by the area resisting it:

$$
\tau = \frac{P}{A_{throat}} = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Required Leg Size**

Rearranging with the allowable shear stress τ_allow:

$$
s = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot L \cdot \tau_{allow}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Insight — Why Throat, Not Leg?** If the weld were checked on the leg, the area would be s × L, giving a higher calculated stress. The throat is the **weakest plane** (smallest area, maximum shear). Designers always use throat area as a **conservative** design basis.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stress Components in Combined Loading

When a fillet weld is subjected to combined forces, the resultant shear stress at any point is the **vector sum** of all components.

### Case A: Direct Shear + Bending (Tee Joint with Eccentric Load)

**Setup:** A bracket welded to a column with vertical load P at eccentricity e from the weld line.

**Step 1 — Direct Shear Component**

$$
q_{direct} = \frac{P}{L} \quad \text{(shear force per unit length, N/mm)}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Component**

Moment M = P × e. The bending stress at distance y from the centroidal axis:

$$
q_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant Shear per Unit Length**

$$
q_{resultant} = \frac{P}{L} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Throat Shear Stress**

$$
\tau = \frac{q_{resultant}}{0.707 \cdot s}
$$

### Case B: Pure Torsion (C-Shaped Weld Under In-Plane Moment)

**Step 1 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia of Weld Group**

For a weld line, treat each segment as a line of length L_i at distance r_i from the centroid:

$$
I_{p} = \sum_{i} L_i \cdot r_i^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{T} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{I_{p}}
$$

**Step 3 — For Thin Lines (Line Weld Approximation)**

The unit shear force per unit length at any point on the weld:

$$
q = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_{p}}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Stress at the Farthest Point**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{max}}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot I_{p}}
$$

### Case C: Combined Direct + Torsion

For a C-shaped weld carrying a vertical load P at distance e from the centroid:

**Step 1 — Direct Shear (Uniform Distribution)**

$$
q_{v} = \frac{P}{L_{total}} \quad \text{(downward, assumed constant along weld)}
$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Shear (at right angles to radius vector)**

$$
q_{T} = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_{p}} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot r}{I_{p}}
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant**

$$
q_{R} = \sqrt{q_{v}^{2} + q_{T}^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Leg Size Required**

$$
s = \frac{q_{R, max}}{0.707 \cdot \tau_{allow}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Bracket Welded to Column

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Problem — Practice This Type**

**Given:** A bracket carrying a vertical load P = **80 kN** is welded to a column using two vertical fillet welds of length **150 mm each**, spaced **200 mm apart** (center-to-center). The load is applied at a distance of **250 mm** from the face of the column. Allowable shear stress = **110 N/mm²**. Find the required leg size.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Weld Configuration**

- Total weld length: L = 2 × 150 = **300 mm**
- Weld group treated as two parallel line welds
- Distance from centroid of weld group to each weld: c = 100 mm
- Direct shear: P = 80,000 N

**Step 2 — Calculate Direct Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{d} = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L} = \frac{80{,}000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 300}
$$

$$
\tau_{d} = \frac{377.3}{s} \quad \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Moment of Inertia of Weld Group**

For two parallel line welds:

$$
I = 2 \cdot L_i \cdot c^{2} = 2 \cdot 150 \cdot (100)^{2} = 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^{3}\text{-equivalent}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Bending Moment**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 80{,}000 \cdot 250 = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Bending Shear Stress**

The bending shear at the extreme weld:

$$
\tau_{b} = \frac{M \cdot c}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot I} = \frac{20{,}000{,}000 \cdot 100}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 3{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
\tau_{b} = \frac{943.1}{s} \quad \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Total Shear Stress (Algebraic Sum)**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{377.3}{s} + \frac{943.1}{s} = \frac{1320.4}{s} \quad \text{N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Equate to Allowable Stress and Solve**

$$
\frac{1320.4}{s} = 110 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{1320.4}{110} = 12.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Adopt Standard Leg Size**

Provide **s = 12 mm** fillet weld (standard sizes: 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm).

$$
\boxed{\,s_{adopted} = 12 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Verification of Minimum Length Criterion**

Minimum length = 4 × s = 4 × 12 = 48 mm. Our L = 150 mm > 48 mm ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
