# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between a fillet weld and a butt weld. State the design strength values as per IS 800:2007 for fillet welds using E41 and E43 electrodes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fillet Weld | Butt Weld |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| Cross-section | Triangular (≈45°) | Square / V / U groove |
| Preparation | Not required (lap or T) | Edge preparation needed |
| Load transfer | Shear on throat | Tension / compression on plate |
| Strength | Lower | Higher (full strength) |
| Cost | Economical | Costlier |

As per IS 800:2007, the design strength of a fillet weld (per unit throat area) is:

$$
f_{wd} = \frac{f_u}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \gamma_{mw}}
$$

For **E41 electrodes (f_u = 410 N/mm², γ_mw = 1.25 for shop)**: f_wd ≈ **189 N/mm²**  
For **E43 electrodes (f_u = 430 N/mm², γ_mw = 1.25 for shop)**: f_wd ≈ **198 N/mm²**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by effective throat thickness? Derive the relationship between throat thickness and leg size for a 45° equal-leg fillet weld.**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective throat thickness (t_e)** is the perpendicular distance from the weld root to the weld face — it is the minimum cross-sectional dimension of the weld and represents the plane along which the weld is assumed to fail in shear.

For a 45° equal-leg fillet weld:

$$
t_e = 0.707 \cdot s
$$

**Derivation:**

The fillet weld cross-section is an isosceles right triangle with legs s and hypotenuse s√2. The throat (altitude to the hypotenuse) is:

$$
t_e = \frac{\text{Area of triangle} \times 2}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{(s^2/2) \times 2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \, s
$$

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) A bracket carrying a vertical load of 120 kN is welded to a column flange using two vertical fillet welds of length 180 mm each, spaced 250 mm apart. The load is applied at an eccentricity of 300 mm from the face of the column. Determine the required leg size of the fillet weld if the allowable shear stress is 110 N/mm². (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Data Given**

P = 120,000 N; L = 2 × 180 = 360 mm; spacing = 250 mm → c = 125 mm;  
e = 300 mm; τ_allow = 110 N/mm²; s = ?

**Step 2 — Direct Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{d} = \frac{P}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot L} = \frac{120{,}000}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 360} = \frac{471.3}{s} \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia of Weld Group**

$$
I = 2 \cdot L_i \cdot c^{2} = 2 \cdot 180 \cdot (125)^{2} = 5{,}625{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{-equivalent}
$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment**

$$
M = P \cdot e = 120{,}000 \cdot 300 = 36 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Bending Shear Stress at Extreme Weld**

$$
\tau_{b} = \frac{M \cdot c}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot I} = \frac{36 \times 10^{6} \cdot 125}{0.707 \cdot s \cdot 5{,}625{,}000} = \frac{1131.6}{s} \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Total Maximum Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{max} = \tau_{d} + \tau_{b} = \frac{471.3}{s} + \frac{1131.6}{s} = \frac{1602.9}{s} \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for s**

$$
s = \frac{1602.9}{110} = 14.57 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Adopt Standard Size**

Provide s = **16 mm** (next higher standard size).

$$
\boxed{\,s_{adopted} = 16 \text{ mm}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Check Minimum Length Criterion**

4s = 4 × 16 = 64 mm < 180 mm ✓ (satisfactory)

---

**(b) Explain the various types of welded joints with neat sketches. State the advantages and limitations of welded joints. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Welded Joints (with Applications):**

1. **Butt Joint** — Plates in the same plane joined at edges. Used in pressure vessels, pipelines, and plate girders. Edge preparation (V, U, J) is required for thickness > 6 mm.

2. **Lap Joint** — One plate overlaps another. Used in sheet metal work, bracing members. Simple and economical; subject to eccentric loading.

3. **Tee Joint** — Two plates meet at 90° forming a T-shape. Used in beam-column connections, stiffener attachment. Single or double fillet welds on both sides.

4. **Corner Joint** — Two plates meet at a right angle along their edges. Used in box sections, frames, tanks.

5. **Edge Joint** — Plates placed edge-to-edge with welds on the edges. Used in light-gauge sheet metal flooring.

**Advantages of Welded Joints:**
- High strength-to-weight ratio
- Permanent, leak-proof joints
- Flexibility in design (any shape/size)
- Smooth profile, no protrusions
- Economical for mass production
- Continuous load transfer, no stress concentrations at bolt holes
- Can join dissimilar metals (with compatible filler)

**Limitations of Welded Joints:**
- Requires skilled labor and quality control
- Heat-affected zone (HAZ) may cause metallurgical changes
- Residual stresses and distortion
- Difficult to inspect (NDT required)
- Brittle failure possible at low temperatures
- Difficult to dismantle for repair

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) A C-shaped fillet weld is used to attach a bracket to a column. The weld consists of three sides of a rectangle: two vertical welds of 150 mm length each at the sides, and one horizontal weld of 200 mm length at the bottom. The bracket carries a vertical load of 60 kN at a horizontal eccentricity of 100 mm from the centroid of the weld group. Calculate the maximum shear stress in the weld if the leg size is 8 mm. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Weld Geometry and Centroid**

Taking the bottom-left corner as origin, the three weld segments are:
- Bottom horizontal: y = 0, length 200 mm
- Left vertical: y from 0 to 150 mm, length 150 mm
- Right vertical: y from 0 to 150 mm, length 150 mm

Because of symmetry, the centroid lies on the y-axis at:
$$
\bar{y} = \frac{200 \cdot 0 + 150 \cdot 75 + 150 \cdot 75}{200 + 150 + 150} = \frac{22{,}500}{500} = 45 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Weld Length and Polar Moment of Inertia**

L_total = 200 + 150 + 150 = **500 mm**

Compute I_x (about centroidal axis):

For the bottom weld (distance = 45 mm from centroid):
$$
I_{x1} = L_1 \cdot d_1^2 = 200 \cdot (45)^2 = 405{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

For each vertical weld (use parallel axis theorem, with d = 30 mm from centroid to its own centroid):
$$
I_{x2} = L_2 \cdot \bar{d}^2 + \frac{L_2^3}{12} = 150 \cdot (30)^2 + \frac{150^3}{12} = 135{,}000 + 281{,}250 = 416{,}250 \text{ mm}^3
$$

For both verticals: I_x2_total = 2 × 416,250 = 832,500 mm³

$$
I_x = 405{,}000 + 832{,}500 = 1{,}237{,}500 \text{ mm}^3
$$

For I_y, all welds lie at the same x-coordinate (zero, by symmetry):
$$
I_y = 0
$$

So I_p = I_x = 1,237,500 mm³

**Step 3 — Torsional Moment**

$$
T = P \cdot e = 60{,}000 \cdot 100 = 6 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Direct Vertical Shear per Unit Length**

$$
q_{v} = \frac{P}{L_{total}} = \frac{60{,}000}{500} = 120 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Critical Point**

The critical point is the top corner of either vertical weld. At this point:
- r = distance from centroid = √(45² + 0²) ≈ vertical offset of top of weld
  - Top of left/right vertical weld is at y = 150 mm; distance from centroid = 150 − 45 = 105 mm
- Both direct shear (downward) and torsional shear (perpendicular to radius) act at this point.

**Step 6 — Torsional Shear at Critical Point**

$$
q_{T} = \frac{T \cdot r}{I_p} = \frac{6 \times 10^{6} \cdot 105}{1{,}237{,}500} = 509.1 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Resultant Shear Force per Unit Length**

At the top of the vertical weld, q_v acts vertically downward and q_T acts horizontally (perpendicular to radius, which is vertical, so q_T is horizontal).

$$
q_{R} = \sqrt{q_{v}^{2} + q_{T}^{2}} = \sqrt{120^2 + 509.1^2} = \sqrt{14{,}400 + 259{,}183} = \sqrt{273{,}583} \approx 523.1 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Throat Shear Stress**

$$
\tau = \frac{q_R}{0.707 \cdot s} = \frac{523.1}{0.707 \cdot 8} = \frac{523.1}{5.656} = 92.5 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{max} = 92.5 \text{ N/mm}^2\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Verification**

Compare with allowable (say 110 N/mm²): 92.5 < 110 ✓ **Safe design**.

---

**(b) Discuss the design considerations for welds subjected to dynamic and fatigue loading. Mention the IS 800:2007 recommendations for fatigue design of welded joints. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Design Considerations for Dynamic / Fatigue Loading:**

1. **Stress Concentration:** Welds have inherent stress concentrations at the toe (weld-to-parent metal interface). Grinding the weld toe smooth (butt welds) can improve fatigue life by 50–100%.

2. **Weld Profile:** Convex welds have higher stress concentration than flat or concave welds. As per IS 800:2007, convexity should be limited.

3. **Weld Defects:** Undercuts, porosity, slag inclusions, lack of fusion, and lack of penetration act as crack initiation sites. All defects must be minimized and inspected by NDT (UT, RT, MPI).

4. **Residual Stresses:** Welding induces tensile residual stresses at the weld toe, which accelerate fatigue crack initiation. Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) or peening can mitigate this.

5. **Residual Stresses + Tension:** The combination of tensile residual stress and applied tensile stress means the effective mean stress is high, reducing fatigue life.

6. **Weld Type and Orientation:** Transverse welds have lower fatigue strength than longitudinal welds. Butt welds with full penetration and ground flush have the best fatigue performance.

**IS 800:2007 Recommendations:**
- Fatigue design uses **stress range** (not peak stress) as per Clause 13.
- Fatigue strength classification into classes (F-class: F1, F2, F3, F4) based on detail type.
- S-N curves (Wöhler curves) provided for each class.
- Partial safety factor γ_mf = 1.0 for fatigue (since stress range controls).
- High-cycle (≥ 10⁵ cycles) and low-cycle regimes considered separately.
- For welded connections, the **fatigue stress range** must be ≤ **f_ft** at the relevant number of cycles.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Weld Design Problems (KTU Board Papers):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.707 factor** — students often use s × L (leg area) instead of 0.707 × s × L (throat area). This **overestimates capacity** by 41% — design is unsafe, full marks lost.
> 2. **Not using parallel axis theorem** — for weld groups with eccentric loading, simply dividing moment by total length gives a wrong answer. Always compute I = Σ L_i × d_i².
> 3. **Mixing parallel and transverse welds** — for combined loading with both directions, use vector sum (Pythagoras), not algebraic sum.
> 4. **Ignoring minimum weld length rule (4s)** — if computed length is < 4s, the size governs and design is invalid.
> 5. **Not adopting standard leg size** — always round UP to next standard size: 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 22, 25 mm.
> 6. **Missing units** — N vs kN, mm vs cm, N/mm² vs MPa (same, but write it clearly).
> 7. **Not specifying code reference** — IS 800:2007 / IS 816:1969 / AWS D1.1 must be cited for any allowable stress.
> 8. **Drawing without labeling throat, leg, root** — sketches without labels lose **2 marks** in 14-mark problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Weld Joint Design (Module 2)**

- **Five Joint Types:** Butt, Lap, Tee, Corner, Edge — know geometry and typical weld type for each.
- **Weld Types:** Fillet (triangular, throat = 0.707s), Groove (square, V, U, J), Plug/Slot, Spot/Seam.
- **Throat Thickness Formula:** t = 0.707 × s (45° equal leg) — memorize.
- **Throat Area:** A = 0.707 × s × L — basis of all strength calculations.
- **Failure Plane:** Always the throat (smallest area, max shear).
- **Direct Shear Stress:** τ = P / (0.707 × s × L).
- **Combined Loading:** Use Pythagorean sum √(τ_perp² + τ_parallel²).
- **Eccentric Loading (Bending + Direct):** Use I = Σ L_i d_i² (parallel axis theorem) and τ_max = M·c / (0.707·s·I) ± P / (0.707·s·L).
- **Torsion:** I_p = I_x + I_y; τ = T·r / (0.707·s·I_p).
- **Minimum Effective Length:** ≥ 4 × leg size (else size governs).
- **Maximum Effective Length in Lap:** ≤ 40 × throat thickness.
- **End Returns:** For end-loaded fillet welds, return length ≥ 2s.
- **Standard Leg Sizes (mm):** 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 18, 20, 22, 25.
- **Allowable Shear Stress (IS 800:2007):** ≈ 110–150 N/mm² (shop) and 100–130 N/mm² (field) depending on electrode and plate grade.
- **Transverse vs Parallel:** Transverse fillet weld is ~30% stronger (full throat + normal stress component) — IS 800 simplifies this.
- **AWS Symbols:** Arrow line + reference line + fillet triangle + leg size + length + pitch (for intermittent welds).
- **Fatigue Concerns:** Weld toe is the crack initiation site; grind smooth, use full-penetration butt welds where possible.
- **NDT for Welds:** Visual, DPT, MPI, UT, RT — required for critical joints (pressure vessels, bridges).
- **Common Industrial Applications:** Structural steel buildings, pressure vessels, bridges, ship hulls, automotive chassis, pipelines, aerospace.
- **Code References to Quote:** IS 800:2007, IS 816:1969, AWS D1.1, ASME Sec. VIII, API 1104.
- **Design Philosophy:** Strength + Economy + Serviceability + Manufacturability + Inspectability.
- **Dimensional Discipline:** Always work in N, mm, N/mm² (MPa). Show substitutions explicitly.
- **Free-Body Diagrams:** Always draw the FBD showing the weld as a line, with forces, moment, and centroid marked.
- **Vector Diagram for Torsion:** Show q_direct, q_torsion, and q_resultant as vectors at the critical point.
- **Final Adopted Size:** Always round UP to the next standard leg size.
- **Verify with Code Checks:** After sizing, verify minimum length, end returns, and parent metal capacity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Welding (EBW): Principle and Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Welding (EBW): Principle and Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** is an advanced **non-conventional fusion welding process** in which a **highly focused, high-velocity beam of electrons** is accelerated onto the workpiece under **high vacuum conditions**. The **kinetic energy** of these accelerated electrons is converted into **intense thermal energy** upon impact with the joint interface, causing localized melting and fusion of the parent metal to form a metallurgical bond.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET416)**, EBW is classified as a **High Energy Density (HED) welding process** with **power densities typically ranging from 10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²**, making it one of the most concentrated energy sources available in modern manufacturing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Microscopic Cannon"

Imagine firing millions of tiny **invisible bullets (electrons)** from a powerful **cannon (electron gun)** at a metal target inside a sealed, air-free chamber. Each bullet strikes with such tremendous speed that the impact instantly converts motion energy into intense heat, melting the metal at a tiny, precise spot. Because the chamber has no air, the bullets travel in a perfectly straight line (no air resistance) and the metal doesn't get contaminated with oxygen or nitrogen.

This is exactly how EBW works — except the "cannon" is an **electron gun**, the "bullets" are **electrons** accelerated by a voltage of **30 kV to 200 kV**, and the "air-free chamber" is a **vacuum chamber (10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ torr)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Accelerating Voltage | **30 – 200 kV** |
| Beam Current | **few mA – 500 mA** |
| Vacuum Level | **10⁻² to 10⁻⁶ torr** |
| Power Density | **10⁶ – 10⁹ W/mm²** |
| Welding Speed | **25 – 2500 mm/min** |
| Depth-to-Width Ratio | **up to 30 : 1** |
| Process Efficiency | **70 – 80%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** EBW belongs to the family of *radiant energy welding processes* and is grouped alongside **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** due to similar high-power-density characteristics. However, EBW uses **electron kinetic energy**, while LBW uses **photon energy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Electron Beam Generation

The principle of EBW is rooted in **three fundamental physical phenomena**:

### Step 1 — Thermionic Emission
A **tungsten filament cathode** is heated to temperatures of **~2500 °C** (typically by resistance heating). At this temperature, the thermal energy of electrons in the filament exceeds the **work function (φ)** of tungsten (~4.5 eV), allowing electrons to be emitted from the surface.

$$J = A_R \, T^2 \, e^{-\phi / k_B T}$$

where:
- $J$ = emission current density (A/m²)
- $A_R$ = Richardson's constant ($\approx 60 \times 10^4 \text{ A/m}^2\text{K}^2$ for tungsten)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\phi$ = work function (eV)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant

### Step 2 — Acceleration Under Electric Field
The emitted electrons are accelerated toward a positively charged **anode** by a potential difference $V$ (typically 30 – 200 kV). The electrical potential energy $eV$ is converted into **kinetic energy** $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.

$$eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At accelerating voltages above **~100 kV**, electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$). For V = 150 kV, $v \approx 0.7c$, and **relativistic corrections** become necessary.

### Step 3 — Magnetic Focusing
**Magnetic lenses** (electromagnetic coils) act like optical lenses for light — they focus the divergent electron beam to a tiny spot of **0.25 – 1.0 mm diameter** at the workpiece surface. The focal length is controlled by adjusting the **lens current**.

### Step 4 — Kinetic Energy → Heat Conversion
When the focused electrons strike the workpiece, their kinetic energy is suddenly absorbed by the lattice atoms, generating intense heat in a microscopic volume.

$$Q = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat generated (J/s = W)
- $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.7 – 0.8 for EBW)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (V)
- $I$ = beam current (A)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $eV = \tfrac{1}{2}m v^2$ | Kinetic energy from accelerating voltage | Joules |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{\tfrac{2eV}{m}}$ | Electron velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Beam power | W |
| 4 | $\text{Power Density} = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}} = \dfrac{VI}{\pi d^2/4}$ | Power per unit area | W/m² |
| 5 | $Q = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$ | Heat generated at workpiece | W |
| 6 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v_{weld}}$ | Heat input per unit length | J/mm |
| 7 | $J = A_R T^2 e^{-\phi/k_BT}$ | Richardson's emission equation | A/m² |
| 8 | $\text{DWR} = \dfrac{\text{Penetration Depth}}{\text{Width at Top}}$ | Depth-to-width ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lambda_e = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{m v}$ | de Broglie wavelength (for beam coherence) | m |
| 10 | $F_{magnetic} = qv \times B$ | Lorentz force on electron (magnetic lens) | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on units:** $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$.

## 2.3 Why Vacuum? — Engineering Justification

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **No electron scattering** | Air molecules would deflect electrons from their straight path |
| **No oxidation** | Prevents oxide formation in molten pool — gives defect-free welds |
| **High breakdown voltage** | Vacuum withstands the high 30–200 kV accelerating field |
| **No contamination** | Reactive metals (Ti, Zr) remain chemically pure |
| **X-ray containment** | Decelerating electrons emit X-rays; vacuum chamber walls absorb them |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry** — Welding of turbine blades, combustion chambers, nozzle assemblies (Inconel, Ti-alloys)
- **Nuclear sector** — Joining of zirconium alloy fuel pins and reactor pressure vessels
- **Automotive** — Welding of dissimilar metal combinations in transmission gears
- **Electronics** — Hermetic sealing of vacuum-tight components
- **Medical devices** — Welding of titanium implants and stainless-steel surgical tools

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Electron Velocity from Accelerating Voltage

**Given:** Accelerating voltage $V = 100 \text{ kV} = 10^5 \text{ V}$

**Goal:** Find the velocity of the accelerated electron.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Write the energy balance equation. The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its kinetic energy gained:

$$W = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known constants:
- $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$
- $V = 10^5 \text{ V}$

$$\frac{1}{2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times v^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^5$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the right-hand side:

$$1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^5 = 1.6 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $v^2$:

$$v^2 = \frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-14}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-14}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}$$

$$v^2 = 0.3516 \times 10^{17} = 3.516 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 5:** Take square root:

$$v = \sqrt{3.516 \times 10^{16}} \approx 1.875 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with speed of light:

$$\beta = \frac{v}{c} = \frac{1.875 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.625$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 1.875 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.625\,c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\beta = 0.625 > 0.1$, the electron is **moderately relativistic**, and a correction factor of $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2} \approx 1.296$ should be applied for high-precision calculations. However, for KTU exam purposes, the classical formula is generally acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Density Calculation

**Given:**
- Accelerating voltage $V = 150 \text{ kV}$
- Beam current $I = 100 \text{ mA} = 0.1 \text{ A}$
- Focused spot diameter $d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

**Goal:** Calculate the power density at the workpiece.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate total beam power:

$$P = V \times I = 150{,}000 \times 0.1 = 15{,}000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate focused spot cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-7}}{4}$$

$$A \approx 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate power density:

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{15{,}000}{1.963 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\text{Power Density} \approx 7.64 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Express in more common engineering units (W/mm²):

$$\text{Power Density} = 7.64 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power Density} \approx 7.64 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2 = 76.4 \text{ kW/mm}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains why EBW produces **deep, narrow welds** — the energy is concentrated in an area smaller than a pinhead, vaporizing a column of metal that becomes the keyhole.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Input Per Unit Length

**Given:**
- $V = 100 \text{ kV}$, $I = 50 \text{ mA}$, $\eta = 0.75$, welding speed $v_{weld} = 100 \text{ mm/s} = 0.1 \text{ m/s}$

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Heat input formula (per KTU/IIW convention):

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v_{weld}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 100{,}000 \times 0.05}{0.1}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 5000}{0.1} = \frac{3750}{0.1}$$

$$\boxed{H = 37{,}500 \text{ J/m} = 37.5 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **far lower** than conventional arc welding (e.g., SMAW ≈ 1000 J/mm), which is why EBW has minimal HAZ and low distortion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters & Their Effects

| Parameter ↑ | Effect on Penetration | Effect on Width | Effect on HAZ | Effect on Spatter |
|-------------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Accelerating Voltage $V$ ↑ | **Strong ↑** | ↑ slightly | ↑ slightly | ↑ |
| Beam Current $I$ ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ |
| Welding Speed ↓ (slower) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| Focus Current (sharp focus) | **Strong ↑** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Working Distance ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — EBW System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HVPS[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 200 kV]
    VAC[Vacuum Pump System 10 Power Minus 4 torr]
    FIL[Tungsten Filament Cathode 2500 C]
    ANO[Anode with Central Aperture]
    EM1[Electromagnetic Lens 1]
    EM2[Electromagnetic Lens 2 Focusing]
    DEF[Deflection Coils X Y Sweep]
    WPC[Workpiece in Vacuum Chamber]
    CHAM[Vacuum Chamber]
    MON[Optical Observation System]
    XRAY[X Ray Shielding Wall]
    CTR[PLC and CNC Controller]

    HVPS -->|Accelerates| FIL
    FIL -->|Thermionic e to Power Minus| ANO
    ANO -->|Collimated Beam| EM1
    EM1 --> EM2
    EM2 --> DEF
    DEF -->|Focused Beam| WPC
    WPC --> CHAM
    VAC --> CHAM
    CHAM --> XRAY
    MON -->|Visual feedback| WPC
    CTR -->|Process control| HVPS
    CTR -->|Lens current| EM2
    CTR -->|Beam path| DEF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Keyhole Formation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Electrons emitted from filament]
    S2[Step 2: Acceleration through 30 to 200 kV]
    S3[Step 3: Magnetic focusing to 0.5 mm spot]
    S4[Step 4: Impact at 10 Power 9 W per mm squared]
    S5[Step 5: Local melting and vaporization]
    S6[Step 6: Keyhole formation deep cavity]
    S7[Step 7: Molten metal flows around keyhole]
    S8[Step 8: Beam moves - keyhole traverses joint]
    S9[Step 9: Solidification under vacuum - no porosity]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Classification of EBW Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EBW[Electron Beam Welding]

    EBW --> HV[High Vacuum EBW]
    EBW --> PV[Partial Vacuum EBW]
    EBW --> NV[Non Vacuum EBW]

    HV --> HVdesc[10 Power Minus 4 to 10 Power Minus 6 torr Best quality Used for reactive metals]
    PV --> PVdesc[10 Power Minus 1 to 10 Power Minus 2 torr Larger workpiece Industrial use]
    NV --> NVdesc[Atmospheric pressure Special guns Limited to thick sections]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Diagram — EBW vs Conventional Arc Welding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EBW[Electron Beam Welding]
        E1[Power Density: 10 Power 9 W per mm squared]
        E2[Penetration: Very deep up to 250 mm]
        E3[HAZ: Very narrow]
        E4[Speed: Very high]
        E5[Atmosphere: Vacuum required]
    end

    subgraph ARC[Conventional Arc Welding SMAW GMAW]
        A1[Power Density: 10 Power 3 W per mm squared]
        A2[Penetration: Shallow 3 to 6 mm per pass]
        A3[HAZ: Wide]
        A4[Speed: Moderate]
        A5[Atmosphere: Open air shielding gas]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Electron Beam Welding. State any two advantages of EBW over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Electron Beam Welding (EBW) is a non-conventional fusion welding process in which a focused beam of high-velocity electrons, accelerated under high vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) by a potential difference of 30–200 kV, is used to join metals. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted to intense heat upon impact, producing welds with extremely high power density (10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²).

**Two advantages (1 mark):**
1. **High depth-to-width ratio (up to 30:1)** — Enables single-pass welding of thick sections, eliminating multi-pass defects.
2. **Very narrow Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** — Reduces distortion and metallurgical degradation, critical for reactive metals like titanium and zirconium.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Why is vacuum essential in Electron Beam Welding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vacuum is essential in EBW for the following reasons:

1. **Prevention of electron scattering (1 mark):** In the presence of air molecules, the electron beam gets deflected and dispersed due to collisions with gas molecules. Vacuum (≤ 10⁻⁴ torr) ensures a collision-free path, allowing the beam to retain its high energy density at the focus point.

2. **Avoidance of oxidation and contamination (1 mark):** Reactive metals (Ti, Zr, Nb) and refractory metals oxidize rapidly at high temperatures. Vacuum eliminates oxygen, producing chemically pure, defect-free welds.

3. **High voltage insulation (1 mark):** The high accelerating voltage (30–200 kV) requires a medium with high dielectric strength. Vacuum provides the necessary electrical insulation between the cathode and anode without arcing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]**

**Q.A.** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle of Electron Beam Welding**. Describe the functions of the major components of an EBW system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EBW machine operates at an accelerating voltage of **120 kV** and a beam current of **80 mA**. The focused spot diameter is **0.4 mm**. Calculate **(i)** electron velocity, **(ii)** total beam power, and **(iii)** power density at the workpiece. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Principle (3 marks):**
The principle of EBW is based on the **conversion of kinetic energy of accelerated electrons into thermal energy** at the workpiece surface. A tungsten filament emits electrons via **thermionic emission**. These electrons are accelerated by a high-voltage electric field (30–200 kV) through a differentially-pumped vacuum system. Magnetic lenses focus the beam to a small spot (≈ 0.5 mm), and on striking the workpiece, the kinetic energy is converted to heat, melting the metal locally. The process operates under high vacuum (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) to prevent electron scattering and metal contamination.

**Major Components and Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Electron Gun (Filament + Anode)** | Generates and accelerates the electron beam |
| **Magnetic Focusing Lens** | Focuses the diverging electrons into a fine spot |
| **Deflection Coils** | Steer the beam along the joint path (X-Y raster) |
| **Vacuum Chamber** | Houses the workpiece under 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr |
| **Vacuum Pump System** | Maintains required vacuum level |
| **High-Voltage Power Supply** | Provides 30–200 kV accelerating voltage |
| **Work Manipulator (CNC Table)** | Moves the workpiece precisely under the beam |
| **Optical Observation System** | Allows real-time viewing of the weld pool |
| **X-Ray Shielding** | Absorbs harmful X-rays generated by decelerating electrons |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- $V = 120 \text{ kV} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ V}$
- $I = 80 \text{ mA} = 0.08 \text{ A}$
- $d = 0.4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$

**(i) Electron Velocity (3 marks):**

Using $eV = \tfrac{1}{2}m_e v^2$:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.2 \times 10^5}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.84 \times 10^{-14}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} = \sqrt{4.22 \times 10^{16}}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 2.054 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.685\,c}$$

**(ii) Total Beam Power (2 marks):**

$$P = V \times I = 1.2 \times 10^5 \times 0.08 = 9600 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P = 9.6 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Power Density (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (4 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\text{Power Density} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{9600}{1.2566 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.64 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Power Density} \approx 7.64 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2 = 76.4 \text{ kW/mm}^2}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** **(a)** With the help of a schematic, explain the **theory of Electron Beam Welding**. Discuss the role of vacuum and the physical principle of energy conversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Electron Beam Welding (EBW)** with **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** on the basis of any **seven key parameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Theory (4 marks):**
The theory of EBW involves the **kinetic-to-thermal energy conversion** of electrons. When a high voltage (30–200 kV) is applied between a heated tungsten cathode and a grounded anode, the electric field accelerates emitted electrons to velocities approaching the speed of light. The kinetic energy of each electron is:

$$KE = eV$$

The total power delivered to the workpiece is:

$$P = V \times I$$

Upon impact, electrons penetrate a few micrometers into the metal and transfer their kinetic energy to the lattice through atomic collisions, generating localized heat. With power densities exceeding **10⁹ W/mm²**, the metal at the focal point **vaporizes**, forming a deep, narrow cavity called a **keyhole**. As the beam moves, the keyhole traverses the joint, and molten metal flows around it to fuse the two parts. Solidification under vacuum yields a defect-free weld with minimal HAZ.

**Role of Vacuum (3 marks):**
1. **Free electron path** — Mean free path of electrons must exceed chamber dimensions; requires ≤ 10⁻⁴ torr.
2. **No oxide formation** — Critical for reactive metals (Ti, Zr, refractory alloys).
3. **High dielectric strength** — Prevents electrical breakdown at 30–200 kV accelerating voltages.
4. **X-ray containment** — Vacuum chamber walls provide radiation shielding.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per well-explained parameter):**

| Parameter | EBW | LBW |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Energy source** | Accelerated electrons (kinetic energy) | Photons from a laser (electromagnetic radiation) |
| **Power density** | 10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm² (higher) | 10⁵ to 10⁷ W/mm² |
| **Vacuum requirement** | Essential (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr) | Not essential (works in air) |
| **Penetration depth** | Very deep (up to 250 mm, single pass) | Limited to ~25 mm; deeper with multi-mode |
| **Weld profile** | High DWR (30:1), narrow and deep | Lower DWR (5:1 to 10:1) |
| **Equipment cost** | Higher (vacuum system + HVPS) | Lower (no vacuum) |
| **Reflectivity issue** | Not affected (uses electrons) | Affected — highly reflective metals (Al, Cu) are difficult |
| **Filler metal** | Usually not required | Sometimes used for thick sections |
| **Joint preparation** | Very precise (no gap) | Less critical |
| **Atmosphere control** | Excellent (vacuum) | Requires shielding gas (e.g., Argon) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing power density units:** Students often write $10^9 \text{ W/mm}^2$ without specifying — the correct value is $\approx 10^9 \text{ W/m}^2$ per some texts, but the most common KTU reference uses $\mathbf{10^6}$ to $\mathbf{10^9 \text{ W/mm}^2}$. Always cross-check with the standard.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to square the spot diameter** in the area formula: $A = \pi d^2/4$, not $\pi d$.
>
> 3. **Neglecting relativistic effects:** For V > 100 kV, the classical $v = \sqrt{2eV/m}$ overestimates velocity. Examiners may deduct marks if relativistic factor $\gamma$ is missing.
>
> 4. **Confusing thermionic emission with photoelectric emission** — EBW uses **thermionic emission** (heat), not photoelectric.
>
> 5. **Forgetting X-ray safety** — EBW generates **bremsstrahlung X-rays** when electrons decelerate; shielding is mandatory in theory questions.
>
> 6. **Writing "filament burns" instead of "filament emits electrons"** — must use the exact term **thermionic emission**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EBW Definition:** Non-conventional fusion welding using a focused high-velocity electron beam under high vacuum.
- **Energy source:** Accelerated electrons (kinetic energy), not photons.
- **Three-stage principle:** (1) Thermionic emission → (2) Acceleration → (3) Magnetic focusing → (4) Impact and heat generation.
- **Key equation:** $eV = \frac{1}{2}m_e v^2$, so $v = \sqrt{2eV/m_e}$.
- **Beam power:** $P = V \cdot I$.
- **Power density:** $\dfrac{VI}{\pi d^2/4}$ — typically $\mathbf{10^6}$ to $\mathbf{10^9 \text{ W/mm}^2}$.
- **Vacuum required:** $\mathbf{10^{-2}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-6} \text{ torr}}$ (mandatory).
- **Accelerating voltage:** $\mathbf{30}$ to $\mathbf{200 \text{ kV}}$.
- **Depth-to-width ratio:** Up to $\mathbf{30:1}$ (extremely narrow and deep welds).
- **Process efficiency:** $\mathbf{70}$ to $\mathbf{80\%}$ (high).
- **Keyhole formation:** Caused by vaporization of metal at high power density.
- **X-ray hazard:** Decelerating electrons emit **bremsstrahlung X-rays** — shielding required.
- **Three variants:** High vacuum, partial vacuum, and non-vacuum EBW.
- **Materials welded:** Reactive metals (Ti, Zr), refractory metals (W, Mo, Nb), stainless steel, dissimilar metals.
- **Applications:** Aerospace (turbines), nuclear (fuel pins), medical (implants), automotive (gears).
- **Comparison reminder:** EBW ≠ LBW — EBW uses **electrons in vacuum**; LBW uses **photons, can be in air**.
- **No filler metal required** in most EBW applications.
- **Solidification rate is very high** → fine grain structure in the weld zone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding Environment, Equipment Used, Process Parameters, and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Welding Environment, Equipment Used, Process Parameters, and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Welding Environment — Formal Definition

In the context of **PEMET416 (Advanced Metal Joining Techniques)** and per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the **welding environment** refers to the complete set of ambient conditions, atmospheric composition, spatial constraints, and protective measures that surround a welding operation. It encompasses three primary domains:

1. **Atmospheric Environment** — The composition of gases (active, inert, reducing) surrounding the arc and weld pool.
2. **Physical Environment** — Workshop conditions including temperature, humidity, ventilation, lighting, and spatial access (indoor vs. field/outdoor).
3. **Human & Safety Environment** — Protection against fumes, UV radiation, spatter, electric shock, and noise through PPE and exhaust systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The welding environment directly governs the **weld metallurgy**. Contaminants such as **O₂, H₂, and N₂** above threshold limits (typically **>50 ppm** for oxygen in TIG/GTAW) cause porosity, embrittlement, and loss of corrosion resistance. Hence, environment control is **not optional** — it is a metallurgical requirement.

### 1.2 Equipment Used — Formal Definition

**Welding equipment** denotes the integrated set of hardware required to deliver controlled thermal energy, filler material, shielding medium, and motion to produce a sound weld joint. For arc welding processes (the focus of Module 3), the equipment chain typically includes:

- **Power Source** (Transformer, Rectifier, Inverter)
- **Wire Feeder Unit** (for semi-automatic/automatic processes)
- **Welding Torch / Electrode Holder / Gun**
- **Shielding Gas Supply** (Cylinder, Regulator, Flowmeter, Hose)
- **Cooling System** (Water or air-cooled)
- **Ground Clamp & Cables**
- **Positioner / Manipulator / Robot** (for automation)

### 1.3 Process Parameters & Characteristics — Formal Definition

**Process Parameters** are the operator-controllable inputs that govern heat generation, material transfer, and bead formation. The four primary parameters in arc welding are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Welding Current | $I$ | Amperes (A) |
| Arc Voltage | $V$ | Volts (V) |
| Travel Speed | $S$ | mm/min or m/min |
| Wire Feed Rate | $WFR$ | m/min |

**Welding Characteristics** are the *outputs* (responses) produced by the interaction of parameters with the environment. They include **Heat Input (HI)**, **Deposition Rate (DR)**, **Penetration**, **Bead Width**, **Reinforcement**, and **Mechanical Properties** of the weld.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Welding Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: Welding is like Cooking a Controlled Fire**

Imagine you are cooking a delicate dish (the weld) on a gas stove (the welding arc) in your kitchen (the welding environment).

- **The Environment (Kitchen):** A clean, draft-free kitchen with proper exhaust (shielding gas + ventilation) prevents the dish from getting contaminated by dust or smoke. A messy kitchen (poor environment) ruins the dish (porous weld).
- **The Equipment (Stove & Utensils):** You need a reliable gas cylinder (shielding gas cylinder), a burner knob (power source), a regulator (gas regulator), and the right pan (torch). Cheap equipment = uneven flame (unstable arc).
- **The Parameters (Recipe):** Flame intensity (current), flame distance (voltage), stirring speed (travel speed), and ingredient addition rate (wire feed speed). Too much heat or too slow stirring overcooks the food (excessive HAZ, distortion).
- **The Characteristics (Taste & Appearance):** The final dish has tenderness (toughness), color (bead appearance), thickness (deposition), and aroma (mechanical properties).

Just as a master chef controls all four dimensions simultaneously, a welding engineer must harmonize environment, equipment, parameters, and characteristics to produce a **defect-free, mechanically sound weld**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Welding Environment — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Atmospheric Environment (Shielding Gas Composition)

The shielding gas serves three critical functions:
1. **Excludes atmospheric gases** (O₂, N₂, H₂) from the arc and molten pool.
2. **Stabilizes the arc** by providing ionizable media.
3. **Influences metal transfer mode** and penetration profile.

**Common Shielding Gases & Their Roles:**

- **Argon (Ar):** Inert, used for TIG/GTAW and MIG/GMAW of stainless steel, aluminum. Pure argon gives **deep, narrow penetration** (cathode cleaning effect on Al).
- **Helium (He):** Inert, hotter arc (~**3000 °C** higher than Ar), used for high-speed welding and thick aluminum sections.
- **CO₂:** Active/reactive gas, used in MIG of mild steel. Produces **deep penetration** but higher spatter.
- **O₂ (2–5%):** Active additive to Ar for MIG of stainless/low-alloy steel to stabilize arc and improve wetting.
- **Ar + CO₂ mixtures:** Most common for mild steel MIG (e.g., **80% Ar + 20% CO₂** called "Corgon"; 92% Ar + 8% CO₂ called "C8").
- **Ar + He mixtures:** For high-quality stainless and nickel alloys.
- **Forming Gas (N₂ + H₂):** Used in plasma welding of stainless steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moisture Control:** Shielding gas dew point must be maintained **below −40 °C** for critical applications (e.g., nuclear, aerospace). Moisture dissociation produces hydrogen, causing **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** and **underbead cracking**.

#### B. Physical Environment Categories

| Environment Type | Description | Typical Process Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Workshop (Indoor)** | Controlled ventilation, fixed positioners, lighting | All processes; preferred for quality-critical welds |
| **Site (Outdoor)** | Wind, rain, dust, temperature variation | Stick (SMAW) preferred; Flux-cored (FCAW) self-shielded variants |
| **Underwater** | Hyperbaric chamber or wet welding | Hyperbaric TIG/MIG; wet SMAW with special electrodes |
| **Space / Vacuum** | No atmosphere | Electron beam, laser, or TIG with local gas shroud |
| **Nuclear / Hazardous** | Remote manipulation mandatory | Remote TIG, laser, or orbital GTAW |

#### C. Human & Safety Environment

- **Fume Extraction:** Welding fumes contain **Mn, Cr, Ni, fluorides, ozone (O₃), NOₓ**. OSHA PEL for Mn = **5 mg/m³ (ceiling)**.
- **UV/IR Protection:** Arc emits UV-B/UV-C causing **arc eye (photokeratitis)**; auto-darkening helmets (shade 9–13) mandatory.
- **Ventilation:** General ventilation **≥ 2000 ft³/min per welder** for non-toxic processes; local exhaust for toxic fume-producing processes.
- **Electrical Safety:** Open-circuit voltage (OCV) typically **60–80 V (DC)** or **50 V (AC)** — must be limited per safety standards.

---

### 2.2 Equipment Used — Detailed Functional Breakdown

#### A. Power Sources

The power source converts mains AC (typically **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** in India) into a controlled welding output.

| Type | Principle | Advantages | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformer (AC)** | Steps down voltage; iron core + copper coil | Rugged, cheap, simple | 60–70% |
| **Engine-Driven Generator** | Diesel engine + DC generator | Portable, field use | 50–60% |
| **Rectifier** | Transformer + diode bridge → DC output | Stable DC arc, smoother | 70–80% |
| **Inverter (IGBT/MOSFET)** | High-freq switching (20–100 kHz) → small transformer → rectified | Lightweight, precise control, high power factor | 85–92% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend:** IGBT-based inverter power sources dominate industry. A **400 A inverter** weighs **~25 kg**, while a comparable transformer-rectifier weighs **~150 kg**.

**Static Characteristics of Power Sources:**
- **Constant Current (CC):** Used for Stick (SMAW) and TIG. Voltage varies with arc length; current nearly constant. Drooping V-I curve.
- **Constant Voltage (CV):** Used for MIG, FCAW, SAW. Current varies with feed speed; voltage nearly constant. Flat V-I curve.

#### B. Wire Feeder Unit

For semi-automatic MIG/FCAW:
- **Push-Type Feeder:** Feeder is in the power source; gun pushes wire over **3–5 m**. Suitable for thin wires (0.6–1.2 mm).
- **Spool Gun:** Small spool on the gun; for soft wires like aluminum to prevent buckling.
- **Push-Pull System:** Separate drive rolls near gun; for aluminum wires up to **10–15 m**.

Wire feed rate (WFR) is the **primary current-setting knob** in CV MIG welding: more wire = more current = more heat.

#### C. Shielding Gas Delivery System

$$\text{Cylinder} \rightarrow \text{Regulator (2-stage)} \rightarrow \text{Flowmeter} \rightarrow \text{Gas Hose} \rightarrow \text{Torch Nozzle}$$

- **Regulator:** Reduces cylinder pressure (~**150 bar** for full Ar cylinder) to working pressure (~**2–4 bar**).
- **Flowmeter:** Measures gas flow; typical MIG flow **12–18 L/min**, TIG **7–12 L/min**.

#### D. Welding Torch / Gun

- **Air-Cooled:** For currents up to ~**200–250 A** (MIG) or ~**150 A** (TIG).
- **Water-Cooled:** For high-current TIG (**>200 A**) and heavy MIG (**>300 A**); uses circulating chilled water (~**5 L/min**).

---

### 2.3 Process Parameters — Detailed Hierarchy

The parameters are categorized as:

**Primary (Electrical) Parameters:**
- Welding Current ($I$) — controls **heat generation** and **deposition rate**.
- Arc Voltage ($V$) — controls **arc length** and **bead width**.

**Secondary (Mechanical) Parameters:**
- Travel Speed ($S$) — controls **heat input per unit length**.
- Wire Feed Rate ($WFR$) — coupled with current in CV MIG.
- Electrode Extension / Stick-Out — affects **resistive heating (I²R)** of the electrode.

**Gas Parameter:**
- Shielding Gas Flow Rate ($Q$) — typically **12–18 L/min** for MIG, **7–12 L/min** for TIG.

---

### 2.4 Welding Characteristics — Detailed Analysis

**Heat Input (HI)** is the most critical derived characteristic, defined as the energy delivered per unit length of weld:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$$

Where:
- $HI$ = Heat Input in **kJ/mm**
- $\eta$ = Arc efficiency factor (dimensionless): Stick = **0.75**, TIG = **0.60**, MIG = **0.80**, SAW = **0.90**, Plasma = **0.60**
- $V$ = Arc voltage in **V**
- $I$ = Welding current in **A**
- $S$ = Travel speed in **mm/s**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance of Heat Input:** It directly governs **penetration**, **HAZ width**, **cooling rate**, **grain size**, and **mechanical properties**. Excessive HI causes **coarse grain, reduced toughness, distortion**. Insufficient HI causes **lack of fusion, lack of penetration, porosity**.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $DR = \dfrac{WFR \cdot A_w \cdot \rho}{10^6}$ | Deposition Rate (mass per time) | kg/h |
| 3 | $P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I$ | Arc Power | W |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{shield}} = \text{Flow} \times \text{Time}$ | Shielding Gas Consumption | L |
| 5 | $A_w = \pi \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of wire (mm²) | mm² |
| 6 | $t_{\text{cool}} \propto \dfrac{1}{(T_8/5)^n}$ | Cooling time relation | s |
| 7 | $E_{\text{elec}} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot L_{\text{ext}}$ | Resistive heat in stick-out | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Heat Input:** The factor of **1000** in the denominator converts W·s/mm to kJ/mm (since $1 \text{ W·s} = 1 \text{ J}$ and $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$). If $S$ is in **mm/min**, divide by **60** instead of **1000**.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipbuilding & Pressure Vessels:** Strict HI limits (e.g., **≤ 2.0 kJ/mm** for thick-plate C-Mn steel) per **AWS D1.1** and **EN 10164** to ensure adequate CTOD toughness.
- **Pipeline Welding (API 1104):** Cellulosic electrodes (E6010) give deep penetration; precise HI control prevents HAZ softening.
- **Aerospace (Ti, Ni Alloys):** Inert chamber welding with O₂ < **50 ppm**, H₂O < **−60 °C** dew point.
- **Automotive Robotic MIG:** Constant-voltage inverter with synergic control matches WFR and voltage automatically.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Expression

The fundamental definition of **Heat Input** emerges from the energy conservation principle applied to the moving arc.

**Step 1: Define Arc Power**

The instantaneous thermal power generated by the arc is the product of arc voltage and welding current:

$$P_{\text{arc}} = V \cdot I$$

Where $V$ is in volts and $I$ is in amperes, giving $P_{\text{arc}}$ in **watts (W)** or **Joules/second (J/s)**.

**Step 2: Account for Process Efficiency**

Not all arc power is transferred to the workpiece. A fraction is lost via **radiation**, **convection**, **spatter**, and **fume**. The **arc efficiency** $\eta$ accounts for this:

$$P_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I$$

**Step 3: Energy Delivered per Unit Length**

If the arc travels along the joint at a constant speed $S$, the time spent depositing energy over a length $L$ is:

$$t = \frac{L}{S}$$

The total energy deposited over length $L$ is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{effective}} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{S}$$

**Step 4: Heat Input per Unit Length**

Dividing total energy by length $L$:

$$HI = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{L} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}$$

This yields units of **J/mm**. To convert to **kJ/mm**:

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$$

This is the canonical heat input formula used across **AWS, ISO, EN, and ASME** codes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention on Units:** If $S$ is expressed in **mm/min**, then the formula becomes $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S_{\text{mm/min}} \cdot 1000}$ because we must convert minutes to seconds.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Heat Input Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Style Problem:** A MIG weld is made on a **10 mm thick mild steel plate** using the following parameters: $V = 24 \text{ V}$, $I = 220 \text{ A}$, $S = 5 \text{ mm/s}$. Given MIG arc efficiency $\eta = 0.80$, calculate the heat input and comment on its suitability for the application.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \\
&= \frac{0.80 \times 24 \times 220}{5 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{0.80 \times 5280}{5000} \\
&= \frac{4224}{5000} \\
&= 0.8448 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to kJ/m:

$$HI = 0.8448 \times 1000 = 844.8 \text{ kJ/m}$$

**Comment:** For **10 mm** mild steel plate, a typical recommended heat input range is **1.0–1.5 kJ/mm** for multi-pass welding. At **0.8448 kJ/mm**, the heat input is on the lower side. This may result in:
- Faster cooling rate → harder HAZ
- Possible lack of fusion if root gap/tolerance is tight
- Lower distortion, which is favorable for thin sections

**Recommendation:** Either **increase current to ~280 A** or **reduce travel speed to ~3.5 mm/s** to bring HI into the 1.0–1.2 kJ/mm optimum range.

---

### 3.3 Deposition Rate Derivation

**Step 1: Wire Volume per Unit Time**

If wire feed rate is $WFR$ (in m/min) and wire diameter is $d$ (in mm), the cross-sectional area of the wire is:

$$A_w = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

The volume of wire fed per minute is:

$$\text{Vol}_{\text{per min}} = WFR \times A_w \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

Converting WFR from m/min to mm/min: $WFR_{\text{mm}} = WFR \times 1000$.

$$\text{Vol}_{\text{per min}} = WFR \times 1000 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 2: Mass per Unit Time (Deposition Rate)**

Multiplying by filler metal density $\rho$ (typical for steel: $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$):

$$DR = WFR \times 1000 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times \rho \quad [\text{g/min}]$$

To convert to **kg/h**:

$$DR = \frac{WFR \times 1000 \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \times \rho \times 60}{10^6} \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$

This simplifies to:

$$DR = \frac{WFR \times \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho}{66.67} \quad [\text{kg/h}]$$

Or, using $WFR$ in m/min and $d$ in mm with $\rho$ in kg/m³:

$$DR_{\text{kg/h}} = \frac{WFR \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho}{4 \times 10^6 \times 60} \times 3600 = \frac{WFR \cdot \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot \rho}{66.67 \times 10^3}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Deposition Rate

**Problem:** Calculate the deposition rate for a **MIG welding** operation using **1.2 mm** diameter steel wire fed at **8 m/min**. Steel density $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_w &= \frac{\pi (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Vol/min} &= WFR \times A_w = 8 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} = 9.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \\
\text{Mass/min} &= 9.05 \times 10^{-6} \times 7850 = 0.0710 \text{ kg/min} \\
DR &= 0.0710 \times 60 = 4.26 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $DR = 4.26 \text{ kg/h}$ (assuming **100% deposition efficiency**; actual deposition efficiency for MIG is ~**95–98%**, so realistic $DR \approx 4.05 \text{ kg/h}$).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter–Characteristic Relationship

This matrix maps the qualitative effect of **increasing** each parameter on each welding characteristic.

| Parameter ↑ | HI | Penetration | Bead Width | DR | Distortion | HAZ Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current ($I$) ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Voltage ($V$) ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (mild) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Travel Speed ($S$) ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | — | ↓ | ↓ |
| Wire Feed Rate ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Gas Flow ($Q$) ↑ | — | ↑ (until optimum, then ↓) | — | — | — | — |
| Stick-Out ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (then ↓) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |

**Reading the Matrix:**
- Increasing **current** has the **strongest effect on penetration** — current density governs arc constriction and electromagnetic pinch force.
- Increasing **voltage** primarily **widens the bead** by lengthening the arc.
- Increasing **travel speed** reduces HI, producing **narrower, deeper-looking** beads but with **less fusion** risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Voltage* effect on penetration. Many students incorrectly believe higher voltage gives more penetration. **Reality:** Voltage primarily widens the bead; **current** drives penetration.

---

### 3.6 Cooling Rate & HAZ Relationship

The **cooling time between 800 °C and 500 °C** ($t_{8/5}$) is a critical metallurgical parameter that determines HAZ microstructure:

$$t_{8/5} = \frac{HI^2}{2\pi \lambda \rho c (T_1 - T_0)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{d}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $c$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $T_1$ = **800 °C** (above which austenite exists)
- $T_0$ = **500 °C** (below which bainite/martensite formation completes for many steels)
- $d$ = plate thickness (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Higher HI → longer $t_{8/5}$ → coarser grain → lower toughness. Codes (e.g., EN 10164) often prescribe **maximum t₈/₅** values for given steel grades and thicknesses.

---

### 3.7 Empirical Penetration Equation (Lancaster Correlation)

For a moving arc on a plate, penetration depth $P$ can be estimated by:

$$P \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\frac{HI}{d \cdot \rho c (T_m - T_0)}}$$

Where:
- $k$ = dimensionless constant (~0.5–1.0)
- $T_m$ = melting temperature of base metal
- $T_0$ = initial plate temperature (preheat)

This shows **penetration increases with the square root of heat input** but inversely with **plate thickness** — explaining why thick plates need **proportionally higher current or multiple passes**.
