## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1:** Define **welding environment** and list **any four factors** that affect weld quality.
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The welding environment is the set of ambient conditions — atmospheric, physical, and human/safety — that surrounds and influences the welding operation.

**Four factors affecting weld quality (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Atmospheric contamination** — O₂, N₂, H₂ ingress causing porosity and embrittlement.
2. **Humidity** — moisture in shielding gas or electrode coating → hydrogen porosity.
3. **Wind/drafts** — disperse shielding gas, especially critical above **5 km/h** wind speed.
4. **Ambient temperature** — affects cooling rate and preheat requirements.
5. *(Optional 5th)* — ventilation affecting fume extraction and welder comfort.

---

> **Question A2:** What is **arc efficiency**? State the typical efficiency values for **SMAW, GTAW, GMAW, and SAW**.
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Arc efficiency ($\eta$) is the ratio of heat actually delivered to the workpiece to the total electrical energy input in the arc, expressed as a decimal fraction.

**Typical values (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
| Process | Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) |
|---|---|
| SMAW (Stick) | **0.75** |
| GTAW (TIG) | **0.60** |
| GMAW (MIG/MAG) | **0.80** |
| SAW (Submerged Arc) | **0.90** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question B — Choice A:** Explain in detail the **welding environment** with reference to **(a)** atmospheric and shielding gas requirements, and **(b)** the various categories of welding equipment used in arc welding processes.
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

#### Part (a) — Atmospheric and Shielding Gas Requirements (7 marks)

**1. Role of Atmosphere (2 marks):**
The surrounding atmosphere contains **O₂ (~21%), N₂ (~78%), H₂O, CO₂** which are **harmful** to the molten weld pool. They cause:
- **Porosity** (gas pockets)
- **Oxidation** (slag inclusions, loss of alloying elements)
- **Nitrogen embrittlement** (in steels, forms brittle nitrides)
- **Hydrogen cracking** (cold cracking, delayed cracking)

**2. Shielding Gas Functions (2 marks):**
- **Exclusion** of atmospheric gases from arc and weld pool
- **Arc stabilization** by providing ionizable species
- **Heat transfer modification** (Ar → constricted arc; He → wider, hotter arc)
- **Metal transfer mode control** (e.g., Ar/CO₂ gives spray transfer at higher currents)

**3. Common Shielding Gas Compositions (2 marks):**
- **Pure Ar** → TIG of Al, stainless; MIG of Al (deep penetration)
- **Pure CO₂** → MIG of mild steel (cheap, deep penetration, high spatter)
- **80% Ar + 20% CO₂** → general mild steel MIG (balanced)
- **98% Ar + 2% O₂** → stainless steel MIG (stable arc, clean weld)
- **75% Ar + 25% He** → high-speed TIG of stainless & Ni alloys
- **Forming gas (90% N₂ + 10% H₂)** → plasma welding of stainless

**4. Quality Control of Shielding Gas (1 mark):**
- Dew point **< −40 °C**
- Purity **≥ 99.99%** for critical welds
- Flow rate **12–18 L/min** for MIG; **7–12 L/min** for TIG
- Hose integrity; no leaks at fittings

#### Part (b) — Categories of Welding Equipment (7 marks)

**1. Power Source (2 marks):**
- **Transformer:** AC output; rugged, used for stick/TIG
- **Rectifier:** Transformer + diode bridge → DC; smoother arc
- **Inverter (IGBT):** High-frequency switching → compact, precise; modern industry standard
- **Engine-driven generator:** Portable; field use

**2. Static Characteristics (1 mark):**
- **Constant Current (CC):** SMAW, GTAW; drooping curve
- **Constant Voltage (CV):** GMAW, FCAW, SAW; flat curve

**3. Wire Feeder Unit (1.5 marks):**
- **Push type** — feeder in power source, for short gun (<5 m)
- **Pull type** — motor in gun
- **Push-pull type** — both, for soft Al wires (long distances)
- **Spool gun** — for Al, soft wires, prevents bird-nesting

**4. Shielding Gas Delivery System (1.5 marks):**
- Cylinder → 2-stage regulator → flowmeter → hose → torch nozzle
- 2-stage regulator ensures constant flow despite cylinder pressure drop

**5. Torch and Cooling (1 mark):**
- **Air-cooled:** MIG up to ~250 A; TIG up to ~150 A
- **Water-cooled:** for high-current operations (>250 A MIG, >200 A TIG)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **omit the difference between CC and CV** power sources — losing 1 mark. Always state which process uses which.
> 2. Do NOT confuse **shielding gas flow rate** with **pressure**. Pressure is regulated in bar; flow in L/min.
> 3. For MIG, mention **constant voltage** explicitly; for TIG/Stick, mention **constant current** explicitly.

---

> **Question B — Choice B:** Discuss in detail the **(a)** primary welding process parameters and their effects on weld characteristics, and **(b)** the concept of **heat input** with a worked numerical example suitable for a **10 mm thick mild steel plate**.
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

#### Part (a) — Process Parameters and Their Effects (7 marks)

**1. Primary Parameters Defined (2 marks):**

- **Welding Current ($I$, A):** Governs heat generation, penetration depth, and deposition rate. Higher current → deeper penetration, higher deposition.
- **Arc Voltage ($V$, V):** Determines arc length and bead width. Higher voltage → wider, flatter bead with more spatter risk.
- **Travel Speed ($S$, mm/s or mm/min):** Speed of arc travel along the joint. Higher speed → less heat per unit length, narrower bead.
- **Wire Feed Rate ($WFR$, m/min):** In MIG, directly coupled with current in CV mode. Higher WFR → higher current and higher deposition.

**2. Effects on Weld Characteristics (3 marks):**

| Parameter ↑ | Penetration | Bead Width | HI | DR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current ↑ | ↑↑↑ (strong) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| Voltage ↑ | ↑ (mild) | ↑↑ (strong) | ↑ | ↑ |
| Travel Speed ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ (strong) | — |

**Key Insights:**
- **Current** dominates penetration via electromagnetic pinch force.
- **Voltage** dominates bead width via arc spread.
- **Travel speed** inversely affects HI most strongly.
- Increasing **stick-out** (electrode extension) raises deposition through resistive heating: $E = I^2 R t$.

**3. Secondary Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Electrode polarity** — DCEP for most arc welding; DCEN for TIG of aluminum (provides cathodic cleaning).
- **Torch angle** — push angle (10–20° forward) gives deeper penetration; drag angle gives wider bead.
- **Gas flow rate** — too low → porosity; too high → turbulence and air ingress.

#### Part (b) — Heat Input Concept and Worked Example (7 marks)

**1. Definition of Heat Input (2 marks):**
Heat Input is the **arc energy transferred to the workpiece per unit length of weld**, accounting for the process efficiency. It governs HAZ microstructure, mechanical properties, and distortion.

$$HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]$$

**2. Significance (2 marks):**
- Controls **HAZ width, grain size, and toughness**
- Higher HI → softer, coarser HAZ (lower strength, lower toughness)
- Lower HI → harder, possibly brittle HAZ; risk of lack of fusion
- Codes specify maximum HI for given material/thickness (e.g., AWS D1.1, EN 10164)

**3. Worked Example (3 marks):**

> A MIG weld on **10 mm mild steel** uses $V = 25 \text{ V}$, $I = 240 \text{ A}$, $S = 4 \text{ mm/s}$. Given $\eta = 0.80$, calculate the heat input.

$$\begin{aligned}
HI &= \frac{0.80 \times 25 \times 240}{4 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{4800}{4000} \\
&= 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $HI = 1.2 \text{ kJ/mm}$ — within the acceptable range of **1.0–1.5 kJ/mm** for 10 mm mild steel multi-pass welding.

**Discussion:** This HI yields a balanced HAZ with adequate toughness, minimal distortion, and good fusion. For higher productivity, increasing current to **280 A** while keeping speed at **4 mm/s** would give $HI = 1.4 \text{ kJ/mm}$ — pushing toward the upper limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** If $S$ is in mm/min, formula becomes $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S_{\text{mm/min}} \cdot 1000}$. Many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Efficiency omission:** Always include $\eta$ in the calculation. Without it, the value is **arc energy**, not **heat input**.
> 3. **Confusion with travel direction:** Mention if it's single-pass or multi-pass; multi-pass welds have **interpass temperature** control which effectively resets the HI per pass.

---

### 5.3 KTU Quick-Reference Summary Table

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Input (HI) | Energy per unit length delivered to weld | 0.5–3.0 kJ/mm |
| Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) | Fraction of arc energy reaching workpiece | 0.60–0.90 |
| Deposition Rate (DR) | Mass of filler deposited per unit time | 1–8 kg/h (MIG) |
| Shielding Gas Flow | Volume of protective gas per minute | 7–20 L/min |
| Open Circuit Voltage | No-load voltage of power source | 50–80 V |
| Dew Point | Moisture indicator in shielding gas | ≤ −40 °C |
| Stick-Out | Electrode extension beyond contact tip | 10–25 mm |

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:**

- [x] **Welding environment** = atmospheric + physical + safety domains.
- [x] **Shielding gases**: Pure Ar (TIG, Al, SS), CO₂ (cheap MIG), Ar+CO₂ (general steel), Ar+O₂ (stainless MIG), Ar+He (high-speed SS).
- [x] **Equipment chain**: Power source → Wire feeder → Torch → Gas delivery → Cooling → Earth.
- [x] **Power source types**: Transformer, Rectifier, Inverter (modern, efficient), Engine-driven.
- [x] **CC vs CV**: CC for Stick/TIG; CV for MIG/FCAW/SAW.
- [x] **Four primary parameters**: Current ($I$), Voltage ($V$), Travel Speed ($S$), Wire Feed Rate (WFR).
- [x] **Current controls penetration**; **Voltage controls bead width**; **Travel speed controls HI inversely**.
- [x] **Heat Input formula**: $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ in **kJ/mm** when $S$ is in **mm/s**.
- [x] **Arc efficiency values**: SMAW = 0.75, TIG = 0.60, MIG = 0.80, SAW = 0.90.
- [x] **Dew point** of shielding gas must be **< −40 °C** for critical welds.
- [x] **Push-type wire feeder** for steel up to **5 m**; **push-pull** for aluminum and longer distances.
- [x] **Spool gun** used for soft aluminum wires to prevent bird-nesting.
- [x] **Heat input affects**: HAZ width, grain size, toughness, distortion, residual stress.
- [x] **Codes**: AWS D1.1, ASME IX, EN 10164, API 1104 all prescribe HI limits.
- [x] **Higher HI → coarser grains, lower toughness, more distortion**.
- [x] **Stick-out resistive heating**: $E = I^2 R L_{\text{ext}}$ — increases deposition in MIG.
- [x] **DCEP** is standard for most arc welding; **DCEN** used for TIG aluminum.
- [x] **Cooling time $t_{8/5}$** is a critical metallurgical parameter; controlled by HI and preheat.
- [x] **Penetration** increases with **square root of HI** but decreases with plate thickness.
- [x] Always mention **arc efficiency** explicitly in heat input calculations — examiners penalize its omission.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld Joint Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weld Joint Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Weld Joint Design?

**Weld Joint Design** is the systematic engineering process of determining the *type*, *size*, *length*, *configuration*, and *layout* of welds required to safely transmit applied loads between two or more structural members without exceeding the allowable stresses prescribed by codes (AWS D1.1, IS 816, IS 800). It bridges the gap between *manufacturing feasibility* and *structural integrity*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Verbatim):** *"Weld joint design involves the determination of weld size, length, throat area, and weld group geometry to ensure that the stresses developed in the weld under service loading do not exceed the permissible values of the weld metal."*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tailor's Stitching"

Think of welding as **sewing two pieces of thick denim (base metal) together**:

| Tailoring Concept | Welding Equivalent | Governing Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Thread thickness | **Leg size ($h$)** | Strength per unit length |
| Stitch length | **Weld length ($L$)** | Total load capacity |
| Stitch orientation (straight vs cross) | **Parallel vs Transverse weld** | Load direction sensitivity |
| Type of stitch (running lock) | **Fillet / Groove / Plug weld** | Load transfer mechanism |
| Thread material strength | **Electrode strength ($S_{ut}$)** | Allowable shear capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a tailor chooses a stronger thread (larger leg size) and longer stitch (longer weld) to carry a heavier load (axial force), a welding engineer selects weld parameters based on **load magnitude**, **load type** (static/dynamic/eccentric), and **joint geometry**.

---

### 1.3 Primary Objectives of Weld Joint Design

1. **Strength Adequacy** — Weld must transfer the design load without failure.
2. **Economy** — Minimum weld metal deposition (cost = filler metal + labour + time).
3. **Manufacturability** — Practical to execute (accessibility, position, distortion control).
4. **Code Compliance** — Adhere to AWS D1.1 / IS 816 / IS 800.
5. **Fatigue Resistance** — Avoid sharp transitions, re-entrant corners, and stress raisers.

---

### 1.4 Fundamental Classification of Welded Joints

The **AWS A2.4** standard recognizes **five basic joint types** that form the foundation of every design problem encountered in the KTU 2024 ESE:

- **Butt Joint** — Members lie in the *same plane*; loads are transferred through a *groove weld*. Highest efficiency (~100%).
- **Lap Joint** — Members *overlap*; loads transferred through *fillet welds* acting in *shear*.
- **Tee Joint** — One member is *perpendicular* to the other; common in brackets, stiffeners.
- **Corner Joint** — Two members meet at an *angle* (typically 90°); used in box sections, frames.
- **Edge Joint** — Members lie *parallel* with edges abutting; primarily for *thin sheets*.

---

### 1.5 Key Engineering Constants (Recall These!)

- **Throat thickness factor** — $t_t = 0.707 \, h$ (for 45° fillet weld geometry, i.e., $\cos 45° = \sin 45°$).
- **Equilateral fillet** — $h = w$ (leg sizes equal) → throat is the *minimum* dimension in the failure plane.
- **Standard electrode designations** — E60 ($\sigma_u = 430$ MPa), E70 ($\sigma_u = 490$ MPa), E90 ($\sigma_u = 620$ MPa), E110 ($\sigma_u = 760$ MPa).
- **AWS allowable shear in weld (static)** — $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \, S_{ut}$ of the **weld electrode** (NOT base metal).
- **Plate material vs weld metal** — The *weaker* of the two governs; usually the weld limits the design.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for allowable shear:** *"Thirty percent of ultimate, that's the static limit"*. Memorize $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \, S_{ut}$. For fatigue / cyclic loading, AWS permits only **$0.30 \times$ Class** stress, far more restrictive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Weld Design: Strength and Geometry

Every fillet weld design in the KTU syllabus reduces to **two questions**:

1. **How strong is the weld throat?** → Governed by $t_t = 0.707 \, h$ and $\tau_{allow}$.
2. **How much load can the throat carry?** → $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{allow}$.

For a **groove (butt) weld**, the failure plane coincides with the base-metal cross-section, hence the design reverts to **tensile / compressive strength of the weaker member**.

---

### 2.2 Fillet Weld Geometry — The 45° Failure Plane

A fillet weld is assumed to fail along a plane **inclined at 45°** to the legs of the weld (this is the *root* of the design code). The relevant dimensions are:

- $h$ = **Leg size** (the largest inscribed isosceles triangle's equal sides).
- $w$ = Actual weld face width (irrelevant for design).
- $t_t = 0.707 \, h$ = **Effective throat thickness** (failure plane).
- $L$ = **Total length of weld** (effective length for symmetric welds).
- $A_{throat} = 0.707 \, h \times L$ = **Throat area** carrying the shear.

> [!NOTE]
> Why 45°? The fillet weld is a triangular section. The plane of *minimum cross-sectional area* (which fails first under pure shear on the weld) makes 45° with the legs, giving area $A = h \cos 45° = 0.707 h$ per unit length. This is the classic **Mariotte / Saint-Venant** shear failure assumption embedded in AWS D1.1.

---

### 2.3 AWS Code Provisions — Size Limits

**Minimum Fillet Weld Size** (per AWS D1.1, Table 5.1) — to ensure the weld is large enough to develop *reasonable* strength without being so small it cracks from rapid cooling:

| Thickness of Thicker Plate, $T$ | Minimum Leg Size, $h_{min}$ |
|---|---|
| $T \le 6$ mm ($\tfrac{1}{4}$ in) | 3 mm ($\tfrac{1}{8}$ in) |
| $6 < T \le 13$ mm ($\tfrac{1}{2}$ in) | 5 mm ($\tfrac{3}{16}$ in) |
| $13 < T \le 19$ mm ($\tfrac{3}{4}$ in) | 6 mm ($\tfrac{1}{4}$ in) |
| $19 < T \le 38$ mm ($1\tfrac{1}{2}$ in) | 8 mm ($\tfrac{5}{16}$ in) |
| $38 < T \le 57$ mm ($2\tfrac{1}{4}$ in) | 10 mm ($\tfrac{3}{8}$ in) |
| $57 < T \le 150$ mm (6 in) | 12 mm ($\tfrac{1}{2}$ in) |
| $T > 150$ mm | 16 mm ($\tfrac{5}{8}$ in) |

**Maximum Fillet Weld Size** — to avoid melting the base plate edge:
- For $T < 6$ mm → $h_{max} = T$
- For $T \ge 6$ mm → $h_{max} = T - 1.5$ mm ($\tfrac{1}{16}$ in)

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_t = 0.707 \, h$ | Throat thickness of equilateral fillet weld | mm |
| 2 | $A_t = 0.707 \, h \, L$ | Throat area of a single fillet weld run | mm² |
| 3 | $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{allow}$ | Axial load capacity of fillet weld | N |
| 4 | $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \, S_{ut,\,weld}$ | AWS allowable shear in weld (static) | MPa |
| 5 | $\tau_{allow} = 0.40 \, S_y$ | Allowable shear (yield-based, AISC alt.) | MPa |
| 6 | $\tau_D = \dfrac{P}{A_t}$ | Direct (primary) shear stress in weld group | MPa |
| 7 | $T = P \cdot e$ | Torsional moment due to eccentric load | N·mm |
| 8 | $J = \sum (x^2 + y^2) \cdot \Delta L$ | Polar moment of inertia of weld group | mm³ |
| 9 | $\tau_T = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$ | Torsional shear at radius $r$ from centroid | MPa |
| 10 | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2}$ | Resultant shear at critical point | MPa |
| 11 | $h_{min}$ (from table) | AWS code minimum leg size | mm |
| 12 | $L_{eff} = L - 2w$ | Effective length (with return welds) | mm |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | Joint efficiency (butt weld ≈ 100%) | % |
| 14 | $t_e = t$ (full pen.) | Effective throat of full-penetration groove weld | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, all **absolute-value style** magnitudes (e.g., the radius $r$) are kept as raw variables; design code references use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation but here they are simplified for clarity.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessel fabrication** (ASME Section VIII) uses fillet welds extensively for nozzle reinforcements.
- **Structural steel buildings** (IS 800, AISC 360) use *eccentrically loaded weld groups* for beam-to-column brackets.
- **Shipbuilding & bridges** require *fatigue-rated welds* with $h_{min}$ strictly enforced to prevent crack initiation.
- **Automotive chassis** — Resistance spot welds (a special fillet-type) are designed via shear-only failure criteria identical in philosophy to the AWS formula $P = 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why 0.707?** Because $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$. The throat of an equilateral fillet weld is the altitude of an isosceles right triangle. Geometrically, $t_t = h \cdot \cos 45° = h / \sqrt{2}$.

---

### 2.6 Eccentric Loading — The Most Tested KTU Concept

When a load $P$ is applied **outside the centroid** of a weld group, the weld experiences:

1. **Direct shear** $\tau_D = P / A_t$ — uniform across the weld.
2. **Torsional shear** $\tau_T = T \cdot r / J$ — variable, maximum at the **point farthest from the centroid**.

The **resultant shear** at the critical (most distant) point is:

$$\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2} \le \tau_{allow}$$

The location of the **centroid** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ for an unsymmetric weld group is found using the first moment of weld length:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i \, L_i}{\sum L_i}, \qquad \bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum y_i \, L_i}{\sum L_i}$$

For **symmetric weld groups** (C-shape, L-shape, rectangular box), the centroid is at the geometric center, and $J$ can be computed using standard formulas for lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Throat Thickness of an Equilateral Fillet Weld

Consider an equilateral fillet weld of leg size $h$. The failure plane is assumed to be the plane of *minimum cross-sectional area*, which is inclined at 45° to the legs.

The altitude of an isosceles right triangle with legs $h$ is:

$$t_t = h \cdot \cos 45° = h \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{h}{1.4142} = 0.7071 \, h$$

For practical engineering, the throat is reported as $0.707 \, h$ (or sometimes $0.70 \, h$ for conservative design).

> The plane of failure is at 45° because the **pure shear** on the throat (no normal stress) gives the lowest resistance — von Mises yield criterion confirms this is the critical plane for a ductile weld metal.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Axial Load Capacity of a Fillet Weld

Let a parallel fillet weld of length $L$ and leg size $h$ carry an axial tensile load $P$ on the parent plate.

**Step 1:** Identify the failure plane.
The failure occurs by shear along the throat plane. The throat area is:

$$A_{throat} = t_t \times L = 0.707 \, h \times L$$

**Step 2:** Apply the allowable shear stress criterion.
The shear stress in the throat must not exceed the AWS allowable:

$$\tau = \dfrac{P}{A_{throat}} = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, h \, L} \le \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the design parameter.
For a *given* load $P$ and *unknown* size $h$:

$$h = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, L \, \tau_{allow}}$$

For a *given* load and *unknown* length $L$:

$$L = \dfrac{P}{0.707 \, h \, \tau_{allow}}$$

**Step 4:** Adjust for the AWS allowable.
The AWS permissible shear (static) is:

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times S_{ut,\,weld\,electrode}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pitfall:** Use $S_{ut}$ of the **weld electrode**, NOT the base plate. A common KTU examiner complaint is students writing $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times S_{ut,\,plate}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Polar Moment of Inertia of a Weld Group

For an unsymmetric weld group with $n$ straight-line segments, the **polar moment of inertia** about the centroidal axis is:

$$J = J_x + J_y$$

where:

$$J_x = \sum \left[ \dfrac{L_i \, (y_i^2)}{12} + L_i \, y_c^2 \right]$$

$$J_y = \sum \left[ \dfrac{L_i \, (x_i^2)}{12} + L_i \, x_c^2 \right]$$

**Reasoning:**
- Each weld segment $L_i$ is treated as a *line* of zero cross-section (since fillet welds are 1D in plan view).
- For a line segment, the *local* moment of inertia about its own centroid is $L_i^3 / 12$.
- The *parallel-axis* shift moves it to the global centroid.

**Final polar moment:**

$$J = \sum \left[ \dfrac{L_i^3}{12} + L_i \, (x_c^2 + y_c^2) \right]$$

where $x_c$ and $y_c$ are the coordinates of the *centroid of each segment* measured from the *weld-group centroid*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result $J$ has units of **mm³** (length cubed), not mm⁴, because the weld is treated as a *line* of unit width. This is a common student confusion.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Fillet Weld in a Lap Joint

**Problem Statement:**
Two mild-steel plates, each **10 mm thick** and **80 mm wide**, are joined by **two parallel fillet welds** (one on each side of the lap) in a lap joint. The plates are of grade **S235** ($S_{ut} = 410$ MPa) and the electrode is **E43** ($S_{ut} = 430$ MPa). Determine the *required weld size* and *minimum weld length* to transmit a factored tensile load of **120 kN** along the plate.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the AWS allowable shear in the weld.**

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times S_{ut,\,E43} = 0.30 \times 430 = 129 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the load-capacity formula for TWO parallel fillet welds.**

$$P = 2 \times 0.707 \, h \, L \, \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 3 — Choose a preliminary leg size and solve for length.**

Let us choose $h = 6$ mm (governed by code min for $T = 10$ mm → $h_{min} = 5$ mm).

$$L = \dfrac{P}{2 \times 0.707 \times h \times \tau_{allow}}$$

$$L = \dfrac{120{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 6 \times 129}$$

$$L = \dfrac{120{,}000}{1094.4} = 109.65 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the AWS length rule.**

The effective length should be at least $4 \times h = 4 \times 6 = 24$ mm, and the actual length at least $4 h$ plus return welds. Adopt $L = 110$ mm.

**Step 5 — Verify base metal strength.**

The base plate cross-section is $A = 80 \times 10 = 800$ mm². The allowable tensile stress in the base plate (with $\sigma_{allow} = 0.60 \, S_y$, $S_y = 235$ MPa) is:

$$\sigma_{allow,plate} = 0.60 \times 235 = 141 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$P_{plate} = 141 \times 800 = 112{,}800 \, \text{N} = 112.8 \, \text{kN}$$

Since the applied load (120 kN) > 112.8 kN, the *plate* is the weaker link. The problem requires the *plate* to be redesigned, not the weld. The weld as designed is **safe**, but the joint efficiency is:

$$\eta = \dfrac{112.8}{120} \times 100 = 94\%$$

> **Design Insight:** A common KTU design issue is that the *weld* is over-designed while the *plate* is under-designed. Always check *both* failure paths.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Eccentrically Loaded C-Shape Weld

**Problem Statement:**
A **bracket** is welded to a column flange using **three fillet welds** forming a **C-shape**:
- Two vertical welds: $L_1 = L_2 = 150$ mm
- One horizontal weld: $L_3 = 200$ mm
- Leg size $h = 8$ mm
- Load $P = 60$ kN applied at eccentricity $e = 200$ mm from the centroid of the weld group.
- Electrode E60 ($S_{ut} = 430$ MPa).

Determine if the weld is safe.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the AWS allowable shear.**

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times 430 = 129 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Locate the centroid of the weld group.**

Place the origin at the geometric center (the open end of the C). The symmetry is about the vertical axis, so $\bar{x} = 0$ for the whole group. But for the *C-shape with one horizontal leg*:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum L_i \, x_i}{\sum L_i} = \dfrac{(150)(0) + (150)(0) + (200)(-100)}{150 + 150 + 200}$$

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{-20{,}000}{500} = -40 \, \text{mm}$$

The horizontal weld at the bottom lies 100 mm below the verticals' mid-height. With the centroid shifted by 40 mm, the *critical* point is the **farthest** weld from the centroid — the top of the vertical welds (at $\bar{x} = 0$, $y = 75 + 40 = 115$ mm) and the bottom-horizontal far end (at $x = -200$ mm, $y = -100 - (-100) = 0$? — coordinate transform required).

After transforming coordinates to the centroid:
- Critical point A (top of left vertical): $r_A = \sqrt{0^2 + 115^2} = 115$ mm
- Critical point B (top of right vertical): same as A by symmetry.
- Critical point C (far end of horizontal): $r_C = \sqrt{(-100 - (-40))^2 + (-100 - (-100))^2}$? — exact coordinates need careful sign tracking.

For brevity (full numerical treatment in Section 5 Question Bank), the **polar moment of inertia** of this C-group is computed next.

**Step 3 — Compute the polar moment of inertia $J$.**

$$J = \sum \left[ \dfrac{L_i^3}{12} + L_i \, (x_c^2 + y_c^2) \right]$$

For the two vertical welds (each 150 mm long, $y_c = \pm 40$ mm from centroid, $x_c = 0$):

$$J_{vert,\,pair} = 2 \times \left[ \dfrac{150^3}{12} + 150 \times (0^2 + 40^2) \right]$$

$$J_{vert,\,pair} = 2 \times \left[ 281{,}250 + 240{,}000 \right] = 2 \times 521{,}250 = 1{,}042{,}500 \, \text{mm}^3$$

For the horizontal weld (200 mm long, $x_c = -100$ mm from centroid, $y_c = -100$ mm):

$$J_{horiz} = \dfrac{200^3}{12} + 200 \times ((-100)^2 + (-100)^2)$$

$$J_{horiz} = 666{,}667 + 200 \times 20{,}000 = 666{,}667 + 4{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}666{,}667 \, \text{mm}^3$$

$$J_{total} = 1{,}042{,}500 + 4{,}666{,}667 = 5{,}709{,}167 \, \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute the direct shear.**

Total throat area:

$$A_t = 0.707 \times h \times \sum L_i = 0.707 \times 8 \times 500 = 2{,}828 \, \text{mm}^2$$

$$\tau_D = \dfrac{P}{A_t} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{2{,}828} = 21.22 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the torsional moment and torsional shear.**

$$T = P \times e = 60{,}000 \times 200 = 12 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}$$

The **critical radius** $r$ is the distance from centroid to the most distant weld point.

$$r_{max} = 115 \, \text{mm (top of vertical)}$$

$$\tau_T = \dfrac{T \times r_{max}}{J} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^6 \times 115}{5{,}709{,}167} = 241.7 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the stresses.**

$$\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2} = \sqrt{21.22^2 + 241.7^2}$$

$$\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{450 + 58{,}418} = \sqrt{58{,}868} = 242.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with allowable.**

$$\tau_{resultant} = 242.6 \, \text{MPa} \gg \tau_{allow} = 129 \, \text{MPa} \implies \textbf{NOT SAFE}$$

**Step 8 — Re-design.** Increase $h$ to 12 mm and recompute (or move the load closer to the centroid). This is left as a design exercise in the question bank.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error in Step 5:** Using the *total* weld length $L = 500$ mm for $r$ (no — $r$ is the *radial distance* from centroid to the **farthest point**, not the weld length). Always compute $r_{max}$ from the centroid to the **most remote weld endpoint**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Parallel vs Transverse Fillet Weld

| Property | Parallel Fillet Weld | Transverse Fillet Weld |
|---|---|---|
| Load direction | Along weld length | Perpendicular to weld length |
| Stress in throat | Pure shear | Combined shear + normal |
| Actual strength | ~70% of transverse | Higher (transverse is ~30% stronger) |
| AWS design strength | Uses same $\tau_{allow}$ | Uses same $\tau_{allow}$ |
| Stiffness | More flexible | Stiffer, less distortion |
| Typical use | Lap joints in shear | Cruciform, T-joints |
| Failure mode | Shear in throat | Shear in throat (assumed) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Weld Design Procedure (Eccentrically Loaded Weld Group)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Load P, Eccentricity e, Weld Size h] --> stepB[Step 1: Identify Weld Group Geometry]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Compute Centroid bar x_bar, y_bar from first moment of weld lengths]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Calculate Total Throat Area A_t = 0.707 h times Sum L_i]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Compute Direct Shear tau_D = P over A_t]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Compute Torsional Moment T = P times e]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Calculate Polar Moment of Inertia J for weld group]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Identify Critical Point Max radius r_max from centroid]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Compute Torsional Shear tau_T = T times r_max over J]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Calculate Resultant Shear tau_R = sqrt tau_D squared plus tau_T squared]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Compare with AWS Allowable tau_allow = 0.30 S_ut]
    stepK --> stepL{Is tau_R less than or equal tau_allow ?}
    stepL -->|Yes| stepM[Design is SAFE. Document h, L, Electrode]
    stepL -->|No| stepN[Increase h or Add Weld Length and Recompute]
    stepN --> stepB
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fillet Weld Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Base Plate 1] --> nodeB[Fusion Zone]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Base Plate 2]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Leg h, horizontal"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Leg h, vertical"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Throat t_t = 0.707 h, inclined 45 deg"]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Root at interface]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Face of Weld, slightly convex]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Failure Plane: Pure Shear on Throat]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[AWS Design Basis]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Classification of Welded Joints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1[Welded Joints] --> type1[Butt Joint - Same Plane]
    root1 --> type2[Lap Joint - Overlap]
    root1 --> type3[Tee Joint - 90 Degree]
    root1 --> type4[Corner Joint - Angle]
    root1 --> type5[Edge Joint - Parallel Edges]

    type1 --> weld1[Groove Weld: V, U, J, Square, Bevel]
    type2 --> weld2[Fillet Weld: Parallel or Transverse]
    type3 --> weld3[Fillet or Groove Weld]
    type4 --> weld4[Fillet or Groove Weld]
    type5 --> weld5[Fillet or Groove Weld]

    weld2 --> app1[Applications: Bracket to Column, Beam Splices]
    weld1 --> app2[Applications: Pressure Vessels, Pipe Welding]
    weld3 --> app3[Applications: Stiffeners, Shipbuilding]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Weld Symbol Interpretation (AWS A2.4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refLine[Reference Line] --> arrow1[Arrow Side Symbol]
    refLine --> otherSide[Other Side Symbol]
    refLine --> tailInfo[Tail: Spec, Process, Electrode]

    arrow1 --> filletSym[Fillet Weld Triangle]
    arrow1 --> grooveSym[Groove Weld Letter: V U J]
    arrow1 --> plugSym[Plug or Slot Weld]

    filletSym --> dim1[Left of Symbol: Leg Size h]
    filletSym --> dim2[Right of Symbol: Length L of Weld]
    filletSym --> dim3[Below Symbol: Pitch for Intermittent Welds]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above are **engineering schematics** of design logic, not to-scale engineering drawings. For actual shop-floor weld symbol interpretation, refer to AWS A2.4 standard symbols and consult the laboratory manual of PEMT416.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**

> Define a **fillet weld**. With a neat sketch, label the terms *leg size*, *throat thickness*, and *root*. State the relationship between throat thickness and leg size for an equilateral fillet weld.

**Model Answer:**

A **fillet weld** is a weld of approximately triangular cross-section joining two surfaces meeting at an angle between 60° and 120° (commonly 90°). The relevant geometric terms are:

- **Leg size ($h$):** The largest equal-sided dimension of the triangular cross-section.
- **Throat thickness ($t_t$):** The minimum distance from the root (junction of the two base-metal surfaces) to the hypotenuse (weld face) of the triangle.
- **Root:** The point where the two base-metal surfaces intersect before welding.

For an **equilateral fillet weld** (legs equal, $h$ = $w$), the throat lies at 45° to each leg:

$$t_t = h \cos 45° = 0.707 \, h$$

```
        |\
        | \
   h    |  \  t_t = 0.707 h
        |   \
        |____\
          h
```

(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark sketch with labels, 1 mark relation)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**

> State the **AWS code provisions** for the minimum size of a fillet weld for plates of thickness 6 mm, 15 mm, 25 mm, and 50 mm. Justify why such a minimum is enforced.

**Model Answer:**

| Plate Thickness $T$ (mm) | AWS Minimum Leg Size $h_{min}$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| 6 | 3 |
| 15 | 6 |
| 25 | 8 |
| 50 | 10 |

**Justification:** The minimum size is enforced to ensure that the weld metal has sufficient mass to dissipate heat and avoid **rapid cooling (chilling effect)**. A very small weld on a thick plate cools too fast, leading to a **hardened, brittle martensitic microstructure** prone to cracking. The minimum-size rule prevents this by ensuring the weld is *not too small* relative to the surrounding heat sink of the base plate.

(3 marks — 1 mark table, 1 mark correct values, 1 mark justification)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** *Answer any one full question from the choice.* Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Two steel plates of size **120 mm × 12 mm** are to be joined by a **double-fillet parallel lap weld**. The weld electrode is **E60** ($S_{ut} = 430$ MPa). Determine the **length of weld** required on **each side** to transmit a factored axial load of **150 kN** along the plate. Assume the weaker member governs the design.

**(b)** A **bracket** is welded to a column using an **L-shaped fillet weld group** of leg size **10 mm**, comprising a vertical weld of length 200 mm and a horizontal weld of length 200 mm, with the load applied at the **free end of the bracket** at an eccentricity of **300 mm** from the centroid of the weld group. The applied load is **80 kN** and the electrode is **E70** ($S_{ut} = 490$ MPa). Verify if the weld is safe. If not, suggest a corrected design.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** AWS allowable shear in the weld:
$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times 430 = 129 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the double-fillet load formula:
$$P = 2 \times 0.707 \times h \times L \times \tau_{allow}$$

Let $h = 6$ mm (minimum for $T = 12$ mm plate).

$$L = \dfrac{P}{2 \times 0.707 \times h \times \tau_{allow}} = \dfrac{150{,}000}{2 \times 0.707 \times 6 \times 129}$$

$$L = \dfrac{150{,}000}{1094.4} = 137.06 \, \text{mm}$$

Adopt $L = 140$ mm on each side.

**Step 3:** Verify the base-metal strength (weaker member).
Allowable tensile stress in base plate (S235, $S_y = 235$ MPa):
$$\sigma_{allow} = 0.60 \times 235 = 141 \, \text{MPa}$$
$$P_{plate} = 141 \times 120 \times 12 = 203{,}040 \, \text{N} = 203.04 \, \text{kN}$$

Since $P = 150$ kN $< 203.04$ kN, the **base plate is safe**.

**Conclusion:** Weld length = **140 mm on each side**, with leg size 6 mm.

> **(7 marks: 2 for allowable, 3 for calculation, 1 for base-metal check, 1 for final answer)**

---

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** AWS allowable shear:
$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times 490 = 147 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Locate the centroid of the L-shaped weld group.
Place origin at the corner. By symmetry (since both segments are 200 mm, one along $x$, one along $y$):
$$\bar{x} = \bar{y} = \dfrac{200 \times 100 + 200 \times 100}{400} = 100 \, \text{mm from the corner}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate polar moment of inertia $J$.
For each segment, $L_i = 200$ mm, with centroid at distance 100 mm from the group centroid.

$$J = 2 \times \left[ \dfrac{200^3}{12} + 200 \times (100^2 + 0^2) \right]$$

(Note: each segment's own centroid lies along one axis; for the horizontal segment, $\bar{x}_c = 100$, $\bar{y}_c = 0$; for the vertical, $\bar{x}_c = 0$, $\bar{y}_c = 100$. Summing both gives the same $J$.)

$$J = 2 \times \left[ 666{,}667 + 2{,}000{,}000 \right] = 2 \times 2{,}666{,}667 = 5{,}333{,}333 \, \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 4:** Compute direct shear.
$$A_t = 0.707 \times 10 \times 400 = 2{,}828 \, \text{mm}^2$$
$$\tau_D = \dfrac{80{,}000}{2{,}828} = 28.29 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compute torsional moment and torsional shear.
$$T = 80{,}000 \times 300 = 24 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}$$

The **critical point** is the far end of the horizontal weld: at distance from centroid $= \sqrt{(200-100)^2 + (0-100)^2} = \sqrt{10{,}000 + 10{,}000} = 141.4$ mm.

$$\tau_T = \dfrac{24 \times 10^6 \times 141.4}{5{,}333{,}333} = \dfrac{3.394 \times 10^9}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 636.4 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Combine stresses.
$$\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{28.29^2 + 636.4^2} = \sqrt{800 + 405{,}005} = \sqrt{405{,}805} = 637.0 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 7:** Compare with allowable.
$$\tau_{resultant} = 637.0 \, \text{MPa} \gg \tau_{allow} = 147 \, \text{MPa} \implies \textbf{NOT SAFE}$$

**Step 8 — Re-design:** Either:
- Increase $h$ to $\sim 25$ mm (cubic scaling of $h$ in $J$ will help slightly), **or**
- Add a **return weld** at the open end of the L (closing it into a box), **or**
- Shift the load closer to the weld centroid (reduce $e$).

> **(7 marks: 1 for allowable, 2 for $J$, 1 for $\tau_D$, 1 for $\tau_T$, 1 for resultant, 1 for design recommendation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **five basic types of welded joints** as per AWS classification. State **two advantages** and **one limitation** of fillet welds over groove welds.

**(b)** A circular bracket of **diameter 200 mm** is welded to a column using a **circumferential fillet weld** of leg size 8 mm. A vertical load of **40 kN** is applied at a distance of **250 mm** from the centroid of the weld circle. Electrode is **E60**. Check the adequacy of the weld.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Solution (a):** *(Sketch-based, 7 marks distribution: 4 for sketches, 2 for advantages/limitations, 1 for neatness)*

The five AWS joint types are:

1. **Butt joint** — Two members in the same plane, joined by a groove weld.
2. **Lap joint** — Two overlapping members joined by fillet welds along the edges.
3. **T-joint** — Two members at 90°, with a fillet or groove weld at the junction.
4. **Corner joint** — Two members at an angle, used for box or frame structures.
5. **Edge joint** — Two parallel members with edges abutting; used for thin sheets.

**Advantages of fillet welds over groove welds:**
1. No edge preparation required (no beveling), saving time and cost.
2. Can accommodate larger gaps and minor misalignments between plates.
3. Easier to inspect visually.

**Limitation of fillet welds:**
- Lower fatigue strength than full-penetration groove welds due to stress concentration at the weld root.

> **(7 marks: 2 for joint sketches, 2 for advantages, 1 for limitation, 2 for clarity of presentation)**

---

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Allowable shear.
$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times 430 = 129 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Weld is a circle of diameter $D = 200$ mm → radius $R = 100$ mm.
Weld length: $L = \pi D = \pi \times 200 = 628.3$ mm.

**Step 3:** Centroid is at the **center of the circle**. For a circular weld line, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = 2 \pi R^3 = 2 \pi \times 100^3 = 6.283 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^3$$

(Derivation: $J = \int r^2 \, dL = \int_0^{2\pi} R^2 \cdot R \, d\theta = 2\pi R^3$.)

**Step 4:** Direct shear.
$$A_t = 0.707 \times 8 \times 628.3 = 3{,}554 \, \text{mm}^2$$
$$\tau_D = \dfrac{40{,}000}{3{,}554} = 11.26 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Torsional moment.
$$T = 40{,}000 \times 250 = 10^7 \, \text{N·mm}$$
$$r_{max} = R = 100 \, \text{mm}$$
$$\tau_T = \dfrac{10^7 \times 100}{6.283 \times 10^6} = 159.2 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Resultant shear at the critical point (on the weld circle, on the line of action of the load):
$$\tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{11.26^2 + 159.2^2} = \sqrt{127 + 25{,}345} = \sqrt{25{,}472} = 159.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 7:** Compare with allowable.
$$\tau_{resultant} = 159.6 \, \text{MPa} > \tau_{allow} = 129 \, \text{MPa} \implies \textbf{NOT SAFE (by 24%)}$$

**Step 8 — Re-design:** Increase $h$ to **10 mm** → $\tau_T$ scales with $1/h$ and $\tau_D$ with $1/h$ (since $A_t$ scales with $h$). New resultant:
$$\tau_{resultant,new} = 159.6 \times \dfrac{8}{10} = 127.7 \, \text{MPa} < 129 \, \text{MPa} \implies \textbf{SAFE}$$

> **(7 marks: 1 allowable, 1.5 $J$, 1 $\tau_D$, 1.5 $\tau_T$, 1 resultant, 1 re-design)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Wrong allowable stress formula** — Using $0.30 \times S_{ut,\,plate}$ instead of $0.30 \times S_{ut,\,weld\,electrode}$ → **–1 to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to find the centroid** of an unsymmetric weld group and using the geometric corner as origin → entire $\tau_T$ is wrong → **–3 to 4 marks**.
> 3. **Adding direct and torsional shears algebraically** (instead of vectorially, $\tau_R = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2}$) → **–1 mark**.
> 4. **Neglecting the base-metal strength check** in a fillet weld design → **–1 mark**.
> 5. **Using $h$ from the weld symbol directly** without checking AWS minimum → **–0.5 mark** (especially for "design" type questions).
> 6. **Sign errors** in coordinate transformation when the centroid is offset → **–1 to 2 marks**.
> 7. **Failing to specify the electrode** (E60 / E70 / E90) explicitly in the final answer → **–0.5 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weld Joint Design**

**Core Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
- $\quad t_t = 0.707 \, h$ (throat thickness)
- $\quad A_t = 0.707 \, h \times L$ (throat area)
- $\quad P = 0.707 \, h \times L \times \tau_{allow}$ (load capacity)
- $\quad \tau_{allow} = 0.30 \times S_{ut,\,weld}$ (AWS static)
- $\quad \tau_{resultant} = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2}$ (combined)
- $\quad \tau_T = T \cdot r / J$ (torsional shear)
- $\quad J = \sum [L_i^3 / 12 + L_i(x_c^2 + y_c^2)]$ (polar moment of weld line)

**AWS Code Memory Anchors:**
- Always use the **electrode $S_{ut}$**, never the base-plate $S_{ut}$.
- $h_{min}$ depends on the **thicker** plate.
- $h_{max} = T - 1.5$ mm for $T \ge 6$ mm.
- Effective length = actual length (if no return welds) or actual $- 2w$ (with returns).
- Weld must be at least $4h$ long, otherwise increase $h$.

**Eccentric Loading Master Steps:**
1. Locate **centroid** of weld group (use first moment of $L_i$).
2. Compute $A_t$ (total throat area).
3. Compute $J$ (polar moment of inertia of weld lines).
4. Find $r_{max}$ to the critical (farthest) point.
5. Apply $\tau_D = P/A_t$ and $\tau_T = T \cdot r_{max} / J$.
6. Combine as $\tau_R = \sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2}$.
7. Compare with $\tau_{allow}$.
8. If unsafe → increase $h$ / add welds / reduce eccentricity.

**Common Exam Pitfalls:**
- Skipping the centroid calculation for unsymmetric weld groups.
- Using $\tau_D + \tau_T$ (arithmetic) instead of $\sqrt{\tau_D^2 + \tau_T^2}$.
- Forgetting to check the **base metal** strength alongside the weld.
- Not verifying the **AWS minimum leg size** for the plate thickness.
- For circular welds, remembering $J = 2\pi R^3$ (not $2\pi R^4$ — there is no area).

**High-Yield Design Heuristics:**
- For **lap joints in pure shear** → use parallel fillet, simple formula suffices.
- For **brackets and moment connections** → always use eccentric load analysis.
- For **cyclic / fatigue loading** → use Class details (A, B, C, D, E, F) per AWS; allowable is much lower (≈ $70$ MPa for E60 at 2 million cycles).
- For **cost reduction** → intermittent welds are permitted if strength is adequate (use the pitch $p$ in the weld symbol).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Applications of Advanced Metal Joining Processes

The **industrial applications of advanced metal joining techniques** refer to the systematic deployment of modern welding, brazing, soldering, and solid-state joining processes across critical engineering sectors where conventional fusion welding fails to meet performance demands. These applications are governed by metallurgical compatibility, joint efficiency, service environment, production volume, and economic viability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Application* in the context of advanced metal joining is defined as the engineering deployment of processes such as **Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Ultrasonic Welding (USW), Diffusion Bonding (DB), and Explosion Welding (EXW)** in aerospace, automotive, marine, nuclear, petrochemical, electronics, and structural fabrication industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

**Analogy — The "Surgical Stitch" vs. "Emergency Tape" Concept:**

Imagine joining two broken metal components. Conventional arc welding is like applying **emergency duct tape** — fast, cheap, but leaves a heat-affected zone (HAZ) and residual stress, much like a messy bandage.

Advanced joining techniques, on the other hand, are like a **precision surgical stitch**:
- **FSW** = solid-state suturing without melting the tissue
- **LBW/EBW** = laser micro-surgery for delicate incisions
- **Diffusion Bonding** = biological tissue grafting at the molecular scale
- **Explosion Welding** = high-velocity impact fusion, similar to cold-welding under shock

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:**
> Each advanced process is selected based on **four pillars**: (1) Base material compatibility, (2) Required joint strength, (3) Service environment (corrosive, cryogenic, high-temperature), and (4) Production economics. A KTU examiner expects you to **map a process to its industrial sector with justification**.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants & Benchmarks)

- **Welding Productivity Benchmark:** $1 \text{ m/min}$ traverse speed (FSW for aluminium alloys)
- **Vacuum Requirement (EBW):** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ **torr**
- **Power Density Hierarchy:** Conventional arc $\approx 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2$ < LBW $\approx 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$ < EBW $\approx 10^7 \text{ W/mm}^2$
- **Explosion Weldability Window (W):** $1 \leq W \leq 12$ where $W = \frac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m}$ (dimensionless)
- **Diffusion Bonding Pressure Range:** $1$ to $50 \text{ MPa}$
- **Ultrasonic Frequency Range:** $20$ to $70 \text{ kHz}$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always quote the power density hierarchy — it is a high-yield KTU question and differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Process-to-Application Mapping

The selection of an advanced joining process for a given application follows a **three-stage decision matrix**:

### Stage 1 — Material Compatibility Analysis
- **Aluminium & Magnesium alloys** → FSW, USW, LBW (low thermal distortion required)
- **Titanium alloys** → EBW, LBW, Diffusion Bonding (reactive at high temperatures)
- **Dissimilar metals** → Explosion Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Diffusion Bonding
- **Refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta)** → EBW (in vacuum), Laser Welding
- **Stainless steels (austenitic)** → FSW, LBW, Plasma arc, A-TIG
- **Carbon & alloy steels** → Electron Beam, Laser-Arc Hybrid, Submerged Arc

### Stage 2 — Service Environment Analysis
- **Cryogenic (LNG, $-196\,^\circ\text{C}$)** → EBW, FSW (no brittle phases)
- **Nuclear (radiation, corrosion)** → EBW, LBW, narrow-gap TIG
- **Offshore/Marine (seawater)** → FSW, Friction Stir Processing (FSP), Underwater hyperbaric welding
- **Aerospace (fatigue, cyclic loading)** → FSW (butt & lap), LBW, Diffusion Bonding
- **Automotive (high-volume)** → Resistance spot, Laser welding ( $> 50$ spots/min$)$
- **Electronics (miniaturization)** → USW, Thermocompression bonding, Soldering

### Stage 3 — Production Economics & Joint Geometry
- **Linear joints (long seams)** → FSW, LBW, SAW, PAW
- **Circular/circumferential joints** → EBW (girth welds), LBW, FSW
- **Lap joints (sheet metal)** → Resistance spot, USW, FSW, Laser spot
- **T-joints & fillet joints** → FSW, Hybrid laser-arc, MAG
- **Tube-to-tubesheet** → Explosive welding, Roll bonding, TIG with backing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Each Process Fits Its Application — The "Why" Behind the "How":**
> - **FSW** is solid-state → eliminates solidification cracks → ideal for **aluminium aerospace fuselages** where HAZ softening is unacceptable.
> - **EBW** operates in vacuum with concentrated heat input → minimal contamination → ideal for **reactive metals (Ti, Zr) in nuclear and aerospace**.
> - **LBW** provides high speed and low distortion via keyhole welding → ideal for **automotive tailor-welded blanks (TWBs)** and **battery pack welding for EVs**.
> - **Explosion Welding** creates metallurgical bonds at sub-microsecond timescales → ideal for **bimetallic clads (Ti-steel, Cu-Al, SS-carbon steel)** in chemical and marine industries.
> - **Diffusion Bonding** produces joints with parent-metal strength → ideal for **turbine blades, honeycomb structures, and aerospace sandwich panels**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exams (Module 3 — Applications)**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application Context** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Power density at the weld spot | Comparing LBW vs. EBW vs. arc | $\text{W/mm}^2$ |
| $H_{in} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length | Process selection for distortion control | $\text{J/mm}$ |
| $W = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m}$ | Explosion welding window (Weldability) | Clad plate production (ASTM B898) | Dimensionless |
| $R_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot k}{Q}$ | Thermal resistance in diffusion bonding | DB of Ti, superalloys | $\text{K/W}$ |
| $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion equation | Diffusion bonding kinetics | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $T_{tool} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot N \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot r_m}{60}$ | FSW tool torque (approximate) | FSW process design | $\text{N·m}$ |
| $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises stress under tool shoulder | FSW plastic deformation analysis | $\text{MPa}$ |
| $v_p = 2 v_c \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Collision point velocity in EXW | Explosion welding jetting | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Electrical resistance (USW, RSW) | Spot weld heat generation | $\Omega$ |
| $\text{PSR} = \dfrac{4 f^2 d^2}{K}$ | Power supply rating (sonic welding) | Ultrasonic welding design | $\text{VA}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In any KTU numerical involving power density, always express area in **$\text{mm}^2$** and power in **W** to obtain $\text{W/mm}^2$. For explosion welding, $\rho$ is in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $v_p$ in $\text{m/s}$, and $R_m$ in $\text{Pa}$ (must be consistent SI).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace Industry:** Boeing 787, Airbus A350, and SpaceX Falcon 9 extensively use FSW for fuselage and cryogenic tank panels.
2. **Automotive Industry:** Tesla, BMW, and Volkswagen deploy **laser-welded tailored blanks** for weight reduction and crash-energy absorption.
3. **Nuclear Industry:** ITER and BHAVINI (India) use **EBW** for vacuum-tight austenitic stainless steel vessels.
4. **Marine Industry:** LNG carriers (LNG Carrier $Q$-Max class) use **9% Ni steel joints** produced by SAW + EBW hybrid routes.
5. **Electronics Industry:** PCB component mounting relies on **ultrasonic and thermocompression bonding** for sub-millimetre pitch interconnects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Explosion Welding Weldability Window

The **weldability window** is the foundational equation in EXW applications, determining whether a clean metallurgical bond forms without melting or brittle-phase formation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define the collision parameters.** When a flyer plate driven by an explosive charge strikes a parent plate at velocity $v_p$ at an angle $\theta$, the dynamic pressure at the collision point is:

$$P_{dyn} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_p^2$$

Here, $\rho$ is the flyer plate density and $v_p$ is the collision (plate) velocity. This is the **kinetic pressure** developed at the jetting zone.

**Step 2: Compare to the material's flow strength.** For a successful bond, the dynamic pressure must exceed the yield strength (or ultimate tensile strength for safety) of the weaker plate:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_p^2 \geq R_m$$

Rearranging this inequality to form a **dimensionless weldability parameter** $W$:

$$W = \frac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m} \geq 1$$

**Step 3: Establish the upper bound.** If $W > 12$, the collision energy is excessive, leading to melting, spall, or brittle intermetallic formation. Therefore, the **acceptable window** for industrial applications is:

$$1 \leq W \leq 12$$

**Step 4: Practical application example.** A titanium Grade 2 plate ($\rho = 4500 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $R_m = 345 \text{ MPa}$) is to be explosion-welded to a carbon steel backing plate. Calculate the permissible flyer velocity range.

**Sub-step 4a — Lower bound (minimum velocity):**
Set $W = 1$:

$$v_{p,min} = \sqrt{\frac{2 R_m}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 345 \times 10^6}{4500}}$$

$$v_{p,min} = \sqrt{1.533 \times 10^5} = 391.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Sub-step 4b — Upper bound (maximum velocity):**
Set $W = 12$:

$$v_{p,max} = \sqrt{12 \times \frac{2 R_m}{\rho}} = v_{p,min} \times \sqrt{12} = 391.6 \times 3.464$$

$$v_{p,max} = 1356.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** For this Ti-steel EXW application, the flyer plate velocity must be controlled between **$391.6 \text{ m/s}$** and **$1356.5 \text{ m/s}$** — this is the **operational design window** for the explosive charge mass selection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density Comparison Across Advanced Processes

This is a frequently tested KTU concept: ranking processes by power density to justify application selection.

### Analytical Derivation:

**Step 1: General definition.** Power density is energy flux per unit area:

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{eff}}$$

where $A_{eff}$ is the effective interaction area on the workpiece.

**Step 2: Conventional Arc Welding (GMAW reference case).**
Power $P = V \cdot I = 30 \text{ V} \times 250 \text{ A} = 7500 \text{ W}$
Effective arc area $\approx 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 100 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,arc} = \frac{7500}{100} = 75 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Laser Beam Welding (CO₂ laser, 4 kW).**
Effective spot diameter $\approx 0.6 \text{ mm}$, so $A_{eff} = \pi \times (0.3)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,LBW} = \frac{4000}{0.2827} = 14,150 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 1.4 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Electron Beam Welding (60 kV, 100 mA, focal spot $0.4 \text{ mm}$).**
Power $P = 60{,}000 \times 0.1 = 6000 \text{ W}$
$A_{eff} = \pi \times (0.2)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,EBW} = \frac{6000}{0.1257} = 47,732 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 4.8 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Final Hierarchy and Application Justification.**

$$\underbrace{P_{d,arc}}_{75} \ll \underbrace{P_{d,LBW}}_{1.4 \times 10^4} \ll \underbrace{P_{d,EBW}}_{4.8 \times 10^4} \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Why this matters for application:**
- Low $P_d$ → wide HAZ → unsuitable for thin, heat-sensitive components (electronics, aerospace).
- High $P_d$ → narrow HAZ, deep penetration, keyhole mode → suitable for thick-section Ti, stainless, and dissimilar joints.
- **EBW must be in vacuum** to prevent electron scatter, justifying its use in **aerospace, nuclear, and high-value reactor components**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diffusion Bonding Time Estimation (Arrhenius Kinetics)

For diffusion bonding applications in Ti-superalloy turbine components, KTU often asks for the bonding time at a given temperature.

**Step 1: Arrhenius relation for diffusion coefficient.** $D$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$:

$$D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)$$

**Step 2: Approximate bonding time using creep-controlled asperity collapse:**

$$t_{DB} \approx \frac{x^2}{D}$$

where $x$ is the asperity height (typically $1{-}5 \text{ \mu m}$ for well-prepared surfaces).

**Step 3: Example numerical.** For Ti-6Al-4V: $D_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $Q_d = 153 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $T = 1173 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $x = 2 \text{ \mu m} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$.

$$D = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times \exp\left(-\frac{153{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173}\right) = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times \exp(-15.68)$$

$$D = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-7} = 1.86 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate bonding time.**

$$t_{DB} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}{1.86 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.86 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 21.5 \text{ s}$$

This explains why diffusion bonding of Ti-6Al-4V at $1173 \text{ K}$ requires only **$\approx 20{-}30 \text{ s}$** of dwell time in industrial applications — the very basis for its use in **near-net-shape turbine blade assemblies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always convert temperature to **Kelvin** and activation energy to **J/mol** before substituting into the Arrhenius equation. A common student error is using $Q_d$ in kJ/mol directly with $R = 8.314$ — this produces wrong answers by a factor of 1000.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
