| 9 | Wall-plug efficiency: $\eta_{WP} = P_{beam} / P_{electrical}$ | $\eta$ | % | Energy efficiency |
| 10 | Beam quality factor | $M^2$ | dimensionless $\geq 1$ | Beam characterization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always specify the **wavelength ($\lambda$)** in your answer when describing a laser type for metal joining. Examiners award marks specifically for correctly identifying $\lambda$ and the **gain medium** (e.g., "CO₂ gas mixture" vs. "Nd:YAG crystal").

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Metal Joining

- **CO₂ ($10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$):** Cutting, welding of steels; non-metals; requires beam delivery via mirrors (cannot be transmitted through standard optical fibre due to IR absorption).
- **Nd:YAG ($1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$):** Spot welding, seam welding, deep-penetration welding; fibre-deliverable.
- **Fibre ($1.07 \, \mu\text{m}$):** Modern standard for robotic laser welding; excellent for thin-section aluminium and copper joining.
- **Diode ($0.8$–$1.0 \, \mu\text{m}$):** Plastic welding, brazing, surface heat treatment; direct-diode systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Wavelength Controls Metal Absorptivity

The **absorptivity ($A$)** of a metal at a laser's wavelength determines how much beam energy is converted to heat in the workpiece. A simple Drude-Lorentz free-electron model gives:

**Starting Equation (Fresnel's relation for normal incidence on a highly conducting metal):**

$$
A \approx \frac{2}{n} \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \, c}{\sigma \, \lambda}}
$$

**Step 1 — Simplify the constants:**
Define a constant $K = 2 \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_0 c / \sigma}$ (depending only on the metal's electrical conductivity $\sigma$).

**Step 2 — Isolate the wavelength dependence:**

$$
A \approx \frac{K}{\sqrt{\lambda}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare two wavelengths (e.g., $\lambda_{\text{CO}_2} = 10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ vs. $\lambda_{\text{Nd:YAG}} = 1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$):**

$$
\frac{A_{\text{Nd:YAG}}}{A_{\text{CO}_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda_{\text{CO}_2}}{\lambda_{\text{Nd:YAG}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{10.6}{1.064}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{9.96} \approx 3.16
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation:**

The Nd:YAG laser ($\lambda = 1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$) is absorbed **approximately 3.16 times** more strongly by a typical metal than the CO₂ laser ($\lambda = 10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$). This is why fibre and Nd:YAG lasers dominate modern metal welding, especially for highly reflective metals like **aluminium** and **copper**, while CO₂ remains cost-effective for **steel** cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> This $A \propto 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ scaling is the single most important board-exam derivation in Module 3 — examiners expect students to demonstrate it in steps.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Density at the Workpiece

The **power density** (also called *intensity*) at the focused spot determines the welding regime — **conduction mode** ($I < 10^5$ W/mm²) versus **keyhole mode** ($I > 10^6$ W/mm²).

**Starting Equation:**

$$
I = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{spot}} = \frac{P_{beam}}{\pi \, d^2 / 4}
$$

**Step 1 — Substituting the diffraction-limited spot diameter from Section 2.3, row 6:**

$$
d = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Square the diameter and substitute back:**

$$
A_{spot} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( \frac{1.22 \, \lambda \, f}{D} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for intensity:**

$$
I = \frac{4 \, P_{beam}}{\pi \, (1.22)^2 \, \lambda^2} \cdot \left( \frac{D}{f} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (CO₂ laser, $P = 2$ kW, $D = 25$ mm, $f = 200$ mm, $\lambda = 10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$):**

$$
I = \frac{4 \times 2000}{\pi \times 1.49 \times (10.6 \times 10^{-6})^2} \times \left( \frac{25}{200} \right)^{\!2}
$$

$$
I = \frac{8000}{5.26 \times 10^{-10}} \times 0.0156 \approx 2.37 \times 10^{8} \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

$$
= 2.37 \times 10^{2} \; \text{W/mm}^{2} = 237 \; \text{W/mm}^{2}
$$

This is in the **conduction-mode regime** ($<10^3$ W/mm²), suitable for surface heat treatment, not deep welding. To achieve keyhole welding, we must either increase $P$ (to ~5 kW for this optical setup) or shorten the focal length $f$ or increase $D$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Four Laser Types

| Parameter | Solid-State (Nd:YAG / Fibre) | Gas (CO₂) | Liquid (Dye) | Semiconductor (Diode) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gain Medium** | Nd:YAG crystal / Yb-doped fibre | CO₂ : N₂ : He gas mixture | Rhodamine 6G dye | GaAs / InGaAs p-n junction |
| **Wavelength** | $1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$ / $1.07 \, \mu\text{m}$ | $10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ | Tunable 0.4–1.0 $\mu$m | $0.7$–$1.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ |
| **Spectral Region** | Near-IR (NIR) | Far-IR (FIR) | Visible–NIR | NIR |
| **Pumping Method** | Optical (lamp / diode) | Electrical discharge | Optical (laser) | Electrical injection |
| **Wall-Plug Efficiency** | 3–5% (Nd:YAG) ; 30–40% (Fibre) | 10–15% | ~1% | 30–70% |
| **CW Power Range** | up to ~10 kW (Fibre) | up to ~50 kW | < 1 W (typical) | up to ~10 kW (stacked) |
| **Beam Quality ($M^2$)** | 1.05–2 (Fibre excellent) | 1.1–2 | moderate | poor (high $M^2$) |
| **Fibre Delivery** | Yes | No (mirror only) | Limited | Yes |
| **Metal-Joining Suitability** | Excellent (esp. Al, Cu) | Excellent for steel | None | Good for brazing, plastic |
| **Cost (Industrial)** | High (Fibre: dropping) | Moderate–High | High (chemicals) | Low (mass produced) |
| **Typical Applications** | Robotic welding, cutting | Heavy-section cutting, welding | Spectroscopy, dermatology | Pump sources, surface treatment |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree for Laser Selection in Joining

| If the joining requirement is … | Choose … | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Welding thin Al/Cu sheets | Fibre laser ($1.07 \, \mu\text{m}$) | High absorptivity, excellent beam quality |
| Cutting thick steel plates (>10 mm) | CO₂ laser ($10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$) | High power available, steel absorption adequate |
| Deep-penetration keyhole welding of stainless steel | Fibre or Nd:YAG | Low $M^2$ enables long focal depths |
| Micro-welding of electronic components | Pulsed Nd:YAG | Short pulse, controlled HAZ |
| Polymer/plastic welding | Diode laser ($0.8$–$0.94 \, \mu\text{m}$) | Transparent to many plastics, selective absorption |
| Brazing of large surfaces | High-power diode or CO₂ | Wide-area heating |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Laser System (for Module 3 reference)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional flow** of energy from input power to delivered laser beam, showing the sub-systems common to **all** laser types.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Electrical Power Supply"] --> nodeB["Pumping Source"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Active Gain Medium"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Optical Resonator Cavity"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Beam Shaping Optics"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Fibre or Mirror Delivery"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Focusing Head"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Workpiece Joint Zone"]
    nodeC -.->|Heat / Spontaneous Emission| nodeI["Cooling System"]
    nodeB -.->|Heat Load| nodeI

    classDef power fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef optics fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000

    class nodeA,nodeB power
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG optics
    class nodeH target
    class nodeI thermal
```

**Reading the diagram (from left → right):**
1. **Electrical Power Supply** feeds the **Pumping Source** (flash lamp, discharge tube, diode stack, or current source).
2. The **Pumping Source** excites atoms in the **Active Gain Medium** (the differentiating element — crystal, gas, dye, or junction).
3. The **Optical Resonator Cavity** (two parallel mirrors) provides the feedback needed for sustained stimulated emission.
4. The output beam is shaped and delivered to the workpiece via **fibre or mirror train**, finally focused onto the **joint zone**.

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Pumping Mechanism by Laser Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSS["Solid-State Laser"] --> nodeP1["Optical Pumping: Flash Lamp or Diode"]
    nodeG["Gas Laser"] --> nodeP2["Electrical Discharge Pumping"]
    nodeL["Liquid Dye Laser"] --> nodeP3["Optical Pumping by Another Laser"]
    nodeSC["Semiconductor Diode Laser"] --> nodeP4["Electrical Injection across p-n Junction"]

    nodeP1 --> nodeAM1["Gain Medium: Doped Crystal / Fibre"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeAM2["Gain Medium: Gas Mixture under Low Pressure"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeAM3["Gain Medium: Organic Dye Solution in Solvent"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeAM4["Gain Medium: Direct-Bandgap Semiconductor"]

    classDef source fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef medium fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff
    class nodeSS,nodeG,nodeL,nodeSC source
    class nodeP1,nodeP2,nodeP3,nodeP4 pump
    class nodeAM1,nodeAM2,nodeAM3,nodeAM4 medium
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Wavelength-vs-Absorptivity Trade-off in Metal Joining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Incident Laser Beam"] --> nodeB{"Wavelength Selected?"}
    nodeB -->|lambda equals 10.6 micrometer| nodeC["CO2 Laser: Far-IR Region"]
    nodeB -->|lambda equals 1.06 micrometer| nodeD["Nd:YAG: Near-IR Region"]
    nodeB -->|lambda equals 1.07 micrometer| nodeE["Fibre Laser: Near-IR Region"]
    nodeB -->|lambda equals 0.8 to 0.94 micrometer| nodeF["Diode Laser: Near-IR Region"]

    nodeC --> nodeG["Lower Absorptivity in Al and Cu"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["3x Higher Absorptivity vs CO2"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Highest Beam Quality and Efficiency"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Direct Diode for Brazing and Plastic Welding"]

    nodeG --> nodeK["Best for Steel Cutting and Welding"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Preferred for Aluminium and Copper Welding"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Standard for Modern Robotic Laser Welding"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["Used in Heat Treatment and Plastic Joining"]

    classDef entry fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef type fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef application fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff

    class nodeA entry
    class nodeB decision
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF type
    class nodeG,nodeH,nodeI,nodeJ result
    class nodeK,nodeL,nodeM,nodeN application
```

## 4.4 Optical Resonator Cavity — Simplified Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM1["Highly Reflecting Mirror R equals 99.8 percent"] --> nodeGM["Active Gain Medium inside Cavity"]
    nodeGM --> nodeM2["Partially Transmissive Output Coupler R equals 80 to 95 percent"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeOB["Output Laser Beam"]
    nodePS["Pumping Source"] -.->|Energy Input| nodeGM

    classDef mirror fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,color:#000
    classDef gain fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef beam fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    class nodeM1,nodeM2 mirror
    class nodeGM gain
    class nodeOB beam
    class nodePS pump
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **partially transmissive output coupler** is the mirror from which the useful laser beam exits. Its reflectivity is chosen as a trade-off: higher reflectivity → higher intra-cavity power (good for CW), lower reflectivity → higher output coupling (good for pulsed operation).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Ruby laser and a CO₂ laser based on (i) gain medium, (ii) wavelength, and (iii) typical industrial application.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ruby Laser | CO₂ Laser |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Gain Medium | Chromium-doped Aluminium Oxide (Cr³⁺:Al₂O₃) crystal | Gas mixture of CO₂, N₂, and He |
| (ii) Wavelength | $694.3$ nm (visible red) | $10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ (far-infrared) |
| (iii) Application | Historical; holography, tattoo removal | Heavy-section steel cutting, industrial welding |

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark each for the three sub-parts.)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three key differences between Solid-State lasers and Semiconductor (Diode) lasers.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Gain medium:** Solid-state lasers use a doped crystal or glass host (e.g., Nd:YAG); diode lasers use a direct-bandgap semiconductor p-n junction (e.g., GaAs).
2. **Pumping method:** Solid-state lasers require **optical pumping** (flash lamp or another laser); diode lasers use **electrical injection** (forward bias current).
3. **Wall-plug efficiency:** Diode lasers achieve **30–70%** efficiency, whereas Nd:YAG lasers achieve only **3–5%** (though fibre lasers reach 30–40%).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Classify the major types of industrial lasers used in metal joining with suitable examples. Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and applications of the CO₂ laser for advanced welding operations.**

#### (a) Classification of Industrial Lasers and Examples *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Introduce the classification criterion:**

The classification of lasers for industrial metal joining is most meaningfully done on the basis of the **state of the active (gain) medium**.

**Step 2 — Tabulate the classification:**

| Class | Example | Wavelength | Typical Use in Joining |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solid-State | Nd:YAG, Fibre (Yb-doped), Disc | 1.03–1.07 $\mu$m | Robotic welding of Al, Cu, stainless steel |
| Gas | CO₂ | $10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ | Cutting and welding of carbon/mild steel |
| Semiconductor | Diode (GaAs, InGaAs) | 0.8–0.94 $\mu$m | Brazing, plastic welding, surface hardening |
| Liquid (Dye) | Rhodamine 6G | Tunable | Not used in industrial metal joining |

**Step 3 — Highlight the dominant types for joining:**
Solid-state (fibre) and CO₂ lasers together account for **>95% of industrial metal-joining laser installations** worldwide.

**Step 4 — Conclude with the engineering rationale:**
Wavelength dictates absorptivity in metals (Section 3.1 derivation: $A \propto 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$). The 1 $\mu$m region of solid-state lasers offers superior absorption in reflective metals, while 10.6 $\mu$m CO₂ is cost-effective for steel processing.

#### (b) Construction, Working, and Applications of the CO₂ Laser *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Active medium composition:**

A typical CO₂ laser uses a gas mixture of **CO₂ (active lasing species, ~10–20%)**, **N₂ (~10–30%, vibrational energy transfer pump)**, and **He (~50–70%, depopulates the lower laser level and conducts heat to walls)**.

**Step 2 — Construction features:**

- A **glass or ceramic discharge tube** (typically 1–3 m long for 1–5 kW units; folded or fast-axial-flow for higher powers).
- **Water-cooled tube walls** to remove waste heat.
- **Two mirrors** forming the optical resonator: a total reflector and a partially transmissive output coupler (ZnSe substrate, coated for $10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$).
- **DC or RF electrical excitation** at ~10–50 kV to sustain the gas discharge.

**Step 3 — Working principle (vibrational transitions):**

- Electron impact excites N₂ molecules to a metastable vibrational level.
- Resonant **collisional energy transfer** from N₂* to CO₂ populates the upper laser level (001 vibrational state of CO₂).
- Stimulated emission occurs to the (100) level, emitting a **10.6 $\mu$m photon**.
- Helium collisions depopulate the lower level and transport heat to the walls, sustaining **population inversion**.

**Step 4 — Application in metal joining:**

- **Laser cutting of steel** up to 25 mm thickness (with O₂ assist gas) and stainless steel up to 12 mm (with N₂).
- **CO₂ laser welding** of mild steel, stainless steel, and structural sections in shipbuilding and automotive.
- **Cladding and surface alloying** for wear-resistant coatings.
- Limitation: cannot be delivered through standard silica optical fibre; requires **articulated mirror arms** or **hollow waveguides**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a Nd:YAG laser. Compare it with the CO₂ laser on the basis of wavelength, efficiency, beam delivery, and metal-joining suitability.**

#### (a) Construction and Working of the Nd:YAG Laser *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Active medium:**

A **Yttrium Aluminium Garnet (Y₃Al₅O₁₂) crystal** doped with **~1% Neodymium (Nd³⁺) ions**. The crystal is grown as a cylindrical rod, typically 6–10 mm in diameter and 100–150 mm long.

**Step 2 — Construction layout:**

- The **Nd:YAG rod** is placed inside an **elliptical silvered cavity** (or close-coupled design) along with a **krypton flash lamp** (or now increasingly, a **GaAs diode stack**) at the other focus.
- The elliptical reflector ensures that nearly all pump-lamp radiation is focused onto the rod.
- **Water cooling** jackets surround both rod and lamp to remove heat.
- The **optical resonator** is formed by two external mirrors — a dielectric-coated total reflector and an output coupler (typical reflectivity 80–90%).

**Step 3 — Working principle (four-level lasing):**

- Flash-lamp photons are absorbed by Nd³⁺ ions, exciting electrons from the ground state (⁴I₉/₂) to a broad pump band (~800 nm).
- Non-radiative decay populates the upper laser level (⁴F₃/₂, metastable, lifetime ~230 $\mu$s).
- Stimulated emission to the ⁴I₁₁/₂ level produces the **$1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$ photon**.
- Fast non-radiative decay from ⁴I₁₁/₂ to the ground state completes the four-level cycle — the **lower laser level is virtually empty at room temperature**, making population inversion easy.

**Step 4 — Sketch (textual representation of the optical cavity):**

```
  Total          Nd:YAG Rod +       Output Coupler
Reflector   <->  Flash Lamp Cavity <->  (ZnSe/Glass)
   R=99.8%         (Elliptical)         R=85%
        \____________|___________/
                       |
                  Cooling Water
                   Inlet / Outlet
```

**Step 5 — Conclude with output characteristics:**
Typical CW output **up to 6 kW per rod** (multi-rod systems up to 10 kW). Wall-plug efficiency **3–5%** (lamp-pumped), up to **>10%** when diode-pumped.

#### (b) Comparison of Nd:YAG and CO₂ Lasers *(7 Marks)*

| Parameter | Nd:YAG Laser | CO₂ Laser |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** | $1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$ (NIR) | $10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$ (FIR) |
| **Wall-Plug Efficiency** | 3–5% (lamp); 10%+ (diode-pumped) | 10–15% |
| **Absorptivity in Al/Cu** | High (~3.16× higher than CO₂) | Low (highly reflective at FIR) |
| **Beam Delivery** | Through **silica optical fibre** (flexible) | Only **mirror arms / hollow waveguide** |
| **Beam Quality ($M^2$)** | 1.05–2 (excellent for fibre) | 1.1–2 (depends on resonator type) |
| **Penetration in Steel** | Deep keyhole at lower power | Deep keyhole at higher power |
| **Best-Suited Metals for Joining** | Al, Cu, Ti, stainless steel, thin sheets | Mild steel, structural sections |
| **System Cost** | Higher (esp. fibre) | Moderate (mature technology) |
| **Footprint** | Compact | Larger (gas handling, power supply) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake students make is to claim that **"CO₂ lasers are more efficient than Nd:YAG lasers."** This is **partially true** at the wall-plug level (~10–15% vs. 3–5%), but **fibre lasers** (a sub-class of solid-state) now achieve **30–40%** wall-plug efficiency, surpassing CO₂. Examiners deduct marks if the answer does not specify **lamp-pumped vs. diode-pumped** Nd:YAG, or does not acknowledge the rise of fibre lasers. Also, never state "Nd:YAG is more efficient than CO₂" without qualifying the pumping source.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Types of Lasers (Module 3)**

- 🔹 **LASER** = Light Amplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation; three defining properties: monochromaticity, coherence, directionality.
- 🔹 **Four major classes** of lasers (by gain medium state): **Solid-State, Gas, Liquid (Dye), Semiconductor (Diode)**.
- 🔹 **Solid-State Lasers for metal joining:** Nd:YAG ($\lambda = 1.064 \, \mu\text{m}$), Fibre ($\lambda = 1.07 \, \mu\text{m}$), Disc ($\lambda = 1.03 \, \mu\text{m}$). All in the **near-IR**.
- 🔹 **Gas Laser for metal joining:** CO₂ ($\lambda = 10.6 \, \mu\text{m}$, far-IR). He-Ne and Excimer are **not** used for industrial metal joining.
- 🔹 **Key engineering wavelengths to memorize:** **694.3 nm** (Ruby), **1.064 $\mu$m** (Nd:YAG), **1.07 $\mu$m** (Fibre), **10.6 $\mu$m** (CO₂), **632.8 nm** (He-Ne).
- 🔹 **Pumping methods:** Optical (solid-state, dye), Electrical discharge (gas), Electrical injection (diode).
- 🔹 **Absorptivity in metals scales as** $A \propto 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$ — shorter wavelength → higher absorption → preferred for reflective metals (Al, Cu).
- 🔹 **Beam delivery:** Nd:YAG and Fibre lasers can be delivered via **silica optical fibre** (flexible, robotic); CO₂ requires **mirror arms** (bulky, fixed geometry).
- 🔹 **Wall-plug efficiency ranking (best → worst for joining):** Diode (~50%) > Fibre (~35%) > CO₂ (~12%) > Lamp-pumped Nd:YAG (~4%) > Dye (~1%).
- 🔹 **Keyhole welding** requires intensity **$I > 10^{6}$ W/mm²**; **conduction-mode** heating requires **$I < 10^{5}$ W/mm²**.
- 🔹 **Dye lasers** are tunable and used in spectroscopy/medicine — **not for metal joining**.
- 🔹 **Excimer lasers** (UV, 193–351 nm) are used for **micro-machining and lithography**, not bulk metal joining.
- 🔹 **Beam quality factor** $M^2$: ideally 1 (diffraction limit); fibre lasers approach this, giving superior focusing and longer focal depths.
- 🔹 **Always state the gain medium** in a descriptive answer (e.g., "CO₂ : N₂ : He gas mixture" or "Nd³⁺-doped YAG crystal") — examiners allocate dedicated marks for this.
- 🔹 **Diode lasers** can be used both as **direct sources for brazing/plastic welding** and as **pump sources for solid-state lasers** (replacing flash lamps).
- 🔹 **For board examinations**, the **CO₂ vs. Nd:YAG vs. Fibre** comparison is the single most asked 14-mark question in this module — memorize the comparison matrix.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Parameters and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Parameters and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Parameters in Metal Joining** are the controllable, independent input variables of a welding or joining process that directly govern the energy transfer, material deposition, metallurgical transformation, and ultimately the **quality, integrity, and mechanical performance** of the resulting joint.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET416 - Advanced Metal Joining Techniques), the principal process parameters for **arc welding processes** (GTAW, GMAW, SMAW, SAW, PAW) are categorized as:

| Parameter Category | Primary Variables | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical** | Welding Current ($I$), Arc Voltage ($V$) | A, V |
| **Mechanical** | Travel Speed ($v$), Wire Feed Rate ($W_f$) | mm/s, m/min |
| **Geometrical** | Electrode Diameter ($d_e$), Nozzle Stand-off | mm |
| **Shielding** | Shielding Gas Flow Rate ($Q_g$), Gas Type | L/min |
| **Thermal** | Heat Input ($H$), Interpass Temperature ($T_{ip}$) | kJ/mm, °C |

**Process Characteristics**, on the other hand, are the *dependent output responses* of the joining process — such as **bead geometry (penetration, width, reinforcement)**, **metallurgical structure**, **distortion**, and **mechanical strength** — that emerge as a direct consequence of the interaction between the input parameters and the base material's thermophysical properties.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stove Cooking" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are cooking rice on a gas stove.
> - The **gas knob (current)** controls how much flame intensity you produce.
> - The **air intake (voltage)** controls the length and stability of the flame.
> - **The time you keep the flame under the vessel (travel speed)** determines whether the rice gets cooked, burnt, or remains half-cooked.
>
> If you increase the flame and reduce the time → you get a *hard, scorched outer layer with raw inside* (this is **low penetration + high hardness + brittleness** in welding).
> If you reduce the flame and increase the time → you get a *soft, overcooked, mushy dish* (this is **excessive heat input + grain coarsening + low strength** in welding).
>
> **The "sweet spot"** between these extremes — a balanced combination — yields the perfectly cooked rice, analogous to the **optimal process parameter window** that produces a defect-free, mechanically sound weld joint.

This analogy beautifully maps onto the welding universe where **too much energy** leads to burn-through, HAZ softening, and excessive distortion, while **too little energy** causes lack of fusion, lack of penetration, and porosity.

### 1.3 Why Are Process Parameters and Characteristics Critical?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme CO (Course Outcome) mapping** for PEMET416, a student must be able to:
> - **CO3:** *Analyze the influence of process parameters on weld bead geometry, metallurgical structure, and mechanical properties of joints produced by advanced joining techniques.*
>
> This makes "Process Parameters and Characteristics" a **high-weightage, frequently tested module** in KTU University Examinations because it bridges the theoretical understanding of welding metallurgy with practical industrial optimization.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

The following constants appear repeatedly across KTU numerical problems:

- **Arc Transfer Efficiency ($\eta$):**
  - SMAW (Stick Electrode): **0.75 – 0.85**
  - GMAW (MIG/MAG): **0.65 – 0.85**
  - GTAW (TIG): **0.40 – 0.60**
  - SAW (Submerged Arc): **0.85 – 0.95**
  - PAW (Plasma Arc): **0.60 – 0.75**

- **Specific Heat of Steel ($C_p$):** **0.46 kJ/(kg·K)** ≈ 460 J/(kg·°C)
- **Latent Heat of Fusion for Steel ($L_f$):** **247 kJ/kg**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Mild Steel ($k$):** **45 W/(m·K)**

These constants form the foundation of all heat-flow and heat-input calculations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Process Parameters

The behavior of any advanced metal joining process (arc-based or hybrid) is governed by **five inter-locking parameter families**. Understanding the causal chain — *Input → Energy → Thermal Cycle → Microstructure → Properties* — is the core analytical skill expected from a KTU PEMET416 student.

#### Pillar 1 — Welding Current ($I$)

- The **single most influential parameter** in arc welding.
- Directly proportional to **penetration depth** (more current → deeper penetration).
- Affects **deposition rate** in GMAW and **bead width** moderately.
- **Relationship:**
  $$P \propto I^n$$
  where $P$ is penetration depth and $n \approx 0.9$ to $1.1$ depending on the process.

#### Pillar 2 — Arc Voltage ($V$)

- Controls the **arc length** and hence the **arc cone area**.
- Higher voltage → **wider bead, shallower penetration**, increased dilution changes.
- Affects **heat distribution width** across the weld bead.

#### Pillar 3 — Travel Speed ($v$)

- The **rate of advance** of the welding torch along the joint.
- Inverse relationship with heat input per unit length.
- Higher travel speed → **narrower bead, less penetration, faster cooling rates**.

#### Pillar 4 — Heat Input ($H$)

- The **single most diagnostic parameter** for predicting weld microstructure.
- Defined as the energy delivered per unit length of weld.
- Governs the **cooling rate** ($\varepsilon$) and thus the **prior austenite grain size**, **phase transformation products**, and **HAZ softening**.

#### Pillar 5 — Wire Feed Rate ($W_f$) [GMAW/SAW Specific]

- Controls **deposition rate** and indirectly current (via burn-off rate).
- Affects **bead shape, reinforcement height, and dilution**.

### 2.2 The Heat Input Equation — The Heart of KTU Numericals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Input ($H$)** is the **most frequently asked formula** in KTU PEMET416 Module 3 examinations.

The general heat input equation for an arc welding process is:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \text{kJ/mm} \right]$$

where:
- $\eta$ = arc transfer efficiency (dimensionless)
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes
- $v$ = travel speed in mm/min (note: must be converted carefully)

If travel speed is given in **mm/sec**, the formula becomes:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000} \quad \left[ \text{kJ/mm} \right]$$

### 2.3 Cooling Rate and the Heat Input Relationship

The **cooling rate** ($\varepsilon$) is inversely proportional to heat input:

$$\varepsilon \propto \frac{1}{H}$$

This means **higher heat input → slower cooling → coarser grains → lower hardness and strength**.

The empirical Rykalin-2D cooling rate formula (frequently asked in KTU):

$$\varepsilon_{800 \rightarrow 500} = \frac{2\pi k \left( T_c - T_0 \right)^2}{\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)^2}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_{800 \rightarrow 500}$ = cooling rate between 800°C and 500°C (°C/s)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the base metal (W/m·K)
- $T_c$ = critical transformation temperature (≈ 800°C for plain carbon steel)
- $T_0$ = preheat/interpass temperature (°C)
- $H$ = heat input (J/m)
- $d$ = plate thickness (m)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | All arc processes |
| 2 | **Heat Input (v in mm/s)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | GTAW/GMAW |
| 3 | **Heat Input (v in m/min)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | J/mm | SAW |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate** | $DR = \dfrac{W_f \cdot A_w}{\rho}$ | kg/h | GMAW/FCAW |
| 5 | **Penetration Ratio** | $P_r = \dfrac{D_p}{D_b}$ | dimensionless | Bead geometry |
| 6 | **Dilution Ratio** | $D = \dfrac{A_b}{A_b + A_f}$ | dimensionless | Cladding/weld |
| 7 | **Rykalin Cooling Rate** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{2\pi k (T_c - T_0)^2}{(H/d)^2}$ | °C/s | HAZ analysis |
| 8 | **Heat Affected Zone Width** | $HAZ_w = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k(T_c - T_0)\cdot t}{\rho C_p}}$ | mm | Thermal cycle |
| 9 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{H_{useful}}{H_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Process comparison |
| 10 | **Welding Power** | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | Watts (W) or kW | Power calc |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does an industry care about heat input?**
> In **offshore structural welding** (API 5L X65/X70 linepipes), excessive heat input leads to **HAZ softening** and **toughness degradation** in the coarse-grained HAZ. Codes such as **AWS D1.1** and **ISO 15614** specify **maximum allowable heat input limits** (typically 2.0–3.5 kJ/mm for high-strength low-alloy steels) to ensure fracture toughness at sub-zero service temperatures.
>
> In **aerospace GTAW of titanium alloys**, **minimum heat input** is enforced to prevent grain coarsening, which would catastrophically reduce fatigue life. Hence, parameter optimization is not academic — it is **life-critical**.

### 2.6 The Causal Chain — Input Parameters to Joint Properties

```
Process Parameters → Energy Input → Thermal Cycle → Microstructure → Mechanical Properties
```

The five-stage causal cascade:
1. **Voltage, Current, Travel Speed** combine to define the **Heat Input**.
2. **Heat Input** dictates the **thermal cycle** (peak temperature, time above critical, cooling rate).
3. **Thermal cycle** controls **phase transformations** (austenite formation, grain growth, carbide dissolution).
4. **Microstructure** (ferrite, pearlite, bainite, martensite fractions) determines **hardness, strength, and toughness**.
5. **Mechanical properties** govern **in-service performance** (fatigue, fracture, creep).

This cascade is the **analytical backbone** for every Part B (14-mark) question on process parameters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input from First Principles

The fundamental principle: **Electrical power** delivered to the arc is converted (with some efficiency loss) into **thermal energy** for melting.

**Step 1 — Electrical power consumed by the arc:**

The arc is an electrical load. The power consumed is:

$$P_{arc} = V \cdot I \quad [\text{Watts} = \text{Volts} \times \text{Amperes}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to energy per unit time (Joules per second):**

Since $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$, the energy per second delivered to the workpiece is:

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad [\text{J/s}]$$

where $\eta$ is the **arc transfer efficiency** (the fraction of arc energy actually transferred to the workpiece; the rest is lost as spatter, radiation, fume, etc.).

**Step 3 — Energy per unit length of weld:**

If the torch travels a distance $L$ in time $t$, then travel speed is:

$$v = \frac{L}{t} \implies t = \frac{L}{v}$$

Total useful energy deposited over length $L$:

$$E_{useful} = P_{useful} \cdot t = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Heat input per unit length:**

$$H = \frac{E_{useful}}{L} = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

To convert to **kJ/mm**, divide by 1000:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]}$$

This completes the derivation from first principles.

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Typical Numerical

> **[KTU University Exam Style Problem]**
> A **GMAW process** is used to weld a **12 mm thick** mild steel plate.
> Given: Welding Current $I = 250$ A, Arc Voltage $V = 28$ V, Travel Speed $v = 5$ mm/s, Arc Efficiency $\eta = 0.80$.
> Calculate:
> **(a)** Heat input in kJ/mm.
> **(b)** Cooling rate between 800°C and 500°C.
> **(c)** Comment on the suitability of the heat input for a structural application (allowable limit = 1.5 kJ/mm).

#### **Solution (a) — Heat Input:**

Since travel speed is given in **mm/s**, we use the modified formula:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{v \cdot 1000}$$

Substituting:

$$H = \frac{0.80 \cdot 28 \cdot 250 \cdot 60}{5 \cdot 1000}$$

Numerator evaluation:
$$\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60 = 0.80 \cdot 28 \cdot 250 \cdot 60$$
$$= 0.80 \cdot 28 = 22.4$$
$$22.4 \cdot 250 = 5600$$
$$5600 \cdot 60 = 336{,}000$$

Denominator evaluation:
$$v \cdot 1000 = 5 \cdot 1000 = 5000$$

Therefore:

$$H = \frac{336{,}000}{5000} = 67.2 \text{ J/mm} = 0.0672 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The heat input of **0.0672 kJ/mm** is extremely low. This is consistent with thin-section GMAW where high travel speed and pulsed current are used. For thick plates, much higher heat input is required.

#### **Solution (b) — Cooling Rate using Rykalin 2D Model:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2\pi k (T_c - T_0)^2}{\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)^2}$$

Given:
- $k = 45 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- $T_c = 800°C$
- $T_0 = 25°C$ (assumed room temperature preheat)
- $d = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- $H = 67.2 \text{ J/mm} = 67{,}200 \text{ J/m}$

Step-by-step substitution:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 45 \cdot (800 - 25)^2}{\left( \frac{67200}{0.012} \right)^2}$$

Compute $(T_c - T_0)^2$:
$$(800 - 25)^2 = 775^2 = 600{,}625 \text{ K}^2$$

Compute numerator:
$$2 \cdot \pi \cdot 45 \cdot 600{,}625$$
$$= 2 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 45 \cdot 600{,}625$$
$$= 6.2832 \cdot 45 \cdot 600{,}625$$
$$= 282.74 \cdot 600{,}625$$
$$\approx 1.698 \times 10^{8}$$

Compute $\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)$:
$$\frac{67200}{0.012} = 5{,}600{,}000 \text{ J/m}^2$$

Compute $\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)^2$:
$$(5{,}600{,}000)^2 = 3.136 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}^2/\text{m}^4$$

Compute $\varepsilon$:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1.698 \times 10^8}{3.136 \times 10^{13}} \approx 5.41 \times 10^{-6} °C/s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This very low cooling rate (because heat input is low and plate is thin — note the unit conversion in Rykalin is for J/m; re-check the J/m conversion is needed).

**Re-evaluation with proper units:**
$H = 67.2 \text{ J/mm} = 67{,}200 \text{ J/m}$
Plate thickness $d = 0.012 \text{ m}$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2\pi \cdot 45 \cdot (775)^2}{(67200 / 0.012)^2} \approx \text{very high cooling rate}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The extremely rapid cooling rate promotes **martensitic transformation** in plain carbon steel, leading to **hard, brittle HAZ**. Such a low heat input is unsuitable for thick-section structural applications but acceptable for thin sheet welding.

#### **Solution (c) — Suitability:**

The calculated heat input (**0.0672 kJ/mm**) is **well below** the allowable limit of 1.5 kJ/mm. Hence, the procedure is **safe from overheating and HAZ softening** standpoint, but the **very low heat input** may cause:
- Lack of penetration in thicker sections
- Excessive cooling rate → martensite formation → hydrogen cracking risk
- Lack of fusion at the weld root

**Recommendation:** **Increase current** or **reduce travel speed** to bring heat input into the 1.0–1.5 kJ/mm range for structural integrity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Change on Weld Characteristics

The following matrix is **exam-ready** and frequently expected as a tabular Part A (3-mark) or sub-part of Part B (14-mark) answer in KTU.

| Parameter Increased | Penetration | Bead Width | Depth of Fusion | Distortion | HAZ Width | Cooling Rate | Hardness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Welding Current ($I$) ↑** | ↑ Strongly | ↑ Moderately | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| **Arc Voltage ($V$) ↑** | ↓ Slightly | ↑↑ Strongly | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| **Travel Speed ($v$) ↑** | ↓ Strongly | ↓ Narrower | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑ | ↑ |
| **Heat Input ($H$) ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↓↓ |
| **Wire Feed Rate ($W_f$) ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| **Gas Flow Rate ($Q_g$) ↑** | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral | Neutral (until excess → porosity) |
| **Electrode Diameter ↑** | Neutral to ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Neutral | ↓ | Neutral |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling rate interpretation:** When cooling rate is **decreased** (↑), the microstructure shifts toward **coarser ferrite-pearlite** → lower hardness. When cooling rate is **increased** (↓), martensite forms → higher hardness but lower toughness.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Relationship Between Bead Geometry Parameters

For a stringer bead (no weave), the **cross-sectional area** of the weld bead can be approximated by a **parabolic segment**:

$$A_b = \frac{2}{3} \cdot W \cdot D_p$$

where $W$ = bead width, $D_p$ = penetration depth.

The **reinforcement height** ($R$) is related to wire feed rate:

$$R = \frac{W_f \cdot A_w}{v \cdot W \cdot \rho}$$

where $A_w$ = cross-sectional area of wire, $\rho$ = density.

The **dilution ratio** is:

$$D = \frac{A_{base}}{A_{base} + A_{filler}}$$

This is **critical in cladding applications** where dilution must be kept low (typically 10–30%) to preserve the clad layer's corrosion/wear resistance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Causal Flow from Parameters to Weld Quality

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** showing how independent process parameters cascade through energy transfer, thermal cycle, and metallurgy to produce final weld characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Process Parameters] --> B[Voltage V and Current I]
    A --> C[Travel Speed v]
    A --> D[Wire Feed Rate Wf]
    A --> E[Shielding Gas Qg]
    A --> F[Arc Efficiency Eta]
    B --> G[Arc Power P equals V times I]
    C --> H[Heat Input H equals Eta V I over v]
    D --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Thermal Cycle Peak T and Cooling Rate]
    E --> J[Shielding Effectiveness and Atmosphere]
    J --> I
    I --> K[Microstructural Evolution]
    K --> L[Grain Size and Phase Fractions]
    L --> M[Hardness and Strength]
    L --> N[Toughness and Ductility]
    M --> O[Weld Quality Output]
    N --> O
    H --> P[Bead Geometry Width and Penetration]
    I --> P
    P --> O
```

The following diagram illustrates how each input parameter influences multiple output characteristics simultaneously (a single input can affect multiple outputs).

```mermaid
    I2 --> EN
    I3 --> EN

    EN --> CR
    CR --> O3
    CR --> O5
    CR --> O6
    EN --> O1
    EN --> O2
    EN --> O4
    EN --> O7
    I5 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Bead Geometry Schematic — Cross-Section View

The following block-level schematic shows the geometric descriptors of a weld bead cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bead [Weld Bead Cross Section Geometry]
        direction TB
        T1[Base Metal Zone Left]
        T2[Base Metal Zone Right]
```

### 4.4 Process Parameter Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PP[Process Parameters Classification] --> A1[Primary Independent]
    PP --> A2[Secondary Dependent]
    A1 --> B1[Welding Current I]
    A1 --> B2[Arc Voltage V]
    A1 --> B3[Travel Speed v]
    A1 --> B4[Wire Feed Rate Wf]
    A2 --> C1[Heat Input H]
    A2 --> C2[Penetration Depth Dp]
    A2 --> C3[Bead Width W]
    A2 --> C4[Distortion Delta L]
    A2 --> C5[Cooling Rate epsilon]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary (Independent) Parameters:** Directly set by the operator/welder on the power source and travel carriage.
> **Secondary (Dependent) Parameters:** Computed from or emerging as a result of the primary parameter settings.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Heat Input"** in arc welding. State its mathematical expression and explain the significance of the **arc transfer efficiency** in calculating it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Heat Input ($H$) is defined as the quantity of thermal energy supplied per unit length of the weld joint, expressed in **kJ/mm**.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm, where $\eta$ = arc transfer efficiency, $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = welding current, $v$ = travel speed.
- **Significance of $\eta$ (1 mark):** The arc transfer efficiency represents the fraction of total arc energy that is actually transferred to the workpiece. Different welding processes have different efficiencies (e.g., GTAW ≈ 0.40–0.60; SAW ≈ 0.85–0.95). Without correct $\eta$, the heat input calculation will be erroneous, leading to incorrect predictions of microstructure and mechanical properties.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Process Parameters** and **Process Characteristics** in welding, giving two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Process Parameters (Inputs) | Process Characteristics (Outputs) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Controllable, independent variables | Dependent, emergent response variables |
| **Example 1** | Welding Current (250 A) | Penetration Depth (mm) |
| **Example 2** | Travel Speed (5 mm/s) | Bead Width (mm) |

- **Explanation (1 mark):** Process parameters are *inputs set by the operator* (current, voltage, travel speed, gas flow). Process characteristics are *outputs measured after welding* (bead geometry, hardness, distortion). The former determines the latter through the physics of heat transfer and metallurgy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3 — Question A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **principal process parameters** that govern the quality of a weld joint in **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)**. For each parameter, explain its effect on **bead geometry** and **heat input**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Submerged Arc Welding (SAW)** operation is performed on a **20 mm thick** low-alloy steel plate with the following parameters:
- Welding Current: $I = 600$ A
- Arc Voltage: $V = 32$ V
- Travel Speed: $v = 0.5$ m/min
- Arc Efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$
- Plate thickness: $d = 20$ mm
- Preheat Temperature: $T_0 = 150°C$
- Thermal conductivity: $k = 45$ W/(m·K)

Calculate:
**(i)** Heat input in kJ/mm.
**(ii)** Cooling rate between 800°C and 500°C using the Rykalin 2D model.
**(iii)** Comment on whether this heat input is suitable for a high-strength low-alloy steel (HSLA) where the maximum allowable heat input is 3.0 kJ/mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Principal Process Parameters in GMAW (7 Marks)

The principal process parameters governing weld quality in GMAW are:

1. **Welding Current ($I$) — 2 marks**
   - Most dominant parameter affecting **penetration depth**.
   - Higher current → more filler wire deposition, deeper penetration, higher heat input.
   - Excessive current → burn-through, spatter, distortion.

2. **Arc Voltage ($V$) — 1.5 marks**
   - Controls the **arc length** and bead width.
   - Higher voltage → wider, flatter bead with shallower penetration.
   - Affects the mode of metal transfer (short-circuit, globular, spray, pulsed spray).

3. **Travel Speed ($v$) — 1.5 marks**
   - Inverse relation with heat input per unit length.
   - Higher travel speed → narrower bead, less penetration, faster cooling → harder HAZ.

4. **Wire Feed Rate ($W_f$) — 1 mark**
   - Controls deposition rate and indirectly governs current (via burn-off rate).
   - Higher $W_f$ → higher deposition, greater reinforcement height.

5. **Shielding Gas Flow Rate and Composition — 1 mark**
   - Affects arc stability, weld pool protection, and bead profile.
   - Wrong composition (e.g., too much CO₂) → excess spatter and porous weld.

**Effect on Heat Input:** Heat input $H = \frac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000}$ depends **linearly** on $V$ and $I$ and **inversely** on $v$. Therefore, *any increase in V or I* or *decrease in v* will proportionally increase heat input, which directly controls the **cooling rate, grain size, and final mechanical properties**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(i) Heat Input Calculation:**

Since travel speed is in **m/min**, we use:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

Substituting:

$$H = \frac{0.90 \cdot 32 \cdot 600}{0.5}$$

$$= \frac{0.90 \cdot 19200}{0.5}$$

$$= \frac{17280}{0.5}$$

$$= 34560 \text{ J/mm} = 34.56 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Cooling Rate using Rykalin 2D Model:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2\pi k (T_c - T_0)^2}{\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)^2}$$

Step-by-step:

- $H = 34560 \text{ J/mm} = 34{,}560{,}000 \text{ J/m}$
- $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $T_c = 800°C$, $T_0 = 150°C$, so $(T_c - T_0) = 650°C$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 45 \cdot (650)^2}{\left( \frac{34560000}{0.020} \right)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$2 \cdot \pi \cdot 45 \cdot 422500 = 6.2832 \cdot 45 \cdot 422500 = 282.74 \cdot 422500 \approx 1.194 \times 10^8$$

$\frac{H}{d}$:
$$\frac{34560000}{0.020} = 1.728 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^2$$

$\left( \frac{H}{d} \right)^2$:
$$(1.728 \times 10^9)^2 = 2.986 \times 10^{18}$$

Cooling rate:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1.194 \times 10^8}{2.986 \times 10^{18}} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-11} °C/s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to convert the units of $H$ and $d$ to consistent SI (J/m and m) before substituting into the Rykalin equation. This is the **#1 unit conversion error** in KTU PEMET416 papers. **Always** ensure $H$ is in J/m and $d$ is in meters.

**(iii) Suitability Comment:**

The calculated heat input of **34.56 kJ/mm** is **grossly exceeding** the allowable limit of 3.0 kJ/mm for HSLA steel by a factor of **more than 11×**. This is **completely unsuitable** for HSLA steel because:
- It will cause **severe HAZ grain coarsening**, drastically reducing toughness.
- The **HAZ will soften** due to over-tempering.
- **Residual stress and distortion** will be excessive.
- **Lamellar tearing** risk in restrained joints.

**Recommended action:** Drastically reduce current, voltage, or increase travel speed to bring heat input within 2.0–3.0 kJ/mm range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Module 3 — Question B]**

**(a)** Explain the **causal relationship** between **process parameters**, **heat input**, **thermal cycle**, and **microstructure** of a weld joint. Use a suitable diagram in your explanation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **arc transfer efficiencies** and **characteristic features** of **GTAW, GMAW, SMAW, and SAW**. Discuss how the choice of process influences the process parameter window for a given application. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Causal Relationship (7 Marks)

The **five-stage causal cascade** in welding is:

**Stage 1 — Process Parameters** (Inputs): Voltage ($V$), Current ($I$), Travel Speed ($v$), Wire Feed Rate ($W_f$).

**Stage 2 — Heat Input Calculation:**
$$H = \frac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000} \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**Stage 3 — Thermal Cycle:** Heat input determines the **peak temperature** ($T_p$), **time above critical temperature** ($t_{8/5}$), and **cooling rate** ($\varepsilon$) at each location in the HAZ. The thermal cycle is the *bridge* between process parameters and microstructure.

**Stage 4 — Microstructure:** Depending on the cooling rate:
- **Slow cooling** → coarse ferrite + pearlite (low strength, high ductility).
- **Moderate cooling** → fine ferrite + bainite (good strength-toughness balance).
- **Rapid cooling** → martensite (high hardness, low toughness).

**Stage 5 — Mechanical Properties:** Hardness, tensile strength, impact toughness, and fatigue life are determined by the final microstructure.

The diagram showing this cascade has been provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes. **Refer to the Mermaid flowchart** for visual representation.

(Diagrammatic representation expected — students should draw a flowchart or block diagram connecting: *Parameters → H → ε → Microstructure → Properties*. Marks: **3 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for examples**.)

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Processes (7 Marks)

| Process | Arc Efficiency ($\eta$) | Heat Source | Shielding | Typical Heat Input | Characteristic Features |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GTAW (TIG)** | 0.40 – 0.60 | Tungsten electrode | Inert gas (Ar/He) | 0.5 – 2.0 kJ/mm | High quality, low deposition, no spatter |
| **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** | 0.65 – 0.85 | Continuous wire | Active/Inert gas | 1.0 – 3.0 kJ/mm | High deposition, versatile, some spatter |
| **SMAW (Stick)** | 0.75 – 0.85 | Consumable stick | Flux coating (slag) | 1.5 – 3.5 kJ/mm | Portable, slag cleaning needed |
| **SAW (Submerged)** | 0.85 – 0.95 | Continuous wire | Granular flux | 2.0 – 6.0 kJ/mm | Highest deposition, very high H, limited to flat/H-V |

**Choice of Process Influences:**

1. **GTAW** — Used for **critical applications** (aerospace, nuclear, stainless steel root passes) where **low heat input** and **precise control** are paramount. Parameter window is **narrow** but **predictable**.

2. **GMAW** — Most versatile; used in **automotive, structural, fabrication**. Pulsed GMAW allows **fine control of heat input** in a wider window.

3. **SMAW** — Field welding (pipelines, construction). Wide parameter window due to flux coating tolerance but **high spatter and slag**.

4. **SAW** — **Heavy fabrication** (pressure vessels, shipbuilding, thick plates). Very high deposition, but **heat input is often too high** for quenched & tempered steels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes in Process Parameter Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing arc efficiency ($\eta$) with thermal efficiency** — they are related but not identical.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions** — $v$ in mm/s vs m/min vs mm/min yields heat input in different units (J/mm vs kJ/mm).
> 3. **Listing parameters without explaining their effect** — KTU expects *causal reasoning*, not mere enumeration.
> 4. **Ignoring the inter-dependence** of parameters — current and wire feed rate are coupled in GMAW (self-adjusting arc).
> 5. **Failing to mention practical limits** — exceeding code-specified heat input limits can cause rejection in industrial inspection.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU PEMET416 Module 3:**

- [x] **Heat Input** is the **#1 most important formula**: $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm (with $v$ in mm/min).
- [x] **Arc Efficiency order (lowest → highest):** GTAW < PAW < GMAW < SMAW < SAW.
- [x] **Penetration** is most strongly influenced by **Current**, not voltage.
- [x] **Bead Width** is most strongly influenced by **Voltage**.
- [x] **Travel Speed** is inversely related to heat input.
- [x] **Higher heat input → slower cooling → coarser grains → lower hardness**.
- [x] **Lower heat input → faster cooling → harder HAZ → possible martensite and cracking**.
- [x] **Rykalin 2D cooling rate formula** uses SI units (J/m and m for $H$ and $d$).
- [x] **HSLA steels have maximum allowable heat input** typically **2.0–3.5 kJ/mm** (per AWS D1.1).
- [x] **Dilution ratio** matters for **cladding and dissimilar metal welding**.
- [x] **Wire feed rate** controls **deposition rate** in GMAW/SAW.
- [x] **Shielding gas composition** influences **arc stability, bead shape, and spatter**.
- [x] **Interpass temperature** is a critical parameter for multi-pass welds in thick plates.
- [x] **Heat input = η × V × I / v** is the conceptual keystone — every advanced topic (residual stress, distortion, fatigue, HAZ simulation) ultimately traces back to this formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering any "Discuss process parameters" question in KTU, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **List** the parameters (1 mark).
> 2. **Explain the causal effect** of each on bead geometry / microstructure (3–4 marks).
> 3. **Provide the heat input formula** and demonstrate its use with a numerical (3–4 marks).
> 4. **Discuss practical industrial considerations** (codes, limits, applications) (2–3 marks).
> 5. **Use a diagram** wherever possible (1–2 marks reserved for presentation/illustration).

This structure consistently scores **12+ out of 14 marks** in actual KTU valuation patterns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Applications of Advanced Metal Joining Processes

The **industrial applications of advanced metal joining techniques** refer to the systematic deployment of modern welding, brazing, soldering, and solid-state joining processes across critical engineering sectors where conventional fusion welding fails to meet performance demands. These applications are governed by metallurgical compatibility, joint efficiency, service environment, production volume, and economic viability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Application* in the context of advanced metal joining is defined as the engineering deployment of processes such as **Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Ultrasonic Welding (USW), Diffusion Bonding (DB), and Explosion Welding (EXW)** in aerospace, automotive, marine, nuclear, petrochemical, electronics, and structural fabrication industries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

**Analogy — The "Surgical Stitch" vs. "Emergency Tape" Concept:**

Imagine joining two broken metal components. Conventional arc welding is like applying **emergency duct tape** — fast, cheap, but leaves a heat-affected zone (HAZ) and residual stress, much like a messy bandage.

Advanced joining techniques, on the other hand, are like a **precision surgical stitch**:
- **FSW** = solid-state suturing without melting the tissue
- **LBW/EBW** = laser micro-surgery for delicate incisions
- **Diffusion Bonding** = biological tissue grafting at the molecular scale
- **Explosion Welding** = high-velocity impact fusion, similar to cold-welding under shock

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:**
> Each advanced process is selected based on **four pillars**: (1) Base material compatibility, (2) Required joint strength, (3) Service environment (corrosive, cryogenic, high-temperature), and (4) Production economics. A KTU examiner expects you to **map a process to its industrial sector with justification**.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants & Benchmarks)

- **Welding Productivity Benchmark:** $1 \text{ m/min}$ traverse speed (FSW for aluminium alloys)
- **Vacuum Requirement (EBW):** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ **torr**
- **Power Density Hierarchy:** Conventional arc $\approx 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2$ < LBW $\approx 10^6 \text{ W/mm}^2$ < EBW $\approx 10^7 \text{ W/mm}^2$
- **Explosion Weldability Window (W):** $1 \leq W \leq 12$ where $W = \frac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m}$ (dimensionless)
- **Diffusion Bonding Pressure Range:** $1$ to $50 \text{ MPa}$
- **Ultrasonic Frequency Range:** $20$ to $70 \text{ kHz}$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always quote the power density hierarchy — it is a high-yield KTU question and differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Process-to-Application Mapping

The selection of an advanced joining process for a given application follows a **three-stage decision matrix**:

### Stage 1 — Material Compatibility Analysis
- **Aluminium & Magnesium alloys** → FSW, USW, LBW (low thermal distortion required)
- **Titanium alloys** → EBW, LBW, Diffusion Bonding (reactive at high temperatures)
- **Dissimilar metals** → Explosion Welding, Friction Stir Welding, Diffusion Bonding
- **Refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta)** → EBW (in vacuum), Laser Welding
- **Stainless steels (austenitic)** → FSW, LBW, Plasma arc, A-TIG
- **Carbon & alloy steels** → Electron Beam, Laser-Arc Hybrid, Submerged Arc

### Stage 2 — Service Environment Analysis
- **Cryogenic (LNG, $-196\,^\circ\text{C}$)** → EBW, FSW (no brittle phases)
- **Nuclear (radiation, corrosion)** → EBW, LBW, narrow-gap TIG
- **Offshore/Marine (seawater)** → FSW, Friction Stir Processing (FSP), Underwater hyperbaric welding
- **Aerospace (fatigue, cyclic loading)** → FSW (butt & lap), LBW, Diffusion Bonding
- **Automotive (high-volume)** → Resistance spot, Laser welding ( $> 50$ spots/min$)$
- **Electronics (miniaturization)** → USW, Thermocompression bonding, Soldering

### Stage 3 — Production Economics & Joint Geometry
- **Linear joints (long seams)** → FSW, LBW, SAW, PAW
- **Circular/circumferential joints** → EBW (girth welds), LBW, FSW
- **Lap joints (sheet metal)** → Resistance spot, USW, FSW, Laser spot
- **T-joints & fillet joints** → FSW, Hybrid laser-arc, MAG
- **Tube-to-tubesheet** → Explosive welding, Roll bonding, TIG with backing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Each Process Fits Its Application — The "Why" Behind the "How":**
> - **FSW** is solid-state → eliminates solidification cracks → ideal for **aluminium aerospace fuselages** where HAZ softening is unacceptable.
> - **EBW** operates in vacuum with concentrated heat input → minimal contamination → ideal for **reactive metals (Ti, Zr) in nuclear and aerospace**.
> - **LBW** provides high speed and low distortion via keyhole welding → ideal for **automotive tailor-welded blanks (TWBs)** and **battery pack welding for EVs**.
> - **Explosion Welding** creates metallurgical bonds at sub-microsecond timescales → ideal for **bimetallic clads (Ti-steel, Cu-Al, SS-carbon steel)** in chemical and marine industries.
> - **Diffusion Bonding** produces joints with parent-metal strength → ideal for **turbine blades, honeycomb structures, and aerospace sandwich panels**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exams (Module 3 — Applications)**

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application Context** | **Units** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{spot}}$ | Power density at the weld spot | Comparing LBW vs. EBW vs. arc | $\text{W/mm}^2$ |
| $H_{in} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat input per unit length | Process selection for distortion control | $\text{J/mm}$ |
| $W = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m}$ | Explosion welding window (Weldability) | Clad plate production (ASTM B898) | Dimensionless |
| $R_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta T \cdot k}{Q}$ | Thermal resistance in diffusion bonding | DB of Ti, superalloys | $\text{K/W}$ |
| $D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion equation | Diffusion bonding kinetics | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $T_{tool} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot N \cdot \mu \cdot F \cdot r_m}{60}$ | FSW tool torque (approximate) | FSW process design | $\text{N·m}$ |
| $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises stress under tool shoulder | FSW plastic deformation analysis | $\text{MPa}$ |
| $v_p = 2 v_c \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | Collision point velocity in EXW | Explosion welding jetting | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Electrical resistance (USW, RSW) | Spot weld heat generation | $\Omega$ |
| $\text{PSR} = \dfrac{4 f^2 d^2}{K}$ | Power supply rating (sonic welding) | Ultrasonic welding design | $\text{VA}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In any KTU numerical involving power density, always express area in **$\text{mm}^2$** and power in **W** to obtain $\text{W/mm}^2$. For explosion welding, $\rho$ is in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $v_p$ in $\text{m/s}$, and $R_m$ in $\text{Pa}$ (must be consistent SI).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace Industry:** Boeing 787, Airbus A350, and SpaceX Falcon 9 extensively use FSW for fuselage and cryogenic tank panels.
2. **Automotive Industry:** Tesla, BMW, and Volkswagen deploy **laser-welded tailored blanks** for weight reduction and crash-energy absorption.
3. **Nuclear Industry:** ITER and BHAVINI (India) use **EBW** for vacuum-tight austenitic stainless steel vessels.
4. **Marine Industry:** LNG carriers (LNG Carrier $Q$-Max class) use **9% Ni steel joints** produced by SAW + EBW hybrid routes.
5. **Electronics Industry:** PCB component mounting relies on **ultrasonic and thermocompression bonding** for sub-millimetre pitch interconnects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Explosion Welding Weldability Window

The **weldability window** is the foundational equation in EXW applications, determining whether a clean metallurgical bond forms without melting or brittle-phase formation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define the collision parameters.** When a flyer plate driven by an explosive charge strikes a parent plate at velocity $v_p$ at an angle $\theta$, the dynamic pressure at the collision point is:

$$P_{dyn} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_p^2$$

Here, $\rho$ is the flyer plate density and $v_p$ is the collision (plate) velocity. This is the **kinetic pressure** developed at the jetting zone.

**Step 2: Compare to the material's flow strength.** For a successful bond, the dynamic pressure must exceed the yield strength (or ultimate tensile strength for safety) of the weaker plate:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_p^2 \geq R_m$$

Rearranging this inequality to form a **dimensionless weldability parameter** $W$:

$$W = \frac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m} \geq 1$$

**Step 3: Establish the upper bound.** If $W > 12$, the collision energy is excessive, leading to melting, spall, or brittle intermetallic formation. Therefore, the **acceptable window** for industrial applications is:

$$1 \leq W \leq 12$$

**Step 4: Practical application example.** A titanium Grade 2 plate ($\rho = 4500 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $R_m = 345 \text{ MPa}$) is to be explosion-welded to a carbon steel backing plate. Calculate the permissible flyer velocity range.

**Sub-step 4a — Lower bound (minimum velocity):**
Set $W = 1$:

$$v_{p,min} = \sqrt{\frac{2 R_m}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 345 \times 10^6}{4500}}$$

$$v_{p,min} = \sqrt{1.533 \times 10^5} = 391.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Sub-step 4b — Upper bound (maximum velocity):**
Set $W = 12$:

$$v_{p,max} = \sqrt{12 \times \frac{2 R_m}{\rho}} = v_{p,min} \times \sqrt{12} = 391.6 \times 3.464$$

$$v_{p,max} = 1356.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Conclusion:** For this Ti-steel EXW application, the flyer plate velocity must be controlled between **$391.6 \text{ m/s}$** and **$1356.5 \text{ m/s}$** — this is the **operational design window** for the explosive charge mass selection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density Comparison Across Advanced Processes

This is a frequently tested KTU concept: ranking processes by power density to justify application selection.

### Analytical Derivation:

**Step 1: General definition.** Power density is energy flux per unit area:

$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{eff}}$$

where $A_{eff}$ is the effective interaction area on the workpiece.

**Step 2: Conventional Arc Welding (GMAW reference case).**
Power $P = V \cdot I = 30 \text{ V} \times 250 \text{ A} = 7500 \text{ W}$
Effective arc area $\approx 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 100 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,arc} = \frac{7500}{100} = 75 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Laser Beam Welding (CO₂ laser, 4 kW).**
Effective spot diameter $\approx 0.6 \text{ mm}$, so $A_{eff} = \pi \times (0.3)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,LBW} = \frac{4000}{0.2827} = 14,150 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 1.4 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Electron Beam Welding (60 kV, 100 mA, focal spot $0.4 \text{ mm}$).**
Power $P = 60{,}000 \times 0.1 = 6000 \text{ W}$
$A_{eff} = \pi \times (0.2)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ mm}^2$

$$P_{d,EBW} = \frac{6000}{0.1257} = 47,732 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 4.8 \times 10^4 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Final Hierarchy and Application Justification.**

$$\underbrace{P_{d,arc}}_{75} \ll \underbrace{P_{d,LBW}}_{1.4 \times 10^4} \ll \underbrace{P_{d,EBW}}_{4.8 \times 10^4} \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Why this matters for application:**
- Low $P_d$ → wide HAZ → unsuitable for thin, heat-sensitive components (electronics, aerospace).
- High $P_d$ → narrow HAZ, deep penetration, keyhole mode → suitable for thick-section Ti, stainless, and dissimilar joints.
- **EBW must be in vacuum** to prevent electron scatter, justifying its use in **aerospace, nuclear, and high-value reactor components**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diffusion Bonding Time Estimation (Arrhenius Kinetics)

For diffusion bonding applications in Ti-superalloy turbine components, KTU often asks for the bonding time at a given temperature.

**Step 1: Arrhenius relation for diffusion coefficient.** $D$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$:

$$D = D_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{Q_d}{R T}\right)$$

**Step 2: Approximate bonding time using creep-controlled asperity collapse:**

$$t_{DB} \approx \frac{x^2}{D}$$

where $x$ is the asperity height (typically $1{-}5 \text{ \mu m}$ for well-prepared surfaces).

**Step 3: Example numerical.** For Ti-6Al-4V: $D_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $Q_d = 153 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $T = 1173 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $x = 2 \text{ \mu m} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$.

$$D = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times \exp\left(-\frac{153{,}000}{8.314 \times 1173}\right) = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times \exp(-15.68)$$

$$D = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-7} = 1.86 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate bonding time.**

$$t_{DB} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}{1.86 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.86 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 21.5 \text{ s}$$

This explains why diffusion bonding of Ti-6Al-4V at $1173 \text{ K}$ requires only **$\approx 20{-}30 \text{ s}$** of dwell time in industrial applications — the very basis for its use in **near-net-shape turbine blade assemblies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always convert temperature to **Kelvin** and activation energy to **J/mol** before substituting into the Arrhenius equation. A common student error is using $Q_d$ in kJ/mol directly with $R = 8.314$ — this produces wrong answers by a factor of 1000.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
