# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: List any three industrial applications of Friction Stir Welding (FSW) and justify why FSW is preferred over conventional arc welding for these applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Aerospace fuselage panels (e.g., Boeing 787):** FSW is solid-state, eliminating solidification cracks and porosity common in fusion welding of Al-alloys. Joint efficiency exceeds **$85\%$**.
2. **Marine and shipbuilding decks:** FSW produces joints with superior fatigue and corrosion resistance in Al-5083/5086 hull structures, with no HAZ softening.
3. **Railway and high-speed train body shells:** FSW enables long, defect-free welds in Al-6xxx extrusions with low distortion, replacing TIG which suffers from HAZ strength loss.

> **Marking distribution:** 1 mark per application + 1 mark justification (cluster) = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Why is Electron Beam Welding (EBW) preferred for joining titanium alloys used in aerospace and nuclear applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. EBW operates in a vacuum ($10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ **torr**), preventing oxidation and contamination of reactive titanium at elevated temperatures — **1 mark**.
2. Extremely high power density ($\approx 10^7 \text{ W/mm}^2$) produces a narrow, deep, parallel-walled weld with minimal HAZ and distortion — **1 mark**.
3. Suitable for thick-section Ti components (up to $200 \text{ mm}$) in a single pass, with no filler metal, ensuring parent-metal mechanical properties for safety-critical aerospace/nuclear parts — **1 mark**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice]`**
**Q: (a) Explain with sketches the principle of Friction Stir Welding. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss in detail the industrial applications of FSW in aerospace, automotive, and marine sectors with specific material and property justifications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Principle of Friction Stir Welding:**
A non-consumable rotating tool consisting of a **shoulder** and a **pin (probe)** is plunged into the joint line between two clamped plates. Frictional heat generated between the tool and workpiece softens the material (without melting). The rotating tool traverses along the joint, and the softened material is **stirred** from the leading edge (advancing side) to the trailing edge (retreating side), consolidating behind the tool to form a solid-state joint.

**Key features (to draw in exam):**
- Advancing side (AS) and Retreating side (RS)
- Pin length = plate thickness minus shoulder plunge depth
- Tilt angle $1^\circ$ to $3^\circ$

**Three zones in FSW microstructure:**
1. **Stir Zone (SZ):** Fine recrystallized grains (dynamic recrystallization)
2. **Thermo-Mechanically Affected Zone (TMAZ):** Deformed grains
3. **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** No plastic deformation, only thermal exposure

**Marking distribution:** 3 marks principle + 2 marks microstructural zones + 2 marks process parameters

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Aerospace Applications:**
- Boeing 787, Airbus A350, SpaceX Falcon 9 use FSW for **Al-Li alloy** fuselage skins, **Al-2xxx** wing panels, and **cryogenic tank domes**.
- **Justification:** No solidification defects; joint strength $> 90\%$ of parent metal; superior fatigue performance at $10^7$ cycles.
- **Example:** The vertical fin of Boeing Delta II rocket uses a single-piece FSW panel replacing 12 riveted panels, saving **$20\%$** mass.

**Automotive Applications:**
- **Tailor-welded blanks** of Al-5182/5754 for closure panels (hoods, doors).
- **Battery trays** in EVs (Tesla Model Y, Ford F-150 Lightning) using FSW for flat-bottomed Al enclosures.
- **Justification:** High production speed ($\approx 1 \text{ m/min}$); no filler metal; minimal distortion in thin sheets.

**Marine Applications:**
- **Hull panels** of Al-5083/5086 in fast ferries, naval vessels (INS Vikrant aircraft carrier).
- **Helicopter deck plates** on offshore platforms.
- **Justification:** Superior seawater corrosion resistance; eliminates brittle HAZ zones prone to stress corrosion cracking.

**Marking distribution:** 2 marks aerospace + 2 marks automotive + 2 marks marine + 1 mark for comparative justification

---

### Question B — Choice 2

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice]`**
**Q: (a) Describe the principle and mechanism of Explosion Welding. Derive the weldability window parameter and state its range. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss the industrial applications of Explosion Welding in the production of bimetallic clads, transition joints, and corrosion-resistant linings with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Principle of Explosion Welding:**
Explosion welding uses the **controlled detonation of an explosive charge** placed above a flyer plate, which is accelerated towards a parent (base) plate. On impact, the oblique collision creates a **high-velocity metallic jet** that ejects surface contaminants and oxides, exposing clean metal surfaces. The collision pressure exceeds the dynamic yield strength, producing a metallurgical bond without bulk melting — characteristic **wavy interface** morphology forms due to Helmholtz instability.

**Derivation of Weldability Window $W$:**

The condition for bond formation requires the dynamic pressure at the jetting zone to exceed the weaker material's ultimate tensile strength:

$$\frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot v_p^2 \geq R_m$$

Define the dimensionless weldability window:

$$W = \frac{\rho \cdot v_p^2}{2 \cdot R_m}$$

Industrial limits:
$$1 \leq W \leq 12$$

- $W < 1$ → no bond (insufficient energy)
- $W > 12$ → melting, spall, brittle intermetallics
- $1 \leq W \leq 12$ → wavy bond with good integrity

**Marking distribution:** 2 marks principle + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks window range

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Bimetallic Clad Plates:**
- **Ti-steel clads** (ASTM B898) for pressure vessels in chlor-alkali and fertilizer industries.
- **SS304-carbon steel clads** (ASTM A264) for hydrocrackers and refinery reactors.
- **Cu-Al clads** for transition joints in aluminium smelters and bus-bar connections.
- **Justification:** EXW enables bonding of metals with vastly different melting points, thermal expansion coefficients, and metallurgical incompatibility.

**Transition Joints:**
- **Aluminium-to-steel transition joints** in ship superstructures (Al deckhouse welded to steel hull).
- **Copper-to-stainless transition joints** in cryogenic pipework for LNG carriers.
- **Justification:** Conventional welding cannot join these pairs without forming brittle intermetallics; EXW's sub-microsecond bonding time suppresses intermetallic growth.

**Corrosion-Resistant Linings:**
- **Zirconium-lined reactors** in nuclear fuel processing.
- **Tantalum-lined vessels** for handling hot concentrated acids ($\text{HCl}$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$).
- **Nickel alloy C-276 clads** for flue gas desulfurization (FGD) scrubbers.
- **Justification:** Provides corrosion resistance of expensive alloy only at the surface, reducing cost by $60{-}80\%$ compared to solid alloy construction.

**Marking distribution:** 2 marks clads + 2 marks transition + 2 marks linings + 1 mark economic justification

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing HAZ with TMAZ in FSW** — students often interchange these zones, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** in Arrhenius diffusion equation ($Q_d$ in J/mol, $T$ in K) — a single unit error loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Stating "EXW is just high-velocity welding"** — examiners expect the **jetting mechanism** and **wavy interface** to be explicitly mentioned.
> 4. **Listing applications without metallurgical justification** — always link the process to the *property requirement* (e.g., "FSW used in aerospace because of no HAZ softening in 2xxx Al-alloys").
> 5. **Mixing up EBW and LBW in vacuum requirements** — EBW **requires** vacuum; LBW does **not** (uses shielding gas).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 Applications**

- ✅ **FSW = Solid-state** → No melting, no solidification cracks → Aerospace & marine primary use.
- ✅ **EBW = High vacuum + highest power density** → Ti, Zr, refractory metals in nuclear/aerospace.
- ✅ **LBW = Highest production speed** → Automotive TWBs, battery packs, electronics.
- ✅ **Explosion Welding** → Weldability window **$1 \leq W \leq 12$** → Bimetallic clads, transition joints.
- ✅ **Diffusion Bonding** → Arrhenius-controlled, near-net-shape → Turbine blades, honeycomb.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic Welding** → $20{-}70 \text{ kHz}$ → Electronics, battery tabs, polymer-metal hybrid joints.
- ✅ **Power density hierarchy:** Arc ($10^2{-}10^3$) < LBW ($10^6$) < EBW ($10^7$) $\text{W/mm}^2$.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H_{in} = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ (units: J/mm).
- ✅ **Always justify process selection** with material + property + environment, not just industry name.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistakes:** Unit inconsistency, missing jetting mechanism in EXW, confusing HAZ/TMAZ in FSW, omitting vacuum requirement in EBW.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Process selection in advanced joining is not arbitrary — it is the deterministic outcome of base material metallurgy, service environment, joint geometry, and economic feasibility. Always justify with all four pillars."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Advantages and Limitations" in Advanced Metal Joining

In the context of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques (PEMET416)**, the phrase *Advantages and Limitations* refers to a structured engineering evaluation of the **merits, capabilities, economic feasibility, metallurgical compatibility, and operational constraints** of the non-conventional joining processes covered in Module 3 — predominantly **solid-state and high-energy-density welding processes** such as Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic Welding (USW), Diffusion Bonding (DB), Explosive Welding (EXW), and Cold Pressure Welding (CPW).

Formally, an **advantage** is any process attribute that delivers superior joint quality, energy efficiency, metallurgical soundness, or economic return relative to conventional fusion welding (e.g., SMAW, GMAW). A **limitation** is a process constraint that restricts material applicability, joint geometry, capital investment, or production throughput.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to critically appraise *why* an advanced process is selected over fusion welding, and *what* conditions disqualify its use. Examiner marks are awarded for **justified comparisons**, not memorized lists.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Right Tool for the Right Job"

Imagine you are a carpenter who owns a hammer, a screwdriver, a glue gun, and a laser cutter. Each tool can join two pieces of wood, but:

- The **hammer (Fusion Welding)** is fast and strong, but shatters delicate wood and creates a lot of "sparks" (heat).
- The **glue gun (Diffusion Bonding)** creates invisible, clean joints but takes a long time and needs a controlled "oven" environment.
- The **laser cutter (Electron/Laser Beam Welding)** is incredibly precise and fast, but costs a fortune to buy and requires a special room (vacuum).

Advanced metal joining techniques are precisely this — **specialized tools**, each engineered for a specific "wood type" (material), "joint shape" (geometry), and "production speed" (cycle time). The *advantages* tell you when to pick that tool; the *limitations* tell you when to put it back in the box.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric — Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**
> 
> The HAZ is the region of base metal whose microstructure and mechanical properties have been altered by the heat of welding. In KTU 2024 evaluation, the **size and severity of the HAZ** is the single most cited advantage/limitation parameter for distinguishing advanced from conventional processes.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

The following quantitative metrics are routinely used in the KTU 2024 Scheme to score answers on this topic:

- **Joint Efficiency (η):** Ratio of welded joint strength to base metal strength, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Heat Input (HI):** Energy delivered per unit length, expressed in **kJ/mm**.
- **Distortion (δ):** Angular or dimensional deviation, expressed in **mm** or **degrees (°)**.
- **Production Rate:** Expressed in **m/min** or **parts/hour**.
- **Vacuum Level:** Expressed in **Pascal (Pa)** or **Torr (1 Torr ≈ 133.32 Pa)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Module 3 Joining Processes

Module 3 of PEMET416 typically groups advanced processes into the following engineering families. Each family carries a distinct advantage-limitation profile.

### Family A — Solid-State Friction-Based Processes
- **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)**
- **Friction Welding (FW)** — continuous-drive and inertia variants
- **Friction Surfacing (FS)**

### Family B — Solid-State Pressure-Based Processes
- **Cold Pressure Welding (CPW)**
- **Explosive Welding (EXW)**
- **Roll Bonding (RB)**
- **Diffusion Bonding (DB)**
- **Forge Welding (ancient but foundational)**

### Family C — High-Frequency / Acoustic Processes
- **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)**
- **Ultrasonic Soldering**

## 2.2 Engineering Advantages — The "Why Choose" Analysis

### 2.2.1 Metallurgical Advantages
- **No Melting (Solid-State):** Because peak temperature stays **below the solidus**, there is **no fusion zone, no solidification cracking, and no cast microstructure**. The joint is formed by **plastic deformation and dynamic recrystallization**.
- **Solute Redistribution is Avoided:** Segregation of alloying elements (a common defect in fusion welding of Al-Cu, Ni-superalloys) does not occur.
- **Retention of Parent Metal Properties:** The base metal's heat treatment (e.g., T6 temper in 6061-T6 aluminium) is largely preserved.
- **Finer Grain Structure:** Dynamic recrystallization in FSW typically produces grains in the **1–10 μm** range, often improving strength via the **Hall-Petch relationship**.

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Advantages
- **High Joint Efficiency (η):** FSW of aluminium alloys routinely achieves **η > 80%**, and for **similar-metal combinations** it can exceed **90–100%** of base metal strength.
- **Low Distortion (δ):** Because the thermal gradient is gentle and the clamping force is high, angular distortion is typically **< 1°** in thin-section FSW.
- **Superior Fatigue Performance:** Absence of porosity and undercut produces S-N curves closely matching the parent metal.

### 2.2.3 Economic & Environmental Advantages
- **No Filler Metal / Shielding Gas:** In pure FSW and USW, no consumable electrode or inert gas envelope is required.
- **No Fume Extraction Needed:** Eliminates the need for expensive ventilation (a KTU-favoured industrial point).
- **Energy Efficient:** FSW consumes roughly **2.5% of the energy** required for equivalent arc welding — a critical Industry-4.0 and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) metric.

### 2.2.4 Geometric Advantages
- **All-Position Capability:** FSW can be performed in **1G, 2G, 3G, and 4G positions** with appropriate fixture design.
- **Welding of Dissimilar Metals:** FSW can join **Al-to-Mg, Al-to-Cu, Al-to-Steel** (with intermediate layers) — a near-impossible task for fusion welding without brittle intermetallics.

## 2.3 Engineering Limitations — The "Why Not" Analysis

### 2.3.1 Mechanical & Process Limitations
- **Exit-Hole / Keyhole Defect (FSW-specific):** When the tool is withdrawn, a **keyhole** is left at the end of the weld — a stress concentrator that must be machined off or avoided using **bobbin-tool FSW**.
- **Limited to Ductile Materials:** Friction-based processes require the material to undergo **plastic flow**. Cast irons, ceramics, and high-strength tool steels cannot be friction-stir welded.
- **Thick-Section Penetration:** Standard FSW is limited to **< 50 mm** in aluminium and **< 25 mm** in steel without multi-pass or stationary shoulder techniques.
- **Anvil/Backing Plate Requirement:** Reactive metals like **titanium and magnesium** require a backing material that does not react with the workpiece.

### 2.3.2 Economic Limitations
- **High Capital Cost (CAPEX):** 
  - FSW machine: **$200,000 – $1,500,000**
  - Electron Beam Welder: **$500,000 – $3,000,000**
  - Diffusion Bonding Press: **$1,000,000+**
- **Tool Wear & Replacement Cost:** FSW tools for steel welding suffer severe wear, requiring **PCD, W-Re, or cBN** tools costing **$500–$2,000 each**.
- **Low Production Rate for Small Batches:** FSW cycle times are competitive for long, straight welds, but uneconomical for short, complex 3D welds.

### 2.3.3 Geometric & Operational Limitations
- **Linear or Simple-Path Welds Only:** FSW is intrinsically a *traversing* process. Robotic FSW exists but is complex and expensive.
- **Clamping Force Requirement:** Friction welding requires **2,000 – 50,000 kN** of axial force, demanding massive machine frames.
- **Vacuum Requirement (for EBW, DB):** Pump-down times of **1–4 hours** per batch make Diffusion Bonding impractical for high-volume production.

### 2.3.4 Metallurgical Limitations
- **Formation of Intermetallics in Dissimilar Joining:** FSW of Al-to-Steel produces **Al-Fe intermetallic compounds (IMCs)** such as FeAl, Fe₃Al, FeAl₃. Thickness above **~10 μm** causes embrittlement.
- **Onion-Ring Feature in FSW:** The characteristic "onion ring" band spacing is a metallurgical signature, not a defect — but examiners often confuse students on this point.
- **Residual Stresses in DB:** Mismatch in coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) between dissimilar sheets can produce **thermal residual stresses** exceeding **100 MPa**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is a high-yield summary of all key equations, boundary conditions, and units that KTU examiners expect students to recall and apply for this topic.

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ | Used to compare weld strength vs. parent metal | % |
| Heat Input (General) | $HI = \dfrac{\eta_{arc} \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | For arc processes; $v$ = travel speed | $\text{kJ/mm}$ |
| FSW Heat Input (Estimate) | $HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F N R}{1000 \cdot v}$ | $\mu$ = coeff. of friction, $F$ = axial force, $N$ = RPM, $R$ = tool radius | $\text{kJ/mm}$ |
| Hall-Petch Strengthening | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$ | Explains grain-refinement benefit in FSW nugget | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Diffusion Bonding Time (Arrhenius) | $t = \dfrac{k \cdot T}{D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)}$ | $Q$ = activation energy, $D_0$ = pre-exponential | $\text{seconds}$ |
| Cold Pressure Bonding Pressure | $P_{min} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{1 + f}$ | $f$ = surface roughness factor | $\text{MPa}$ |
| Explosive Welding Velocity | $V_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E}{\rho}}$ | $E$ = explosive energy, $\rho$ = flyer plate density | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Bimetallic Strip Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T L^2}{8 t}$ | $\alpha$ = CTE; quantifies DB distortion | $\text{mm}$ |
| Vacuum Pressure Conversion | $1 \, \text{Torr} \approx 133.32 \, \text{Pa}$ | Used in EBW / DB chamber specification | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Tool Rotational Speed (FSW) | $v / \omega \text{ Ratio} = \dfrac{v_{travel}}{\omega \cdot r_{tool}}$ | Process window parameter | $\text{mm/rev}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The *vertical pipe symbol* $|x|$ is forbidden inside KTU answer table cells. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid table-breaking syntax errors. KTU examiners deduct zero marks for clean typesetting, but students lose presentation marks for broken layouts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These advantage-limitation trade-offs are not academic — they drive multibillion-dollar industry decisions:

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** FSW of fuselage panels — advantage exploited (no HAZ, low distortion); limitation accepted (capital cost).
- **Shipbuilding (Navy LCS, Destroyers):** FSW of aluminium superstructures — exploited for **aluminium-to-aluminium** joints.
- **Nuclear Industry:** Explosive welding of **zirconium-to-stainless-steel** transition joints — exploited for **dissimilar bonding** impossible by fusion.
- **Electronics Packaging:** Ultrasonic welding of **copper battery tabs** — exploited for **solid-state, low-heat input** connection of lithium-ion cells.
- **Heat Exchangers:** Diffusion bonding of titanium plates for plate-fin heat exchangers in **aircraft ECS** — exploited for **100% leak-tight, high-pressure** capability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Friction Stir Welding Heat Input

The total heat generated in Friction Stir Welding arises from two sources: **(i)** friction at the tool-workpiece interface, and **(ii)** plastic deformation in the surrounding material. For analytical simplicity, the dominant friction component is derived below.

### Step 1 — Define the Frictional Shear Stress
At the tool shoulder-workpiece interface, the local shear stress is given by:

$$
\tau = \mu \cdot p
$$

Here, $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction** (typically 0.3–0.5 for FSW) and $p$ is the **local contact pressure** in **Pa**.

### Step 2 — Define the Rotational Velocity at Radius r
A point on the shoulder at radius $r$ from the tool axis moves with tangential velocity:

$$
v_t = 2 \pi f \cdot r
$$

where $f$ is the **rotational frequency in Hz** and $r$ is in **metres (m)**. Tangential velocity is in **m/s**.

### Step 3 — Compute Local Heat Generation Rate
The differential frictional power generated on an annular ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$ is:

$$
d\dot{Q} = \tau \cdot v_t \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

This is the **rate of heat generation in Watts (W)**.

### Step 4 — Integrate Over the Shoulder Radius
Integrating from the pin radius $r_p$ to the shoulder radius $R$:

$$
\dot{Q}_{friction} = \int_{r_p}^{R} \mu \cdot p \cdot (2 \pi f \cdot r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{friction} = 4 \pi^2 \mu p f \int_{r_p}^{R} r^2 \, dr
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\int_{r_p}^{R} r^2 \, dr = \left[ \dfrac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_p}^{R} = \dfrac{R^3 - r_p^3}{3}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify
Substituting back:

$$
\dot{Q}_{friction} = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi^2 \mu p f \left( R^3 - r_p^3 \right)
$$

This is the **total frictional power in Watts**.

### Step 6 — Convert to Heat Input per Unit Length
Dividing by the tool traverse speed $v$ (in m/s) and converting to **kJ/mm**:

$$
HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{friction}}{1000 \cdot v \cdot 1000} \cdot 1000 = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{friction}}{v}
$$

$$
HI_{FSW} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu p f \left( R^3 - r_p^3 \right)}{3 \, v \times 10^6} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

A simplified empirical form commonly used in KTU problems:

$$
HI_{FSW} \approx \dfrac{2 \pi \mu F N R}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

where $F$ is the axial force in **N**, $N$ is rotational speed in **rev/min (rpm)**, $R$ is the shoulder radius in **mm**, and $v$ is the traverse speed in **mm/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** FSW heat input is typically **0.2 – 1.0 kJ/mm**, whereas GMAW of the same aluminium joint requires **1.5 – 3.0 kJ/mm**. This ~3× to 5× reduction is the primary metallurgical advantage of FSW.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Assessment

The following **Analytical Matrix** is the single most examination-relevant tool for this topic. It cross-tabulates the eight (8) most-asked Module 3 processes against the most-cited advantage and limitation parameters.

| Process | Primary Advantage | Primary Limitation | Best-Suited Material | Typical Joint Efficiency $\eta$ | Capital Cost Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | No HAZ, no fusion defects | Exit-hole defect at tool retraction | Al-alloys (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx) | 80–100% | High |
| **Friction Welding (FW)** | Short cycle time (sec) | Round cross-sections only | Steel shafts, valve stems | 95–100% | Medium |
| **Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** | No heat, no flux, fast (< 1 s) | Limited to thin foils (< 2 mm) | Cu, Al foils, battery tabs | 70–90% | Low |
| **Cold Pressure Welding (CPW)** | True room-temperature bonding | Requires high deformation (> 60%) | Al, Cu, Pb, Zn | 80–100% | Low |
| **Explosive Welding (EXW)** | Joins vast dissimilar pairs | Safety licensing, low repeatability | Ti-SS, Al-Steel, Cu-SS | 90–100% | High (regulated) |
| **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** | 100% dense, no micro-defects | Long cycle (1–4 h), vacuum req. | Ti, Ni-superalloys, ceramics | 95–100% | Very High |
| **Roll Bonding (RB)** | Continuous strip production | Limited to flat geometries | Al-St, Cu-Al, SS-Cu | 70–90% | Medium |
| **Forge Welding** | No specialist equipment | Highly operator-skill dependent | Low-carbon steel (historically) | 60–80% | Very Low |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Joint Efficiency Calculation

**Problem (typical KTU numerical):**
A friction stir welded joint in **AA 6061-T6 aluminium** has an ultimate tensile strength of **245 MPa**, while the parent metal UTS is **310 MPa**. Calculate the joint efficiency and comment on whether this value is acceptable per aerospace standards (η ≥ 80% required).

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Quantities
- Joint UTS: $\sigma_{joint} = 245 \, \text{MPa}$
- Base metal UTS: $\sigma_{base} = 310 \, \text{MPa}$

### Step 2 — Apply the Joint Efficiency Formula
$$
\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values
$$
\eta = \dfrac{245}{310} \times 100
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Result
$$
\eta = 0.7903 \times 100 = 79.03 \%
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
The calculated efficiency is **79.03%**, which is **just below the aerospace acceptance threshold of 80%**. Possible corrective actions a KTU examiner would credit:

- **Increase rotational speed** $N$ → finer grains → higher nugget strength.
- **Reduce traverse speed** $v$ → better consolidation → less tunnel defect.
- **Tilt the tool by 2–3°** → improves material flow.
- **Use a conical-threaded pin** → enhances vertical mixing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students often forget to **multiply by 100** and write $\eta = 0.79$ instead of $79\%$. The unit **%** is mandatory in KTU answer scripts.

The diagram below maps the functional flow of the Friction Stir Welding process, showing how the *advantage* (no fusion) and *limitation* (keyhole defect) arise from the tool-workpiece interaction.

```mermaid

    B --> C[Rotate FSW Tool at omega rpm]
    C --> D[Plunge Tool Shoulder into Joint Line]
    D --> E[Tool Pin Enters Plasticized Material]
    E --> F[Frictional Heat Generated T below Solidus]
    F --> G[Material Undergoes Plastic Flow Around Pin]
    G --> H[Dynamic Recrystallization Forms Fine Grains]
    H --> I[Tool Traverses Along Joint Direction]
    I --> J[Continuous Solid-State Bond Forms]
    J --> K{Process End Trigger}
    K -->|Tool Retraction| L[Exit Hole / Keyhole Defect Remains]
    K -->|Complete Traverse| M[Dense Solid-State Weld Achieved]
    L --> N[Optional Friction Stir Processing FSP to Close Hole]
    N --> M
    M --> O[End: Quality Inspection NDT and Tensile Test]
```
