# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of Friction Stir Welding over conventional fusion welding processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Friction Stir Welding (FSW) offers the following advantages over conventional fusion welding:

1. **Solid-State Bonding with No Fusion Zone:** The peak temperature during FSW remains below the solidus temperature of the base metal. As a result, common fusion-related defects such as porosity, solidification cracking, and liquation cracking are completely avoided. *(1 mark)*

2. **Superior Mechanical Properties and Joint Efficiency:** Due to fine dynamic recrystallization grains in the stir zone (typically 1–10 μm), the FSW nugget exhibits high strength. Joint efficiencies of **80–100%** are routinely achieved in aluminium alloys. *(1 mark)*

3. **Environmentally Clean Process:** FSW does not require shielding gas, filler metal, or fume extraction. It is energy-efficient, consuming only about **2.5%** of the energy required for arc welding of similar joints. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Mention any three major limitations of Diffusion Bonding as a metal joining process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Long Cycle Time:** Diffusion bonding requires sustained hours of isothermal holding (typically **1–4 hours** at 0.5–0.8 Tₘ), making it uneconomical for high-volume production. *(1 mark)*

2. **Vacuum or Controlled Atmosphere Requirement:** To prevent oxidation of the bonding surfaces, the process demands a high-vacuum environment of **< 10⁻³ Pa**, which adds significant capital and operational cost. *(1 mark)*

3. **Limited to Specific Geometries and Materials:** Diffusion bonding is best suited for simple, planar or cylindrical geometries and ductile materials. Complex 3D shapes and high-strength tool steels cannot be effectively diffusion bonded. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1 — Memorized Lists:** Writing a bullet list of 10 advantages and 10 limitations *without connecting them to a specific process or application* earns only **50% marks**. Always *justify* each point with **why** and **how**.
> - **Pitfall 2 — Confusing FSW and Friction Welding:** FSW uses a non-consumable tool traversing a joint line; classical Friction Welding joins two parts by relative rotation. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for mixing them up.
> - **Pitfall 3 — Missing Units:** Always quantify. Write *"3 kJ/mm heat input"* not *"low heat input."* KTU 2024 scheme marks numerical quantification **twice as heavily** as qualitative statements.
> - **Pitfall 4 — Ignoring Cost:** Engineering decisions are governed by economics. A complete answer must mention **CAPEX, OPEX, and payback period** for each process discussed.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **advantages of Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** over conventional arc welding processes, with reference to metallurgical, mechanical, and economic factors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **major limitations of FSW** and indicate the engineering strategies adopted to overcome each limitation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Advantages of FSW (7 Marks)

**1. Metallurgical Advantages (3 marks)**

The defining metallurgical advantage of FSW is that it is a **solid-state joining process**, with peak temperatures typically reaching **0.8 Tₘ** of the base metal — well below the solidus. This means:

- **No fusion zone** is formed; the weld nugget consists of dynamically recrystallized fine grains, which obey the **Hall-Petch relationship** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \dfrac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$. Finer grains → higher yield strength. *(1 mark)*
- **No solidification cracking** because there is no liquid-to-solid transformation. *(0.5 marks)*
- **No porosity** since gases are not trapped in a freezing weld pool. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Retention of base metal temper and precipitate structure** — critical for heat-treated aluminium alloys like 6061-T6. *(1 mark)*

**2. Mechanical Advantages (2 marks)**

- **High joint efficiency** of 80–100% of base metal UTS. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Low angular distortion** of typically **< 1°** due to symmetric thermal field. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Superior fatigue strength**, with S-N curves closely matching parent metal. *(0.5 marks)*
- **All-position welding capability** (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G). *(0.5 marks)*

**3. Economic and Environmental Advantages (2 marks)**

- **No filler metal, no shielding gas, no spatter, no slag.** This translates to direct consumable cost savings. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Energy consumption** of FSW is approximately **2.5% of arc welding** for equivalent joints. *(0.5 marks)*
- **No fumes or UV radiation** — eliminates need for expensive fume extraction and heavy worker PPE. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Automation-friendly** — easily integrated with CNC and robotic systems for Industry 4.0 lines. *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Limitations of FSW (7 Marks)

**1. Exit-Hole / Keyhole Defect (1.5 marks)**
At the end of the weld, when the tool is withdrawn, a **keyhole** is left. This is a stress concentrator and is unacceptable in fatigue-loaded structures.
- **Mitigation:** Use **bobbin-tool FSW** (two shoulders, no keyhole), **retractable pin tools**, or **friction stir processing (FSP)** to close the hole.

**2. Limited Material Applicability (1.5 marks)**
FSW requires the material to undergo plastic flow at the operating temperature. Hard, brittle materials (cast iron, ceramics, hardened tool steel with HRC > 50) cannot be FSWed.
- **Mitigation:** Use **stir-cast composites** with lower melting matrices, or apply **hybrid FSW + induction preheat** for high-strength steels.

**3. Thick-Section Limitation (1.5 marks)**
Standard FSW is limited to ~50 mm in Al and ~25 mm in steel due to the inability of the tool to generate sufficient heat at depth.
- **Mitigation:** **Multi-pass FSW**, **stationary shoulder FSW**, **bobbin-tool FSW**, or **underwater FSW** for thick titanium sections.

**4. Tool Wear and Cost (1 mark)**
Welding steels at > 500°C causes severe tool wear. Standard H13 tool steel is consumed within **~10 m** of steel FSW.
- **Mitigation:** Use **PCD (Polycrystalline Diamond)**, **W-Re**, or **cBN (cubic Boron Nitride)** tools, plus active cooling.

**5. Geometric Restriction (1 mark)**
FSW is intrinsically a **traversing process** — best for straight or gently curved welds. 3D welds are difficult.
- **Mitigation:** **Robotic FSW** with 6-axis arms; gantry-style machines for aerospace.

**6. Clamping Force and Machine Rigidity (0.5 marks)**
Required axial forces of **2,000–50,000 kN** demand massive machine frames.
- **Mitigation:** Use **hydro-pneumatic clamping** and **inertia-based FSW** variants.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare the **advantages and limitations of Explosive Welding (EXW) and Diffusion Bonding (DB)** for joining dissimilar metals. Highlight one industrial application of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **advantages of Ultrasonic Welding (USW)** for micro-joining in electronics and battery applications, and explain the **limitations** that restrict its use to thin foils. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): EXW vs DB Comparison (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Explosive Welding (EXW) | Diffusion Bonding (DB) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Type** | Solid-state, impact-driven | Solid-state, diffusional |
| **Temperature** | Near ambient (no external heat) | 0.5–0.8 Tₘ (high) |
| **Pressure Source** | Detonation shock wave | Hydraulic press |
| **Joining Time** | Microseconds | 1–4 hours |
| **Vacuum Required** | No | Yes (< 10⁻³ Pa) |
| **Dissimilar Joining** | Excellent (Al-Steel, Ti-SS, Cu-SS) | Good (limited by CTE mismatch) |
| **Joint Strength** | 90–100% parent metal | 95–100% parent metal |
| **Production Volume** | Low (one shot per charge) | Low–Medium (batch) |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate, but high regulatory cost | Very high (vacuum + heating) |
| **Safety / Regulation** | Strict explosive licensing required | Standard industrial safety |

**Advantages of EXW (1.5 marks):**
- Can join virtually **any combination of dissimilar metals** that are metallurgically incompatible for fusion welding.
- Forms a characteristic **wavy interface** (with proper stand-off distance) that mechanically interlocks the plates.
- **Industrial Application:** Zirconium-to-stainless-steel transition joints in **nuclear reactor pressure vessels** (BWR/PWR).

**Advantages of DB (1.5 marks):**
- Produces joints with **100% theoretical density** and no interface waviness.
- Allows bonding of **non-metals (ceramics, composites)** with metallic interlayers.
- **Industrial Application:** **Plate-fin heat exchangers** in aircraft environmental control systems (ECS), using titanium or aluminium-silicon.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): USW Advantages and Limitations (7 Marks)

**Advantages of USW (4 marks):**

1. **True Solid-State, No External Heat:** Joining occurs at ambient temperature through interfacial friction at **20–40 kHz**, eliminating the HAZ entirely. Critical for heat-sensitive battery electrodes and electronic components. *(1 mark)*

2. **Ultra-Fast Cycle Time:** Each weld takes **< 1 second**, ideal for high-volume production of lithium-ion battery packs, solar cell ribbon tabbing, and consumer electronics. *(1 mark)*

3. **No Filler, Flux, or Shielding Gas:** Reduces material cost and eliminates post-weld cleaning. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Energy Efficient and Environmentally Clean:** Power consumption is **< 100 W per weld**, with no fumes, spatter, or UV radiation. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Joining of Dissimilar Combinations:** USW can bond **Cu-Al, Al-Ni, Cu-Cu, Al-Al** combinations common in battery packs. *(0.5 marks)*

6. **Automation-Ready:** Easily integrated into robotic battery pack assembly lines. *(0.5 marks)*

**Limitations of USW (3 marks):**

1. **Thickness Limitation:** The acoustic energy attenuates rapidly through metal thickness. USW is restricted to **foils < 2 mm thick**. For thicker sections, ultrasonic spot welding can be used up to **3–4 mm** in soft aluminium but with rapidly decreasing strength. *(1 mark)*

2. **High Clamping Force Sensitivity:** Insufficient clamping pressure → interfacial slip, no bonding. Excessive pressure → tool marks and deformation. The **process window** is narrow. *(0.5 marks)*

3. **Limited Weld Area:** Each ultrasonic weld is a small spot, typically **3–12 mm diameter**. Long welds require multiple overlapping spots, reducing throughput and creating stress concentration. *(0.5 marks)*

4. **Tool (Horn) Wear:** The ultrasonic horn is subject to cyclic fatigue at **20 kHz** and wears at the contact face, requiring periodic re-machining or replacement. *(0.5 marks)*

5. **Material Property Dependent:** Highly work-hardened materials (e.g., fully hard temper 301 stainless steel) cannot be ultrasonically welded due to limited ductility. *(0.5 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEMET416 Module 3**

- **FSW is the flagship solid-state process** — always mention **no HAZ, no fusion, dynamic recrystallization, joint efficiency > 80%** as the primary advantages.
- **Keyhole defect is the #1 limitation** of FSW — counter with **bobbin-tool, retractable pin, or FSP** techniques.
- **Diffusion Bonding = high CAPEX, long cycle, vacuum needed** — use only when **100% density and no interface defects** are mandatory.
- **Explosive Welding = unique for dissimilar metal bonding** — but **safety licensing** is the operational bottleneck.
- **Ultrasonic Welding = thin foil champion** — < 1 second cycle, ambient temperature, ideal for **battery tabs and electronics**.
- **Cold Pressure Welding = room-temperature large-deformation process** — needs **> 60% deformation** to break surface oxide film.
- **Forge Welding = ancient but pedagogically important** — demonstrates the foundational principle of solid-state bonding by plastic deformation.
- **Joint Efficiency formula** $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{joint}}{\sigma_{base}} \times 100$ — must be in **%**, not decimals.
- **FSW Heat Input** is **3× to 5× lower** than arc welding — this is the metallurgical foundation of FSW's advantages.
- **Intermetallic layer** in dissimilar FSW (Al-Fe) must be **< 10 μm** to avoid embrittlement.
- **Hall-Petch strengthening** explains why FSW nugget is sometimes **stronger** than base metal.
- **Always quantify** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards numerical statements with **2× marks** over qualitative ones.
- **Draw a comparison table** for any 7+ mark question on this topic — it organises the answer and earns **presentation marks**.
- **Mention an industrial application** for every process discussed — e.g., FSW → Boeing 787 fuselage; EXW → nuclear transition joints; DB → aircraft heat exchangers; USW → EV battery tabs.
- **Final revision mantra:** *Solid-state = no HAZ, but tool/machine limit; Fusion = faster, but metallurgical risk; Choice = governed by material, geometry, volume, and cost.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plasma Arc Welding (PAW): Theory, Transferred Arc and Non-transferred Arc Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plasma Arc Welding (PAW): Theory, Transferred Arc and Non-transferred Arc Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)** is an advanced arc welding process in which a **constricted arc** is established between a **tungsten electrode (non-consumable)** and the **workpiece (transferred arc mode)** — or between the electrode and a water-cooled copper constricting nozzle (non-transferred arc mode). The arc is forced through a fine bore nozzle, which raises its **current density**, **energy density**, and **temperature** to levels far exceeding those of conventional Tungsten Inert Gas (TIG) welding. The resulting **plasma jet** (ionized gas, the **fourth state of matter**) is used to fuse the base metal.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sunbeam Through a Pinhole" Analogy

Imagine sunlight falling on a sheet of paper. The paper just gets warm. But if you focus that same sunlight through a magnifying glass onto a single point, the paper **ignites**. The energy per unit area (energy density) was multiplied simply by *concentrating* the flow.

PAW applies this same principle to an electric arc:
- In ordinary **TIG welding**, the arc spreads out loosely between the electrode tip and the workpiece — like unfocused sunlight.
- In **Plasma Arc Welding**, the arc is **squeezed (constricted)** by a water-cooled copper nozzle before it exits. This **constriction** dramatically increases the **temperature (15,000–30,000 °C)**, **velocity (Mach 1 or supersonic)**, and **energy density (10–100× higher than TIG)** of the jet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasma — The Fourth State of Matter:**
> When a gas is heated to extreme temperatures, electrons are stripped from atoms, producing a soup of **ions + free electrons + neutral atoms**. This ionized, electrically conductive gas is called **plasma**. On Earth, plasma is rare; in the universe, it is the most common state (stars, lightning, auroras, neon signs, the Sun's surface ~5,500 °C; welding plasma reaches 20,000–50,000 °C).

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Plasma temperature (PAW jet core) | **15,000 – 50,000 °C** |
| Ionization potential of Argon (Ar) | **15.7 eV** |
| Ionization potential of Helium (He) | **24.5 eV** |
| Ionization potential of Hydrogen (H₂) | **13.6 eV** |
| Arc velocity (PAW) | **Subsonic to supersonic (Mach 1 to Mach 2)** |
| Energy density of PAW vs TIG | **~10× to 100× higher than TIG** |
| Constricting nozzle bore diameter | **0.76 – 4.8 mm** |
| Argon as plasma gas (most common) | > **99.95 % purity** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Constriction — How PAW Works

The key to PAW lies in **three simultaneous constricting effects** that compress the arc into a narrow, super-energetic column. Let us break this down step by step.

**Step 1 — Thermal Constriction**
- The water-cooled copper nozzle surrounding the arc is at a very low temperature.
- It cools the outer periphery of the arc column, raising its electrical resistance at the edges.
- Current is forced to flow through the **hot, conductive core**, squeezing the arc inward.

**Step 2 — Mechanical (Orifice) Constriction**
- The nozzle has a small, precise orifice (bore) of diameter typically 0.76–4.8 mm.
- The arc is physically forced through this narrow opening.
- This converts a wide, low-pressure arc into a narrow, high-velocity plasma jet.

**Step 3 — Magnetic (Electromagnetic) Constriction — The Pinch Effect**
- The arc current flows in a column; parallel current-carrying conductors **attract** each other.
- This inward magnetic force (Lorentz / pinch force) further compresses the plasma column.
- The combined effect produces a jet with **extreme temperature, velocity, and momentum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "Pilot Arc" is Needed:**
> The main arc (especially in transferred mode) cannot self-start because the open-circuit voltage (~80 V DC) of typical PAW power sources is insufficient to ionize the gas column reliably across the stand-off distance. A small **high-frequency / high-voltage pilot arc (~5000–10,000 V, ~10–30 A)** is struck first between the electrode and the nozzle. This ionizes the gas, providing a conductive path. Only then is the main welding arc (between electrode and workpiece) initiated.

### 2.2 Gas System — The Three-Gas Architecture

A PAW torch typically uses **three distinct gas streams**, each with a specific engineering role:

1. **Plasma Gas (Primary / Orifice Gas):**
   - Flows through the constricting nozzle, becomes ionized, and forms the plasma jet.
   - Common gases: **Argon (most common), Argon + Hydrogen (2–15 % H₂), Helium**.
   - Hydrogen raises arc voltage and adds heat.
   - Flow rate: **0.2 – 6 L/min** (depending on current and nozzle).

2. **Shielding Gas (Secondary Gas):**
   - Surrounds the plasma jet as it exits the nozzle, protecting the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination (O₂, N₂, H₂O vapor).
   - Typically the **same composition** as the plasma gas but at higher flow rate.
   - Flow rate: **5 – 30 L/min**.

3. **Backing Gas (Trailing / Underbead Gas):**
   - Inert gas (usually Ar or Ar + H₂) supplied to the **root side** of the joint.
   - Prevents oxidation and porosity on the underside of the weld.
   - Critical for stainless steels, titanium, and reactive metals.

### 2.3 The Two Operating Modes (Current Range Defines Them)

| Mode | Current Range | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Micro-plasma** | **0.1 – 25 A** | Piloted arc; stable at very low currents | Foils, thin sheets, mesh, instrumentation |
| **Melt-in (conduction)** | **25 – 200 A** | Plasma jet melts metal; no keyhole | Medium thickness |
| **Keyhole** | **> 100 A (typically 200–400 A)** | Plasma jet **pierces** through the plate, forming a vapor cavity | Full penetration in single pass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micro-plasma welding is a unique PAW capability.** Conventional TIG becomes unstable below ~10 A because the arc is not constricted and tends to wander. PAW's constricted pilot arc remains stable down to **0.1 A**, enabling welding of foils as thin as **0.025 mm** — impossible by almost any other process.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (Arc Welding, KTU Standard):** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | $H$ in **kJ/mm**, $V$ = arc voltage (**V**), $I$ = current (**A**), $S$ = travel speed (**mm/min**), $\eta$ = thermal efficiency (dimensionless) | Total heat delivered per unit length of weld. **For PAW: η ≈ 0.55–0.85** depending on mode. |
| 2 | **PAW efficiency (η)** | Micro-plasma: η ≈ **0.30–0.50**; Melt-in: η ≈ **0.50–0.70**; Keyhole: η ≈ **0.60–0.85** | Higher than TIG (η ≈ 0.40–0.60) due to concentrated jet. |
| 3 | **Energy Density (E_d):** $E_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{jet}}$ | $E_d$ in **W/mm²**, $A_{jet}$ = cross-sectional area of plasma jet exit (**mm²**) | Quantifies concentration of energy. PAW: 10²–10⁴ W/mm²; TIG: ~10–10² W/mm². |
| 4 | **Current Density (J):** $J = \dfrac{I}{A_{electrode}}$ | $J$ in **A/mm²**, $A_{electrode}$ = electrode tip area | PAW uses a **blunted (rounded) tungsten tip** — not pointed as in TIG — to spread current and reduce tungsten erosion. |
| 5 | **Plasma Gas Flow Rate (Argon):** | Micro-plasma: **0.2–0.5 L/min**; Keyhole: **1.5–6 L/min** | Too low → unstable arc; too high → extinguishes arc. |
| 6 | **Stand-off Distance** | **3 – 12 mm** typical (closer = higher heat, deeper penetration) | Distance from nozzle exit to workpiece. |
| 7 | **Constriction Effect (qualitative):** $T_{PAW} \gg T_{TIG}$ | Temperature amplification factor ≈ **2–4×** | Mechanical + thermal + magnetic constriction raise temperature dramatically. |
| 8 | **Power:** $P = V \cdot I$ | $P$ in **W** (or kW) | Welding power. |
| 9 | **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width — empirical (PAW):** | **Smaller than TIG** for same heat input | Narrow, parallel-sided HAZ. |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of PAW in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** Welding of thin-walled stainless-steel honeycomb structures, turbine blades, jet engine casings, rocket fuel tanks (Titanium Ti-6Al-4V).
- **Nuclear:** Stainless-steel fuel cladding, cryogenic vessels.
- **Electronics & Instrumentation:** Hermetic sealing of relays, sensors, instrument cases (micro-plasma).
- **Chemical & Process Industry:** Tube-to-tube-sheet welding of heat exchangers, longitudinal welds in stainless-steel pipes.
- **Medical:** Welding of titanium and stainless-steel implants and surgical instruments.
- **Automotive:** Specialized high-precision joints.
- **Shipbuilding & Defence:** Joining of armor plates in keyhole mode for single-pass full penetration.

> [!TIP]
> **PAW vs TIG — KTU Favourite Comparison Question (be ready):**
> PAW has (i) higher energy density, (ii) higher temperature, (iii) faster travel speed for same penetration, (iv) narrower HAZ, (v) stable low-current operation, (vi) square butt joint without edge preparation in keyhole mode. **Disadvantages:** (i) higher equipment cost, (ii) more complex torch, (iii) higher noise (~100 dB) and UV/IR radiation, (iv) requires skilled operator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input in PAW (KTU Board-Standard Treatment)

The **heat input** is the single most important quantity an examiner tests for any welding process. Below is a fully worked derivation tailored to the KTU expectation level.

**Step 1 — Electrical Power Generated by the Arc:**
The arc is a load. With voltage $V$ (in volts) and current $I$ (in amperes), the electrical power generated is:

$$P_{elec} = V \cdot I \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

This is the total rate at which electrical energy is delivered to the arc.

**Step 2 — Thermal Efficiency (η):**
Not all electrical energy is converted to useful heat in the workpiece; some is lost to:
- Cooling water in the nozzle (large fraction in PAW),
- Radiation and convection from the arc column,
- Heating of the plasma gas itself.

The fraction that actually reaches the workpiece is the **thermal efficiency** $\eta$. So the useful heat power is:

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

**Step 3 — Heat per Unit Length (Heat Input):**
If the torch travels at speed $S$ mm/min, then in **one minute** the arc covers $S$ mm of weld. The heat supplied in that one minute is $P_{useful} \times 60$ J (since power × time = energy; 1 minute = 60 s).

To express this **per millimetre of weld length**, divide by $S$:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

The factor **1000** converts Joules to kilojoules. **This is the KTU standard heat-input formula** — memorize it verbatim.

**Step 4 — Heat Input in Alternative Units (kJ/m):**
Some textbooks express it per metre of weld:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 100} \quad \text{[kJ/m]} \quad \text{(with S in mm/min)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units discipline is heavily penalized in KTU valuation.** Always carry units through every step. A typical examiner's "valuation shortcut" — if your final answer is dimensionally correct, you get full marks on units.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Keyhole PAW (14-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]:**
A **keyhole PAW** operation on a 6 mm thick stainless-steel plate uses the following process parameters:
- Arc voltage: $V = 30$ V
- Welding current: $I = 250$ A
- Travel speed: $S = 250$ mm/min
- Thermal efficiency for keyhole PAW: $\eta = 0.70$

**Calculate:**
(a) The welding power.
(b) The heat input in kJ/mm.
(c) The energy density of the plasma jet, given a nozzle bore diameter of 3.0 mm.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(a) Welding Power:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 30 \text{ V} \times 250 \text{ A} = 7500 \text{ W} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(b) Heat Input:**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} = \frac{0.70 \times 30 \times 250 \times 60}{250 \times 1000}$$

Simplify numerator: $0.70 \times 30 = 21$; $21 \times 250 = 5250$; $5250 \times 60 = 315{,}000$

Simplify denominator: $250 \times 1000 = 250{,}000$

$$H = \frac{315{,}000}{250{,}000} = 1.26 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(c) Energy Density:**

Nozzle exit area: $A_{jet} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (3.0)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 9}{4} = 7.069 \text{ mm}^2$

$$E_d = \frac{P}{A_{jet}} = \frac{7500 \text{ W}}{7.069 \text{ mm}^2} \approx 1061 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Result:** This energy density (~10³ W/mm²) is roughly **10–100× higher than TIG**, confirming PAW's superior concentration of energy.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Micro-Plasma Mode (Conceptual + 7-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]:**
A micro-plasma welding operation is performed on a 0.5 mm thick Inconel foil with:
- $V = 22$ V, $I = 8$ A, $S = 200$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.40$, nozzle bore = 1.0 mm.

**(a)** Calculate the heat input.
**(b)** Comment on the stability of the process compared to TIG at the same current.

**Solution:**

**(a)** $H = \dfrac{0.40 \times 22 \times 8 \times 60}{200 \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.40 \times 22 \times 8 \times 60}{200{,}000} = \dfrac{4224}{200{,}000} = 0.02112 \text{ kJ/mm} = 21.12 \text{ J/mm}$

**(b)** At 8 A, TIG would be **highly unstable** — the arc would wander, lift off, and the process would be uncontrollable. In PAW, the constricted pilot arc **remains stable** because:
- The nozzle mechanically constrains the arc column.
- Pilot arc is sustained by a separate low-power source (~10 A).
- The result: **stable, repeatable welds on foils as thin as 0.025 mm.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Transferred vs Non-Transferred Arc Comparison (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | **Transferred Arc PAW** | **Non-Transferred Arc PAW** |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc path** | Electrode → **Workpiece** | Electrode → **Nozzle** (workpiece is *outside* the arc circuit) |
| **Pilot arc** | Required to initiate main arc | The main arc *is* the pilot arc |
| **Plasma jet** | Plasma jet forms between electrode tip and workpiece; constricting nozzle only **partially** constricts | Plasma jet forms **inside** the nozzle and is **fully constricted**, then ejected toward workpiece |
| **Energy transfer to workpiece** | Direct (conduction + kinetic + radiative from arc column terminating on workpiece) | Indirect (plasma jet impinges on workpiece like a flame) |
| **Voltage** | Higher (~25–40 V) — because the arc spans the stand-off distance | Lower (~20–30 V) — arc is short (electrode-to-nozzle) |
| **Current** | Higher (100–400 A typical for keyhole) | Lower (typically < 200 A) |
| **Heat intensity on workpiece** | Very high — suitable for **keyhole / full penetration** | Lower — suitable for **surface heating, melting, spray coating** |
| **Typical application** | **Welding** (most common PAW configuration) | **Plasma cutting** (low current), **plasma spraying**, **transferred-arc + non-transferred hybrid cutting of thicker plates**, surface hardening |
| **Electrode wear** | Moderate (workpiece completes the circuit) | Higher (arc root at nozzle can heat the nozzle) |
| **KTU mnemonic** | "T = To the workpiece" | "N = Nozzle is the anode" |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick memory trick for KTU viva:**
> - **Transferred** = arc **transfers** from electrode **to the workpiece** (workpiece is an electrode).
> - **Non-transferred** = arc stays **inside the torch** (between electrode and nozzle); workpiece is just a passive target for the hot plasma jet.

---

### 3.5 Equipment Block Architecture — A KTU-Relevant Decomposition

A complete PAW system integrates **six functional subsystems**, each with a specific engineering role:

1. **Power Source** — DC constant-current (CC) or pulsed DC; 100–400 A typical, OCV 80 V.
2. **Pilot Arc Unit (HF + HV starter)** — 5–10 kV, 10–30 A pilot.
3. **Plasma Torch** — Tungsten electrode, water-cooled constricting nozzle, gas passages, shielding cup.
4. **Plasma Gas Console** — Flow meter / regulator (typically rotameter) for plasma gas.
5. **Shielding Gas Console** — Separate flow meter for shielding gas.
6. **Cooling Water Circulator** — Cools both torch and nozzle (recirculating chiller at ~20–30 L/min).

(Drawn in detail in Section 4.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Complete PAW System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Power Source DC Constant Current] --> TORCH[PAW Torch]
    PILOT[Pilot Arc Unit HV HF Starter] --> TORCH
    PGAS[Plasma Gas Console Argon or Ar plus H2] --> TORCH
    SGAS[Shielding Gas Console Argon] --> TORCH
    COOL[Cooling Water Circulator] --> TORCH
    TORCH --> WP[Workpiece Weld Zone]
    BGAS[Backing Gas Argon Root Side] --> WP
    WP --> OUT[Plasma Jet Keyhole Weld]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Comparison of Transferred vs Non-Transferred Arc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TR[Transferred Arc PAW]
        E1[Tungsten Electrode Cathode] -->|Arc 25 to 40 V| W1[Workpiece Anode]
        N1[Constricting Nozzle] -. Constricts arc path .-> W1
        P1[Pilot Arc Electrode to Nozzle] -. Initiates .-> E1
    end

    subgraph NT[Non Transferred Arc PAW]
        E2[Tungsten Electrode Cathode] -->|Arc 20 to 30 V| N2[Water Cooled Nozzle Anode]
        JET[Plasma Jet ejected] --> W2[Workpiece Passive Target]
        N2 -. Forms .-> JET
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Process Flow of a PAW Weld (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Set parameters V I S gas flow] --> A2[Strike pilot arc HF HV]
    A2 --> A3{Pilot arc stable}
    A3 -- No --> A2
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Initiate main arc transferred]
    A4 --> A5[Plasma jet forms keyhole or melt pool]
    A5 --> A6[Shielding gas protects pool]
    A5 --> A7[Backing gas protects root]
    A6 --> A8[Travel at speed S]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[End of weld]
    A9 --> A10[Decay arc and post flow shielding gas]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Three Operating Modes Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q{Thickness of plate and current} --> A1[Below 1 mm 0.1 to 25 A]
    Q --> A2[1 to 6 mm 25 to 200 A]
    Q --> A3[Above 6 mm 200 to 400 A]
    A1 --> B1[Micro Plasma Mode Conduction like]
    A2 --> B2[Melt in Mode Conduction]
    A3 --> B3[Keyhole Mode Full Penetration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no Mermaid "physical" drawing of the torch?**
> Mermaid is a flowchart/diagram engine — not a CAD tool. Attempting a photorealistic cross-section of a PAW torch in Mermaid produces cluttered, illegible output. Following the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram-fallback protocol**, I have instead rendered the **functional and process-level block architecture** (electrical, gas, cooling, sequencing, mode-decision) — the same level of information that a KTU examiner expects in a board exam sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model 3-mark]**
**Define Plasma Arc Welding. State any two advantages of PAW over TIG welding.**

**Model Answer:**
Plasma Arc Welding is an arc welding process in which a **constricted arc** is established between a non-consumable tungsten electrode and the workpiece, and the arc is forced through a water-cooled copper constricting nozzle to produce a high-velocity, high-temperature **plasma jet** for welding.

**Two advantages over TIG (out of many):**
1. **Higher energy density** (10–100× TIG) due to triple constriction (thermal + mechanical + magnetic), enabling deeper penetration and faster travel speeds.
2. **Stable operation at very low currents** (down to 0.1 A in micro-plasma mode), enabling welding of foils and thin sheets where TIG becomes unstable.
3. Narrower HAZ and lower distortion for the same heat input.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model 3-mark]**
**What is the difference between a transferred arc and a non-transferred arc in PAW?**

**Model Answer:**
In a **transferred arc**, the main welding arc is established between the **tungsten electrode and the workpiece**; the workpiece itself acts as one of the electrodes, so heat is delivered directly to the joint. It is used for welding.

In a **non-transferred arc**, the arc is established between the **tungsten electrode and the water-cooled copper constricting nozzle**; the arc never touches the workpiece. The plasma jet is ejected through the nozzle orifice and impinges on the workpiece like a flame. It is used for plasma cutting, surface heating, and plasma spraying, where high direct electrical contact is not desired.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "transferred" with "non-transferred" arc sketches — examiners check the arrow direction of the arc in the diagram (3–4 marks lost on a wrong sketch alone).
> 2. **Always state units** in the heat-input calculation; marks are reserved specifically for unit correctness.
> 3. **Memorize the η values** for each mode (Micro, Melt-in, Keyhole) — wrong efficiency values lead to wrong H and a 7-mark loss.
> 4. **Do NOT call** PAW a "consumable-electrode" process — it is **non-consumable** tungsten, exactly like TIG.
> 5. **Gas flow units**: use **L/min** (not L/s) to match KTU textbooks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Chosen (Path: Welding Focus)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Module 3, 14 marks, 2 sub-parts]**

**Q. (a) Explain with a neat sketch the principle of Plasma Arc Welding. (7 marks)**
**Q. (b) Compare transferred arc and non-transferred arc PAW with respect to (i) arc path, (ii) voltage, (iii) applications, and (iv) pilot arc requirement. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
(Diagram: see Section 4.2 — Transferred Arc PAW block.)
PAW uses a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** held inside a water-cooled torch. A high-frequency, high-voltage (~5–10 kV) **pilot arc** is first struck between the electrode and the **constricting copper nozzle**, ionizing the argon plasma gas. Once ionized, the **main arc** is transferred from the nozzle to the **workpiece**. The constricting nozzle has a small bore (0.76–4.8 mm) that squeezes the arc, producing a high-velocity, high-temperature plasma jet (15,000–30,000 °C). Three constriction effects — thermal, mechanical, and magnetic (pinch) — concentrate the energy onto a small area of the workpiece, producing deep penetration or even a **keyhole** at high current. **Shielding gas** protects the weld pool; **backing gas** protects the root.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Arc path | Electrode → **Workpiece** | Electrode → **Nozzle** (workpiece is passive) |
| (ii) Voltage | Higher (25–40 V) | Lower (20–30 V) |
| (iii) Applications | Welding (keyhole & melt-in) | Plasma cutting, surface heating, spraying |
| (iv) Pilot arc | Required to start | The pilot arc *is* the main arc |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Path: Process Parameters / Numerical)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, 14 marks, 2 sub-parts]**

**Q. (a) What are the various operating modes of PAW? Explain each in 2–3 lines. (7 marks)**
**Q. (b) In a keyhole PAW operation, the following data is given: V = 32 V, I = 280 A, S = 200 mm/min, η = 0.75. Calculate the heat input in kJ/mm. If the nozzle bore is 2.5 mm, find the energy density of the plasma jet in W/mm². (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The three operating modes of PAW are:

1. **Micro-plasma mode (0.1 – 25 A):** The pilot arc is the main arc; conduction-mode melting; the arc is stable at extremely low currents; used for welding foils, wires, and thin sheets (e.g., 0.025 mm stainless steel, instrument diaphragms).

2. **Melt-in mode (25 – 200 A):** The plasma jet melts the base metal in a **conduction-limited** pool (no keyhole forms); used for medium-thickness plates (1–6 mm) with edge preparation.

3. **Keyhole mode (> 100 A, typically 200–400 A):** The plasma jet is forceful enough to **pierce** the plate, forming a **vapor cavity (keyhole)** through the thickness. Molten metal flows around the keyhole; full penetration is achieved in a **single pass** without edge preparation — a unique PAW capability for plates up to ~12 mm thick.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $V = 32$ V, $I = 280$ A, $S = 200$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.75$, $d = 2.5$ mm.

**(i) Heat Input:**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} = \frac{0.75 \times 32 \times 280 \times 60}{200 \times 1000}$$

Numerator: $0.75 \times 32 = 24$; $24 \times 280 = 6720$; $6720 \times 60 = 403{,}200$
Denominator: $200 \times 1000 = 200{,}000$

$$H = \frac{403{,}200}{200{,}000} = 2.016 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

**(ii) Energy Density:**

Jet area: $A_{jet} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (2.5)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25}{4} = 4.909 \text{ mm}^2$

Power: $P = V \cdot I = 32 \times 280 = 8960$ W

$$E_d = \frac{P}{A_{jet}} = \frac{8960}{4.909} \approx 1825 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Result:** $H = 2.016$ kJ/mm, $E_d \approx 1825$ W/mm². **(7 marks awarded if units are correct and steps are shown.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — 60-second recap:**

- **PAW = Non-consumable tungsten electrode + constricted arc + plasma jet.**
- **Plasma = 4th state of matter** (ionized gas; ions + free electrons + neutrals).
- **Three constriction effects:** Thermal, Mechanical (orifice), Magnetic (pinch). Memorize the names.
- **Three gases:** Plasma gas (Ar or Ar+H₂), Shielding gas (Ar), Backing gas (Ar, root side).
- **Three modes:** Micro-plasma (≤ 25 A, foil), Melt-in (25–200 A, conduction), Keyhole (≥ 200 A, full penetration).
- **Pilot arc (HF/HV, ~5–10 kV, ~10–30 A):** Always required to ionize gas before main arc.
- **Transferred arc:** Electrode ↔ **Workpiece** (workpiece = anode). Higher voltage. Used for **welding**.
- **Non-transferred arc:** Electrode ↔ **Nozzle** (workpiece = passive). Lower voltage. Used for **cutting, spraying**.
- **Heat input formula (MEMORIZE):** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm
- **Thermal efficiency η:** Micro ≈ 0.30–0.50; Melt-in ≈ 0.50–0.70; Keyhole ≈ 0.60–0.85.
- **Blunted (rounded) tungsten tip** is used in PAW (not pointed like in TIG) — to spread current and avoid tungsten erosion.
- **Advantages vs TIG:** higher energy density, faster travel, narrower HAZ, stable at low current, keyhole single-pass full penetration.
- **Disadvantages:** costly equipment, complex torch, high noise and UV/IR radiation, skilled operator required.
- **Applications:** aerospace (turbine blades, Ti alloys), nuclear (stainless steel), instrumentation (hermetic seals), tube-to-tubesheet welding, micro-welding of foils.
- **Stainless steel root protection:** always use **backing gas** (Ar) to prevent sugaring (oxide) on the root bead.
- **Nozzle bore (constricting orifice):** 0.76–4.8 mm. Smaller bore = higher energy density, more constriction.
- **Plasma gas rule of thumb:** Micro → 0.2–0.5 L/min; Keyhole → 1.5–6 L/min Ar.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — PAW: Theory, Transferred & Non-Transferred Arc Techniques.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equipment and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques**, the term *"Equipment and Applications"* refers to the systematic study of the **specialized hardware systems, machine subsystems, and process peripherals** that enable non-conventional joining processes such as **Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Plasma Arc Welding (PAW), Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Ultrasonic Welding (USW), Diffusion Bonding, and Explosion Welding**, together with the **mapped industrial deployment domains** (aerospace, nuclear, automotive, biomedical, microelectronics, shipbuilding) where each equipment configuration is industrially validated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (CO3 Aligned):** Equipment denotes the integrated assembly of **power supply, beam/torch generation unit, motion control system, vacuum/atmospheric chamber, workpiece manipulator, monitoring & feedback electronics, and safety interlocks** required to execute a specific advanced joining process. Application refers to the engineering context (material, thickness range, joint geometry, production volume, quality requirement) where the equipment is economically and metallurgically justified.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef preparing food using different cooking methods**:
- A **microwave oven** (like EBW/LBW) cooks from the inside using invisible energy waves with extreme precision.
- A **pressure cooker** (like Diffusion Bonding) uses heat + sustained pressure over time to fuse materials at the molecular level without melting.
- A **kitchen blowtorch** (like PAW) is a super-charged flame that melts metals precisely.
- A **stirring spoon in a thick gravy** (like FSW) doesn't melt the gravy but mechanically mixes it until it blends — FSW similarly stirs metals plastically without melting.

In each analogy, the **equipment is the "tool"** (oven, cooker, torch, spoon) and the **application is the "recipe"** (what dish are you making, for whom, and how often). Just as you wouldn't use a microwave to bake a wedding cake, you wouldn't use Laser Beam Welding to join two thick ship hull plates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Equipment Selection in KTU Framework:**
> 1. **Material compatibility** (reactive metals like Ti → EBW; dissimilar metals → Diffusion Bonding)
> 2. **Thickness regime** (foil/sheet → LBW/USW; thick plate → EBW/FSW)
> 3. **Production economics** (low-volume precision → EBW; high-volume → FSW/PAW)

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics and constants** dominate the quantitative analysis of advanced joining equipment:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Industry Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power density | $P_d$ | $10^3$ – $10^9$ W/mm² | EBW: $10^7$ W/mm²; LBW: $10^6$ W/mm² |
| Welding speed | $v$ | 0.1 – 10 m/min | Application dependent |
| Vacuum pressure | $p$ | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{-4}$ Pa | EBW chamber standard |
| Beam focal diameter | $d_f$ | 0.1 – 2.0 mm | LBW: 0.2–0.6 mm |
| Frequency (USW) | $f$ | **20 kHz, 35 kHz, 40 kHz, 70 kHz** | Industrial standard |
| Heat input | $H$ | 0.05 – 20 kJ/mm | KTU 2024 focus area |

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]}$$

where $\eta$ = process efficiency, $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $v$ = travel speed (mm/s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Advanced Joining Equipment

The equipment landscape for advanced joining is organized by the **energy source category** and the **physical state of the joint during formation**:

#### A. High-Energy Beam Equipment

**1. Electron Beam Welding (EBW) Equipment**

EBW equipment operates on the principle of accelerating electrons in a high-voltage field and focusing them magnetically onto the workpiece in a vacuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operational Logic of EBW:**
> - Electrons emitted from a heated **tungsten filament cathode** (operating at ~2000–2800°C).
> - Accelerated through a potential difference of **30–200 kV**.
> - Focused using **electromagnetic lenses** (magnetic focusing coils).
> - The kinetic energy of electrons converts to heat upon impact: $E = eV$.
> - Process executed in **vacuum** ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa) to prevent electron scattering.

Major Subsystems of EBW Equipment:
- **Electron gun** (cathode, anode, bias electrode)
- **High-voltage power supply** (30–200 kV DC)
- **Magnetic focusing & deflection coils**
- **Vacuum chamber** (with multiple lock chambers)
- **Workpiece manipulator** (XY table, rotary table)
- **Beam current & focus monitoring system**
- **CNC controller** for path programming
- **Viewing system** (optical microscope or CCD camera through viewport)

**2. Laser Beam Welding (LBW) Equipment**

LBW uses a coherent, monochromatic, collimated photon beam generated by stimulating a lasing medium.

| LBW Equipment Type | Active Medium | Wavelength | Power Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO₂ Laser | CO₂/N₂/He gas mixture | **10.6 μm** | 1–50 kW |
| Nd:YAG Laser | Yttrium Aluminum Garnet doped with Neodymium | **1.064 μm** | 0.1–6 kW |
| Fiber Laser | Yb-doped optical fiber | **1.07 μm** | 0.1–100 kW |
| Disc Laser | Yb:YAG disc | **1.03 μm** | 1–16 kW |
| Diode Laser | Semiconductor GaAs | **0.8–0.98 μm** | 0.1–10 kW |

LBW Equipment Subsystems:
- **Laser resonator** (with pump source, gain medium, mirrors/fibers)
- **Beam delivery system** (optical fibers for fiber/disc lasers; articulated arms for Nd:YAG; mirrors for CO₂)
- **Focusing head** (collimating + focusing lenses, typically ZnSe for CO₂)
- **Process gas shielding unit** (Argon/He for coaxial or side shielding)
- **Cooling system** (chiller for laser source)
- **Motion system** (galvanometer scanners, robotic arms, gantries)
- **Filler wire feeder** (optional, for hybrid LBW-MAG)

---

#### B. Plasma-Based Equipment

**Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) Equipment**

PAW uses a constricted arc between a tungsten electrode and the workpiece (transferred arc) or between the electrode and the nozzle (non-transferred arc).

Key Equipment Components:
- **Plasma torch** (water-cooled copper nozzle constricting the arc)
- **TIG power source** (typically DC, constant current, with high-frequency arc starting)
- **Shielding gas panel** (Argon primary; Argon + 2–8% H₂ for stainless steel)
- **Plasma gas control unit** (separate from shielding gas flow)
- **Pulsing unit** (for pulsed PAW to control HAZ)
- **Wire feeder** (optional, for PAW-GTAW hybrid)
- **Travel carriage or robot**

The **plasma gas orifice** constricts the arc to a column of 1–3 mm diameter, achieving power densities of $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm².

---

#### C. Solid-State Joining Equipment

**1. Friction Stir Welding (FSW) Equipment**

FSW uses a non-consumable rotating tool that plunges into the workpiece and traverses along the joint line, generating frictional + adiabatic heating to plasticize the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **FSW Tool Geometry** — The Heart of the Equipment:
> - **Shoulder** (diameter 10–50 mm) — generates bulk of frictional heat
> - **Probe/Pin** (diameter 4–20 mm) — drives material flow
> - Common pin profiles: **cylindrical, threaded, conical, triflute, Re-stir, MX-Triflute**

FSW Machine Subsystems:
- **Heavy-duty spindle** (rotational speed 100–3000 RPM, torque up to 100 N·m)
- **Z-axis load capacity** (10–100 kN vertical forging force)
- **Tilting mechanism** (0–5° tilt for butt joint optimization)
- **Tool steel grade** (H13 tool steel, W-Re alloys, polycrystalline cubic boron nitride — PCBN for ferrous)
- **Backing plate / anvil** (for thermal sink and mechanical support)
- **Clamping system** (rigid, prevents workpiece deflection)
- **Force-torque monitoring system**
- **Argon shrouding** (when joining reactive metals like Ti or Mg)
- **CNC controller** (5-axis capability for 3D contours)

**2. Ultrasonic Welding (USW) Equipment**

USW uses high-frequency (typically 20–70 kHz) mechanical vibrations combined with moderate static clamping pressure to create a solid-state bond.

USW Equipment Subsystems:
- **High-frequency generator** (converts 50 Hz mains to 20/35/40/70 kHz)
- **Piezoelectric transducer** (converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration)
- **Booster** (amplifies amplitude)
- **Sonotrode/Horn** (delivers vibration to workpiece, tuned to λ/2 wavelength)
- **Anvil** (support surface, often textured with knurling)
- **Pneumatic/hydraulic actuator** (clamps with 0.5–10 kN force)
- **Amplitude control** (typical 10–50 μm peak-to-peak)

---

#### D. Solid-State Diffusion & Special Equipment

**Diffusion Bonding (DB) Equipment**

Diffusion bonding applies simultaneous heat (typically 0.5–0.8 $T_m$) and pressure (1–20 MPa) for extended time (minutes to hours) under controlled atmosphere (vacuum or inert gas) to achieve atomic-level bonding without melting.

Equipment Components:
- **Vacuum hot press furnace** (vacuum level $10^{-3}$ Pa)
- **Induction or resistance heating** (to T = 0.5–0.8 T_m)
- **Hydraulic ram** (1–50 MPa capacity)
- **Atmosphere control** (vacuum / Ar / H₂)
- **PID temperature controllers** (±2°C accuracy)
- **Load cell** for pressure feedback

**Explosion Welding (EXW) Equipment**

EXW uses controlled detonation of an explosive charge to accelerate one plate (flyer) onto a stationary base plate at velocities of 200–800 m/s, creating a metallurgical bond through jetting action.

Field Setup:
- **Explosive charge** (typically Ammonium Nitrate + Fuel Oil — ANFO; or PETN-based sheets)
- **Stand-off distance** (controlled by spacer legs, typically 0.5t to 2t, where t = plate thickness)
- **Detonation system** (detonator, booster, main charge)
- **Backing anvil** (sand bed or steel plate)
- **Open-air or sand-filled pit** (for safety and acoustic containment)

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v \times 1000}$ | Heat input per unit length | kJ/mm |
| 2 | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A_{focus}}$ | Power density at focal spot | W/mm² |
| 3 | $E_{electron} = eV$ | Kinetic energy of accelerated electron | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $v_{det} = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Detonation velocity for EXW flyer | m/s |
| 5 | $t_{c} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4 v_{weld}}$ | Time per unit length at weld zone | s/mm |
| 6 | $D_{diff} = D_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{-Q}{RT}\right)$ | Diffusion coefficient (DB) | m²/s |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{A_{joint}}{A_{total}}$ | Joint efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{USW} = F \cdot \mu \cdot \omega \cdot A$ | USW power generation | W |
| 9 | $\text{WPD} = \dfrac{\omega}{v}$ | FSW Weld Pitch (Revolutions per mm) | rev/mm |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of USW vibration | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions:**
> - EBW requires $p < 10^{-1}$ Pa for beam coherence.
> - FSW requires $T_{peak} > 0.7 T_m$ for adequate plasticization.
> - LBW absorption requires photon energy matching material bandgap ($E_g$); reflectivity of Al at 1.06 μm = ~95%, hence need for surface preparation.
> - USW frequency must exceed audible range (>20 kHz) to avoid acoustic nuisance.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where Does This Matter in Practice?

The **equipment-to-application mapping** drives multi-billion dollar industrial decisions:

1. **Aerospace** (Airbus A350, Boeing 787): LBW + FSW for fuselage skin-stringer lap joints; EBW for Ti alloy hydraulic fittings.
2. **Nuclear** (fuel rod end-plugs, ITER): EBW for Zr-alloy tubes (vacuum avoids O₂/N₂ pickup causing embrittlement).
3. **Automotive EV battery packs**: LBW for Cu-Al busbar welding (low heat input to avoid intermetallic formation).
4. **Shipbuilding**: FSW for Al-alloy deck panels (5 m/min welding of 12 mm plates).
5. **Medical implants**: LBW for Ti-6Al-4V spinal cages and orthopedic components (precision, low distortion).
6. **Electronics packaging**: USW and Thermo-compression bonding for Cu wire bonding in semiconductor chips.
7. **Bimetallic plates for chemical reactors**: EXW for Ti-steel and Al-steel transition joints (impossible by fusion welding).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electron Beam Power Density

The **kinetic energy** of an electron accelerated through a potential $V$ is:

$$E_k = eV$$

where:
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electronic charge)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage (V)

If $n$ electrons strike the workpiece per second, the **beam power** is:

$$P_{beam} = n \cdot eV = I \cdot V$$

where $I$ = beam current (A).

When focused to a focal spot of diameter $d_f$, the **power density** is:

$$P_d = \frac{P_{beam}}{A_{focus}} = \frac{IV}{\frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}} = \frac{4IV}{\pi d_f^2}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style numerical):**

*Calculate the power density of an EBW system operating at 150 kV with a beam current of 50 mA, focused to a spot diameter of 0.5 mm.*

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 150 \text{ kV} = 150{,}000 \text{ V} \\
I &= 50 \text{ mA} = 0.050 \text{ A} \\
d_f &= 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
P_{beam} &= I \cdot V = 0.050 \times 150{,}000 \\
P_{beam} &= 7500 \text{ W} = 7.5 \text{ kW} \\
A_{focus} &= \frac{\pi (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \\
A_{focus} &= \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6}}{4} \\
A_{focus} &= 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2 \\
P_d &= \frac{7500}{1.963 \times 10^{-7}} \\
P_d &= 3.82 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^2 \\
P_d &= 3.82 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

This power density ($\approx 3.8 \times 10^4$ W/mm²) is sufficient to produce a deep, narrow keyhole weld — the **hallmark signature** of EBW.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — FSW Heat Generation Model

In FSW, heat is generated by two mechanisms:
1. **Friction at shoulder-workpiece interface** (dominant contribution ~80%)
2. **Plastic deformation in the stir zone** (secondary contribution ~20%)

The **total heat generation** from the shoulder is derived as follows:

$$Q_{shoulder} = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot \omega \cdot R_{shoulder}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (typically 0.3–0.5 for FSW)
- $F_n$ = normal (axial) force (N)
- $\omega$ = rotational speed (rad/s)
- $R_{shoulder}$ = mean shoulder radius (m)

The plastic deformation heat is:

$$Q_{plastic} = \tau_{yield} \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V_{SZ}$$

where $\tau_{yield}$ is shear yield stress, $\dot{\gamma}$ is shear strain rate, and $V_{SZ}$ is stir zone volume.

**Total heat input per unit length:**

$$H_{FSW} = \frac{Q_{total}}{v_{weld}} \quad \text{[J/m]}$$

**Worked Example:**

*For an Al-6082-T6 FSW with shoulder diameter 18 mm, axial force 8 kN, rotational speed 1000 RPM, friction coefficient 0.4, and welding speed 100 mm/min, calculate heat generation rate from friction alone.*

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{shoulder} &= \frac{18}{2} = 9 \text{ mm} = 0.009 \text{ m} \\
F_n &= 8000 \text{ N} \\
\omega &= \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s} \\
\mu &= 0.4 \\
Q_{shoulder} &= 0.4 \times 8000 \times 104.72 \times 0.009 \\
Q_{shoulder} &= 3015.94 \text{ W} \approx 3.02 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diffusion Bonding Bonding Parameter

The **diffusion bonding process** is governed by Fick's first law. The diffusion flux $J$ between two mating surfaces is:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial c}{\partial x}$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, given by the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$D = D_0 \exp\left(\frac{-Q}{RT}\right)$$

The **fraction of bonded area** $X$ evolves over time following:

$$X = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{K t}{T^m}\right)$$

where $K$ is a material-process constant, $t$ is time, $T$ is absolute temperature, $m$ is a process exponent (typically 1–2).

**Worked Example:**

*A Ti-6Al-4V diffusion bonding operation is conducted at 850°C for 90 minutes. Calculate the fraction of bonded area, given $K = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ (units consistent) and $m = 2$.*

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 850 + 273 = 1123 \text{ K} \\
t &= 90 \times 60 = 5400 \text{ s} \\
X &= 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 5400}{(1123)^2}\right) \\
X &= 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{0.27}{1{,}261{,}129}\right) \\
X &= 1 - \exp(-2.14 \times 10^{-7}) \\
X &\approx 2.14 \times 10^{-7} \text{ (negligible!)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation:** Diffusion bonding on a macroscopic length scale requires either very long times or elevated temperatures. In practice, surface roughness asperity creep mechanisms (Stage 1), plastic deformation (Stage 2), and finally diffusion-controlled pore elimination (Stage 3) work in series. The simplified exponential model must be used with **calibrated material constants**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment vs. Application Decision Map

| Process | Power Density (W/mm²) | Typical Thickness Range | Joint Efficiency | Capital Cost (USD) | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EBW** | $10^7$ – $10^9$ | 0.05–250 mm | 95–100% | 500k–5M | Aerospace, nuclear |
| **LBW (CO₂)** | $10^6$ | 0.5–25 mm | 90–95% | 200k–2M | Heavy fabrication, shipbuilding |
| **LBW (Fiber)** | $10^6$ | 0.2–12 mm | 92–97% | 100k–1.5M | EV batteries, sheet metal |
| **PAW (keyhole)** | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | 1–10 mm | 95–100% | 50k–300k | Tube welding, precision fabrication |
| **FSW** | Solid-state | 1–50 mm | 85–95% | 200k–2M | Al aerospace, ship panels |
| **USW** | Solid-state | <3 mm (foil/sheet) | 80–90% | 30k–150k | Cu-Al wires, battery tabs |
| **Diffusion Bonding** | Solid-state | 0.1–10 mm | 90–100% | 300k–3M | Turbine blades, bimetallics |
| **Explosion Welding** | High strain rate | 1–50 mm | 95–100% | Low (field setup) | Clad plates, transition joints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Matrix:** Higher power density enables **deeper penetration with narrower HAZ** (keyhole mode), favoring reactive/refractory metals. Solid-state processes (FSW, USW, DB) eliminate melting-related defects (porosity, hot cracking, solidification cracking) — making them indispensable for **alloys with poor fusion weldability** (Al-Cu 2xxx, Al-Zn 7xxx, Ti, Mg).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — EBW Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HVPS[High Voltage Power Supply 30 to 200 kV DC] --> GUN[Electron Gun Assembly]
    GUN -->|emits electrons| ACCEL[Acceleration Column]
    ACCEL -->|focused beam| MAGCOIL[Magnetic Focusing Coils]
    MAGCOIL --> DEFCOIL[Deflection Coils for Beam Steering]
    DEFCOIL --> CHAMBER[Vacuum Chamber 10 power -2 to 10 power -4 Pa]
    CHAMBER --> WORKPIECE[Workpiece on XY Rotary Table]
    CHAMBER --> VIEWPORT[Optical Viewport with CCD Camera]
    CHAMBER --> LOCK1[Entry Lock Chamber]
    CHAMBER --> LOCK2[Exit Lock Chamber]
    HVPS --> CTRL[CNC Controller]
    CTRL --> MONITOR[Beam Current and Focus Monitor]
    CTRL --> DRIVE[Servo Drives for Manipulator]
    MONITOR --> CTRL
    LOCK1 --> CHAMBER
    LOCK2 --> CHAMBER
```

**Description of the architecture:** The **High-Voltage Power Supply** energizes the **Electron Gun Assembly**, which houses the heated tungsten filament cathode. Emitted electrons are accelerated through the **Acceleration Column** and shaped by **Magnetic Focusing Coils** into a tight beam. **Deflection Coils** allow beam steering for scanning or seam tracking. The beam enters the **Vacuum Chamber** (critical to prevent electron scattering by gas molecules) where it impinges on the **Workpiece** mounted on a multi-axis **XY Rotary Table**. Operators monitor via **CCD Camera** through a **Viewport**. Material loading/unloading occurs through **Lock Chambers** that maintain the main chamber vacuum. The **CNC Controller** orchestrates the entire sequence with feedback from the **Beam Monitor**.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — LBW Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[Power Supply Unit 3 phase rectified DC] --> PUMP[Pump Source Lamp or Diode Stack]
    PUMP -->|optical excitation| RESON[Laser Resonator Cavity]
    RESON -->|coherent beam| BEAMDEL[Beam Delivery System Optical Fiber or Mirrors]
    BEAMDEL --> COLL[Collimating Lens Unit]
    COLL --> FOCUS[Focusing Lens ZnSe for CO2]
    FOCUS --> GASNOZ[Coaxial Nozzle with Shielding Gas]
    GASNOZ -->|beam strikes| WP[Workpiece on XYZ Stage]
    WP --> SENSOR[Process Sensor Pyrometer or Photodiode]
    SENSOR --> FEEDBACK[Real Time Feedback Controller]
    FEEDBACK --> PSU
    PSU --> CHILLER[Industrial Chiller Loop]
    CHILLER --> RESON
    GASNOZ --> GASIN[Argon or Helium Inlet Panel]
```

**Description:** The **Power Supply Unit** drives the **Pump Source** (flash lamp, diode stack, or fiber-coupled diodes), which pumps the **Laser Resonator Cavity** containing the gain medium (CO₂ gas, Nd:YAG crystal, or Yb-doped fiber). The emitted laser beam is transported via **Beam Delivery System** (optical fiber for fiber lasers; articulated mirror arm for CO₂) through a **Collimating Lens** and into a **Focusing Lens** that concentrates the beam to a focal spot of 0.1–0.6 mm diameter. A **Coaxial Nozzle** delivers inert shielding gas (Argon or Helium) to protect the molten pool. The **Process Sensor** captures emitted thermal radiation for **Real-time Feedback Control** of power. An **Industrial Chiller** removes waste heat from the laser source to maintain beam quality.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — FSW Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SPINDLE[High Torque Spindle Motor 100 to 3000 RPM] --> TOOL[FSW Tool Shoulder and Probe]
    TOOL -->|plunges into| WP[Workpiece Clamped Rigidly]
    WP --> ANVIL[Backing Plate and Anvil]
    SPINDLE --> LOADCELL[Load Cell for Axial Force]
    SPINDLE --> TORQUE[Torque Sensor]
    LOADCELL --> CTRL[CNC Controller]
    TORQUE --> CTRL
    CTRL --> XYZ[XYZ Gantry or Robotic Arm]
    XYZ --> CTRL
    CTRL --> TILT[Tilt Mechanism 0 to 5 Degrees]
    TILT --> SPINDLE
    WP --> CLAMP[Pneumatic or Hydraulic Clamping]
    CLAMP --> CTRL
    WP --> SHROUD[Argon Shroud for Reactive Alloys]
```

**Description:** The **High-Torque Spindle Motor** rotates the **FSW Tool** (comprising shoulder + probe). The tool plunges into the **Workpiece** (rigidly clamped by **Pneumatic/Hydraulic Clamping**) which rests on a **Backing Plate**. **Load Cell** and **Torque Sensor** provide real-time feedback to the **CNC Controller**, which commands the **XYZ Gantry** to traverse along the joint line. A **Tilt Mechanism** orients the tool at 0–5° for optimal material flow. For reactive alloys (Ti, Mg), an **Argon Shroud** envelops the joint to prevent oxidation.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Diffusion Bonding Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HEATER[Induction or Resistance Heating Coils] --> FURNACE[Vacuum Hot Press Furnace]
    FURNACE --> CHAMBER[Inert Chamber Vacuum or Ar]
    CHAMBER --> RAM[Hydraulic Ram 1 to 50 MPa]
    RAM --> PISTON[Graphite or Inconel Piston]
    PISTON --> SAMPLE[Workpiece Stack between Platens]
    CHAMBER --> PID[PID Temperature Controller]
    RAM --> LOAD[Load Cell with Pressure Gauge]
    PID --> HEATER
    LOAD --> RAM
    FURNACE --> VAC[Vacuum Pump System Rotary plus Diffusion]
    VAC --> CHAMBER
    SAMPLE --> THERMOC[Embedded Thermocouples]
    THERMOC --> PID
```

**Description:** **Heating Coils** (induction or resistance) raise the temperature inside a **Vacuum Hot Press Furnace** to 0.5–0.8 $T_m$ of the workpiece material. The **Hydraulic Ram** applies controlled pressure (1–50 MPa) through a **Graphite/Inconel Piston** onto the **Workpiece Stack** placed between platens. The **Vacuum Pump System** maintains an inert environment (vacuum or argon). **PID Temperature Controller** regulates temperature using feedback from **Embedded Thermocouples**. A **Load Cell** ensures precise pressure control throughout the bonding cycle.

---

### 4.5 Comparative Equipment Footprint — Visual Reference

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A[Aerospace and Nuclear]
        EBW1[EBW Vacuum Chamber]
        LBW1[LBW Fiber Laser Cell]
    end
    subgraph B[Automotive and Shipbuilding]
        FSW1[FSW Gantry System]
        PAW1[PAW Orbital Tube Welder]
    end
    subgraph C[Electronics and Medical]
        USW1[USW Benchtop Unit]
        LBW2[Micro LBW Workstation]
    end
    subgraph D[Heavy Industry]
        EXW1[EXW Open Field Setup]
        DB1[Diffusion Bonding Furnace]
    end
    A -->|precise thin to thick refractory| F1[Use Case Precision Critical]
    B -->|high productivity medium thickness| F2[Use Case Volume Production]
    C -->|micro joining clean room| F3[Use Case Miniaturization]
    D -->|clad and bimetallic joints| F4[Use Case Specialty Alloys]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> *List the major subsystems of an Electron Beam Welding (EBW) equipment and state the typical vacuum pressure maintained during operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The major subsystems of EBW equipment are:
1. **Electron gun assembly** — containing a heated tungsten filament cathode, anode, and bias electrode for electron emission.
2. **High-voltage DC power supply** — providing 30–200 kV acceleration voltage.
3. **Magnetic focusing and deflection coils** — for shaping and steering the electron beam.
4. **Vacuum chamber** — with entry and exit lock chambers to maintain vacuum integrity.
5. **Workpiece manipulator** — XY table and rotary table driven by servo motors.
6. **Beam monitoring and CNC control system** — for process parameter regulation.
7. **Viewing system** — optical or CCD camera through viewport.

The typical operating vacuum pressure is **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ Pa** (i.e., $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ mbar).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
> *What are the principal components of a Friction Stir Welding (FSW) tool, and why is PCBN used for ferrous material FSW?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The principal components of an FSW tool are:
1. **Shoulder** — a flat or scrolled disk (10–50 mm diameter) that generates the majority of frictional heat and confines the plasticized material.
2. **Probe (Pin)** — a profiled cylindrical, threaded, conical, or triflute pin (4–20 mm diameter) that drives material flow and creates the stirred weld nugget.

**PCBN (Polycrystalline Cubic Boron Nitride)** is used for FSW of ferrous materials (steels, titanium) because:
- It retains hardness and wear resistance at temperatures exceeding 1000°C (above the softening point of tool steels like H13).
- It avoids the severe tool degradation that H13 tool steel suffers when welding steels above 600°C.
- It enables FSW of high-melting-point alloys without frequent tool changes.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Explain with a labeled block diagram the functional architecture of a Laser Beam Welding (LBW) system. Discuss the differences between CO₂, Nd:YAG, and Fiber lasers in terms of wavelength, beam delivery, and typical industrial applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A continuous-wave Nd:YAG laser delivers 3 kW of power to a workpiece focused to a spot diameter of 0.8 mm. The welding speed is 2 m/min. Calculate the power density at the focal spot and the heat input per unit length, assuming process efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Comment on the implications for welding stainless steel (AISI 304). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Laser Comparison (7 marks):**

The LBW system comprises:
- **Power supply unit** (3-phase rectified DC source)
- **Pump source** (flash lamps or diode stacks)
- **Laser resonator cavity** (containing gain medium)
- **Beam delivery system** (fiber or articulated arm)
- **Focusing optics** (collimating + focusing lenses)
- **Shielding gas nozzle** (coaxial Ar/He)
- **Workpiece positioning system** (XYZ gantry or robot)
- **Process monitoring** (pyrometer/photodiode + feedback controller)
- **Cooling system** (chiller)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CO₂ Laser | Nd:YAG Laser | Fiber Laser |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | **10.6 μm** | **1.064 μm** | **1.07 μm** |
| Beam delivery | Mirrors/articulated arm | Optical fiber (limited) | Optical fiber (high efficiency) |
| Absorption by metals | Poor (high reflectivity) | Good | Excellent |
| Beam quality (BPP) | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| Wall-plug efficiency | ~10% | ~3–5% | **~30–40%** |
| Typical applications | Thick plate, non-metals, shipbuilding | Precision joining, repair welding, automotive | Sheet metal, EV batteries, micro-welding |

**Key Insights:**
- CO₂ laser's 10.6 μm wavelength is poorly absorbed by metals (high reflectivity), requiring **absorptive coatings or higher power densities**.
- Fiber laser's high wall-plug efficiency and excellent beam quality make it the **preferred choice for industrial mass production**.
- Nd:YAG occupies a middle ground — historically dominant but increasingly replaced by fiber lasers.

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given:
$$P = 3000 \text{ W}, \quad d_f = 0.8 \text{ mm}, \quad v = 2 \text{ m/min}, \quad \eta = 0.85$$

**Step 1: Focal spot area**
$$A_{focus} = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.8)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.64}{4} = 0.503 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Power density**
$$P_d = \frac{P}{A_{focus}} = \frac{3000}{0.503} = 5964 \text{ W/mm}^2 \approx 5.96 \times 10^3 \text{ W/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Heat input**
$$v = 2 \text{ m/min} = \frac{2000}{60} = 33.33 \text{ mm/s}$$
$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot P}{v \times 1000} = \frac{0.85 \times 3000}{33.33 \times 1000} \text{ (in kJ/mm)}$$
$$H = \frac{2550}{33333} \text{ kJ/mm}$$
$$H = 0.0765 \text{ kJ/mm} = 76.5 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 4: Implications for AISI 304 stainless steel**
- AISI 304 has thermal conductivity ~16 W/(m·K) and melting point ~1400°C.
- Heat input of 76.5 J/mm is in the **low-to-moderate range** — sufficient for deep penetration in thin sections without excessive HAZ.
- Stainless steel is susceptible to **sensitization** (Cr carbide precipitation at 450–850°C) if HAZ cooling is slow.
- The high power density ($5.96 \times 10^3$ W/mm²) ensures **keyhole mode welding**, producing a narrow deep weld with minimal HAZ — favorable for austenitic stainless steel to avoid sensitization and distortion.
- Recommend **argon or helium shielding** to prevent oxidation; backing gas for full-penetration welds to preserve root-side corrosion resistance.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
>
> **(a)** Describe the working principle and equipment subsystems of Plasma Arc Welding (PAW). Differentiate between **transferred arc** and **non-transferred arc** PAW with industrial application examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare EBW and LBW equipment for the welding of a 10 mm thick titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) aerospace component. Discuss equipment selection based on (i) weld pool protection, (ii) joint accessibility, (iii) distortion control, and (iv) economic considerations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PAW Working Principle and Arc Modes (7 marks):**

**Working Principle:** PAW uses a constricted arc between a non-consumable tungsten electrode and the workpiece. A copper nozzle with a small orifice (1–3 mm) constricts the arc plasma, increasing its **energy density** and **stiffness**. Separate gas streams are used:
- **Plasma gas** (typically Argon, sometimes with H₂) — flows through the constricting nozzle, forms the plasma column.
- **Shielding gas** (Argon or Ar + H₂) — protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Subsystems:** (i) Power source (DC, constant current, with HF starter), (ii) Plasma torch with water-cooled nozzle, (iii) Gas control panel with separate plasma & shielding flowmeters, (iv) Wire feeder (optional), (v) Travel carriage or robot, (vi) Pulsing unit, (vii) Cooling water circulator.

**Comparison of Arc Modes:**

| Feature | Transferred Arc | Non-Transferred Arc |
|---|---|---|
| Arc termination | Between electrode and **workpiece** | Between electrode and **nozzle (anode)** |
| Power density | Higher (5–10× conventional TIG) | Moderate |
| Workpiece heating | Direct | Indirect (via plasma jet) |
| Typical current | 100–400 A | 50–200 A |
| Applications | Keyhole welding of thick plates, tube welding, stainless steel | Surfacing, plasma spraying, cutting of non-conductive materials |

**Example applications:**
- **Transferred arc PAW:** Orbital welding of stainless steel tubes (e.g., pharmaceutical/food piping), aerospace honeycomb structures.
- **Non-transferred arc PAW:** Plasma arc cutting of stainless steel plates up to 50 mm thick; plasma spray coating of turbine blades.

**(b) EBW vs. LBW for Ti-6Al-4V Aerospace Component (7 marks):**

**Given:** 10 mm thick Ti-6Al-4V aerospace structural component.

| Criterion | EBW | LBW (Fiber Laser) | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Weld pool protection** | Excellent — high vacuum eliminates O₂, N₂, H₂ contamination | Good — inert gas shrouding; some risk of pickup if gas coverage inadequate | **EBW superior** for reactive alloys |
| **(ii) Joint accessibility** | Limited — workpiece must fit vacuum chamber; locks slow loading | Excellent — fiber delivery allows remote welding; 3D contours accessible | **LBW superior** for complex geometries |
| **(iii) Distortion control** | Excellent — very narrow HAZ (~1 mm) due to low total heat input despite high power density | Excellent — keyhole mode produces low distortion | **EBW marginally better** due to lower total HAZ |
| **(iv) Economics** | High capex ($500k–$5M); slow chamber pump-down; low throughput | Moderate capex ($200k–$1.5M); high throughput; lower operating cost | **LBW favorable** for production runs |
| **Joint quality (Ti-6Al-4V)** | Excellent — virtually no porosity, no contamination | Excellent — but requires perfect gas shielding | Both acceptable |

**Final Selection Logic:**
- For **single-unit or low-volume precision aerospace parts** (e.g., turbine engine casings, hydraulic manifolds): **EBW preferred** due to unmatched purity.
- For **high-volume production aerospace skins** (e.g., fuselage panels): **Fiber LBW preferred** due to speed, flexibility, and cost.
- For **hybrid approach**: EBW for thick sections + LBW for thin skin-to-stringer joints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing PAW with TIG:** Students often write "PAW uses tungsten electrode like TIG" without explaining the **constricting nozzle** and **separate plasma gas** — both critical differentiators.
> 2. **Wrong formula units:** Heat input formula $H = \eta VI / v$ — students forget to convert $v$ from m/min to mm/s. The factor of 1000 is for converting to kJ/mm.
> 3. **Skipping subsystem diagrams:** A question worth 7 marks on EBW/LBW **must** include a labeled block diagram. Text alone scores ≤4 marks.
> 4. **Confusing PAW power density with TIG:** PAW power density is $10^5$ – $10^6$ W/mm², NOT $10^3$ W/mm² (which is conventional TIG).
> 5. **Forgetting process efficiency $\eta$:** CO₂ laser $\eta$ ≈ 0.1; Fiber laser $\eta$ ≈ 0.35; Nd:YAG $\eta$ ≈ 0.05. This is frequently asked in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 6. **Mixing up FSW tool terminology:** "Pin" and "Probe" are interchangeable but "Shoulder" and "Probe" are NOT the same — shoulder generates bulk heat, probe drives flow.
> 7. **Diffusion bonding error:** Students forget that DB requires $T \approx 0.7 T_m$ (not 0.5 $T_m$ as in annealing). Misreading this loses 2–3 marks.
> 8. **EBW vacuum units:** $10^{-2}$ Pa is **low vacuum** (rough); $10^{-4}$ Pa is **high vacuum**; $10^{-7}$ Pa is **ultra-high vacuum**. KTU requires correct distinction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Equipment and Applications**
>
> - [x] **EBW**: Vacuum $10^{-2}$–$10^{-4}$ Pa; 30–200 kV; power density $10^7$ W/mm²; deep narrow keyhole; used for reactive metals.
> - [x] **LBW CO₂**: $\lambda = 10.6$ μm; needs absorptive coatings; mirror delivery.
> - [x] **LBW Fiber**: $\lambda = 1.07$ μm; best wall-plug efficiency ~35%; fiber delivery; preferred for industry.
> - [x] **LBW Nd:YAG**: $\lambda = 1.064$ μm; historic standard; replaced by fiber in most applications.
> - [x] **PAW**: Constricting nozzle + separate plasma gas; transferred/non-transferred arc; used for tube welding & cutting.
> - [x] **FSW**: Solid-state; tool = shoulder + probe; WPD (Weld Pitch Density) = $\omega / v$; H13 for Al, PCBN for steel.
> - [x] **USW**: 20 kHz/35 kHz/40 kHz/70 kHz; piezoelectric transducer + booster + sonotrode; for foil/sheet joining.
> - [x] **Diffusion Bonding**: $T = 0.5$–$0.8 T_m$; pressure 1–20 MPa; vacuum atmosphere; atomic diffusion mechanism.
> - [x] **Explosion Welding**: Stand-off = $0.5t$–$2t$; flyer velocity 200–800 m/s; for bimetallic clad plates.
> - [x] **Heat input formula**: $H = \eta V I / (v \times 1000)$ kJ/mm.
> - [x] **Power density formula**: $P_d = P / A_{focus}$ W/mm².
> - [x] **Application triangle**: Material × Thickness × Volume = equipment choice.
> - [x] **Aerospace**: EBW + FSW + LBW dominate.
> - [x] **Nuclear**: EBW for Zr-alloy tubes (vacuum avoids embrittlement).
> - [x] **Automotive EV**: LBW + USW for Cu-Al busbar & battery tab joining.
> - [x] **Bimetallic/Clad plates**: Explosion welding is the standard.
> - [x] **Turbine blades**: Diffusion bonding for superalloy joining.
> - [x] **KTU 2024 Focus Areas**: Equipment block diagrams, numerical on heat input & power density, application-mapping tables, comparison matrices.
