topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET416 - ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction, Theory, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: INTRODUCTION, THEORY, AND APPLICATIONS OF ADVANCED METAL JOINING TECHNIQUES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Metal Joining Techniques** refer to a class of specialized welding and joining processes that employ non-conventional energy sources (electron beams, laser beams, plasma, friction, ultrasonic vibrations, etc.) or operate under extreme conditions (underwater, vacuum, explosive environments) to produce joints that are either impossible or impractical to achieve using conventional arc welding methods (SMAW, GMAW, GTAW).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, Module 4 consolidates the study of **high-energy-density joining processes (HEDJP)**, **solid-state joining processes**, and **special-environment joining processes**. These processes are characterized by:

- **High Power Density**: Often exceeding **10⁶ W/mm²** (compared to ~10² W/mm² for conventional arc welding).
- **Minimal Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ)**: Resulting in lower distortion and superior metallurgical properties.
- **Precision and Repeatability**: Suitable for aerospace, nuclear, automotive, and microelectronics industries.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sun and a Magnifying Glass"** 🔍☀️

Imagine trying to light a piece of paper using two methods:
1. **Holding a matchstick near it** (Conventional arc welding — broad, diffuse heat, slow, large affected area).
2. **Focusing sunlight through a magnifying glass onto a single point** (Advanced joining like Laser or Electron Beam — intense, concentrated, precise, instant melting at a tiny spot).

This is exactly what advanced joining techniques achieve. By **focusing enormous energy into a microscopic spot**, they melt or fuse metals with surgical precision. For processes like Friction Stir Welding, the analogy shifts to **stirring a thick paste with a hot spoon** — no melting, just plastic deformation and mixing.

### Classification Overview (Module 4 Scope)

| Category | Processes Covered |
|----------|-------------------|
| **High Energy Density Processes** | Electron Beam Welding (EBW), Laser Beam Welding (LBW), Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) |
| **Solid-State Joining** | Friction Stir Welding (FSW), Diffusion Bonding, Ultrasonic Welding |
| **Special-Environment Joining** | Underwater Welding, Explosive Welding |

> [!TIP]
> The **penetration-to-width ratio (D/W)** is the key performance metric in these processes. Conventional arc welding has D/W ≈ 1:1, while EBW can achieve **D/W ≈ 25:1** — meaning a deep, narrow, almost knife-cut weld profile.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 High Energy Density Processes (HEDP)

### 2.1.1 Electron Beam Welding (EBW)

**Working Principle:**
A focused beam of **high-velocity electrons** is accelerated through a potential difference (typically **30 kV to 200 kV**) and focused on the workpiece in a **vacuum chamber** (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr). The kinetic energy of electrons converts to heat upon impact, melting and vaporizing the metal.

**Why Vacuum?**
- Prevents electron scattering by air molecules.
- Prevents oxidation of the molten pool.
- Critical for reactive metals like **titanium**, **zirconium**, and **niobium**.

**Key Modes of Operation:**
1. **Keyhole Mode** (most common) — Deep penetration, vapor pressure ejects molten metal, creating a capillary.
2. **Conduction Mode** — Shallow, wide weld pool (similar to GTAW).
3. **Pulsed Mode** — Pulsed beam for heat input control.

### 2.1.2 Laser Beam Welding (LBW)

**Working Principle:**
**LASER** = **L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation. A coherent, monochromatic, high-intensity light beam is focused on the joint.

**Types of Lasers Used in Welding:**
- **CO₂ Laser** (λ = 10.6 μm) — Most common in industry, high power (up to 25 kW).
- **Nd:YAG Laser** (λ = 1.06 μm) — Fiber-optic deliverable, suitable for metals.
- **Fiber Lasers** and **Disc Lasers** — Modern, high-efficiency alternatives.

**Two Interaction Modes:**
1. **Conduction Mode**: Power density < 10⁶ W/mm² — shallow weld.
2. **Keyhole Mode**: Power density > 10⁶ W/mm² — deep penetration.

### 2.1.3 Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)

**Working Principle:**
A constricted arc is created by passing a tungsten arc through a **water-cooled copper nozzle** with a small orifice. The constriction raises the **enthalpy** and **temperature** of the arc plasma.

**Two Variants:**
- **Transferred Arc (TA)**: Workpiece is part of the electrical circuit — high heat concentration.
- **Non-Transferred Arc (NTA)**: Arc is between tungsten and nozzle — used for plasma spraying/cutting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Microplasma** welding operates at currents as low as **0.1 A**, suitable for foils and thin sheets.

---

## 2.2 Solid-State Joining Processes

### 2.2.1 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

**Working Principle:**
A **non-consumable rotating tool** with a specially designed pin and shoulder is plunged into the joint line. Frictional heat softens the material (without melting), and the tool's stirring action plasticizes and forges the metal together.

**Key Innovation by TWI (1991):**
- Invented at **The Welding Institute (TWI), UK, in 1991**.
- Operates **below the melting point** — solid-state, no fusion-related defects.
- Particularly revolutionary for **aluminum alloys** (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series).

**Tool Components:**
1. **Shoulder** — Generates most of the frictional heat; contains the plasticized material.
2. **Pin (Probe)** — Stirs the material; can be threaded, fluted, or feature reentrant features.
3. **Scrolled Shoulder** — Some designs incorporate scroll features for additional material flow.

### 2.2.2 Diffusion Bonding (DB)

**Working Principle:**
Two clean, flat surfaces are held together under **pressure** at elevated temperature (**0.5 to 0.8 Tₘ**, where Tₘ = melting point in Kelvin) for a prolonged period. Atomic diffusion across the interface forms a metallurgical bond.

**Three Stages of Bond Formation:**
1. **Initial Deformation** — Surface asperities deform plastically.
2. **Grain Boundary Diffusion** — Diffusion along bonded interface.
3. **Volume Diffusion** — Homogenization, void elimination.

### 2.2.3 Ultrasonic Welding (USW)

**Working Principle:**
High-frequency (**20–40 kHz**) ultrasonic vibrations are applied under static pressure to the workpieces. The combined effect of friction and micro-deformation at the interface breaks down surface oxides and creates a solid-state bond.

---

## 2.3 Special-Environment Joining

### 2.3.1 Underwater Welding

**Two Main Methods:**
- **Wet Welding** — Done directly in water using specially coated electrodes (e.g., **rutile-coated**, **basic-coated**, **cellulosic**). Limited depth (~50 m). Used for emergency repairs.
- **Dry Welding (Hyperbaric)** — Chamber pressurized with gas mixture (helium + oxygen) at pressure equal to water depth. Higher quality, used for offshore platforms.

### 2.3.2 Explosive Welding (EXW)

**Working Principle:**
Controlled **detonation of an explosive charge** accelerates one metal plate (flyer) onto another (base plate) at velocities of **200–800 m/s**. The collision creates a **jet** that strips surface oxides, and the high pressure produces a metallurgical bond with characteristic **wavy interface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **wavy interface morphology** is a signature feature of EXW bonds, indicating high-quality metallurgical bonding.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU FORMULA SHEET / CHEAT SHEET

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Density (P_d)** | $P_d = \dfrac{P}{A}$ where $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ | W/mm² | Compares processes |
| **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$ | J/mm | All welding processes |
| **Penetration Depth (Electron Range)** | $R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{\rho}$ (V in volts, $\rho$ in kg/m³) | m | EBW range in material |
| **Depth-to-Width Ratio** | $D/W$ | dimensionless | Quality indicator |
| **Diffusion Bonding Time** | $t \propto \dfrac{x^2}{D}$ where $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | s | DB kinetics (Fick's Law) |
| **Friction Stir Heat Generation** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu P \omega R^3$ | W | FSW thermal model |
| **Explosive Weld Window** | $\beta = \dfrac{v_p \cdot \rho_p}{\sqrt{2 \cdot H_v}}$ | dimensionless | EXW bonding criterion |
| **Laser Power Density** | $P_d = \dfrac{4P}{\pi f^2}$ where $f$ = focal spot diameter | W/mm² | LBW threshold check |

> [!TIP]
> **Convention Tip for KTU Exams**: In power density tables, remember the threshold: **>10⁶ W/mm² → Keyhole mode**; **<10⁵ W/mm² → Conduction mode**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace**: EBW for turbine blades, LBW for airframe components.
- **Automotive**: FSW for aluminum body-in-white structures, LBW for tailor-welded blanks.
- **Nuclear**: PAW for stainless steel reactor components, EBW for fuel cladding.
- **Marine**: Underwater welding for ship hull repair, EXW for bimetallic transition joints.
- **Electronics**: USW for battery tab connections, micro-resistance welding.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Electron Penetration Depth in EBW

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is critical for understanding why EBW can weld thick sections in a single pass without multiple passes.

**Statement:**
The range (penetration depth) of an electron in a material under accelerating voltage V is given by the **Schwarz formula** (or modified Thomson-Whiddington law):

$$R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \frac{V^2}{\rho}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider an electron of mass $m_e$ and charge $e$ accelerated through potential difference $V$.

The kinetic energy gained by the electron is:

$$E_k = eV = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$$

**Step 2:** The electron loses energy primarily through **inelastic scattering** with atomic electrons. The energy loss per unit path length $(dE/dx)$ in a material of density $\rho$ and atomic number Z is described by the **Bethe-Bloch equation** (non-relativistic approximation):

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{2\pi e^4 N_A Z \rho}{m_e v^2 A}$$

where $A$ is the atomic mass and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number.

**Step 3:** Substituting $v^2 = 2eV/m_e$:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{\pi e^3 N_A Z \rho A^{-1}}{V}$$

**Step 4:** The total range $R$ is obtained by integrating from initial energy $E_0 = eV$ to zero:

$$R = \int_0^{E_0} \frac{dE}{-dE/dx}$$

**Step 5:** Performing the integration (assuming $\rho$ constant):

$$R = \int_0^{eV} \frac{V \, dE}{\pi e^3 N_A Z \rho A^{-1}}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluating the integral:

$$R = \frac{(eV)^2}{2 \pi e^3 N_A Z \rho A^{-1}} = \frac{V^2}{2 \pi e N_A Z \rho A^{-1}}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting physical constants ($N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C), and averaging for typical metals (Z/A ≈ 0.5), we obtain the empirical relation:

$$\boxed{R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \frac{V^2}{\rho} \text{ meters}}$$

**Step 8: Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
- Accelerating voltage: $V = 150 \text{ kV} = 150{,}000 \text{ V}$
- Material: Steel with $\rho = 7860 \text{ kg/m}^3$

Substituting:

$$R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{(150{,}000)^2}{7860}$$

$$R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \times \frac{2.25 \times 10^{10}}{7860}$$

$$R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.863 \times 10^{6}$$

$$R = 6.012 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0601 \text{ mm} \approx 60 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important**: This is the single-electron range. In practice, the **focused beam penetration** in EBW is governed by keyholing (vapor pressure) and extends much deeper — typically 5–300 mm in one pass.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Generation in Friction Stir Welding

**Statement:** The heat generated per unit time during FSW can be estimated by treating the tool shoulder as a sliding cylinder.

**Model Assumption:** Heat is generated by **Coulomb friction** at the shoulder-workpiece interface and by **plastic deformation** in the stir zone.

**Step 1:** Consider the shoulder as a disk of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ under axial pressure $P$, with friction coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 2:** A differential ring element at radius $r$ has:
- Area: $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$
- Normal force: $dF = P \cdot dA = 2\pi P r \, dr$
- Sliding velocity: $v = r \omega$
- Frictional force: $dF_f = \mu \cdot dF = 2\pi \mu P r \, dr$

**Step 3:** Differential power dissipated:

$$dQ = dF_f \cdot v = 2\pi \mu P r \, dr \cdot r \omega = 2\pi \mu P \omega r^2 \, dr$$

**Step 4:** Integrating from $0$ to $R$ (shoulder radius):

$$Q = \int_0^R 2\pi \mu P \omega r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \mu P \omega \cdot \frac{R^3}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Final result:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \pi \mu P \omega R^3}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Illustration:**
- $\mu = 0.4$ (typical for aluminum on steel at high temperature)
- $P = 50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $\omega = 1000 \text{ rpm} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$
- $R = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \pi (0.4)(50 \times 10^6)(104.72)(0.01)^3$$

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \pi (0.4)(50 \times 10^6)(104.72)(10^{-6})$$

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 0.4 \times 50 \times 104.72 \times 10^{-6+6}$$

$$Q = 2.0944 \times 0.4 \times 50 \times 104.72$$

$$Q = 4386 \text{ W} \approx 4.39 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> This analytical result explains why FSW tool design (shoulder diameter) is critical — a small increase in $R$ leads to an $R^3$ increase in heat generation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Process vs. Penetration

| **Process** | **Power Density (W/mm²)** | **Typical Penetration** | **D/W Ratio** | **Atmosphere** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** | 10–100 | 3–6 mm | ~1:1 | Ambient |
| **GTAW** | 100–1000 | 3–10 mm | ~1:1 | Inert gas |
| **PAW** | $10^3$–$10^5$ | 6–12 mm | 2:1 to 3:1 | Argon/Helium |
| **LBW** | $10^5$–$10^7$ | 1–25 mm | 5:1 to 10:1 | Shielding gas |
| **EBW** | $10^6$–$10^9$ | 5–300 mm | 25:1 | Vacuum |
| **FSW** | N/A (mechanical) | 1–50 mm | ~1:1 | Ambient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Conversion Logic**: The keyhole penetration regime is enabled when **vapor pressure** of the molten metal exceeds the **surface tension + hydrostatic pressure**. This is why power density > $10^6$ W/mm² is the threshold.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Electron Beam Welding. Mention any two advantages of EBW over conventional arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Electron Beam Welding (EBW) is a fusion welding process in which a high-velocity focused beam of electrons, accelerated through a high potential difference (30–200 kV), is made to impinge on the joint. The kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into heat upon impact, causing localized melting and fusion of the metal in a vacuum environment (typically 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr).

**Two Advantages over conventional arc welding:**
1. **Extremely high power density** (10⁶ to 10⁹ W/mm²) enables deep penetration in a single pass — up to 300 mm in steel without edge preparation.
2. **Minimal HAZ and low distortion** because the heat input is concentrated and rapid, leading to superior mechanical properties and negligible warping.
3. (Optional) **Vacuum environment** prevents contamination, making it ideal for reactive metals like titanium and zirconium.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Friction Stir Welding? State the significance of the tool shoulder.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Friction Stir Welding (FSW) is a solid-state joining process developed at TWI, UK in 1991, in which a non-consumable rotating tool with a specially designed pin and shoulder is traversed along the joint line. Frictional heat softens the material below its melting point, and the stirring action of the pin plasticizes and forges the metal into a metallurgical bond.

**Significance of the tool shoulder:**
1. The shoulder generates **~80% of the total frictional heat**, which is the primary source of thermal energy for material plasticization.
2. It **confines the plasticized material** beneath it, preventing it from being expelled and ensuring forging pressure is maintained.
3. It provides the **upward forging force** that consolidates the joint, eliminating volumetric defects and porosity.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle, process parameters, and key applications of **Laser Beam Welding (LBW)** with neat schematic representation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **theory and mechanism of Diffusion Bonding**, including the various stages of bond formation and the influence of process parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A(a): Laser Beam Welding (7 marks)

**Working Principle:**

Laser Beam Welding uses a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of light generated by stimulated emission of radiation. The beam is focused using lenses (for CO₂ lasers) or optical fibers (for Nd:YAG and fiber lasers) to achieve power densities typically in the range of **10⁵ to 10⁷ W/mm²**.

At power densities **below 10⁶ W/mm²**, the laser operates in **conduction mode**, where heat flows into the material by conduction, producing shallow, wide welds.

At power densities **above 10⁶ W/mm²**, the **keyhole mode** is established. The intense beam vaporizes the metal, creating a vapor cavity (keyhole) that allows the beam to penetrate deeply. The keyhole acts as a blackbody absorber, enhancing energy coupling.

**Process Parameters:**
1. **Laser Power (P)** — Determines penetration; typically 1–25 kW for industrial systems.
2. **Welding Speed (v)** — Inversely related to penetration depth.
3. **Focal Spot Diameter (f)** — Smaller spot → higher power density.
4. **Focal Position** — Above, on, or below the workpiece surface affects weld profile.
5. **Shielding Gas (typically Helium or Argon)** — Prevents oxidation of the molten pool and plasma plume suppression.
6. **Pulse Parameters** (in pulsed LBW) — Peak power, pulse duration, frequency.

**Key Applications:**
- **Automotive Industry**: Tailor-welded blanks for car body panels (different thickness/grade sheets welded together for weight reduction).
- **Aerospace**: Welding of titanium airframe components, engine casings.
- **Electronics**: Seam welding of battery cans, micro-joining of electronic components.
- **Medical Devices**: Welding of stainless steel surgical instruments and implants.
- **Shipbuilding**: Panel welding with high deposition rate CO₂ lasers.

**Schematic (text representation):**

```
[Laser Resonator] → [Beam Delivery Optics] → [Focusing Lens] → [Workpiece]
                         ↑                            ↑
              [Beam Shutter]              [Shielding Gas Nozzle]
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A(b): Diffusion Bonding (7 marks)

**Theory and Definition:**

Diffusion Bonding (DB) is a solid-state joining process in which two carefully prepared surfaces are brought into intimate contact at an elevated temperature (typically 0.5 to 0.8 Tₘ, where Tₘ is the melting point in Kelvin) under a controlled pressure for a sufficient duration to form a metallurgical bond through atomic diffusion.

**Mechanism — Three Stages of Bond Formation:**

**Stage 1: Initial Deformation and Asperity Contact (t₁):**
- Surface asperities (peaks and valleys) come into contact under applied pressure.
- Plastic deformation occurs at the asperities due to localized high stress.
- True contact area increases; interfacial voids form between contact points.

**Stage 2: Grain Boundary Diffusion and Pore Elimination (t₂):**
- At elevated temperature, atoms diffuse along the bonded interface (grain boundary diffusion).
- Voids shrink and isolated pores form.
- Initial bond islands grow and coalesce.

**Stage 3: Volume Diffusion and Final Homogenization (t₃):**
- Atoms diffuse through the bulk (volume diffusion) following Fick's law.
- Residual pores are eliminated; interface disappears.
- Grain boundaries migrate across the original interface, creating a fully metallurgical bond.

**Fick's Law (Mathematical Foundation):**

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}, \quad \text{where} \quad D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

Here, $J$ = diffusion flux, $D$ = diffusivity, $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor, $Q$ = activation energy, $R$ = universal gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature.

**Process Parameters and Their Influence:**

| **Parameter** | **Effect on Bond Quality** |
|---|---|
| **Temperature (T)** | Higher T → faster diffusion → stronger bond; but risk of grain growth |
| **Pressure (P)** | Higher P → faster asperity collapse → reduced porosity |
| **Time (t)** | Longer t → more diffusion → better bond; risk of coarsening |
| **Surface Roughness** | Lower Ra → less time needed for bonding |
| **Atmosphere** | Vacuum or inert gas prevents oxide formation |

**Applications:**
- Joining of dissimilar metals (e.g., titanium to stainless steel).
- Aerospace components (titanium honeycomb structures, nickel superalloy turbine blades).
- Nuclear fuel cladding (zirconium alloys).
- Ceramic-to-metal joints (using interlayers).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **working principle, equipment, and applications of Plasma Arc Welding (PAW)**. Compare it with conventional TIG welding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **theory, process parameters, and applications of Underwater Welding**, including both wet and dry methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B(a): Plasma Arc Welding (7 marks)

**Working Principle:**

Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) is an extension of the Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) process. A tungsten electrode is positioned **inside** a water-cooled copper nozzle with a constricting orifice. The arc is forced through this small orifice, which:
- **Constricts** the arc cross-section,
- **Increases** the arc's current density, temperature, and velocity,
- Results in a **high-enthalpy plasma jet** that exits the nozzle at velocities exceeding the speed of sound (in some configurations).

**Equipment Setup:**
1. **Power Source** — DC constant current (similar to TIG).
2. **Tungsten Electrode** — Positioned inside the nozzle (non-consumable).
3. **Plasma Gas** — Usually Argon, fed through the nozzle.
4. **Shielding Gas** — Argon or Argon-Helium mixture around the outer envelope.
5. **Water-Cooled Copper Nozzle** — Critical for thermal management and arc constriction.
6. **Pilot Arc** — A low-current arc is first established between electrode and nozzle (using HF or high voltage) to initiate the main arc (especially in non-transferred mode).

**Two Variants:**
- **Transferred Arc (TA)**: The arc jumps from the electrode to the workpiece; the workpiece is part of the electrical circuit. Higher energy concentration.
- **Non-Transferred Arc (NTA)**: The arc is between the electrode and the nozzle; primarily used for plasma cutting and spraying.

**Comparison with Conventional TIG Welding:**

| **Feature** | **PAW** | **TIG/GTAW** |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Constriction** | Yes (water-cooled nozzle) | No (free-burning arc) |
| **Power Density** | Higher ($10^3$–$10^5$ W/mm²) | Lower ($10^2$–$10^3$ W/mm²) |
| **Penetration** | Up to 12–15 mm in one pass | 3–6 mm typical |
| **Welding Speed** | Higher | Lower |
| **Orifice Gas** | Plasma gas (high velocity) | No plasma gas |
| **Applications** | Stainless steel tubes, aerospace | General fabrication |

**Applications of PAW:**
- **Aerospace**: Honeycomb structures, jet engine components.
- **Tube and Pipe Manufacturing**: Stainless steel tube mills (high-speed longitudinal welding).
- **Nuclear Industry**: Stainless steel reactor components.
- **Microplasma Welding**: Welding of foils, mesh, and thin sheets (currents as low as 0.1 A).
- **Automotive**: Sensor housings, exhaust components.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B(b): Underwater Welding (7 marks)

**Introduction:**

Underwater welding refers to welding operations performed in a marine or aquatic environment. It is essential for:
- Repair of offshore structures (oil rigs, platforms, pipelines).
- Ship hull repair and maintenance.
- Submarine structural work.
- Underwater pipeline construction.

**Classification:**

### 1. Wet Underwater Welding

**Method:** The welding is performed directly in the water using specially designed, water-resistant electrodes.

**Process Details:**
- The arc burns in the surrounding water, which vaporizes and forms a **bubble** around the weld zone.
- The arc is **not shielded** by inert gas; protection is provided by the electrode coating (slag and gas shield).
- The welder-diver uses waterproof electrodes and equipment.

**Electrode Types Used:**
- **Rutile-coated (E6013-type)** — Most common, good operability, limited depth.
- **Cellulosic-coated (E6010-type)** — High hydrogen content, deep penetration, favored for wet welding.
- **Basic-coated (E7018-type)** — Low hydrogen, better mechanical properties, requires careful handling.

**Limitations:**
- Maximum practical depth: **~50 m** (beyond this, pressure affects arc stability).
- **Hydrogen-induced cracking** is a major concern due to rapid cooling.
- **Porosity** from gas entrapment.
- **Limited weld quality** compared to dry welding.
- **Safety hazards**: Electric shock, decompression sickness, marine life.

**Applications:**
- Emergency ship hull repairs.
- Temporary fixes on offshore platforms.
- Salvage operations.

### 2. Dry Underwater Welding (Hyperbaric Welding)

**Method:** The welding is performed in a **pressurized chamber** (habitat) that is:
- Sealed around the work area.
- Filled with a **breathing gas mixture** (typically **Helium + Oxygen**, or Helium + Argon + Oxygen) at a pressure equal to the surrounding water pressure.

**Advantages:**
- Welding quality approaches that of surface welding.
- Better visual control and inspection.
- Reduced risk of hydrogen cracking (controlled atmosphere).
- Higher mechanical properties of the weld.

**Types of Hyperbaric Chambers:**
1. **Wet Habitat** — Chamber is open at the bottom (diver can swim in/out).
2. **Dry Habitat** — Chamber is sealed; welder is transported in pressurized.

**Process Variants:**
- **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** — Most common in hyperbaric.
- **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — Also used with special electrodes.
- **Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** — Emerging for hyperbaric applications.

**Applications:**
- Permanent repairs of offshore platforms and pipelines.
- Submarine pressure hull repairs.
- Deep-sea pipeline tie-ins.
- Nuclear power plant cooling pipe repairs.

**Key Differences Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Wet Welding** | **Dry Hyperbaric Welding** |
|---|---|---|
| **Environment** | Open water | Pressurized chamber |
| **Gas Shielding** | From electrode coating | External inert gas (Ar/He) |
| **Quality** | Lower | Near surface quality |
| **Depth** | Up to ~50 m | Up to 300+ m |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high |
| **Setup Time** | Minimal | Extensive |

**Key Safety Codes & Standards:**
- **AWS D3.6M** — Underwater Welding Code (American Welding Society).
- **ASME Section IX** — Pressure vessel and pipeline welding qualifications.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUTS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EBW with LBW**: Students often interchange "electron" and "photon" in definitions. **Remember**: EBW uses **charged particles in vacuum**; LBW uses **photons (light) in air or shielding gas**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the vacuum in EBW**: Many students describe EBW without mentioning the vacuum environment. This is a **guaranteed 1-mark loss** in KTU exams.
>
> 3. **Mistaking FSW as a fusion process**: FSW is **solid-state** — there is **NO melting** in the stir zone. Writing that "the metal melts and solidifies" in FSW will cost 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong pressure range in Diffusion Bonding**: Students often quote diffusion bonding pressure as 100+ MPa. The actual range is typically **5–50 MPa** with high temperature compensating.
>
> 5. **Confusion between PAW and Plasma Cutting**: PAW uses lower current and **transferred arc** for welding; plasma cutting uses higher current and **non-transferred arc** for material removal.
>
> 6. **Underwater Welding Depth**: Writing "unlimited depth" for wet welding is wrong — practical limit is **~50 m**. For dry welding, mark down to **300+ m** for high-pressure hyperbaric systems.
>
> 7. **Explosive Welding**: Do **NOT** confuse the "flyer plate" terminology with the "base plate." The flyer is the one that detonates, the base is stationary.
>
> 8. **Units in Numerical Problems**: KTU strictly checks units. Always express penetration in **mm**, power density in **W/mm²**, and heat input in **J/mm**.

- **Diffusion Bonding**: Solid-state; 0.5–0.8 Tₘ; pressure-assisted atomic diffusion.
- **Ultrasonic Welding**: 20–40 kHz vibrations + static pressure; thin foils/electronics.
- **Underwater Welding**: Wet (coated electrodes) vs. Dry (hyperbaric chamber).
- **Explosive Welding**: Detonation-driven flyer plate; wavy interface; 200–800 m/s.

### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize
- **Power density threshold for keyhole mode**: > **10⁶ W/mm²**.
- **FSW invention**: **TWI, UK, 1991**.
- **EBW vacuum level**: **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ torr**.
- **Ultrasonic frequency**: **20–40 kHz**.
- **Diffusion Bonding temperature**: **0.5 to 0.8 Tₘ**.
- **Explosive welding flyer velocity**: **200–800 m/s**.
- **Microplasma current**: as low as **0.1 A**.
- **Typical EBW penetration**: up to **300 mm in one pass**.

### ✅ Key Formulas
- Power Density: $P_d = P/A$ where $A = \pi d^2/4$
- Heat Input: $H = \eta V I / v$
- Electron Range: $R = 2.1 \times 10^{-11} \cdot V^2 / \rho$
- FSW Heat: $Q = (2/3) \pi \mu P \omega R^3$
- Fick's Law: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$

### ✅ Key Comparisons
- **D/W ratio**: EBW (25:1) > LBW (10:1) > PAW (3:1) > Conventional arc (1:1).
- **Atmosphere**: EBW (vacuum); LBW/PAW (shielding gas); FSW/USW/DB (ambient or vacuum).

### ✅ Key Applications Mapping
- **Aerospace**: EBW (turbine blades), FSW (aluminum airframes), LBW (tailor-welded blanks).
- **Nuclear**: EBW (fuel cladding), PAW (reactor components).
- **Marine**: Underwater welding (hulls), EXW (bimetallic transitions).
- **Electronics**: USW (battery tabs), micro-PAW (sensor packages).

### ✅ Examiner's Favorite Topics
1. EBW vs. LBW comparison.
2. FSW tool design and material flow.
3. Stages of Diffusion Bonding.
4. Power density vs. weld mode (conduction vs. keyhole).
5. Wet vs. dry underwater welding.

A well-labeled table comparing two processes (e.g., EBW vs. LBW) can earn you **bonus presentation marks** as per KTU 2024 evaluation rubrics.
